bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below 
!
ip multicast-routing
ip pim version 1
ip pim dense-mode
!
ip multicast route-limit 1500 1460
ip multicast vrf ABC route-limit 1500 1460
!
bestPracticeDescription: To limit the number of multicast routes (mroutes) that can be added to a multicast routing table, use the 'ip multicast route-limit' command in global configuration mode. To disable this configuration, use the 'no' form of this command. The 'ip multicast route-limit' command limits the number of multicast routes that can be added to a router and generates an error message when the limit is exceeded. If the user sets the threshold argument, a threshold error message is generated when the threshold is exceeded, and the message continues to occur until the number of mroutes reaches the limit set by the limit argument. The mroute warning threshold must not exceed the mroute limit.Global mroute state limiters are used to limit the number of mroutes that can be added to the global table on a router. Configuring a global mroute state limiter can protect a router in the event of a multicast DoS attack (by preventing mroutes from overrunning the router).Per VRF mroute state limiters are used to limit the number of mroutes that can be added to an MVRF table on a Multicast VPN (MVPN) provider edge (PE) router. Configuring per MVRF mroute state limits can be used to ensure the fair sharing of mroutes between different MVRFs on an MVPN PE router. IP multicast is a bandwidth-conserving technology that reduces traffic by delivering a single stream of information simultaneously to potentially thousands of businesses and homes. Applications that take advantage of multicast include video conferencing, corporate communications, distance learning, and distribution of software, stock quotes, and news.This module contains a technical overview of IP multicast. IP multicast is an efficient way to use network resources, especially for bandwidth-intensive services such as audio and video.
bestPracticeRecommendation: This command is a best practice to be configured on all routers in a PIM domain. The command is used to control the multicast routes in the PIM domain. It is applied per device and helps the administrator monitor the domain and plan or investigate if there is an increase in the routes through the preconfigured threshold.
bestPracticeTitle: ip multicast route-limit command is not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: AAA authentication login console group
bestPracticeDescription: Access to the console must be protected with authentication. This requires configuration of the console to use AAA.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure RADIUS or TACACS+ authentication for use by the console. Note: In case RADIUS or TACACS servers are unreachable,the system falls back to local authentication by default.
bestPracticeTitle: Console not protected by AAA

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router ospfv3 process_id
log adjacency changes detail
bestPracticeDescription: This enables logging of OSPFv3 adjacency/neighbor changes. Unexpected neighbor resets might indicate high error rates or high packet loss in the network and should be investigated.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable OSPFv3 adjacency logging to assist in identifying OSPFv3 adjacency state changes.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPFv3 Neighbor Logging Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If other reliable methods of monitoring the memory utilization are used (RMON,external polling),then this command may not be required. Some platforms do not separate I/O memory and Processor memory. Even though this command is accepted on that platform,it will likely have little effect.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: memory free low-watermark processor threshold
bestPracticeDescription:
The Memory Threshold Notification feature allows you to mitigate low-memory conditions on a device. This feature uses two methods to accomplish this: Memory Threshold Notification and Memory Reservation. Memory Threshold Notification generates a log message in order to indicate that free memory on a device has fallen lower than the configured threshold. This enables a device to generate a notification when available free memory falls lower than the specified threshold, and again when available free memory rises higher than the specified threshold.
This feature was introduced in 12.0(26)S, 12.2(18)S, and 12.3(4)T, so only devices where the feature is available will be considered for best practice matching.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Proactive monitoring of free processor memory is recommended to avoid unplanned outages in devices that exhaust free memory. Specific threshold settings will depend on platform and features implemented. It is recommended to baseline performance of the devices in the network,and from that baseline determine appropriate thresholds for the specific network environment. As a rule of thumb,though,any time free memory goes below 15%,it should be investigated as this could have a negative impact on operation of the device. Ensure that you have also implemented a system to capture,monitor,and alert on the syslog messages in real-time.
bestPracticeTitle: Memory Threshold Notifications (Processor) Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If a different facility is used throughout the network, by design, then this exception is not applicable.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Please review information for logging facility.
bestPracticeDescription: Syslog facilities are used to separate messages when received by a syslog server. The default facility for Cisco devices is local7. This is also the default used by CiscoWorks. This exception is highlighting devices changed from the default setting
bestPracticeRecommendation: Review the changed setting and ensure the syslog configuration on the syslog server is properly handling messages for the facility. If there is no reason for using a different facility, the recommendation is to use local7, especially if CiscoWorks is the syslog daemon.
bestPracticeTitle: Syslog facility default not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: With NTP authentication enabled, unauthenticated packets are still accepted, which is the lowest validity. To prevent a device from accepting NTP updates from unauthorized servers, use an access list.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
!
ntp authentication-key 777 md5 cisco-ntp
ntp authenticate
ntp trusted-key 777
ntp update-calendar
ntp server 10.100.0.1 key 777 prefer
!
bestPracticeDescription: NTP is an important tool for troubleshooting and event correlation. Any network device that is accepting NTP should be protected by a firewall or access lists, or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized usage. NTP is disabled by default.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is a good practice in a large network to synchronize time across all devices using NTP.  However, falsifying time or a time source is a useful tool to mask any unauthorized activity. Network devices should accept time only from authorized servers. Use both access lists and MD5 to protect NTP listening devices.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP authentication not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: The network administrator may want to permit ICMP unreachable messages in certain environments. For example, ICMP type 3, code 1; code 3; and code 4 are required for the SMTP e-mail gateway, Path MTU discovery, etc.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface interface_name_number
snip
ipv4 unreachables disable
!
bestPracticeDescription: If the Cisco IOS XR software receives a nonbroadcast packet destined for itself that uses a protocol it does not recognize, it sends an ICMP unreachable message to the source. Also, if the software receives a datagram that it cannot deliver to its ultimate destination because it knows of no route to the destination address, it replies to the originator of that datagram with an ICMP host unreachable message. The ipv4 unreachables command affects all types of ICMP unreachable messages and is enabled by default in support of RFC 792. However, ICMP unreachables can potentially be used to map a network by correlating which addresses generate ICMP unreachables and which do not for networks, hosts, protocols, and ports.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Control ICMP unreachable responses in certain environments, especially along untrusted edges between administrative domains, with the interface command ipv4 unreachables disable on affected interfaces. ICMP unreachables covers network, host, protocol, and port unreachable messages. Also, in IOS XR software, the ICMP unreachable messages are rate-limited default. The default value is one ICMP destination unreachable message every 500 milliseconds. Using the configuration command icmp ipv4 rate-limit unreachable [DF] milliseconds.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv4 ICMP Unreachables Enabled on All Interfaces

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: enable graceful-restart command using 'graceful-restart' command under mpls ldp mode.
bestPracticeDescription: LDP graceful restart, provides a control plane mechanism to ensure high availability, allows detection and recovery from failure conditions while preserving Non-Stop Forwarding (NSF) services. Graceful restart is a way to recover from signaling and control plane failures without impacting forwarding. Without LDP graceful restart, when an established session fails, the corresponding forwarding states are cleaned immediately from the restarting and peer nodes. In this case LDP forwarding will have to restart from the beginning, causing a potential loss of data and connectivity. The LDP graceful restart capability is negotiated between two peers during session initialization time, in FT SESSION TLV. In this typed length value (TLV), each peer advertises the following information to its peers: Reconnect time: the maximum time that other peer will wait for this LSR to reconnect after control channel failure. Recovery time: Max time that other peer has on its side to reinstate or refresh its states with this LSR. This time is used only during session reestablishment after earlier session failure. FT flag: This flag indicates whether a restart could restore the preserved (local) node state. Once the graceful restart session parameters are conveyed and session is up and running, graceful restart procedures are activated.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable graceful-restart to detect and recover from failure conditions while preserving Non-Stop Forwarding (NSF) services. Graceful restart is a way to recover from signaling and control plane failures without impacting forwarding.
bestPracticeTitle: MPLS LDP graceful-restart is not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
In some situations,rapid bootup time may be preferable to waiting to run full diagnostics. In mission critical,single-point-of-failure situations some customers may wish to quantify the impact of complete versus minimal (or bypass) bootup diagnostics through specific lab testing/proof-of-concept type activities (i.e. chassis/module/OS involved may influence a switch's total boot-time).

Important: the "complete" diagnostic CLI was present in some IOS versions before officially supported (i.e. configurable in 12.1(8b)E13,but not "officially" supported). The minimum "officially-supported" IOS versions are detailed above in the Recommendations section.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure diagnostic level complete using the following commands:
"diagnostic level complete"        - IOS 12.1E / IOS 12.2SY
"diagnostic bootup level complete" - IOS 12.2SX
bestPracticeDescription:
"Complete" diagnostics during bootup helps ensure the switch is ready to perform normal operations. The full diagnostic outputs may be useful for both the customer and Cisco TAC engineering in identifying hardware related problems during acceptance testing or troubleshooting activities i.e. following an unscheduled crash or restart event.

The default diagnostic level of "minimal" (or the "bypass" level option available in IOS 12.1E or 12.2SY) is less likely to identify lower-level hardware problems and therefore may impact the time taken to diagnose some types of hardware-related failure.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends switches be set to run "complete" diagnostics to provide maximum fault detection and prevent outages during normal operations.

The complete diagnostics feature is currently supported across the following platforms:

Cat6500 Sup2/PFC2; IOS 12.2(17d)SXB,12.1(11b)E,12.2(14)SY onwards 
Cat6500 Sup720; IOS 12.2(14)SX onwards 
Cat7600 Sup720; IOS 12.2(14)SX onwards 
Cat7600 Sup2/PFC2 IOS 12.1(11b)E onwards
bestPracticeTitle: Complete Power-on Diagnostics Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
snmp-server source-interface trap   
or 
snmp-server host  source-interface
bestPracticeDescription: SNMP will choose an address from the interface IP address close to the trap destination. Configuring the SNMP traps from a known and consistent address will assist with correlation and analysis when the events are received by the trap receiver.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Specify the source interface for traps. This allows security devices such as firewalls to identify the source of the SNMP packet.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP trap source not defined

bestPracticeCaveat:
Resource Reservation Protocol (RSVP) Multiprotocol Label Switching traffic engineering (MPLS TE), Internet Group Management Protocol Version 2 (IGMPv2), and other protocols that use IP options packets may not function in drop or ignore modes.
On the Cisco 10720 Internet router, the 'ip option ignore' command is not supported. Only drop mode (the 'ip option drop' command) is supported.
The 'ip option ignore' command (ignore mode) is supported only on the Cisco 12000 series router.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Drop packets with ip options using the command:
!
ip options drop
!
bestPracticeDescription: The ACL IP Options Selective Drop feature allows a router to filter IP options packets, thereby mitigating the effects of these packets on a router and downstream routers, and perform the following actions: Drop all IP options packets that it receives and prevent options from going deeper into the network. Ignore IP options packets destined for the router and treat them as if they had no IP option.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Benefits of Using ACL IP Options Selective Drop that Drop mode filters packets from the network and relieves downstream routers and hosts of the load from options packets. Drop mode minimizes loads to the Route Processor (RP) for options that require RP processing on distributed systems. Previously, the packets were always routed to or processed by the RP CPU. Now, the ignore and drop forms prevent the packets from impacting the RP performance.
bestPracticeTitle: IP options allowed

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the memory traps with the 'snmp-server enable traps memory' command in the global configuration mode.
bestPracticeDescription: To enable a device to send Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications when memory pool buffer usage reaches a new peak, use the 'snmp-server enable traps memory' command in global configuration mode. To stop notifications from being generated, use the 'no' form of this command. SNMP notifications can be sent as traps or inform requests. This command enables both traps and inform requests. This command enables or disables memory buffer peak (cempMemBufferNotify) notifications. When they are enabled, these notifications are sent when the value of the maximum number of buffer objects changes. In current releases of Cisco IOS software, this command has the same behavior if you use or omit the 'bufferpeak' keyword.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the memory traps to ensure that notifications for memory events are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: Memory SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
line vty
 exec-timeout 10
bestPracticeDescription: Idle sessions consume lines and could prevent legitimate users from accessing the device. Any setting of exec-timeout greater than 30 minutes is considered too high. The default timeout is 30 minutes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure terminal sessions to time out after an appropriate interval of inactivity, generally between 10 to 20 minutes, but not more than the default 30 minutes.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY exec timeout too high

bestPracticeCaveat: In the event that this is a nonredundant system connected to a nonredundant system, NSF will not be possible. However, as technologies have evolved, and cost points have constantly come down, it is expected that in many topologies, many of the components will have, at some point, redundant capabilities. As such, it is highly advantageous to preprovision network devices to support NSF such that future device upgrades do not require changes to adjacent devices to enable the feature. In this recommendation, operational simplicity and consistency dictate that NSF should be enabled in the configuration templates for all devices in the network to minimize human error causing traffic impact on a failover operation.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below 

!
version 15.0
!
router eigrp 100
 network 10.0.0.0
 network 50.0.0.0
 network 20.0.0.0
 no auto-summary
 nsf
!
bestPracticeDescription: Nonstop Forwarding (NSF) awareness allows an NSF-aware router to assist NSF-capable and NSF-aware neighbors to continue forwarding packets during a switchover operation or during a well-known failure condition.The EIGRP Nonstop Forwarding Awareness feature allows an NSF-aware router that is running Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) to forward packets along routes known to a router performing a switchover operation or in a well-known failure condition.This capability allows the EIGRP peers of the failing router to retain the routing information that it has advertised and to continue using this information until the failed router resumes normal operation and is able to exchange routing information.The peering session is maintained throughout the entire NSF operation. Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol(EIGRP) is an advanced distance-vector routing protocol that is used on a computer network for automating routing decisions and configuration. The protocol was designed by Cisco Systems as a proprietary protocol, available only on Cisco routers.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended that all redundant systems have NSF configured. It is recommended that all non redundant systems, which connect to NSF-capable devices, have NSF enabled to support the NSF operation.
bestPracticeTitle: NSF for EIGRP is not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no router ospf name
bestPracticeDescription: In IOS-XR, every OSPF process is spawned as an instance under the parent OSPF process. An instance is started even for an unused process, a process that is not bound to any interface on the router. An instance has its own set of overheads, and so it is a best practice to limit the number of ospf processes to the required minimum and remove unused processes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Delete any unused OSPF process.
bestPracticeTitle: Unused OSPF process configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure ' ntp source ' or 
'ntp source-interface ' or
'ntp server .* use-vrf' in the global config mode
bestPracticeDescription: This command sets the default IP source address for the NTP packets. Hard coding the NTP source address is easier to use Access-Control Lists (ACLs) to manage NTP security.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Any interface could be used as the source interface. The reason we recommend out-of-band management is that the management traffic uses a different infrastructure and path than production traffic, rather than inband traffic. This means the management traffic uses the same path than the production traffic, in Data Centers. If  inband management is used, the recommendation is to use Loopback because loopback interface is not susceptible to issues faced with physical interfaces such as bad interface or device connection failure. Also, the device will be seen at the NMS always with the same IP regardless of the outgoing interface / path it uses. If you prefer out-of-band management, the first choice is the mgmt0, which has the Nexus switches. Also if you are using the Nexus as L2 switch, you do not have dynamic routing, that is, Loopback is hard to use / not usable for operating. Overall, the recommended source interface could be loopback or management interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP source interface not defined

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
no arp $$ip-address $$hardware-address $$encapsulation-type
or
no arp [vrf $$vrf-name] $$ip-address $$hardware-address $$encapsulation-type
bestPracticeDescription: Most hosts support dynamic resolution, meaning it is not neccesary to specify static ARP cache entries. Static entries are permanent entries that map a network layer address (IPv4 address) to a data-link layer address (MAC address). When configured it may function as a work around or temporary caveat.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The Best Practice recommendation is not to configure static ARP cache entries.
bestPracticeTitle: arp (vrf vrf-name) configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Minimize the "load-interval" value, minimum value is 30, as in 30 seconds.
bestPracticeDescription: The "Load-Interval" argument defines the sampling rate for interface throughput on a particular interface. The best practice is to set this as low as possible for an accurate sampling rate. An accurate sampling rate assists in troubleshooting and applications which require accurate throughput information such as DOCSIS Load-balancing, Subscriber Traffic Enforcement, and QoS.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends minimum load-interval value, it is "load-interval 30" on this CMTS.
bestPracticeTitle: Load-Interval not minimized

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Router(config-if)# switchport trunk allowed vlan {none |{{add | except | remove} vlan[, vlan[, vlan[, ...]]}}
bestPracticeDescription: Clearing VLANs from Trunks helps prevent STP being propogated to switches that don't need it for that VLAN
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends pruning VLANs from trunks where they are not needed to maximize STP stability.
bestPracticeTitle: VLAN trunk allowed list not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: To enable this, add the command:"log-adjacency-changes" under each "router isis" section.
bestPracticeDescription: If running IS-IS routing protocol, there is a possibility of generating a syslog message when there is a neighbor status change. This rule is verifying that the generation of such a log message is enabled. Use the log adjacency changes command to monitor IS-IS adjacency state changes; it may be very useful when monitoring large networks. Default an IS-IS instance to generate a log message when an Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS) adjacency changes state (up or down) is disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended for a configuration to log neighbor changes on a router running IS-IS. This will give the network operating center (the team monitoring the network) an alarm if there is a change in the IS-IS neighbor status.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS Neighbor Logging not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: MPP has no SYSLOGcapability to detect IPv4 host that are denied to access the router.

Configuration Example:
control-plane
 management-plane
  inband
   interface all
    allow Telnet peer
     address ipv4 $IPv4_prefix/$mask
bestPracticeDescription: Management Plane Protection has the capability to filter on IP prefixes within hardware (LPTS). The network element has not enabled MPP address IPv4.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure global cable wideband auto-reset.
bestPracticeDescription:
The command cable wideband auto-reset has IOS reset DOCSIS 3.0 modems, when a bonding group becomes available either due to configuration change, configuration addition, or existing bonding group changes.
This helps service providers ensure their DOCSIS 3.0 subscribers are not operating in DOCSIS 2.0 mode or earlier if the resources are available for DOCSIS 3.0 mode.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends in most cases to enable cable wideband auto-reset as this is a desirable behavior. Typically when a bonding group is now available, you want those modems capable of taking advantage of the new bonding group to reset and come into DOCSIS 3.0 operating mode.
Some providers purposely choose to leave this configuration out or turn off cable wideband auto-reset. If this is the design, then one should consider clear cable modem wideband registered-traditional-docsis instead of initiating reset D3.0 capable modem when changes are made to bonding group.
bestPracticeTitle: Cable Optimization - Wideband Auto-Reset

bestPracticeCaveat: Cisco NX-OS does not support the SNMPv3 noAuthNoPriv security level.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Use the 'snmp-server host' command to configure SNMP version 3 traps.

snmp-server host ip-address {traps | informs} version 3 {auth | noauth | priv} username 

!
snmp-server host 10.26.200.89 traps version 3 auth GiaasSnmpUser 
!
bestPracticeDescription: SNMPv3 provides secure access to devices by a combination of authenticating and encrypting frames over the network. The security features provided in SNMPv3 are the following: Message integrity?Ensures that a packet has not been tampered with in-transit. Authentication?Determines the message is from a valid source. Encryption?Scrambles the packet contents to prevent it from being seen by unauthorized sources.SNMPv3 provides for both security models and security levels. A security model is an authentication strategy that is set up for a user and the role in which the user resides. A security level is the permitted level of security within a security model. A combination of a security model and a security level determines which security mechanism is employed when handling an SNMP packet.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommend to use SNMPv3 to increase security because it has authentication and encryption capabilities for username, passwords, and payload data.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP v3 Traps not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
'no ipv4 redirects' under interface	configuration


RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config)# interface gigabitethernet 0/1/1/0
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-if)# no ipv4 redirects
bestPracticeDescription: ICMP redirect messages are used by routers to notify the hosts on the data link that a better route is available for a particular destination. Cisco routers send ICMP redirects when all of these conditions are met: The interface on which the packet comes into the router is the same interface on which the packet gets routed out. The subnet or network of the source IP address is on the same subnet or network of the next-hop IP address of the routed packet. The datagram is not source-routed. The kernel is configured to send redirects. (By default, Cisco routers send ICMP redirects. The interface subcommand no ip redirects can be used to disable ICMP redirects.)
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to disable ICMP redirects. It improves security because if someone inserts another router on the network that the admins may not know about, it will not send the devices traffic to the other questionable device.
bestPracticeTitle: ICMP redirects not disabled on an Interface

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
By default, each IP address assigned by a DHCP Server comes with a one-day lease, which is the amount of time that the address is valid. To change the lease value for an IP address, use the following command in DHCP pool configuration mode:
!
ip dhcp pool 1
  lease 30
!
bestPracticeDescription: In order to trace, audit, and investigate suspicious activity, DHCP servers must have the minimum duration of the lease time configured to 30 days or more.
bestPracticeRecommendation: DHCP lease should be 30 days or more.
bestPracticeTitle: DHCP lease time low or infinite

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
snmp-server ifindex persist
bestPracticeDescription: One of the identifiers most commonly used in SNMP-based network management applications is the interface index (ifIndex) value. IfIndex is a unique identifying number associated with a physical or logical interface; as far as most software is concerned, the ifIndex is the "name" of the interface. Use the 'snmp-server ifindex persist' command to enable ifIndex persistence on all interfaces with entries in the ifIndex table of the IF-MIB. When enabled, this command retains the mapping between the ifName object values and the ifIndex object values (generated from the IF-MIB) persistent during reloads, allowing for consistent identification of specific interfaces using SNMP. Defaults: Global interface persistence is disabled. Config -- Global if "snmp-server ifindex persist" = not configured exception == true else exception == false endif;
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Although there is no requirement in the relevant RFCs that the correspondence between particular
ifIndex values and their interfaces be maintained across reboots, applications such as device inventory, 
billing, and fault detection increasingly depend on the maintenance of this correspondence. Therefore, 
it is recommended to turn on ifindex persistence.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP Interface Index Persistence not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Static routes to NULL0, dialer and dhcp are not subject to this rule.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Static routes should include both the next-hop ip address and the outbound interface to prevent recursive lookups and to prevent packet looping if code version uses summary-routes when next-hop address is not in the routing table.  

This configuration applies to both global routes and vrf-lite routes.

Generic Syntax: ip route [vrf ]    

Here is a sample config:
Serial Interface:
Router(config)#ip route 172.31.10.0 255.255.255.0 Serial1/0

Device(config)#ip route vrf tstvrf 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 Serial1/1

Ethernet Interface:
Router(config)#ip route 192.0.2.0 255.255.255.254 Ethernet1/2 192.0.2.4

Device(config)#ip route vrf tstvrf 10.0.0.0 255.255.255.0 Ethernet1/4 10.1.1.1
bestPracticeDescription: Certain code trains may encounter routing loops when recursive static routes are configured. This rule covers all code trains, as upgrades/downgrades to affected versions may encounter problems (such as 12.2SR code). The problem occurs when the route to a specific next-hop address is removed from the routing table, and a less specific route resides in the routing table. Code trains such as 12.2SR will use the less specific route and may cause routing loops. If a static route is pointed to an interface and the next hop IP address is not specified, the route is inserted into the routing table only when the interface is active. This configuration is not recommended because when the static route points to an interface and has no next hop information, the router considers each of the hosts within the range of the route to be directly connected through that interface and performs Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) on the Ethernet for every destination that the router finds through the default route because the router considers all of these destinations as directly connected to that interface. This kind of static route, especially if it is used by many packets to many different destination subnets, can cause high processor utilization and a very large ARP cache(along with memory allocation failures). Therefore, this kind of static route is not recommended. If there is the possibility that the interface with the next hop goes down and the next hop would become reachable through a recursive route, the both the next hop IP address and the alternate interface through which the next hop should be found need to be specified. The addition of the alternate interface enables the static route installation to become more deterministic.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
It is highly recommended that the static routes should include both the next-hop address and the outbound interface to ensure that recursive lookups are not triggered. 
In the case of a serial interface, the specification of the outbound interface is sufficient because a serial interface is a point-to-point interface. 
If the outbound interface is an Ethernet interface, then both the outbound interface and the next hop address must be configured.
bestPracticeTitle: Static Route Missing Parameters

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
controller SONET R/S/I/P
report lias
bestPracticeDescription: SONET Reports line alarm indication signal (LAIS) has not been configured, which is disabled by default. Reporting an alarm means that the alarm can be logged to the SYSLOG. SONET alarm hierarchy rules dictate only the most severe alarm of an alarm group is reported. Whether an alarm is reported or not, you can check the current state of masked alarm (a problem indication that is a candidate for an alarm) by displaying the "Masked Alarms" line in the 'show controllers sonet' command output.
bestPracticeRecommendation: For SONET root cause analysis configure 'report lias'.
bestPracticeTitle: report lais (SONET) not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Set the 'ssh timeout' to a default of 30 seconds using: 'no ssh timeout' or set a value to be less than 60 seconds.
bestPracticeDescription: The 'ssh timeout' command is used to configure the timeout value for user authentication to AAA. If the user fails to authenticate itself to AAA within the configured time, the connection is aborted. If no value is configured, the default value of 30 seconds is used.
bestPracticeRecommendation: 'ssh timeout' should not be configured for more than 60 seconds.
bestPracticeTitle: High SSH Timeout is Not Recommended

bestPracticeCaveat: You cannot use MD5 encryption with protocols that require clear text passwords, such as Challenge Handshake Authentication Protocol (CHAP).

Configuration as given below :
!
username testuser secret 5 32kjdudTkw72L
!
bestPracticeDescription: Global user accounts can be created on devices as a method of authentication, especially for AAA fallback if the primary methods are not available. If a username isn't required or permitted by local security policy, it should be removed.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
In later versions of sofware, the username password can be protected with MD5.  The
command syntax "username (someuser) secret (somepass) is available in software 
later than 12.0(18)S, 12.1(8a)E, and 12.2(8)T and will yield an MD5 password.  It is
recommended to protect the local user account with MD5.

router(config)#username testuser secret testpass
router#sho run | i username
username testuser secret 5 enCr1pteDk3Y

This feature should also be enabled when upgrading to newer software that supports it.
bestPracticeTitle: A user account is not protected with MD5

bestPracticeCaveat:
This rule only applies for the following Cisco IOS versions:
12.2(18)S and later in 12.2S
12.0(26)S and later in 12.0S
12.2SXB and later in 12.2SX*
all 12.2SR* trains
12.3(4)T and later in 12.3T
12.4 and later

Note that this rule does not apply to Catalyst 2360/2960/3560/3650/3750/4500/4900.  CSCsb65707 would cause one of these switches to not reload if 'file verify auto' is configured.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
In global configuration, enable 'file verify auto'.  This can be done as follows:

router#configure terminal
router(config)#file verify auto
router(config)#exit
router#
bestPracticeDescription: Image verification is accomplished by verifying the compressed Cisco IOS image checksum. Image verification allows users to automatically verify the integrity of all Cisco IOS images. Thus, users can be sure that the image is protected from accidental corruption, which can occur at any time during transit, starting from the moment the files are generated by Cisco until they reach the user (which includes the time when it is downloaded from Cisco.com or during the installation process). A reload on a corrupted file may result in Device outage. The 'file verify auto command' enables image verification globally; that is, all images that are to be copied (via the copy command) or reloaded (via the reload command) are automatically verified. Although both the copy and reload commands have a /verify keyword that enables image verification, this keyword must be issued each time during a copy or reload. The 'file verify auto' command enables image verification by default so it is not needed to specify image verification multiple times.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Network administrators can use the 'file verify auto' global configuration command to enable verification of all images that are either copied using the 'copy privileged' EXEC command or loaded using the 'reload privileged' EXEC command. These images are automatically verified for image file integrity.
bestPracticeTitle: IOS Image Verification not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the authentication traps with the  'snmp-server enable traps snmp authentication' command  in the global config mode.
bestPracticeDescription: To enable the RFC 1157 Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications, use the 'snmp-server enable traps snmp' command in global configuration mode.  An authenticationFailure(4) trap signifies that the sending protocol entity is the addressee of a protocol message that is not properly authenticated.  While implementations of the SNMP must be capable of generating this trap, they must also be capable of suppressing the emission of such traps via an implementation-specific mechanism.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the authentication traps to ensure that notifications for authentication events are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: Authentication SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: In routers that have a small number of interfaces, you can choose to manually set the 'passive-interface' command on the interfaces where adjacency is not desired, instead of using the 'passive-interface default' command. Also, the rule does not apply if all layer 3 interfaces are designed to participate in the EIGRP domain.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Under the 'router eigrp' block of configuration, configure the following. The parameters in front of the 'no passive-interface' command are those interfaces that need to participate in EIGRP and form router adjacencies.

router eigrp 10
 passive-interface default
 no passive-interface gigabitethernet 0/0/0
 network 10.0.0.0
 network 192.0.2.0
 no auto-summary
bestPracticeDescription: In large service provider and Enterprise networks, some distribution-layer routers often have a large number of interfaces, for example, at the WAN edge. A common practice to facilitate the configuration of a routing protocol on such routers is to enable the routing processes on a network range matching several of the interfaces. While this technique facilitates the configuration of the routing protocol, enabling routing indiscriminately on several or all interfaces may increase the chances for the insertion of unauthorized routing peers. Also, unnecessary routing protocol exchanges increase CPU overhead on the router. To prevent these problems, one can set all interfaces as passive by default with the 'passive-interface default' command. This command changes the configuration logic to a default passive; therefore, interfaces where router adjacencies are expected need to be configured with the 'no passive-interface' command. Setting an interface as passive disables the sending of routing updates on that interface; hence, adjacencies will not be formed in Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) or Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP). However, the particular subnet will continue to be advertised to other interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure 'passive-interface default' under the EIGRP routing process and specifically enable the interfaces where routing adjacencies are to be formed, using the 'no passive-interface ' command. This gives more control for administering EIGRP. There is no need to send hellos and form adjacencies on user VLANs or stub networks where there are no EIGRP-speaking neighbors on those interfaces, as doing so would just consume more CPU cycles and potentially allow insertion of unauthorized routing peers. It is especially important to turn off routing protocol exchanges in switched LAN environments or when there are a lot of L3 interfaces on the router, and not all are participating in EIGRP.
bestPracticeTitle: Default EIGRP Passive Interface not configured.

bestPracticeCaveat:
Authorization is only supported with TACACS+.

There may be times when you do not want to run authorization from a particular interface or line. To stop authorization activities on designated lines or interfaces, use the none method keyword. If you select this method, authorization is disabled for all actions.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: aaa authorization exec [default|NAMEDLIST] group [tacacs+|NAMEDMETHOD] local if-authenticated
bestPracticeDescription: Command authorization with TACACS+ and AAA provides a mechanism that permits or denies each command that is entered by an administrative user. When the user enters EXEC commands, Cisco IOS sends each command to the configured AAA server. The AAA server then uses its configured policies in order to permit or deny the command for that particular user.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Authorization should be configured using the default or a named method for EXEC to network devices.  When the AAA server is not reachable:

 - The 'local' method will allow for authorization based on local authorization defined using the 'username' global configuration command.
 - The 'if-authenticated' command will allow users to have access to the functions they request as long as they have been previously authenticated.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA authorization EXEC shell not configured properly

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable password strength-check using the global command 'password strength-check'.
bestPracticeDescription:
When you enable password-strength checking, the NX-OS software only allows you to create strong
passwords. The characteristics for strong passwords include the following: 
1. At least eight characters long 
2. Does not contain many consecutive characters (such as "abcd") 
3. Does not contain many repeating characters (such as "aaabbb") 
4. Does not contain dictionary words 
5. Does not contain proper names 
6. Contains both uppercase and lowercase characters 
7. Contains numbers
bestPracticeRecommendation: Not having this configuration in place may lead to security concerns within the network.
bestPracticeTitle: Password strength-check disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for Rapid PVST plus

 spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst
bestPracticeDescription: Rapid PVST+(Per vlan spanning tree) is the IEEE 802.1w (RSTP) standard implemented per VLAN. A single instance of STP runs on each configured VLAN (if you do not manually disable STP). Each Rapid PVST+ instance on a VLAN has a single root switch.You can enable and disable STP on a per-VLAN basis when you are running Rapid PVST+.Rapid PVST+ is enabled by default on the default VLAN (VLAN1) and on all newly created VLANs in software. Rapid PVST+ interoperates with switches that run legacy IEEE 802.1D STP.Rapid PVST+ uses point-to-point wiring to provide rapid convergence of the spanning tree.The spanning tree reconfiguration can occur in less than 1 second with Rapid PVST+ (in contrast to 50 seconds with the default settings in the 802.1D STP).Using Rapid PVST+, STP convergence occurs rapidly.Each designated or root port in the STP sends out a BPDU every 2 seconds by default.On a designated or root port in the topology, if hello messages are missed three consecutive times, or if the maximum age expires, the port immediately flushes all protocol information in the table.A port considers that it loses connectivity to its direct neighbor root or designated port if it misses three BPDUs or if the maximum age expires.This rapid aging of the protocol information allows quick failure detection. The switch automatically checks the PVID. STP(Spanning tree protocol) Overview STP is a Layer 2 link management protocol that provides path redundancy while preventing loops in the network. For a Layer 2 Ethernet network to function properly, only one active path can exist between any two stations. Multiple active paths among end stations cause loops in the network. If a loop exists in the network, end stations might receive duplicate messages. Switches might also learn end-station MAC addresses on multiple Layer 2 interfaces. These conditions result in an unstable network. Spanning-tree operation is transparent to end stations, which cannot detect whether they are connected to a single LAN segment or a switched LAN of multiple segments.The STP uses a spanning-tree algorithm to select one switch of a redundantly connected network as the root of the spanning tree. The algorithm calculates the best loop-free path through a switched Layer 2 network by assigning a role to each port based on the role of the port in the active topology.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Rapid PVST+ should be enabled.
bestPracticeTitle: Rapid PVST Plus not used or enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Please be aware that additional Memory Consumption will be caused because of the addition of this configuration.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
1. 'enable' 
2. 'configure terminal' 
3. 'warm-reboot [count number] [uptime minutes]' 
4. 'exit' 
5. 'show warm-reboot'
bestPracticeDescription: The Warm Reload feature allows users to reload their routers without reading images from storage. That is, the Cisco IOS image reboots without ROM monitor mode (ROMMON) intervention by restoring the read-write data from a previously saved copy in the random-access memory (RAM) and by starting execution without either copying the image from flash to RAM or self-decompression of the image.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The overall availability of your system improves because the time to reboot your router is significantly reduced.
bestPracticeTitle: Warm reload not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: aaa accounting commands {default | list-name} {start-stop | stop-only}{none | method}
bestPracticeDescription: Accounting commands are used for logging of sessions and to create an audit trail by recording certain user- or system-generated actions. When AAA accounting is activated, the router reports these attributes as accounting records, which are then stored in an accounting log on the security server.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure AAA accounting for commands, if AAA is configured.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA Accounting Commands not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If other reliable methods of monitoring the memory utilization are used (RMON, external polling), then this command may not be required. Some platforms do not separate I/O memory and Processor memory.  Even though this command is accepted on that platform, it will likely have little effect.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: memory free low-watermark io threshold
bestPracticeDescription:
The Memory Threshold Notification feature allows you to mitigate low-memory conditions on a device. This feature uses two methods to accomplish this: Memory Threshold Notification and Memory Reservation. Memory Threshold Notification generates a log message to indicate that free memory on a device has fallen lower than the configured threshold. This enables a device to generate a notification when available free memory falls lower than the specified threshold, and again when available free memory rises higher than the specified threshold.
This feature was introduced in 12.0(26)S, 12.2(18)S, and 12.3(4)T, so only devices where the feature is available will be considered for best practice matching.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Proactive monitoring of free I/O memory is recommended to avoid unplanned outages in devices that exhaust free memory. Specific threshold settings will depend on platform and features implemented. It is recommended to baseline performance of the devices in the network and from that baseline determine appropriate thresholds for the specific network environment. As a rule of thumb, though, any time free memory goes below 15%, it should be investigated as this could have a negative impact on operation of the device. Ensure that you have also implemented a system to capture, monitor, and alert on the syslog messages in real-time.
bestPracticeTitle: Memory Threshold Notifications (I-O) Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below,

router ospf 100
 log-adjacency-changes
 timers throttle spf 10 1000 90000
 network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0

In the above example the start interval is set at 10 milliseconds (ms), the wait/hold interval at 1000 milliseconds, and the maximum wait time is set at 90,000 milliseconds.
bestPracticeDescription: The OSPF Shortest Path First Throttling feature makes it possible to configure Shortest Path First (SPF) scheduling in millisecond intervals and to potentially delay SPF calculations during network instability. SPF is scheduled to calculate the Shortest Path Tree (SPT) when there is a change in topology. One SPF run may include multiple topology change events. The interval at which the SPF calculations occur is chosen dynamically and is based on the frequency of topology changes in the network. The chosen interval is within the boundary of the user-specified value ranges. If network topology is unstable, SPF throttling calculates SPF scheduling intervals to be longer until topology becomes stable.SPF calculations occur at the interval set by the timers throttle spf command. The wait interval indicates the amount of time to wait until the next SPF calculation occurs. Each wait interval after that calculation is twice as long as the previous one until the wait interval reaches the maximum wait time specified.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SPF minimum hold time should be set to a value fewer than 10 sec.
bestPracticeTitle: SPF Hold Time Set To High Value

bestPracticeCaveat:
An important consideration for deployment is that Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF) switching must be enabled for Unicast RPF to function. This command has been enabled by default as of Cisco IOS version 12.2. If it is not enabled, administrators can enable it with the global configuration command 'ip cef'.
Also, this exception is highly applicable to devices which are considered to be on edge of a network.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) requires Cisco Express Forwarding to function properly on a device and it is enabled on a per-interface basis.
The 'ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx' command enables Unicast RPF in strict mode. 

Router (config)# ip cef
Router(config)# interface Ethernet0/0
Router(config-if)# ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx l2-src

To enable loose mode, administrators can use the 'any' option to enforce the requirement that the source IP address for a packet must appear in the routing table. 

Router (config)# ip cef
Router (config)# interface serial5/0/0
Router (config-if)# ip verify unicast source reachable-via any

The 'allow-default' option may be used with either the 'rx' or 'any' option to include IP addresses not specifically contained in the routing table. 
The 'allow-self-ping' option should not be used because it could create a denial of service condition. 

An access list such as the one that follows also may be configured to specifically permit or deny a list of addresses through Unicast RPF:
interface FastEthernet 0/0
ip verify unicast source reachable-via {rx | any} [allow-default]
[allow-self-ping] [list]
bestPracticeDescription: Network administrators can use Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (Unicast RPF) to help limit the malicious traffic on an enterprise network. This security feature works by enabling a router to verify the reachability of the source address in packets that are being forwarded. In many enterprise environments, it is necessary to use a combination of strict mode and loose mode Unicast RPF. Strict Mode RPF: If the source address is in the FIB and reachable only through the interface on which the packet was received, the packet is passed. The syntax for this method is `ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx?. Exists-Only (or Loose Mode) RPF: If the source address is in the FIB and reachable through any interface on the router, the packet is passed. The syntax for this method is `ip verify unicast source reachable-via any?. If Unicast RPF does not find a reverse path for the packet, Unicast RPF can drop or forward the packet, depending on whether an ACL is specified in the Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding command. If an ACL is specified in the command, then when (and only when) a packet fails the Unicast RPF check, the ACL is checked to determine whether the packet should be dropped (using a deny statement in the ACL) or forwarded (using a permit statement in the ACL). If no ACL is specified in the Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding command, the router drops the forged or malformed packet immediately and no ACL logging occurs. The router and interface Unicast RPF counters are updated.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The choice of the Unicast RPF mode that will be used will depend on the design of the network segment connected to the interface on which Unicast RPF is deployed. Administrators should use Unicast RPF in strict mode on network interfaces for which all packets received on an interface are guaranteed to originate from the subnet assigned to the interface. A subnet composed of end stations or network resources fulfills this requirement. Such a design would be in place for an access layer network or a branch office where there is only one path into and out of the branch network. No other traffic originating from the subnet is allowed, and no other routes are available past the subnet.
bestPracticeTitle: Unicast reverse path disabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
1) This option is required only when customer has the Nexus based setup with vPC feature and is running with Layer-3 SVI?s.
2) N7K NX-OS version 4.2(6) and above is supported in this feature. If the customer is running the previous version to this 4.2(6) version, then it is recommended to upgrade to the later versions and enable the features.  Please consult with your Cisco support engineer.
3) N5K NX-OS version 5.0(2)N2(1) and above is supported in this feature. If the customer is running the previous version to 5.0(2)N2(1) version, then it is recommended to upgrade to the later versions and enable the features.  Please consult with your Cisco support engineer.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below 
In the  vPC domain configuration mode, enable 'ip arp synchronize' . For example:
!
feature vpc
vpc domain 1
ip arp synchronize
!
bestPracticeDescription: The Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) table sync feature overcomes the delay involved in ARP table restoration that can be triggered when one of the switches in the vPC domain goes offline and comes back online and also when there are peer-link port channel flaps. Performance improvement in convergence times for unicast traffic is the result of this feature. To improve performance, turn on the ARP sync feature. By default, it is disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: This feature is added to Cisco NX-OS Release 4.2(6) for N7K to address table synchronization across vPC peers using the reliable transport mechanism of the Cisco Fabric Service over Ethernet (CFSoE) protocol. The IP ARP synchronize and IPv6 nd synchronize commands must be enabled as it supports faster convergence of address tables between the vPC peers. This convergence is designed to overcome the delay involved in ARP table restoration for IPv4 or ND table restoration for IPv6 when the peer-link port-channel flaps or when a vPC peer comes back online. This feature is also introduced in Nexus 5000 Series platforms from 5.0(2)N2(1) onwards.
bestPracticeTitle: With vPC feature ARP_Synchronize feature is not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure AAA authentication with the following command:

aaa authentication login default {group group-list [none]| local | none}

This command configures the default authentication methods.

The ?group-list? argument consists of a space-delimited list of group names. The group names are the following:
?	radius ? Uses the global pool of RADIUS servers for authentication.
?	named-group ? Uses a named subset of TACACS+ or RADIUS servers for authentication.

The local method uses the local database for authentication. The none method uses the username only.
The default login method is local, which is used when no methods are configured or when all the configured methods fail to respond.
bestPracticeDescription: In order to have secure access to the switch, AAA needs to be invoked on the Nexus devices. Following this configuration, local database, TACACS+, or RADIUS servers can be used to log into device remotely. The AAA feature verifies the identity of, grant access to, and track the actions of users managing an Cisco NX-OS device. Cisco NX-OS devices support Remote Access Dial-In User Service (RADIUS) or Terminal Access Controller Access Control device Plus (TACACS+) protocols. Based on the user ID and password combination that is provided, Cisco NX-OS devices perform local authentication or authorization using the local database or remote authentication or authorization using one or more AAA servers. Without AAA server availability, network operators need to have physical access to the device until connectivity to AAA server is restored. This can be the result of network connectivity issues, the failure of AAA server, or incorrect configuration. But, the impact is not being able to access a device remotely for the administration as well as the user connectivity.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure AAA authentication, as it identifies users, including login and password dialog, challenge and response, messaging support, and encryption depending on the security protocol that is selected.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA authentication not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: controller SONET R/S/I/Pais-shut
bestPracticeDescription: To enable automatic insertion of a Line Alarm Indication Signal (LAIS) in the sent SONET signal whenever the SONET port enters the administrative shutdown state, use the 'ais-shut' command in SONET configuration mode.When the line is placed in administrative shutdown state, use the 'ais-shut' command to send a signal to downstream equipment indicating a problem with the line. The 'ais-shut' command is ignored if Automatic Protection Switching (APS) is running for the corresponding port, because the setting must be enabled for proper APS operation.For SONET ports that do not have hardware support for LAIS insertion, the 'ais-shut' command is disabled. The 'ais-shut' command is disabled by default; no AIS is sent.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is advised to implement 'ais-shut (SONET)' under the controllers SONET, to allow faster IGP convergence when issueing a shutdown on a PoS interface.
bestPracticeTitle: ais-shut (SONET) not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Older IOS devices may not support SSH at all or may not support SSHv2.  Software upgrade may be required to get support for SSHv2.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: ip ssh version 2
bestPracticeDescription: SSHv2 features both security and feature improvements over SSHv1. Better security, for example, comes through Diffie-Hellman key exchange and strong integrity checking via message authentication codes. New features of SSHv2 include the ability to run any number of shell sessions over a single SSH connection. Version 2: - Strong cryptographic integrity check - Supports password changing - Any number of session channels per connection (including none) - Full negotiation of modular cryptographic and compression algorithms,including bulk encryption,MAC,and public-key Version 1: - Weak CRC-32 integrity check; admits an insertion attack in conjunction with some bulk ciphers. - Exactly one session channel per connection (requires issuing a remote command even when you don't want one) - Negotiates only the bulk cipher; all others are fixed.
bestPracticeRecommendation: By default both SSHv1 and SSHv2 are supported on most platforms.  Recommendation is to enable SSHv2 only.  SSHv1 is deprecated in IOS via CSCuy44742.
bestPracticeTitle: SSHv1 enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: When doing local loopback testing on the router,down-when-looped must be removed before the loopback is set on the interface. It needs to be re-added once the local loopback testing has been completed. Failure to remove down-when-looped during local loopback testing will result in packets not being forwarded out of the interface being tested.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure for all in-service sonet controllers: 
controller sonet 0/1/0/0
 down-when-looped
bestPracticeDescription: A SONET controller will not inform the system that it is down when loopback is detected.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable a SONET controller to inform the system that it is down when loopback is detected.
bestPracticeTitle: SONET controller does not go down when loopback is detected

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
controller SONET R/S/I/P
path
report pais
bestPracticeDescription: SONET Reports path alarm indication signal (PAIS) errors has not been configured, which is by default disabled. Reporting an alarm means the alarm can be logged to the SYSLOG. SONET alarm hierarchy rules dictate that only the most severe alarm of an alarm group is reported. Whether an alarm is reported or not, you can check the current state of masked alarm (a problem indication that is a candidate for an alarm) by displaying the "Masked Alarms"line in the 'show controllers sonet' command output.
bestPracticeRecommendation: For SONET path root cause analysis configure 'report pais'.
bestPracticeTitle: report pais (SONET path) not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below 
!
no interface loopback 1
!
bestPracticeDescription: Configuring a loopback interface on an AP might generate an IAPP GENINFO storm on the network and disrupt network traffic.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Do not configure loopback interfaces on Cisco access points.
bestPracticeTitle: Loopback Interface Configured on Access Point

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
router bgp 7558
  bgp graceful-restart
!
bestPracticeDescription: To enable graceful restart support, use the 'bgp graceful-restart' command in an appropriate configuration mode, and also to advertise graceful restart capability to neighboring routers. When graceful restart is enabled, the BGP graceful restart capability is negotiated with neighbors in the BGP OPEN message when the session is established. If the neighbor also advertises support for graceful restart, then graceful restart is activated for that neighbor session. If the neighbor does not advertise support for graceful restart, then graceful restart is not activated for that neighbor session even though it is enabled locally. If the 'bgp graceful-restart' command is entered after some BGP sessions are established, a restart is required for those sessions before graceful restart takes effect. Use the 'clear bgp' command to restart sessions. Note: The 'bgp graceful-restart' command with no options must be used to enable graceful restart before using the 'bgp graceful-restart purge-time', 'bgp graceful-restart restart-time', 'bgp graceful-restart stalepath-time', or 'bgp graceful-restart graceful-reset' commands.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to implement 'bgp graceful-restart', to increase network services availability.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP graceful-restart is not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: port-channel load-balance src-dst ip-l4port-vlan
bestPracticeDescription: Port-channel load balancing is used to distribute the traffic load across each of the links in the port channel to ensure efficient utilization of each link. Port-channel load balancing on the Cisco Nexus 7000 Series can use MAC address or IP address, Layer 4 port numbers, source address, destination address, or both source and destination addresses.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The recommended best practice is to use the source and destination Layer 4 port and VLAN ID for the load balancing decision.
bestPracticeTitle: Load Balancing not using src-dst ip-l4port-vlan

bestPracticeCaveat:
Root guard forces a port to be designated always. Loop guard is effective only if the port is root port or an alternate port, which means that their functions are mutually exclusive. Therefore, loop guard and root guard cannot be enabled on a port at the same time.
Loop guard and UDLD functionality partially overlap, partly in the sense that both protect against STP failures that unidirectional links cause. These two features are different in the approach to the problem and also in functionality. Specifically, there are specific unidirectional failures that UDLD is unable to detect, such as failures that are caused by a CPU that does not send BPDUs. Additionally, the use of aggressive STP timers and RSTP mode can result in loops before UDLD can detect the failures.
Loop guard does not work on shared links or in situations where the link has been unidirectional since the linkup. In the case of a link that has been unidirectional since the linkup, the port never receives BPDUs and becomes designated. This can be normal behavior, so loop guard does not cover this particular case. UDLD does provide protection against such a scenario
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for loopguard

spanning-tree mode [stp|rstp|mst]
spanning-tree loopguard default
bestPracticeDescription: Loop guard helps prevent bridging loops that could occur because of a unidirectional link failure on a point-to-point link. When enabled globally, loop guard applies to all point-to-point ports on the system. Loop guard detects root ports and blocked ports and ensures that they keep receiving BPDUs from their designated port on the segment. If a loop-guard-enabled root or blocked port stop receiving BPDUs from its designated port, it transitions to the blocking state, assuming there is a physical link error on this port. The port recovers from this state as soon as it receives a BPDU.You can enable loop guard on a per-port basis. When you enable loop guard, it is automatically applied to all of the active instances or VLANs to which that port belongs. When you disable loop guard, it is disabled for the specified ports. Disabling loop guard moves all loop-inconsistent ports to the listening state.The STP loop guard feature provides additional protection against Layer 2 forwarding loops (STP loops). An STP loop is created when an STP blocking port in a redundant topology erroneously transitions to the forwarding state. This usually happens because one of the ports of a physically redundant topology (not necessarily the STP blocking port) no longer receives STP BPDUs. In its operation, STP relies on continuous reception or transmission of BPDUs based on the port role. The designated port transmits BPDUs, and the non-designated port receives BPDUs.When one of the ports in a physically redundant topology no longer receives BPDUs, the STP conceives that the topology is loop free. Eventually, the blocking port from the alternate or backup port becomes designated and moves to a forwarding state. This situation creates a loop.The loop guard feature makes additional checks. If BPDUs are not received on a non-designated port, and loop guard is enabled, that port is moved into the STP loop-inconsistent blocking state,instead of the listening / learning / forwarding state. Without the loop guard feature, the port assumes the designated port role.The port moves to the STP forwarding state and creates a loop. Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) is a Layer 2 protocol that runs on bridges and switches. The specification for STP is IEEE 802.1D. The main purpose of STP is to ensure that you do not create loops when you have redundant paths in your network. Loops are deadly to a network.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends that you enable loop guard globally on a switch network with physical loops. You can enable loop guard globally on all ports. Effectively, the feature is enabled on all point-to-point links. The point-to-point link is detected by the duplex status of the link. If duplex is full, the link is considered point-to-point.
bestPracticeTitle: Loopguard not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: Make sure that SSH is first turned on and verified before disabling the telnet server, in order to prevent lockout of the network element within production. In full inband lock out console access is the only alternative to access the network element.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable SSH and verify SSH access.
Perform for all telnet servers enabled for all vrf's:
      no telnet vrf $$any-vrf-name ipv4 server
bestPracticeDescription: Telnet server has been enabled on the network element.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Consider a more secure network element access protocol like SSH.
bestPracticeTitle: Telnet server is enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below

router eigrp 100
 eigrp router-id 10.255.241.18
 network 10.153.2.196
 network 10.255.241.18
bestPracticeDescription: The router ID is set to the IP address of a loopback interface if one is configured. If no loopback interfaces are configured, the router ID is set to the highest IP address configured on a physical interface. To ensure deterministic behavior the EIGRP router-id should be configured to a chosen address. If a router id isn't set for EIGRP, it is possible that duplicate router ids will prevent external EIGRP routes from being installed in the topology table. To have deterministic behavior, an EIGRP router id should be created on all EIGRP speaking devices.To set the router ID used by Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) when communicating with its neighbors, use the eigrp router-id command in router configuration mode, address-family configuration mode, or service-family configuration mode. To remove the configured router ID, use the no form of this command.EIGRP automatically selects an IP address to use as the router ID when an EIGRP process is started. The highest local IP address is selected and loopback interfaces are preferred. The router ID is not changed unless the EIGRP process is removed with the no router eigrp command or if the router ID is manually configured with the eigrp router-id command.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The EIGRP router id may be set with the "eigrp router-id" command in later IOS versions.
Note: EIGRP neighbors will reset automatically when this command is configured. In earlier codes a highest IP address and loopback interface may be used to set the router-id.
bestPracticeTitle: No EIGRP router-id configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: 'no snmp-server packetsize'
bestPracticeDescription: The snmp server packet size should have a default value of 1500 bytes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: If the snmp server packet size is not the default, investigate why the change was made, and ensure that the configuration is optimized.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP Server Packet Size Not Default

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-

!
spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst
spanning-tree pathcost method long
!
interface ethernet 1/4
 spanning-tree cost 1000
!
bestPracticeDescription: To set the default path-cost calculation method, use the spanning-tree pathcost method command. To return to the default settings, use the no form of this command. This command applies only to the Rapid Per VLAN Spanning Tree Plus (PVST+) spanning tree mode, which is the default mode. When you are using MST spanning tree mode, the device uses only the long method for calculating path cost; this is not user-configurable for MST. The long path-cost calculation method uses all 32 bits for path-cost calculations and yields valued in the range of 2 through 2,000,000,000. The short path-cost calculation method (16 bits) yields values in the range of 1 through 65535. This command does not require a license.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Spanning Tree was designed before the availability of 10Gbps links, the default STP reference cost for 10Gbps links is considered inadequate as bandwidths which are a multiple of 10Gbps will appear to have the same cost. To avoid this issue, the Spanning Tree Protocol can use an alternative method for determining path cost, known as the long path cost method. The long method uses 32 bit values for port path costs.
bestPracticeTitle: STP Path Cost

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Admin-Config:
'fpd auto-upgrade'
bestPracticeDescription: As of IOS XR release 4.0.1, the 'fpd auto-upgrade' feature has become available, which reduces an IOS XR upgrade by one system reload. When an IOS XR upgrades to a higher release and fpd requires an upgrade, in order to be compatible with the target IOS XR release, the system requires to be reloaded twice without the 'fpd auto-upgrade' feature being enabled. By enabling the 'fpd auto-upgrade' feature in admin configuration mode, the system will upgrade the FPD and upgrade to the new IOS XR release through one system-wide reload.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Consider enabling the 'fpd auto-upgrade' feature.
bestPracticeTitle: fpd auto-upgrade feature not enabled in admin configuration

bestPracticeCaveat:
OSPFv2 nonstop routing (NSR) feature added on Release 3.6.0 and above 
Related DDTS: CSCsr00755 NSR: FM script to trigger RP FO doesn't kick in reliably in proc restart
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-

!
router ospf 1
 router-id 10.10.10.10
 nsr
 nsf
!
bestPracticeDescription: Nonstop routing (NSR) allows an RP failover, process restart, or in-service upgrade to be invisible to peer routers and ensures that there is minimal performance or processing impact. Routing protocol interactions between routers are not impacted by NSR. NSR is built on the warm standby extensions. NSR alleviates the requirement for Cisco NSF and IETF graceful restart protocol extension. Cisco IOS XR NSF for OSPF Version 2 allows for the forwarding of data packets to continue along known routes while the routing protocol information is being restored following a failover. With NSF, peer networking devices do not experience routing flaps. During failover, data traffic is forwarded through intelligent line cards while the standby Routing protocols, such as OSPF, run only on the active RP or DRP and receive routing updates from their neighbor routers
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable the nonstop routing (NSR) in combination with None Stop Forwarding (NSF) for all Open Shortest Path First (OSPFv2) routing protocol processes.
bestPracticeTitle: NSR (OSPFv2) Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
'cable logging layer2events' 
'no cable logging layer2events' 
By default, the DOCSIS events are saved to the general logging buffer on the Cisco CMTS. The default logging buffer size is 4096 entries. 
Use the 'show cable logging' command to check whether the logging feature is enabled and the status of the logging buffer. 
Examples:
The following example shows how to clear the log buffer that contains a bad IP source address error message: 
Router# show cable logging summary
Cable logging: BADIPSOURCE Enabled
Total buffer size (bytes): 1000000
Used buffer size (bytes) : 36968
Logged messages : 231
bestPracticeDescription: Cloned Cable Modems are detected and tracked with system logging. Due to the large number of Data-over-Cable Service Interface Specifications (DOCSIS) layer 2 messages typically seen in a production network, a separate log is available to segregate these messages. If the 'cable logging layer2events' command in global configuration mode is configured, Cloned Cable Modem messages are removed from the system log (syslog) and placed instead in the cable layer2logging.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Save selected DOCSIS events that are specified in the Cisco CMTS MIB Registry to the cable logging buffer (instead of to the general logging buffer). This command supports Cloned Cable Modem Detection in Cisco IOS Release 12.3(21)BC and later releases.
bestPracticeTitle: Cable logging layer2events

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration :-

!
aaa new-model
!
aaa authentication login default group ACS line
aaa authentication enable default group ACS enable
aaa authorization console
aaa authorization exec default group ACS if-authenticated 
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ACS none 
aaa accounting connection default start-stop group ACS
aaa session-id common
!
bestPracticeDescription: Accounting allows for the collecting and sending of security information used for billing, auditing, and reporting, such as user identities, start and stop times, and executed commands. Accounting enables network managers to track the services that users are accessing as well as the amount of network resources that they are consuming. The command 'aaa accounting connection' provides information about all outbound connections made from the network access server, such as Telnet, LAT, TN3270, PAD, and rlogin.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends when AAA is enabled then enable command 'aaa accounting connection' provides information about all outbound connections made from the network access server, such as Telnet, LAT, TN3270, PAD, and rlogin.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA Accounting Connection not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router eigrp
  router-id
bestPracticeDescription: The router ID is set to the IP address of a loopback interface if one is configured. If no loopback interfaces are configured, the router ID is set to the highest IP address configured on a physical interface. To ensure deterministic behavior the EIGRP router-id should be configured to a chosen address. If a router id is not set for EIGRP, it is possible that duplicate router ids will prevent external EIGRP routes from being installed in the topology table. To have deterministic behavior, an EIGRP router id should be created on all EIGRP speaking devices.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The EIGRP router id may be set with the eigrp router-id command in NX-OS versions. 
      Note: EIGRP neighbors will reset automatically when this command is configured. A high IP address and loopback interface may be used to set the router-id.
