bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure 
  'no ip forwards protocol udp'
OR configure all the following commands to disable forwarding of specific User Datagram Protocol (UDP) broadcast packets.
  'no ip forward-protocol udp tftp'
  'no ip forward-protocol udp domain'
  'no ip forward-protocol udp time'
  'no ip forward-protocol udp netbios-ns' 
  'no ip forward-protocol udp netbios-dgm' 
  'no ip forward-protocol udp tacacs'
bestPracticeDescription: Routers situated in the edge of a network that have constant exposure to customer premises equipment (CPE) devices (usually not trusted devices from the security perspective) need to have specific protection against specific protocols, especially the type of protocols that make use of "broadcast" packets to reach out to their neighbors. Netbios is one of those protocols among many others.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is highly recommended to have the following list of protocols disabled in Edge routers to prevent the "broadcast" packets that are received on specific interfaces and would need to be broadcasted in the remaining interfaces of that router if such commands are not applied. 'no ip forward-protocol udp tftp', 'no ip forward-protocol udp domain', 'no ip forward-protocol udp time', 'no ip forward-protocol udp netbios-ns', 'no ip forward-protocol udp netbios-dgm', 'no ip forward-protocol udp tacacs'. The commands mentioned before are the minimum required/recommenced protocols to disable. However, it is highly recommended to configure "no ip forward-protocol udp" alone if it is a feasible option for the customer.
bestPracticeTitle: Forwarding of one or more UDP protocols has not been disabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
If you are running a Cisco NX-OS Release prior to 5.2, up to one instance of NTP is supported on the entire platform. You must configure NTP in the default virtual device context (VDC), and you are automatically placed in the default VDC unless you specify otherwise.
If you are running Cisco NX-OS Release 5.2 or later, multiple instances of NTP are supported, one instance per VDC. By default, Cisco NX-OS places you in the default VDC unless you specifically configure another VDC.
Best Practice rules do not differentiate between default and non-default configurations, so it is possible to have false alarms for this rule.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
ntp server or
ntp peer or
ntp master
bestPracticeDescription: Network Time Protocol (NTP) is used to synchronize time across devices in the network. This is essential for troubleshooting to ensure that logs from different devices on the network can be correlated.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable NTP on all devices in the network.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure MAC move notification by enabling it globally using the following global command:
'mac address-table notification mac-move'
(Note:  on some platforms, the command has an additional hyphen: 'mac-address-table notification mac-move')
bestPracticeDescription: MAC address move notification detects possible faults in the network by logging MAC moves using syslog and/or SNMP traps. Frequent moves of hosts normally should not occur in a stable network. A high number of MAC moves can indicate a possible loop or misconfigured host.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends configuring MAC move notification to detect frequent MAC moves and spot possible problems in the network.
bestPracticeTitle: MAC address move notification not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Check if logging level is configured with level 7, then raise an exception. Example: logging server 192.168.1.6 7
bestPracticeDescription: Each internal system software component of Cisco NX-OS that is capable of logging using the syslog facility can be assigned one of eight severity levels that range from level 0( emergencies) through level 7 debug). The selected severity level determines the level, granularity, and frequency of messages generated for that component.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Unless specifically required, you are advised to avoid logging at level 7. Logging at level 7 produces an elevated CPU load on the device that can lead to device and network instability.
bestPracticeTitle: Logging Level set to Debugging

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Apply an access list to restrict access to specific source and destination IP addresses.
!
snmp-server community [community string] RO [ACL]
snmp-server community [community string] RW [ACL]
!

Or,
!
snmp-server community mgr view restricted RW ipv6
snmp-server community mgr view restricted RO ipv6 
!
bestPracticeDescription: SNMPv3 is the recommended SNMP version because of the additional security authentication and encryption mechanisms. If an SNMPv3 capable management server is not available, SNMP version 1 and 2c can be enabled using the snmp-server community command. In order to increase security, an access list should be used when SNMP v1 or v2c is used to restrict access. This is especially critical if RW string is configured.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Access control lists (ACLs) should always be applied to community strings (SNMP v1 and v2c) to restrict access to specific source and destination IP addresses.  Create access-lists which contain the addresses or address ranges of servers and workstations which require access to SNMP and configure these access lists onto the community strings. At minimum configure the address range of the internal network to prevent outside access and address spoofing.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP community string not protected by access list

bestPracticeCaveat: If running code prior to 3.7, see CSCsm36321.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: snmp-server ifmib stats cache
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco IOS XR statistics infrastructure can be configured to maintain a cache of IF-MIB statistics for all interfaces. Use the 'snmp-server ifmib stats cache' global command to enable snmpd retrieving these cached IF-MIB statistics. Accessing cached statistics is less CPU-intensive than accessing distributed statistics.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enabling of retrieval of cached statistics is advised.
bestPracticeTitle: snmp-server ifmib stats cache Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If the peer initiates the negotiation, and the local configuration specifies PFS, the remote peer must perform a PFS exchange, or the negotiation will fail.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable PFS by using the command 'set pfs' under crypto map and crypto dynamic configuration modes. An example: crypto dynamic-map mymap 100 set	pfs group1 ! ! crypto map mymap1 100 ipsec-isakmp set pfs group1 !
bestPracticeDescription:
During negotiation, the 'set pfs' command causes IP Security (IPSec) to request perfect forward secrecy (PFS) when requesting new security associations for the crypto map entry. The default (group1) is sent if the 'set pfs' statement does not specify a group. If the peer initiates the negotiation, and the local configuration specifies PFS, the remote peer must perform a PFS exchange, or the negotiation will fail. If the local configuration does not specify a group, a default of group1 will be assumed, and an offer of either group1 or group2 will be accepted. If the local configuration specifies group2, that group must be part of the peer's offer, or the negotiation will fail. If the local configuration does not specify PFS, it will accept any offer of PFS from the peer.
PFS adds another level of security because if one key is ever cracked by an attacker, then only the data sent with that key will be compromised. Without PFS, data sent with other keys also could be compromised.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable PFS to add another level of security because if one key is ever cracked by an attacker, then only the data sent with that key will be compromised. Without PFS, data sent with other keys also could be compromised.
bestPracticeTitle: PFS Disabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Another option is to configure netconf agent.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: xml agent
bestPracticeDescription: To enable Extensible Markup Language (XML) requests over a dedicated TCP connection and enter XML agent configuration mode, use the xml agent command in global configuration.
bestPracticeRecommendation: XML is advised to be considered as the preferred method of managing IOS XR network elements.
bestPracticeTitle: XML agent not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Many lower-end routers (for example, the Cisco 2500 series or the Cisco 2600 series) do not have hardware clocks, so this command is not available on those platforms.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: ntp update-calendar
bestPracticeDescription: Some platforms have a battery-powered hardware clock, referred to in the command-line interface (CLI) as the "calendar," in addition to the software based system clock. The hardware clock runs continuously, even if the router is powered off or rebooted. If the software clock is synchronized to an outside time source via NTP, it is a good practice to periodically update the hardware clock with the time learned from NTP. Otherwise, the hardware clock will tend to gradually lose or gain time (drift), and the software clock and hardware clock may become out of synchronization with each other. The ntp update-calendar command will enable the hardware clock to be periodically updated with the time specified by the NTP source. The hardware clock will be updated only if NTP has synchronized to an authoritative time server.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable ntp update-calendar, where supported.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP Update Calendar Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
As a best practice, configure "set-overload-bit on-startup " under "router isis" in the router config mode

Configuration as below,

router isis
 passive-interface Loopback0
 net 12.0020.0200.2002.00
 set-overload-bit on-startup
bestPracticeDescription: Setting the overload bit provides the router adequate time to build its BGP and CEF tables prior to the router being used as a transit node.When a router runs out of system resources (memory or CPU), it cannot store the link-state database or run shortest path first (SPF). In this situation, the router should alert other routers within its area by setting a particular bit in its link-state packets (LSPs). When other routers detect that this bit is set, they will not use this router for transit traffic but they will use it for packets destined to the overloaded router's directly connected networks and IP prefixes.In IS-IS, a router immediately floods its own LSP even before sending complete sequence number PDU (CSNP) packets. The overload bit is thereby used to advise the rest of the network not to route transit traffic through the newly reloaded router.You can configure a router to advertise its LSP with the overload bit for a specific amount of time after a reload. When the timer expires, the overload bit is cleared and the LSP is re-flooded.This new functionality is useful to Internet Service Providers (ISPs) who run both Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) and IS-IS to avoid a couple of "black hole" scenarios. Setting the overload bit for a fixed amount of time right after a reload ensures that the router does not receive transit traffic while the routing protocol is still converging.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to set the overload bit on-startup to avoid any potential routing loop issues.
bestPracticeTitle: Overload bit on startup Not set under IS-IS

bestPracticeCaveat:
1. This feature is available only on platforms that support a Personal Computer Memory Card International Association (PCMCIA) Advanced Technology Attachment (ATA) disk. There must be enough space on the storage device to accommodate at least one Cisco IOS image (two for upgrades) and a copy of the running configuration. IOS Files System (IFS) support for secure file systems is also needed by the software.
2. It may be possible to force removal of secured files using an older version of Cisco IOS software that does not contain file system support for hidden files.
3. This feature can be disabled only by using a console connection to the router. With the exception of the upgrade scenario, feature activation does not require console access.
4. You cannot secure a bootset with an image loaded from the network. The running image must be loaded from persistent storage to be secured as primary.
5. Secured files will not appear on the output of a dir command issued from an executive shell because the IFS prevents secure files in a directory from being listed. ROM monitor (ROMMON) mode does not have any such restriction and can be used to list and boot secured files. The running image and running configuration archives will not be visible in the Cisco IOS dir command output. Instead, use the 'show secure bootset' command to verify archive existence.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
To stores a secure copy of the primary bootset in persistent storage.
!
secure boot-config
!
bestPracticeDescription: The Cisco IOS Resilient Configuration feature is intended to speed up the recovery process. The feature maintains a secure working copy of the router image and the startup configuration at all times. These secure files cannot be removed by the user. This set of image and router running configuration is referred to as the primary bootset. The following factors were considered in the design of Cisco IOS Resilient Configuration: The configuration file in the primary bootset is a copy of the running configuration that was in the router when the feature was first enabled. The feature secures the smallest working set of files to preserve persistent storage space. No extra space is required to secure the primary Cisco IOS image file. The feature automatically detects image or configuration version mismatch. Only local storage is used for securing files, eliminating scalability maintenance challenges from storing multiple images and configurations on TFTP servers. The feature can be disabled only through a console session.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enables this feature on router to secure and maintain a working copy of the running image and configuration so that those files can withstand malicious attempts to erase the contents of persistent storage (NVRAM and flash. When this feature is enabled, it is not possible to alter or remove these backup files. You are advised to enable this feature to prevent both inadvertent and malicious attempts to delete these files.
bestPracticeTitle: IOS Software Resilient Configuration secure boot-config disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Disable ipv6 unreachables on ipv6 enabled interfaces.
interface FastEthernet0/0
 no ip address
 duplex auto
 speed auto
 ipv6 address 2001:2341::1/64
 ipv6 enable
 no ipv6 unreachables
bestPracticeDescription: If the Cisco IOS software receives a nonbroadcast packet destined for itself that uses a protocol it does not recognize, it sends an ICMPv6 unreachable message to the source. If the software receives a datagram that it cannot deliver to its ultimate destination because it knows of no route to the destination address, it replies to the originator of that datagram with an ICMP host unreachable message. The "ipv6 unreachables" command affects all types of ICMP unreachable messages and is enabled by default. However, ICMP unreachables can potentially be used to map a network by correlating which addresses generate ICMP unreachables and which do not for networks, hosts, protocols, and ports. In the past, this rule was not properly run against interface configurations.This rule checks all the interfaces on a device with an IPv6 address and are not shutdown, and gives an exception if not even one interface has "no ipv6 unreachable".
bestPracticeRecommendation: A customer should control ICMPv6 unreachable responses in certain environments, especially along untrusted edges between administrative domains with the command "no ipv6 unreachables" on affected interfaces. ICMP unreachables cover network, host, protocol, and port unreachable messages.This rule will result in a "false positive" for devices where all interfaces are in a trusted domain.  This is not something that can be easily determined from a running configuration.  Customers should use the exception list to determine if affected devices should or should not implement this change on any of its edge interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: ICMPv6 unreachables enabled on all interfaces of this device

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the config traps with the 'snmp-server enable traps config' command.
bestPracticeDescription: This command controls Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications for configuration change events, as defined in the CISCO-CONFIG-MAN-MIB.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the config traps to ensure that notifications for configuration change events are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: Configuration Management SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
Should be enabled in concert with 'bgp graceful-restart'.

For ASR9K: In Release 5.3.0 and later, the BGP Nonstop Routing (NSR) functionality is enabled by default. To disable NSR, use the "nsr disable" command. In releases prior to R5.3.0, NSR is disabled by default, and must be configured manually.

http://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/routers/asr9000/software/asr9k_r5-3/general/release/notes/reln_530a9k.html#concept_947076113993462387C596A590652012
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-

!
router bgp 65535
 nsr
 bgp router-id 10.10.10.1
 address-family ipv4 unicast
!
 neighbor 20.20.20.1
  remote-as 65530
!
bestPracticeDescription: This rule checks if a network element that runs the BGP process has any of its neighbors enabled with Nonstop Routing (NSR). The 'nsr' command enables the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) NSR with stateful switchover (SSO) feature and enables provider edge (PE) routers to maintain the BGP state with customer edge (CE) routers and ensure continuous packet forwarding during events that could interrupt service. When the BGP NSR with SSO feature is configured, in the event of an RP switchover, the PE router uses BGP NSR with SSO to maintain BGP state for eBGP peering sessions with CEs that are not NSF-aware. Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) Nonstop Routing (NSR) with Stateful Switchover (SSO) reduces impact on customer traffic during route processor (RP) switchovers (scheduled or unscheduled events), extending high availability (HA) deployments and benefits at the edge.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to use of Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) Nonstop Routing (NSR) with Stateful Switchover (SSO) reduces impact on customer traffic during route processor (RP) switchovers (scheduled or unscheduled events), extending high availability (HA) deployments and benefits at the edge.
Customer is encouraged to investigate BGP NSR as a network availability feature. Enable BGP NSR and enable 'bgp graceful-restart'.
bestPracticeTitle: NSR BGP Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:

configure
tacacs-server host host-name port port-number
tacacs-server host host-name timeout seconds
tacacs-server host host-name key [0 | 7] auth-key
tacacs-server host host-name single-connection
tacacs source-interface type instance vrf vrf-name
Repeat Step 2 through Step 5 for each external server to be configured.
Use the commit or end command.

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#tacacs-server host YY.YY.YY.YY port 49

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#tacacs-server ipv4 dscp cs2
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#tacacs-server timeout 6
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#aaa accounting exec default start-stop group tacacs+
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#aaa accounting commands default start-stop group tacacs+
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#aaa authorization exec default group tacacs+ local

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:CRS-PE3(conf t)#commit

bestPracticeDescription: In order to have secure access to the router, AAA needs to be invoked on the Cisco IOS XR routers.Following this configuration, local database, TACACS+, or RADIUS servers can be used to log into device remotely. Without AAA server availability, network operators would have to have physical access to device until connectivity to AAA server is restored. This can be result of one of the following: - Network connectivity issues - Failure of AAA server - Incorrect configuration However, the impact is not being able to access a device remotely for administration as well as user connectivity.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure AAA authentication to have secure device access.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA not configured

bestPracticeCaveat:
If no router ID is configured in BGP, BGP attempts to use the global router ID if available.
Otherwise, BGP uses the highest IP address configured on a loopback interface.

If the BGP router-ID command is not used to configure a router ID, and an IP address is not configured on any loopback interface or no global router ID is configured, BGP neighbors remain down.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure a router-id for router bgp process by issuing:
router bgp [tag]
 bgp router-id
bestPracticeDescription: The BGP router-id command under Router BGP configuration mode provides an administrative router-identifier for BGP.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Strongly recommend that the bgp router-id command is configured to prevent unnecessary changes to the router ID (and consequent flapping of BGP sessions).
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Router ID not explicitly configured

bestPracticeCaveat: Command may not be available in all product families, such as Cisco AS5300 Series Universal Gateways and Cisco Aironet 1400 Series.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable security authentication failure rate of three and logging with the command:
router(config)# security authentication failure rate 3 log
bestPracticeDescription: The 'security authentication failure rate' command provides enhanced security access to the router by generating syslog messages after the number of unsuccessful login attempts exceeds the configured threshold rate. This command ensures that there are no continuous failures to access the router.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The best practice is to configure a maximum threshold of 3 consecutive unsuccessful login attempts, and to enable the generation of syslog messages, as shown in the following configuration.
router(config)# security authentication failure rate 3 log
This configuration causes access to the router to be locked for a period of 15 seconds after three unsuccessful login attempts, disabling the dictionary method of attack. In addition to locking access to the router, this configuration causes a log message to be generated after three unsuccessful login attempts, warning the administrator of the unsuccessful login attempts.
bestPracticeTitle: Security authentication failure rate disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Assign a BGP Router ID router bgp  bgp router-id
bestPracticeDescription: At the startup of the BGP process, the BGP router ID is set to the highest IP address of all loopback interfaces. If no loopback interfaces are configured, the router ID is set to the highest IP address of all physical interfaces in the UP/UP state. The router ID will not change even if an interface goes in the down-state or a loopback interface is deleted and its IP address is chosen as the BGP router ID. But it will change if the BGP process is reset for any reason (reload, "clear ip bgp *", etc.). The router ID is always changed if the BGP router ID is manually configured with the command "bgp router-id" (available from certain IOS releases on), even if the IP address does not change, and all sessions are reset. If all other metrics are equal,the BGP router ID (as originator-id) will be used as the tie-breaker for the BGP best path."bgp router-id" (available from certain IOS releases on), even if the IP address does not change, and all sessions are reset. If all other metrics are equal,the BGP router ID (as originator-id) will be used as the tie-breaker for the BGP best path.
bestPracticeRecommendation: To ensure deterministic behavior, the BGP router ID should be configured to a chosen address that does not need to be configured on any interface. It is strongly recommended to use the IP address of an interface, preferably loopback. Care must be taken to use a unique IP address.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Router ID not explicitly configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
The following example shows how to configure a system to allow itself to be synchronized by a peer from access list.
!
access-list 40 permit 1.1.1.5
access-list 40 permit 1.1.1.6
!
ntp access-group peer 40
!
bestPracticeDescription: In addition to an NTP authentication schema NTP can be protected through use of an access-list to further limit access privileges. If the source IP address matches the access-lists for more than one access type, the first type is granted. If no access groups are specified, all access types are granted to all systems. If any access groups are specified, only the specified access types will be granted.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure an access-list to control how an NTP-enabled device handles related functions.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP not protected by ACL

bestPracticeCaveat: This rule excludes devices using "ip dhcp pool", "ip helper-address", or "ip dhcp snooping" in their configurations.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no service dhcp
bestPracticeDescription: By default, the DHCP server is enabled but doesn't appear on "show run". If DHCP services are not required, it should be disabled. All unnecessary services should be disabled on a network device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Determine if DHCP services are required, and disable if not needed.
bestPracticeTitle: DHCP server enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: 'snmp-server location '
bestPracticeDescription: The Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) location is the SNMP MIB variable "sysLocation". This can be queried remotely to determine the location of this device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the SNMP location to be the actual location for the device or a reference string to information in a location database.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP location not defined

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Remove the unused IS-IS process(es) via the "no router isis " configuration command.
bestPracticeDescription: This rules detects if an unused IS-IS routing process is in a device and should be removed. It checks if there is an IS-IS process without IS-IS interfaces defined. Although the amount of resources consumed may not be significant, it is a good practice to remove the unused IS-IS process.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Remove all unused IS-IS processes.
bestPracticeTitle: Unused IS-IS process

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
multicast-routing
 vrf test address-family ipv4
  rate-per-route
 !
 vrf test2 address-family ipv6
  rate-per-route
 !
 address-family ipv4
  rate-per-route
 !
 address-family ipv6
  rate-per-route
bestPracticeDescription: Individual (source, group [S, G]) rate calculations have not been enabled for all multicast routing and vrf address-family vrf or global IPv4 or IPv6. The outputs then show up in sh mfib route stat loc x/y/z. Rate-per-route may allocate the rate counter for up to 22K S,Gs. (It's a fifo to allocate the counters.)
bestPracticeRecommendation: Consider enabling multicast-routing rate-per-route to have additional multicast information.
bestPracticeTitle: Multicast-routing Rate-per-route Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure in Admin:
diagnostic monitor syslog
bestPracticeDescription: The 'diagnostic monitor syslog' command enables the generation of a syslog message when a health-monitoring test fails.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure 'diagnostic monitor syslog' in order to generate diagnostic syslog messages.
bestPracticeTitle: Diagnostic Monitor Syslog Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the command 'ip dhcp relay information option'.
bestPracticeDescription: The relay information option commands add a special option called Option 82, or the relay information option, to the relayed DHCP packet when the CMTS relays DHCP packets. Option 82 is populated with a suboption, the Agent Circuit-ID, which references the physical interface on the CMTS where the DHCP request was heard. In addition to this, another suboption, the Agent Remote ID, is populated with the 6-byte MAC address of the cable modem from which the DHCP request was received or through which the DHCP request was passed. So, for example, if a PC with MAC address 99:88:77:66:55:44 that is behind cable modem aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff sends a DHCP request, the CMTS will forward the DHCP request setting the Agent Remote ID suboption of Option 82 to the MAC address of the Cable Modem, aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff. By having the Relay Information Option included within the DHCP request from a customer premises equipment (CPE) device, the DHCP server is able to store information about which CPE belongs behind which cable modems. This becomes especially useful when 'cable source-verify dhcp' is configured on the CMTS, as the DHCP server is able to reliably inform the CMTS about not only what MAC address a particular client should have, but also which cable modem to which the particular client is meant to be connected.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable 'ip dhcp relay information option'. If the 'cable source-verify dhcp' feature is enabled,also enable 'ip dhcp relay information option' and 'no ip dhcp relay information check'.
bestPracticeTitle: IP DHCP Relay Information Option Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If the router running pre 3.5.0 release is managed only through inbound management, which means no MgmtEth ports and no Console access (OOB) configured. During upgrade process to 3.5.0 - 3.5.2, not following the IOS XR upgrade procedure, inbound inbound management management traffic will not reach the Management Application(s). Best Practices MgmtEth and/or Console/AUX access should be enabled for every network element.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
control-plane
management-plane
inband
interface all
allow all
bestPracticeDescription: This rule checks for any IOS XR release if Management Plane Protection (MPP) is configured, this in order to give pre 3.5 an upgrade headsup and for 3.5. Starting 3.5.0 till 3.5.2 a new feature is introduced which allows mgmt traffic only on the interfaces which are configured for management plane. By default only control management ethernet and console ports are enabled to allow management traffic so in order to use other interfaces they have to be explicitly configured for 3.5.0 - 3.5.2 images.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure Management Plane Protection (MPP) for restricting management packets for designated interfaces, which provides greater security control over management to the device. For Network Elements running IOS XR releases prior to 3.5.0 consider to take proactive action to include, within the upgrade procedure to configure MPP, when upgrading the network element to an IOS XR release 3.5.0 - 3.5.2.  Through integration of CSCsl64079 the MPP default has become disabled and has been implemented in 3.5.3, 3.6.0 and 3.7.0 or later. Best Practise is to add MPP to the network standard.
bestPracticeTitle: Management Plane Protection (MPP) not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface tunnel-te1 ipv4
 unnumbered Loopback0
 signalled-bandwidth 1 ---- Some value for signaling BW needed when FA is used on a TE tunnel
 destination 12.0.0.3
 forwarding-adjacency ---------- path-option 1
 explicit name xxxxx
bestPracticeDescription: The MPLS-TE Forwarding Adjacency (FA) feature allows a network administrator to handle a traffic engineering, label-switched path (LSP) tunnel as a link in an Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) network based on the Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm. A forwarding adjacency can be created between routers regardless of their location in the network. Without a signaling bandwidth, the FA feature will not work in IOS XR.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to enable signalling bandwidth on traffic engineering (TE) tunnel if Forwarding Adjacency (FA) is intended to be used on a MPLS TE tunnel
bestPracticeTitle: Signaling Bandwidth not Enabled for a TE Tunnel Configured with Forwarding Adjacency

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example protocol :-

OSPF with Nonstop Routing :-

!
nsr process-failures switchover
!
router ospf 1
nsr
bestPracticeDescription: NSR is a method to achieve High Availability (HA) of the routing protocols. TCP connections and the routing protocol sessions are migrated from the active RP to standby RP after the RP failover without letting the peers know about the failover. Currently, the sessions terminate and the protocols running on the standby RP reestablish the sessions after the standby RP goes active. Graceful Restart (GR) extensions are used in place of NSR to prevent traffic loss during an RP failover, but GR has several drawbacks. If NSR not configured, a process failure of the active TCP or its applications (for example LDP, BGP, and so forth) can cause sessions to go down, and NSR is not provided. If there is any NSR configured on the network element, then configure the nsr process-failures switchover.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use of "nsr process-failures switchover" command to let the RP failover be used as a recovery action when the active TCP or active LDP restarts
bestPracticeTitle: NSR Process-failures Switchover Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: In the global configuration mode,enter 'snmp-server enable traps EIGRP' command to enable EIGRP traps.
bestPracticeDescription: SNMP traps for the EIGRP enables an agent to notify the management station of significant events related to EIGRP by way of an unsolicited SNMP message. Use this message for troubleshooting events related to routing/EIGRP.
bestPracticeRecommendation: EIGRP traps are helpful to log the event related to EIGRP process and can be used for troubleshooting when required.
bestPracticeTitle: EIGRP SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure multiple tacacs servers.
!
tacacs-server host 
tacacs-server host 
!
aaa authentication group 
server 
server 
!
bestPracticeDescription: This rule checks if redundant Tacacs server are configured. The AAA servers used in an environment should be redundant and deployed in a fault-tolerant manner. This approach helps to ensure that interactive management access as SSH access is possible if an AAA server is unavailable.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The AAA servers that are used in an environment should be redundant and deployed in a fault-tolerant manner. This approach helps ensure that interactive management access,such as SSH access,is possible if an AAA server is unavailable.  When you design or implement a redundant AAA server solution,keep these considerations in mind:
  - Availability of AAA servers during potential network failures
  - Geographically dispersed placement of AAA servers
  - Load on individual AAA servers during steady-state and failure conditions
  - Network latency between network access servers and AAA servers
  - AAA server databases synchronization
bestPracticeTitle: Redundant Tacacs servers not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no tcp window-size
bestPracticeDescription: IOS XR network TCP/IP stacks should not be tuned for TCP window size. The default value of the TCP window should be adequate to allow fast file transfers between the TCP/IP stacks. CCO Note: Do not use this command unless you clearly understand why you want to change the default value.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The Best Practice recommendation is not to configure TCP/IP windows size on IOS XR platfrom.
bestPracticeTitle: tcp window-size none default value

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below :
!
switch virtual domain 11
 switch mode virtual
 mac-address use-virtual
 dual-active detection pagp trust channel-group 9
 dual-active detection pagp trust channel-group 10
 dual-active detection pagp trust channel-group 11
!
OR

Dual-active detection BFD configured:
!
switch virtual domain 100
dual-active pair interface GigabitEthernet1/5/1 interface GigabitEthernet2/5/1 bfd
!
OR 

TWO interfaces on DIFFERENT switches with "dual-active fast-hello". First number in the interface is the switch ID.
! 
Switch 1
interface GigabitEthernet1/1/2
 dual-active fast-hello
! 
Switch 2
interface GigabitEthernet2/1/2
 dual-active fast-hello
!
bestPracticeDescription: Control plane traffic maintains the state machine synchronization between the two VSS chassis. Any disruption or failure to communicate on the VSL link leads to a catastrophic instability in VSS. The switch member that assumes the role of hot-standby keeps the constant communication with the active switch. The role of the hot-standby switch is to assume the active role as soon as it detects a loss of communication with its peer via the VSL link. This transition of roles is normal when either triggered via switchover (user initiated) or the active switch has some trouble. However, during a fault condition, there is no way to differentiate that either remote switch has rebooted or whether the links between the active and hot-standby switches have become inoperative. In both cases, the hot-standby switch immediately assumes the role of an active switch. This can lead to what is known as the dual-active condition in which both switch supervisors assume that they are the in-charge of control plane and start interacting with network as active supervisors.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The best way to avoid exposing your network to a dual-active condition is to apply the following best practices:
* Diversify VSL connectivity with redundant ports, line cards, and internal system resources.
* Use diverse fiber-optic paths for each VSL link. In the case of a single conduit failure, a dual-active condition would not be triggered.
* Manage traffic forwarded over the VSL link using capacity planning for normal and abnormal conditions. 
Not only is it critical to avoid the dual-active condition, it is also important to detect such a condition and take steps to recover quickly.
The following methods are available to detect the dual-active condition:
* Enhanced PAgP
* Fast-Hello VSLP framework-based hello
* Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) 
Cisco recommends using enhanced PAgP and fast-hello detection in combination with Layer-3 MEC topology to the core for convergence less than one second for both Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) and Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (IGRP).
* Avoid using BFD detection with Enhanced IGRP and OSPF because recovery is complex and variable when core connectivity from the VSS is Layer-3 MEC-based.
* For OSPF, use Enhanced PAgP or fast-hello (both coupled with MEC) if possible for convergence times less than one second.
* Use VSLP fast-hello with MEC as replacement for BFD to ensure better convergence without the complexity of recovery and topological restrictions (the exception to this recommendation is when OSPF is implemented in an ECMP-based topology).
bestPracticeTitle: Dual-active detection not configured for VSS domain

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
line vty
 exec-timeout 10
!
line console
 exec-timeout 10
!
bestPracticeDescription: Exec timeout defines the interval that the EXEC command interpreter waits until user input is detected.To set the interval that the EXEC command interpreter waits for user input before it terminates a session, run the exec-timeout line configuration command. The exec-timeout command must be used to log out sessions on a vty or physical terminal line (tty) that is left idle (inactive). By default in Cisco NX-OS, sessions are set to disconnect after 30 minutes of inactivity.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The default timeout varies for different NX-OS releases. Sometimes the default is that timeout is disabled (i.e. 0 minutes). In recent code, the default timeout is 30 minutes.  A timeout value of ten or fifteen minute must be set and is usually acceptable for most security policies.
bestPracticeTitle: Console exec timeout too high

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for WarmStart SNMP Traps

snmp-server enable traps snmp warmstart
snmp-server community public RO 1
snmp-server community private RW 1
snmp-server host 192.168.1.1 abc
bestPracticeDescription: Simple Network Management Protocol(SNMP): SNMP is an application-layer protocol that provides a message format for communication between managers and agents. The SNMP system consists of an SNMP manager, an SNMP agent, and a MIB. The SNMP manager can be part of a network management system (NMS) such as CiscoWorks. The agent and MIB reside on the switch. To configure SNMP on the switch, you define the relationship between the manager and the agent.The SNMP agent contains MIB variables whose values the SNMP manager can request or change. A manager can get a value from an agent or store a value into the agent. The agent gathers data from the MIB, the repository for information about device parameters and network data. The agent can also respond to a manager?s requests to get or set data.An agent can send unsolicited traps to the manager. Traps are messages alerting the SNMP manager to a condition on the network. Traps can mean improper user authentication, restarts, link status (up or down), MAC address tracking, closing of a TCP connection, loss of connection to a neighbor, or other significant events.The stack master handles the SNMP requests and traps for the whole switch stack. The stack master transparently manages any requests or traps that are related to all stack members. When a new stack master is elected, the new master continues to handle SNMP requests and traps as configured on the previous stack master, assuming that IP connectivity to the SNMP management stations is still in place after the new master has taken control. Simple Network Management Protocol(SNMP) Traps: SNMP traps are used and the role they play in the management of a data network.SNMP traps enable an agent to notify the management station of significant events by way of an unsolicited SNMP message.In this diagram, the setup on the left shows a network management system that polls information and gets a response. The setup on the right shows an agent that sends an unsolicited or asynchronous trap to the network management system (NMS). WarmStart SNMP Traps: A warmStart trap signifies that the SNMPv2 entity, acting in an agent role, is reinitializing itself such that its configuration is unaltered.The snmp device performs a software reload.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the warmstart SNMP traps to ensure that notifications for warmstart events are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: WarmStart SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If the SNMP server is implemented redundantly with sufficient high-availability using 1 IP address, the exception can be ignored.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: 'snmp-server host  '
bestPracticeDescription: Sending SNMP traps to an external trap receiver is critical for effectively managing a network. In order to provide redundancy in NMS systems, traps should be sent to at least two different IP hosts.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure a second SNMP trap receiver for redundancy.
bestPracticeTitle: No redundant SNMP trap receiver

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no logging A.B.C.D or no logging X:X::X
bestPracticeDescription: Logging syslog to an external syslog server is critical for effectively managing a network. However, too many syslog destinations can put extra load on the device and use up bandwidth unnecessarily.  During a minor or major outage, a syslog storm could have a multiplying effect on traffic loads on lower speed links and lead to dropped messages.  In addition, it puts an administrative strain on an organization to manage syslogs sent to numerous servers.   In most networks, a primary, backup, and disaster recovery destination will be all that is needed.  Another consideration is the management of the configuration on each device when a new syslog destination needs to be turned up.  Using a UDP traffic multiplexer tool (such as syslog-ng, Samplicator, etc.) allows for you to replicate a single flow to many receivers.  Turning up a new receiver only requires a change in the one tool versus changing potentially hundreds or thousands of devices.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Do not configure more than four syslog destinations. Unneeded destinations should be removed from the configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: Too many syslog servers

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
controller SONET R/S/I/P
report lrdi
bestPracticeDescription: SONET Report line remote defect indication (lrdi) errors has not been configured, which is disabled by default. Reporting an alarm means the alarm can be logged to the SYSLOG. SONET alarm hierarchy rules dictate only the most severe alarm of an alarm group is reported. Whether an alarm is reported or not, you can check the current state of masked alarm (a problem indication that is a candidate for an alarm) by displaying the "Masked Alarms" line in the 'show controllers sonet' command output.
bestPracticeRecommendation: For SONET root cause analysis configure 'report lrdi'.
bestPracticeTitle: report lrdi (SONET) not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router isis
  no hello padding

interface
  no isis hello padding
bestPracticeDescription: IS-IS hellos are padded to the MTU of the interface. This can result in wasted bandwidth, especially on "fat pipe" circuits. Disabling hello padding saves some network bandwidth and prevents problems associated with MTU mismatches from being detected before adjacencies are formed. On interfaces operating in point-to-point mode when hello padding is disabled, Cisco routers still send padded hellos until the adjacency with the neighbor is up. To eliminate hello padding even before an adjacency is up use the always keyword.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Disable hello padding with the "no hello padding" command in the "router isis" block, or with the "no isis hello padding" in the interface block of any interface participating in ISIS.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS Hello Padding Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: This feature was introduced in 12.3(14)T and 12.2(33)SRE.  Some Catalyst 4500s running 15.0(2)SG do not have this command available.  In those cases either the exception can be ignored or a code upgrade can be planned in consultation with your AS Engineer.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configure as below :-
!
username user1 privilege 15 password 0 test
!
aaa new-model
aaa local authentication attempts max-fail 5
aaa authentication login default local
!
bestPracticeDescription: The Login Password Retry Lockout feature, added in Cisco IOS Software Release 12.3(14)T, allows you to lock out a local user account after a configured number of unsuccessful login attempts. Once a user is locked out, their account is locked until you unlock it. An authorized user who is configured with privilege level 15 cannot be locked out with this feature. The number of users with privilege level 15 must be kept to a minimum. Note that authorized users can lock themselves out of a device if the number of unsuccessful login attempts is reached. Additionally, a malicious user can create a denial of service (DoS) condition with repeated attempts to authenticate with a valid username.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure a maximum number of failed attempts before the local user account is locked out.The system administrator cannot be locked out. Authorized users can lock themselves out because there is no distinction between an attacker who is guessing passwords and an authorized user who is entering the password incorrectly multiple times.A denial of service (DoS) attack is possible (that is, an authorized user could be locked out by an attacker if the username of the authorized user is known to the attacker) but can be mitigated by using access control lists (ACLs) on the VTY lines.
bestPracticeTitle: Local user account is not protected against potential brute-force attacks

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'cable throttle-ranging' globally.
bestPracticeDescription:
'Cable Throttle-Ranging' automatically increases the Cable Insertion Interval to prevent Cable Modem Ranging from overwhelming the CMTS CPU. High numbers of Cable Modems ranging simultaneously impose a large CPU overhead for certain UBR10K platforms with Route Processors such as the ESR-PRE2 and ESR-PRE4.
Using this global command allows Cisco IOS to automatically increase the insertion interval, leaving a longer interval (more than the default 60 ms) for Cable Modem when there are many Cable Modems ranging. This situation usually arises during cutovers or mass modem deletions and reregistration.
By increasing the insertion interval, this leaves a longer interval before the Cable Modem can contend for a ranging opportunity, which leads to reducing the rate at which modems range.
Not having this command should be fine with UBR10Ks with newer UBR-MC2020 and ESR-PRE4 processors; however, as a general precaution, Cisco recommends to have this command present. Even if no contention occurs, this command will not negatively affect Cable Modem Ranging.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable 'cable throttle-ranging' globally.
bestPracticeTitle: Cable throttle-ranging not enabled; possible high CPU during mass reregistration

bestPracticeCaveat: If a customer is using a web browser interface to manage its network devices, then the HTTP server is required. If the router is to support secure web-based applications, it will also need the HTTP secure-server. The device should be protected by an access list as a minimum. Strong authentication should be considered.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Issue the 'no ip http secure-server' command.
bestPracticeDescription: Any network device that is running an HTTP secure-server should be protected by a firewall, an access list, strong authentication, or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized usage.
bestPracticeRecommendation: All unnecessary services should be disabled on a device if you do not have a need for them in your network.
bestPracticeTitle: HTTP secure-server is enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Perform this change during a maintenance window in case of any service interruption.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router ospf 
 router-id 1.1.1.1
bestPracticeDescription: If the OSPF router-id command is not configured, then by default the router ID is the highest IP version 4 (IPv4) address for an interface on the router, with any loop back interface taking precedence. This has a potential for conflict RID.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is a good practice to use the router-id command explicitly. Specify a unique 32-bit numeric value for the router ID. This action ensures that OSPF can function regardless of the interface address configuration. The router-id number uniquely identifies the router within an Autonomous System. When the router's OSPF Router ID is changed, the router's OSPF software must be restarted before the new Router ID takes effect. Before restarting in order to change its Router ID, the router must flush its self-originated LSAs from the routing domain or they will persist for up to MaxAge minutes.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF router ID not explicitly configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: authenticate
bestPracticeDescription: NTP is an important tool for troubleshooting and event correlation. Any network device accepting NTP should be protected by a firewall or access lists, or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized usage. Use the authenticate command to enable NTP authentication. When NTP authentication is enabled, the system will not synchronize to a time source unless it carries one of the authentication keys specified by the trusted-key command. Packets failing the authentication check will be dropped. NTP authentication is disabled by default.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is a good practice in a large network to synchronize time across all devices using NTP. However, falsifying time or a time source is a useful tool to mask any unauthorized activity. Network devices should accept time only from authorized servers. Use both access lists, trusted-key and MD5 to protect NTP listening devices.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP authentication not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
controller SONET R/S/I/P
path
report prdi
bestPracticeDescription: SONET reports path remote defect indication (PRDI) errors has not been configured, which is disabled by default. Reporting an alarm means the alarm can be logged to the SYSLOG. SONET alarm hierarchy rules dictate that only the most severe alarm of an alarm group is reported. Whether an alarm is reported or not, you can check the current state of masked alarm (a problem indication that is a candidate for an alarm) by displaying the "Masked Alarms" line in the 'show controllers sonet' command output.
bestPracticeRecommendation: For SONET path root cause analysis configure 'report prdi'.
bestPracticeTitle: report prdi (SONET path) not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: CDP has legitimate use for troubleshooting network issues; however,on interfaces directly connected to network edges or devices not in the same administrative domain,CDP should be disabled to prevent data mining.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
'no cdp enable (global)' 
or 
'no cdp enable (interface)'
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) is media- and protocol-independent and runs on all Cisco-manufactured equipment including routers, bridges, access servers, and switches. CDP is useful for troubleshooting and building topology information by network management system (NMS) platforms.  Due to the importance of the information obtainable through CDP, care should be exercised in deploying this protocol, especially at the network edge. CDP is enabled globally by default and doesn't appear in a 'show run'.
bestPracticeRecommendation: If CDP is required globally on a device,it can be disabled on a per-interface basis using the 'no cdp enable' command.  CDP should be disabled on all interfaces facing a different administrative domain. Your configuration can also carry an explicit 'no cdp enable' if CDP is not globally required or permitted on the device.
bestPracticeTitle: CDP is enabled globally and active on all interfaces.

bestPracticeCaveat: If a customer is using a web browser interface to manage the 2900XL and 3500XL switches using the Cluster Management Software (CMS), then the HTTP server is required.  It should be protected by an access list at a minimum.  Strong authentication should be considered.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no ip http server
bestPracticeDescription: Any network device that is running an HTTP server should be protected by a firewall, an access list, strong authentication, or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized use. This feature is enabled by default on Cisco 2900XL and 3500XL switches.
bestPracticeRecommendation: All unnecessary services should be disabled on a device if they are not needed by the network.
bestPracticeTitle: HTTP server enabled on 2900XL 3500XL switch

bestPracticeCaveat: This exception is not applied to critical interfaces like uplinks and key servers. If the customer is not willing to dedicate any interfaces of 7K to end users, then do not follow this best practice recommendation.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
'interface ' 
	'no snmp trap link-status'
bestPracticeDescription: Disable Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) trap notifications on end-user interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: NXOS switches should be sending interface traps for critical interfaces (for example, uplinks, key servers), which is the default behavior. However, interface traps for end-user interfaces are usually not beneficial and should be turned off.
bestPracticeTitle: Interface traps not disabled on at least one interface

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Admin Configure:
diagnostic schedule location
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco IOS XR online diagnostics allows the user to test and verify hardware functionality while connected to a live network. Scheduled diagnostics help ensure system High Availability (HA). If a problem is detected, online diagnostics help isolate the location of the problem; allowing the user to identify and replace the hardware. Online diagnostics are also used for system acceptance of new hardware. The online diagnostics contain tests that check different hardware components and verify the data path and control signals. Nondisruptive online diagnostic tests can be scheduled or run on-demand. On-demand diagnostics run from the command line interface (CLI); scheduled diagnostics run at user-designated intervals or specified times when the system is connected to a live network. Online diagnostics is one of the requirements for high HA. HA is a set of quality standards that seek to limit the impact of equipment failures on the network. A key part of HA is detecting hardware failures and taking corrective action while the system continues to run in a live network. Online diagnostics detect hardware failures and provide feedback to high availability software components. Note: Online diagnostics is supported on line card (LC) and route processor (RP) CPU0 nodes. Offline diagnostics is supported on all nodes except line card service processor (SP) nodes. Note: The Cisco IOS XR Diagnostics Package must be installed to access the Cisco IOS XR diagnostics. See Cisco IOS XR Getting Started Guide for information on installing packages. Node 0/RP1/CPU0 [RP] [SDR: Owner] Active Packages: disk0:hfr-diags-3.x.0 <<<< To configure the diagnostic schedule, use the diagnostic schedule command in administration configuration mode. Note: Use of the online diagnostics schedule node-id locations test all may interrupt network service. In IOS XR 3.3 all scheduled tests are none-disruptive, but in future releases this may not be the case. In IOS XR 3.4.0 the test 'non-disruptive' can be selected instead of 'all'.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Most Online Diagnostics schedule are default disabled and should be scheduled according to hardware configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: Online Diagnostics Schedule Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Disable exec ability on the line aux if the port is not used.
bestPracticeDescription: The purpose of an auxiliary port is to connect to an external modem. On regular connectivity failure, once configured, this modem can be used as a backup demand-dial connection to another location, or as a way to dial-in to the router for troubleshooting. If not secured, then this could be vulnerable.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure 'no exec' under line aux which are not being used to ensure misuse of the dial-up system.
bestPracticeTitle: Exec enabled on line aux

bestPracticeCaveat: New feature in IOS in 12.1(5)T, 12.0(11)S, and 12.2(9)S. Also introduced on 6500 Native platform in 12.1(13)E.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
snmp-server ifindex persist
or
snmp ifmib ifindex persist (in 12.2SR code)
bestPracticeDescription: One of the identifiers most commonly used in SNMP-based network management applications is the interface index (ifIndex) value. IfIndex is a unique identifying number associated with a physical or logical interface; as far as most software is concerned, the ifIndex is the "name" of the interface.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Although there is no requirement in the relevant RFCs that the correspondence between particular ifIndex values and their interfaces be maintained across reboots, applications such as device inventory, billing, and fault detection increasingly depend on the maintenance of this correspondence. Therefore, it is recommended to turn on ifindex persistence, were available in IOS code.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP Interface Index Persistence not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface 
 description
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco NX-OS provides a means to affix text descriptors to individual interfaces. These descriptors are helpful in troubleshooting and can be used to identify a service carrier, circuit numbers, or other information used by network operations personnel for problem diagnosis.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Meaningful interface descriptions should be configured on all interfaces. In the description, include a simple string describing the interface or subnet, and include the subnet address and mask. For WAN, include Telco service numbers, neighbor devices, etc.
bestPracticeTitle: No interface description

bestPracticeCaveat: TACACS+ must be supported and enabled on the AAA server.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure AAA accounting with TACACS+ server group: 

feature tacacs+
tacacs-server host 
tacacs-server key 
!
aaa group server tacacs+ 
!
aaa accounting default group
bestPracticeDescription: When AAA accounting is configured, the command sends information about each EXEC or configuration command that is entered back to the configured TACACS+ servers. The information sent to the TACACS+ servers includes the command executed, the date it was executed, and the username of the user entering the command. Command accounting is not supported using RADIUS.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
AAA Accounting for each level of commands ensures there is accountability for use of privileged commands on the device. This allows tracking the activities of the device users.
The Nexus switches by default store user accounting records locally (show accounting log). This local accounting cant be disabled, and the accounting log is maintained after reload. The advantage of using AAA accounting is that the logs are centralized and cant be deleted, while on the switches anyone could delete the accounting log (clear accounting log).
With TACACS+ accounting, the information about the command executed is also sent to the TACACS+ server. Thus it is recommended to use TACACS+ rather than RADIUS.
bestPracticeTitle: TACACS AAA Accounting not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
aaa new-model 
aaa authentication login default group (radius | tacacs) line OR aaa new-model aaa authentication login NAMED_LIST group (radius | tacacs) line line aux | login authentication NAMED_LIST
bestPracticeDescription: Access to the aux should be protected with strong authentication. This requires configuration of the aux to use AAA.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure RADIUS or TACACS+ authentication for use by the console. You may also configure a password for the line, though this should be the fallback method.
bestPracticeTitle: The aux not protected by AAA

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: tcp timestamp
bestPracticeDescription:
The TCP timestamp allows a more accurate measure of the round-trip time of a packet.  Use the 'tcp-
timestamp' command in global configuration mode. If a time stamp is not used, a TCP sender deduces the
round-trip time when an acknowledgment of the packet is received.  This is not a very accurate method
because the acknowledgment can be delayed, duplicated, or lost. If a time stamp is used, each packet
contains a time stamp identifying the packets when acknowledgments are received and the round-trip
time of that packet. TCP is defined in RFC 793 as a means to provide reliable transmission between hosts
in packet-switched computer networks.  RFC 1323 introduces the TCP timestamps option to increase the
performance of TCP.  This feature is most useful in Long Fat Network (LFN) where the bandwidth
* delay product is long.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Consider using the TCP timestamp command to increase the performance of TCP application between Network Elements and TCP/IP Applications.
bestPracticeTitle: tcp timestamp not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: This is an optional best practice. Some customers may choose to not implement.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: aaa accounting commands 1 default start-stop group tacacs+
bestPracticeDescription:
As described in RFC 2865 for RADIUS and RFC 1492 for TACACS, these protocols provide strong authentication to protect against unauthorized access to network devices.  AAA Accounting for each level of commands ensures there is accountability for use of privileged commands on the router.  Privilege levels range from 1 to 15, with 15 being the highest level.  Some organizations may want to implement additional levels of commands where 1 might be a help desk and 15 are network administrators.The record keeping can be set to stop-start or stop-only, though command accounting generated Stop records.  Cisco supports command accounting with TACACS+.

In later versions of IOS, the running configuration format for this command changed and this rule was updated to support those changes.  Example:

aaa accounting commands 1 default
 action-type start-stop
 group tacacs+
bestPracticeRecommendation: Accounting should be configured using the default or a named method, for each level of user access to network devices.  Customers who have more stringent security requirements, may want to implement additional levels such as 1, 7, and 15 for increasing levels of command access.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA Accounting for command level 1 not configured properly

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
!
tacacs-server host 10.6.7.8 port 49
key 7 13061E010803
!
tacacs-server host 10.2.3.4 port 49
key 7 01100F175804 !
bestPracticeDescription: Specify a redundant TACACS+ host server, use the 'tacacs-server host' command in global configuration mode. The key keyword must be entered last because it uses a line (text with breaks) rather than a string (text only, with no breaks). Any text and line breaks up to the time the user presses Enter can be used as part of the key. Multiple 'tacacs-server host' commands can be used to specify additional hosts. Cisco IOS XR software searches for hosts in the order in which they are specified. Defaults: No TACACS+ host is specified. The port keyword, if not specified, defaults to the standard port 49. The timeout keyword, if not specified, defaults to 5 seconds.
bestPracticeRecommendation: 'tacacs-server host' should have more than one TACAS+ server configured, for high availability TACACS+ infrastructure.
bestPracticeTitle: tacacs-server host has only one server configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: ipv6 conflict-policy static
bestPracticeDescription: To enable IP Address Repository Manager (IPARM) conflict resolution, use the IPv6 'conflict-policy' command in global configuration mode. Default is the lowest rack/slot if no conflict policy is configured. The policy tells IPARM what address to select from if the addresses conflict. The policy then forces the address in conflict to become inactive.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure IPv6 conflict-policy static, to keep the existing interface running across new address configurations.
bestPracticeTitle: ipv6 conflict-policy not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure 
'ntp peer '
OR
'ntp server '.
bestPracticeDescription: The Network Time Protocol (NTP), RFC 1305, synchronizes timekeeping among a set of distributed time servers and clients and allows events to be correlated when system logs are created or other time-specific events occur. Time correlation of events is extremely valuable in two cases: troubleshooting and security audits. Care should be taken to protect the time sources and data, and encryption is recommended so that key events are not erased either intentionally or unintentionally. NTP configuration commands can also be run from global configuration mode by preceding the command string with the 'ntp' keyword.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable NTP either as a client/server or broadcast client.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Define local user account using the command:
'username  {password | secret} '
bestPracticeDescription: Local login should be set as a contingency in case the authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) server cannot be accessed. In such cases, local username and password need to be configured to provide local authentication.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to ensure that the local database is not empty while using local database as backup.
bestPracticeTitle: Local username not defined when local auth used as backup AAA
