bestPracticeCaveat:
MOP can be used to download a system image to diskless stations. Verify if MOP is required 
on a particular interface.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
!
interface FastEthernet0/0/0  
no mop enabled 
no mop sysid
!
bestPracticeDescription: Maintenance Operations Protocol (MOP) provides a way to perform primitive maintenance operations on DECnet systems. For example, MOP can be used to download a system image to a diskless station. It is a collection of functions that can operate without the presence of the DEC Digital Network Architecture (DNA) layers between the network management and data link layers. This allows access to nodes that exist in a state in which only data link layer services are available or operational. It can be used on ethernet networks.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Disable MOP on ethernet interfaces, if MOP is not required for operation.It is a general security best practice to disable all features that are not specifically required.
bestPracticeTitle: MOP is enabled on one or more interface.

bestPracticeCaveat: None
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Reduce vpc system priority to a value lower than default value.
It's value should be lower than access switch connected below.
bestPracticeDescription:
For LACP, the system priority is used to negotiate which peer must start to offer the capabilities of LACP PortChannel. In case of vPC, it is recommended that the vPC switch will be the peer which starts to offer the capabilities and timers of a LACP PortChannel.
The default value used on server and switches is 32667, so the vPC switch must have a significant lower value.
In case of an environment where from Distribution / Aggregation down to Access spanning tree is used, we recommend to have the lowest value on the Distribution / Aggregation for example: 1, then the next lower level on the Access switches for example: 8192.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Use a vpc system priority which is less than default value.
bestPracticeTitle: vPC system priority is default

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Under appropriate line, add "no exec-timeout" to restore the default or set to a specific time other than 0 0.
bestPracticeDescription: Idle sessions consume lines and could prevent legitimate users from accessing the device. If "exec-timeout" is set to zero minutes and zero seconds, this means that timeout has been disabled. If no input is detected during the interval, the EXEC facility resumes the current connection. If no connections exist, the EXEC facility returns the terminal to the idle state and disconnects the incoming session. To disable the EXEC timeout function so that the EXEC session never timeouts, enter the 'exec-timeout 0 0' command. To set the interval that the EXEC command interpreter waits until user input is detected, use the 'exec-timeout' command in line template configuration mode. Defaults are 10 minutes and 0 seconds.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Exec-timeout 0 0 should not be implemented on production network elements. The network sessions must timeout after a predefined period of user inactivity.
bestPracticeTitle: Exec timeout disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below
!
router bgp 1
 address-family ipv4 vrf TEST
  neighbor 10.1.1.2 remote-as 2
  neighbor 10.1.1.2 activate 
  neighbor 10.1.1.2 maximum-prefix 200 
!
bestPracticeDescription: The BGP Maximum-Prefix feature allows you to control how many prefixes can be received from a neighbor. By default, this feature allows a router to bring down a peer when the number of received prefixes from that peer exceeds the configured Maximum-Prefix limit. This feature is commonly used for external BGP peers, but can be applied to internal BGP peers also.An enhancement to this feature is introduced in Cisco IOS Software Release 12.0(22)S and 12.2(15)T. The enhancement allows the user to automatically reestablish a peering session that has been brought down because the configured Maximum-Prefix limit is exceeded. No intervention from the network operator is required when this feature is enabled.BGP prefixes are stored by a router in memory. The more prefixes a router holds, the greater is the memory consumed by BGP. Both malicious and inadvertent (misconfiguration) processes can cause excessive prefixes to be seen by a BGP peer. To prevent memory exhaustion, it is important to configure the maximum number of prefixes accepted on a per-peer basis. Administrators are advised to configure limits for each BGP peer. The BGP maximum-prefix feature allows users to control the number of prefixes that can be installed from a neighbor. When configured, this feature will bring down a neighbor relationship when the number of received prefixes from that peer exceeds the configured maximum-prefix limit. The maximum-prefix threshold would be set with some margin of error over a target value to accommodate some degree of unexpected changes in topology.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The Maximum-Prefix feature is useful when, at a change of outbound policy at the remote peering site, a router starts to receive more routes than the router memory can take. If this same router is peering with BGP and also performs critical routing functions within a network, this overhead could turn out badly. A BGP problem could disrupt internal network connectivity. With the 'neighbor maximum-prefix' command, it is possible to protect a router against this situation. 
When the customer plans to use this feature, they should consider these key points: 
1) Know how many routes the remote BGP peering router normally sends. 
2) Set a threshold a little higher than the number of BGP prefixes expected to be received during normal operations. 
3) Know the action to take in case the remote BGP peer sends more prefixes than those expected. Available actions could either be to bring down the session, to keep the BGP neighbor relationship down until you use the
'clear ip bgp x.x.x.x' command, or, alternatively, to only log a warning message.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP maximum-prefix protection not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Under 'router isis [tag]' configure 'nsf {cisco, ietf}'.
bestPracticeDescription: It is recommended to implement Nonstop Forwarding (NSF) within high availability networks. On Cisco IOS XR software, NSF minimizes the amount of time a network is unavailable to its users following a Route Processor (RP) failover. The main objective of NSF is to continue forwarding IP packets and perform a graceful restart following an RP failover. ISIS NSR function is provided by "nsf cisco" only. When a router restarts, all routing peers of that device usually detect the device went down and then came back up. This transition results in what is called a routing flap, which could spread across multiple routing domains. Routing flaps caused by routing restarts create routing instabilities; which are detrimental to the overall network performance. NSF helps to suppress routing flaps in NSF-aware devices, thus reducing network instability. NSF allows for the forwarding of data packets to continue along known routes while the routing protocol information is being restored following an RP failover. When the NSF feature is configured, peer networking devices do not experience routing flaps. Data traffic is forwarded through intelligent line cards while the standby RP assumes control from the failed active RP during a failover. The ability of line cards to remain up through a failover and to be kept current with the Forwarding Information Base (FIB) on the active RP is key to NSF operation. When the Cisco IOS XR router running Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS) routing performs an RP failover, the router must perform two tasks to resynchronize its link-state database with its IS-IS neighbors. First, it must relearn the available IS-IS neighbors on the network without causing a reset of the neighbor relationship. Second, it must reacquire the contents of the link-state database for the network. The IS-IS NSF feature offers two options when configuring NSF: - IETF NSF - Cisco NSF If neighbor routers on a network segment are NSF aware, meaning neighbor routers are running a software version that supports the IETF Internet draft for router re-startability, they assist an IETF NSF router in restarting. With IETF NSF, neighbor routers provide adjacency and link-state information to help rebuild the routing information following a failover. In Cisco IOS XR software, Cisco NSF checkpoints (stores persistently) all the state necessary to recover from a restart without requiring any special cooperation from neighboring routers. The state is recovered from the neighboring routers, but only using the standard features of the IS-IS routing protocol. This capability makes Cisco NSF suitable for use in networks in which other routers have not used the IETF standard implementation of NSF. Note: If IETF NSF is configured on the Cisco IOS XR router and a neighbor router does not support IETF NSF, the affected adjacencies flap, but nonstop forwarding is maintained to all neighbors that do support IETF NSF. A restart reverts to a cold start if no neighbors support IETF NSF. NSF allows an IS-IS instance to restart using checkpointed adjacency and link-state packet (LSP) information, and to perform restart with no impact on its neighbor routers. In other words, there is no impact on other routers in the network due to the destruction and recreation of adjacencies and the system LSP.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable NSF for routing protocol IS-IS.
bestPracticeTitle: nsf (IS-IS) disabled

bestPracticeCaveat: The network administrator may desire to permit ICMP unreachable messages in certain environments. For example, ICMP type 3, code 1; code 3, and code 4 are required for the SMTP email gateway.  It is also not recommended to implement this comand on interfaces associated with IPsec VPNs.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
The following example enables the generation of ICMP unreachable messages, as appropriate, on an interface :-
!
interface FastEthernet0/1/0
 ip address (IP) (Subnet)
 no ip unreachables
!
bestPracticeDescription: If the Cisco IOS software receives a non-broadcast packet destined for itself that uses a protocol it does not recognize, it sends an ICMP unreachable message to the source. Also, if the software receives a datagram that it cannot deliver to its ultimate destination because it knows of no route to the destination address, it replies to the originator of that datagram with an ICMP host unreachable message. The "ip unreachables" command affects all types of ICMP unreachable messages and is enabled by default in support of RFC 792. However, ICMP unreachables can potentially be used to map a network by correlating which addresses generate ICMP unreachables and which do not for networks, hosts, protocols, and ports. In the past, this rule was not properly run against interface configurations. Iif you find an exception here, this isn't something new a customer may have implemented. This rule checks all the interfaces on a device with an IP address and are not shutdown, and gives an exception if not even one interface has "no ip unreachable".
bestPracticeRecommendation: A customer should control ICMP unreachable responses in certain environments, especially along untrusted edges between administrative domains with the command "no ip unreachables" on affected interfaces. ICMP unreachables covers network, host, protocol, and port unreachable messages. Also, in certain software, the ICMP unreachable messages can be rate-limited using the command "ip icmp rate-limit unreachable". This rule will result in a "false positive" for devices where all interfaces are in a trusted domain.  This is not something that can be easily determined from a running configuration.  Customers should use the exception list to determine if affected devices should or should not implement this change on any of its edge interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: ICMP unreachables enabled on all interfaces of this device.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
controller SONET0/1/2/3
path
ais-shut
!
!
or
controller preconfigure SONET0/1/2/3
path
ais-shut
!
!
bestPracticeDescription: Enable automatic insertion of Path Alarm Indication Signal (PAIS) in the sent SONET signal whenever the SONET path enters the administratively down state. Use the 'ais-shut' command to enable automatic insertion of PAIS in the appropriate sent SONET path overhead whenever the corresponding SONET path enters the administratively down state.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
It is advised to implement 'ais-shut (SONET path)' under the controllers SONET 'path' sub-mode, to
allow faster IGP convergence when issueing a shutdown on a POS interface.

bestPracticeCaveat: The rule logic looks for ?no shutdown? under the interface and throws exception if the best practice isn?t followed. As per best practice,  by default interfaces should be in "shutdown" state.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure "switchport trunk native vlan " for trunked interfaces.
bestPracticeDescription: A trunk port can carry non-tagged packets simultaneously with the 802.1Q tagged packets. When you assign a default port VLAN ID to the trunk port, all untagged traffic travels on the default port VLAN ID for the trunk port, and all untagged traffic is assumed to belong to this VLAN. This VLAN is referred to as the native VLAN ID for a trunk port. That is, the native VLAN ID is the VLAN that carries untagged traffic on trunk ports. Native VLAN ID numbers must match on both ends of the trunk, and must be a VLAN not used for any data traffic within the network. This applies also for peer-link trunks, as they must follow the same security guidelines as normal trunks.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Non default native VLAN must be defined on any L2 trunk ports that include individual ethernet ports as well as L2 Port-Channels and vPC Peer-Links.
bestPracticeTitle: Native VLAN must be defined on any L2 trunk interfaces

bestPracticeCaveat: Older versions of IOS may not support ISIS authentication. Support for this feature was first integrated into releases 12.0(21)ST, 12.2(11)S, 12.0(22)S, 12.2(13)T, and 12.2(14)S.  Devices running older software and running ISIS might be reported as violating this best practice. While it won't be possible to apply authentication on those devices, it is recommended to consider upgrading software versions to a version that supports authentication to meet this best practice security recommendation.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable Authentication by configuring the ISIS process and all ISIS-enabled interfaces.
Example:

key chain isis
 key 1
  key-string test
!
router isis 
 net 48.2700.0001.0001.00
 authentication mode md5
 authentication key-chain isis
!
interface Ethernet0/0
 ip address 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
 ip router isis 
 isis circuit-type level-1
 isis authentication mode text
 isis authentication key-chain isis
bestPracticeDescription:
IS-IS Authentication provides for protection at the protocol level, preventing unauthorized injection 
of routing messages into the network.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure authentication for all IS-IS adjacencies.  This will provide enhanced protection for routing information and prevent the malicious insertion of invalid routing information into the customer's network.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS MD5 Authentication is not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router bgp
  timers bgp keepalive hold-time
bestPracticeDescription: The timers actually used in connection with the neighbor may not be the same as those configured with this command. The actual timers are negotiated with the neighbor when the session is established. If this command is configured for a neighbor group or neighbor address family group, all neighbors using the group inherit the configuration. Values of commands configured specifically for a neighbor override inherited values. The negotiated hold time is the minimum of the configured time and the hold time received from the neighbor. If the negotiated hold time is 0; keepalives are disabled. The configured value for the keepalive must not exceed one-third of the negotiated hold time. If it does, a value of one-third of the negotiated hold time is used. It is not advisable to have BGP Holdtime Interval set to a value of less than 20 seconds. By default, the Holdtime Interval is 180 seconds.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Change the BGP Holdtime Interval with the timer's BGP option to a value higher than 20 seconds or consider another BGP Fast Convergence mechanism such as BFD. BFD is a more scalable way to achieve fast failure detection.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Holdtime Interval Less Than 20 Seconds

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: router ospfv3 xyz graceful-restart
bestPracticeDescription: To increase network services availability when running OSPFv3, graceful restart should be enabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is advised to implement 'router ospfv3' 'graceful-restart', to increase network services availability.
bestPracticeTitle: Graceful-Restart (OSPFv3) Disabled

bestPracticeCaveat: The modules must support Distributed EtherChannel (DEC). If they don't, new hardware may be required. EtherChannel verifies port properties on all physical ports before it aggregates compatible ports into a single logical port. Configuration guidelines and restrictions vary for different switch platforms. Consult these guidelines and restrictions to avoid bundling problems. For example, if quality of service (QoS) is enabled, EtherChannels are not formed when bundling Catalyst 6500/6000 series switching modules with different QoS capabilities. In single supervisor chassis, having all channel ports on the supervisor doesn't impact resiliency, so these cases can be ignored.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Below Config for EtherChannel members to distribute over two or more linecards (or multiple chassis for VSS pairs)

!
switch mode virtual           			
!
interface Port-channel2
 no switchport
 no ip address								
 switch virtual link 2
 mls qos trust cos
 no mls qos channel-consistency
!
!
interface TenGigabitEthernet1/1/4
 no switchport
 no ip address
 mls qos trust cos
 channel-group 2 mode on				
! 													
! 													
!
interface TenGigabitEthernet2/1/4
 no switchport
 no ip address
 mls qos trust cos
 channel-group 2 mode on		
!
bestPracticeDescription: All Ethernet interfaces on all modules support EtherChannel (maximum of eight interfaces) with no requirement that interfaces be physically contiguous or on the same module.The Virtual Switching System (VSS) allows two Cisco Catalyst 6500 or 4500 chassis to bond together so that is seen as a single virtual swich to the rest of the network. Other devices will see the VSS configured 6500 as a single device which means it?s possible to use multi chassis etherchannel and protocols like spanning-tree will only see a single switch.
bestPracticeRecommendation: All EtherChannels should be distributed across multiple linecards to increase resiliency against a module failure. For VSS configurations, etherchannels should be split between chassis.
bestPracticeTitle: EtherChannel not distributed over different linecards or multiple chassis for VSS pairs

interface MgmtEth $$instance
ipv4 address $$IP-address/$$netmask
OR/AND
ipv6 address $$IP-address/$$netmask
bestPracticeDescription: This rule validates whether at least one interface MgmtEth has an IPv4 or IPv6 address configured on each Route Processor.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Best Practises is to have one interface MgmtEth instances of each installed Route Processor connected to the Network Management Network Ethernet Switch.  For standardisation use first MgmtEth port on the RP.

bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to define an access list of networks and/or devices that are authorized to connect to infrastructure devices.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Add the "description" command in MSDP Peer Configuration mode for every MSDP Peer.
bestPracticeDescription: This rule explains steps to take when a description is missing from at least one Multicast Source Discovery Protocol (MSDP) Peer definition. Configuring a "description" makes MSDP peers easier to identify and helps in troubleshooting.
bestPracticeRecommendation: To add descriptive text to the configuration for an MSDP peer, use the description command in peer configuration mode. You can see this description with the "show msdp peer" command.
bestPracticeTitle: MSDP description not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
enable subslot: no hw-module subslot subslot-id shutdown 
remove subslot: obsolete configuration: no hw-module subslot subslot-id shutdown.
bestPracticeDescription: The hw-module subslot should be used to administratively shut down a specific shared port adapter (SPA). Use the 'hw-module subslot shutdown' command in global configuration mode. This command administratively shuts down the SPA in the specified subslot. Subslots that are shut down still have power, but cannot load or operate Cisco IOS XR software. The shutdown should be a temporary configuration change, which is expected to be within the configuration only a limited amount of time. This rule checks the presence of MSDP peer(s) in shutdown.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Review the hw-module subslot shutdowns within the configuration and determine if these subslots may 
have become obsolete.
bestPracticeTitle: hw-module subslot shutdown configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no rp mgmtethernet forwarding
bestPracticeDescription: Switching from the line card to the route processor Management Ethernet interfaces should not be enabled. The IOS XR network devices should only forward user-data between ingress to egress line cards. Traffic switched from ingress line card to egress processor Management Ethernet interfaces are handled by the RP CPU; which may negatively impact IP convergence. The route processor Management Ethernet interfaces are designed for traffic to and from the Route Processor's TCP/IP stacks. Defaults: Switching is disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The Best Practice recommendation is not to implement user-data packet switching from the line card to the route processor Management Ethernet interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: rp mgmtethernet forwarding enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
If all subinterfaces require dampening, then apply dampening to the main interface only. Applying
configuration to large numbers of subinterfaces requires an abundance of memory and increases the
time required to process the configuration during boot and failover.
Default Interface dampening for current IOS XR releases may dampen an interface upto 30 minutes.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below:
 
interface 
dampening 1 750 2000 1

! Above will dampen interface upon 3 interface transitions from up to down within 1 minute to a maximum of 1 minute.
! If 1 minute is considered too long, use carrier-delay as workaround.
bestPracticeDescription: This rule performs checks for all non-shutdown physical/logical interfaces types that should have interface dampening enabled. Excluded are the following interface types: - Loopback - Null0 - MgmtEth - Packet Over SONET (POS) - all sub-interfaces. Event dampening suppresses a constantly unstable interface until it remains stable for a period of time.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to implement dampening on all main physical/logical interfaces, except Loopback, Null0, MgmtEth, sub-interfaces, and Packet Over Sonet (POS). Packet Over Sonet has 'dampening' behavior within the SONET specification.
bestPracticeTitle: Interface Dampening not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: This rule excludes devices using "ip helper-address", "ip dhcp bootp ignore' and "ip dhcp pool" in their configurations. Also, it may not be possible to disable bootp on low end switches such as the Catalyst 2950, 3560, 3750, and others. In those cases, the rule exception can be ignored.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
12.2(8)T or older: no ip bootp server

12.2(8)T or greater: ip dhcp bootp ignore
bestPracticeDescription: Any network device that is running BOOTP services should be protected by a firewall or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized usage. By default, the BOOTP server is enabled but doesn't appear on "show run". When you disable the BOOTP server, access to the BOOTP ports causes the Cisco IOS software to send an "ICMP port unreachable" message to the sender and discards the original incoming packet.
bestPracticeRecommendation: All minor services should be disabled on your system if you do not have a need for them in your network. In the case of BOOTP service, your configuration should carry an explicit "no ip bootp server" or "ip dhcp bootp ignore" unless expressly needed.
bestPracticeTitle: BOOTP server is enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
The zones have come obsolete by the modular power distribution unit. When the following PIDs are installed in the chassis then this rule can be ignored:
  CRS-16LCC-PSH-AC or CRS-16LCC-PSH-DC
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
interface TenGigE0/0/0/0
!
interface TenGigE0/4/0/0
!
interface TenGigE0/8/0/0
!
interface TenGigE0/12/0/0
!
interface TenGigE0/13/0/0
!
bestPracticeDescription: Network Design should not place all MSCs into the same Power Supplies A0/B0 (Zone 1 & 2), A1/B1 (Zone 3 & 4) or A2/B2 (Zone 5 & 6). The AC or DC power system distributes power in the chassis through six load zones, which provide power redundancy and reliability. Each load zone receives power from both bus bars (A and B), which ensures each card and module in the chassis is powered by both power shelves. A line card chassis can lose a single power module or an entire power shelf and still have the power to operate. For a load zone to lose complete power, a power module in each power shelf would have to fail. Overall recommendation: Place intra-core links on differenct MSC in different power zones [Zone1, {Zone3|Zone4}, Zone6] within the same rack, for Multichassis place intra-core lines over Rack [0,1,2..n]. The same design rule may be applied for Edge-connections.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to Place MSC in the following slot order: 
0, 4, 8,  12 then
1, 5, 9,  13 then
2, 6, 10, 14 then
3, 7, 11, 15

In the case of multi-chassis, place MSCs in round robin fashion: rack[0..n].
bestPracticeTitle: MSC placement in same load zone or MSC sequentially installed.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
banner login ^
$(hostname).$(domain) line=$(line)
Banner-login-text
bestPracticeDescription: Usage of login banners provides acceptable use parameters and may have legal basis depending on local jurisdiction. When a user connects to the router, the message-of-the-day (MOTD) banner (if configured) appears first, followed by the login banner and prompts. After the user successfully logs in to the router, the EXEC banner or incoming banner is displayed, depending on the type of connection. For a reverse Telnet login, the incoming banner is displayed. For all other connections, the router displays the EXEC banner. Follow the banner login command with one or more blank spaces and the delimiting character (c). Then enter one or more lines of text, terminating the message with the second occurrence of the delimiting character (c). To create a customized banner that is displayed before the username and password login prompts, use the banner login command in global configuration mode. Banner tokens may be used to provide NOC personel with device identification: wl-pe05-crs.cisco.com line=vty0. This allowes one standardization of banner login for a single network management administrative domain.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Create appropriate device banners to state specifically what constitutes authorized use and the
actions taken for unauthorized access. Do not advertise site name or network information that may
provide information to unauthorized users.
bestPracticeTitle: Banner login not configured

bestPracticeCaveat:
Line cards must be reloaded after disabling the default scale. This is done to ensure that the hw-module command configuration takes immediate effect.
hw-module fib ipv6 scale internet-optimized-disable.IPv4/IPv6 uRPF configuration requires the hw-module fib ipv6 scale internet-optimized-disable command for all types of cards, both TCAM cards and non-TCAM cards. By default, IPv6 uses internal memory for prefixes. Therefore, you need to configure the hw-module fib ipv6 scale internet-optimized-disable command and then reload the line card.

https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/iosxr/ncs5xx/system-security/70x/b-system-security-cg-70x-ncs540/b-system-security-cg-65x-ncs540_chapter_0111.html
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
If URPF is not configured then configure as follows :
!
Interface Serial0/1/2/0/1:0
ipv4 address 172.30.28.1 255.255.255.252
ipv4 verify unicast source reachable-via any
!
!
bestPracticeDescription: Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (URPF) is a mechanism for validating the source IP address of packets received on a router. A router configured with URPF performs a reverse path lookup in the FIB table to validate the presence of the source IP address. If the source IP address is listed in the table, then it indicates that the source is reachable and valid. If source IP address cannot be located in the FIB table, the packet is treated as malicious by the router and discarded.
bestPracticeRecommendation: CISCO highly recommends to configure uRPF checking. Unicast RPF checking allows you to mitigate problems caused by malformed or forged (spoofed) IP source addresses that pass through a router. Malformed or forged source addresses can indicate denial-of-service (DoS) attacks based on source IP address spoofing.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv4 uRPF not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below ;
!
aaa authentication login VTY LINE-AUTHEN-LOGIN group SGO local
!
bestPracticeDescription: In order to have secure access to the router, AAA needs to be invoked on the Cisco IOS XR router devices. Following this configuration, local database, TACACS+, or RADIUS servers can be used to log into device remotely. Without AAA server availability, network operators would have to have physical access to device until connectivity to AAA server is restored. This can be result of one of the following: - Network connectivity issues - Failure of AAA server - Incorrect configuration However; the impact is not being able to access a device remotely for administration as well as user connectivity.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure 'aaa authentication login' in order to increase network element security level for login access.
bestPracticeTitle: aaa authentication login not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below :

aaa new-model
aaa authentication login default group radius local
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ enable
aaa authentication enable default group tacacs+ enable
bestPracticeDescription: As described in RFC 2865 for RADIUS and RFC 1492 for TACACS, these protocols provide strong authentication methods to protect access to network devices. Using "none" means to "use no authentication."If the device has AAA Authentication login default group tacacs+ local in the configuration, it's first preference is TACACS. If the TACACS is reachable, but no user has configured on it, it will not fallback and try to search in the local databasde. It will display% Authentication failed message.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Authentication should be configured using a default or named list method, to secure access to network 
devices. There should also be a fallback method in the event AAA services are not available. 
This fallback method could be the enable password, a line password, or a locally configured user 
account.  The method "none" should not be used.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA authentication login uses a method of none

bestPracticeCaveat: The feature configuration best practise is not able to verify AAA server config's. Radius / Tacacs+ server should have user accounts that include root-system and cisco-support. Rule is only checking Admin config at this time. If the usernames are configured in the global config, then this exception can be ignored.  This rule only applies to ASR9K and CRS, not to NCS.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure on XR Router:
 username [user-id]
  group root-system
  ! For Network Operator Staff

  group cisco-support
  ! For Cisco Technical Assistence Engineers.

Or

 username [user-id]
   group root-system
  ! For Network Operator Staff 
  group cisco-support
  ! For Cisco Technical Assistence Engineers.

AAA Server: 
 Cisco Secure ACS: the following changes need to be made on the tacacs server:
        1.  Check the privilege level box and add whatever privilege level you want to assign
        2.  check the custom attributes box and add the user groups that you want to assign to that privilege level by using the following syntax:

  task*#root-system, #cisco-support

 Tacacs+ Freeware: the following changes need to be made on the tacacs server:
   1.  Configure the user or group with the correct privilege level
   2.  Configure the 'optional task' parameter with the user groups that you want to assign to that privilege level by using the following syntax:

  optional task = "#root-system, #cisco-support"

 RADIUS Vendor-Specific Attribute (VSA)
  Cisco-AVPair = "shell:tasks=#root-system, #cisco-support"
bestPracticeDescription: The predefined group root-system may be specified only by root-system users while configuring administration. If the group command is used in admin configuration mode, only root-system and cisco-support can be specified.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Advised is to have at least one username under Admin and/or Global configuration group with root-system and/or cisco-support.
bestPracticeTitle: No Username with group root-system or cisco-support

bestPracticeCaveat: In flat topologies, proxy-arp may be required.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-
!
interface FastEthernet0/0/0
 ip address 10.10.10.1 255.255.255.0
 no ip proxy-arp
!
bestPracticeDescription: The Cisco IOS software uses proxy ARP (as defined in RFC 1027) to help hosts with no knowledge of routing determine the media addresses of hosts on other networks or subnets. Normally, ARP transactions are confined to a particular LAN segment. A Cisco router can act as an intermediary for ARP, responding to ARP queries on selected interfaces and thus enabling transparent access between multiple LAN segments. Proxy ARP effectively extends a LAN at layer 2 across multiple segments. Cisco routers perform proxy ARP by default on all IP interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use of Proxy ARP on only between LAN segments at the same trust level, and only when needed to support flat network architectures.  Disable proxy ARP where it is not needed on IP configured interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: Proxy ARP is enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure ipv4 conflict-policy static,to keep the existing interface running across new address
configurations.
bestPracticeDescription:
To enable IP Address Repository Manager (IPARM) conflict resolution, use the IPv4 'conflict-policy'
command in global configuration mode. Default is the lowest rack/slot if no conflict policy is configured.
The policy tells IPARM what address to select from if the addresses conflict. The policy then forces the
address in conflict to become inactive.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Configure ipv4 conflict-policy static to keep the existing interface running across new address
configurations.
bestPracticeTitle: ipv4 conflict-policy not configured

bestPracticeDescription: Use the 'logging hostnameprefix' command to append a hostname prefix to messages sent to syslog servers from the router. You can use these prefixes to sort the messages being sent to a given syslog server from different networking devices. Use the logging command to specify a syslog server host as a destination for syslog messages. To append a hostname prefix to system logging (syslog) messages logged to syslog servers, use the 'logging hostnameprefix' command in global configuration mode. Advice is to use the same name as configured for 'hostname $$name'
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Use the logging hostnameprefix command to append a hostname prefix to messages sent to syslog
servers from the router.  Use these prefixes to sort the messages being sent to a given syslog server
from different networking devices.

Advice is to use the same name as stated in the global configuration command 'hostname'.

The notification type is: (1) ciscoConfigManEvent. Defaults: SNMP notifications are disabled by default.

bestPracticeCaveat: Switch interfaces configured as access port can be used to interconnect switches. It is not recommend to enable portfast on these interface unless the network topology does not contain layer2 loops.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for Spanning-tree portfast

interface fastEthernet 1/1
spanning-tree portfast
bestPracticeDescription: PortFast causes a switch or trunk port to enter the spanning tree forwarding state immediately, bypassing the listening and learning states. Use PortFast on switch or trunk ports that are connected to a single workstation, switch, or server to allow those devices to connect to the network immediately, instead of waiting for the port to transition from the listening and learning states to the forwarding state.When the switch powers up, or when a device is connected to a port, the port enters the spanning tree listening state. When the Forward Delay timer expires, the port enters the learning state. When the Forward Delay timer expires a second time, the port is transitioned to the forwarding or blocking state.When you enable PortFast on a switch or trunk port, the port is immediately transitioned to the spanning tree forwarding state.Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) is a Layer 2 protocol that runs on bridges and switches. The specification for STP is IEEE 802.1D. The main purpose of STP is to ensure that you do not create loops when you have redundant paths in your network. Loops are deadly to a network.
bestPracticeRecommendation: PortFast is used to bypass normal Spanning Tree operation on access ports to speed up connectivity between end-stations and the services they need to connect to after link initialization. On some protocols, such as IPX/SPX, it is important to see the access port in forwarding mode immediately after the link state has gone up in order to avoid GNS problems. Cisco recommends that STP PortFast be enabled for all active host ports and disabled for switch-switch links and ports not in use.
bestPracticeTitle: Spanning-tree portfast not enabled on access or edge port

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure "ospf auto-cost reference-bandwidth " with correct reference bandwidth under "router ospf " command.

Configuration as below,

router ospf 1

 log-adjacency-changes

 passive-interface default
 no passive-interface GigabitEthernet1/1
 no passive-interface Tengigabitethernet2/1
 network 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
 network 20.10.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
!
bestPracticeDescription: The OSPF metric is calculated as reference bandwidth divided by bandwidth, with reference bandwidth equal to 10^8(100Mbps) by default, and bandwidth determined by the bandwidth command. This is true for fast ethernet interfaces. However, this is not true for gigabit and 10 gigabit interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: According to Cisco's Best Practices, if any interfaces with higher speed than 100Mbps, then reference bandwidth needs to be modified accordingly for the correct OSPF metric calculation.

bestPracticeCaveat:
1. This feature needs to be configured on both sides of the peered link for the session to establish.  This cannot be configured on only one side. It protects the eBGP peering session in the incoming direction only and has no effect on outgoing IP packets or the remote router. 
2. When configuring the BGP Support for TTL Security Check feature to support an existing multihop peering session, you must first disable the 'neighbor ebgp-multihop router' configuration command by entering the 'no neighbor ebgp-multihop' command before configuring this feature with the 'neighbor ttl-security router' configuration command. These commands are mutually exclusive, and only one command is required to establish a multihop peering session. If you attempt to configure both commands for the same peering session, an error message will be displayed in the console. 
3. The effectiveness of this feature is reduced in large-diameter multihop peerings. In the event of a CPU utilization-based attack against a BGP router that is configured for large-diameter peering, you may still need to shut down the affected peering sessions to handle the attack. 
4. This feature is not effective against attacks from a peer that has been compromised inside your network. This restriction also includes BGP peers that are not part of the local or external BGP network but are connected to the network segment between the BGP peers (for example, a switch or hub that is used to connect the local and external BGP networks).
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
TTL security can be configured using the command below:

router bgp 100
no synchronization
bgp log-neighbor-changes
neighbor 10.1.1.1 remote-as 100
neighbor 10.1.1.1 ttl-security hops 2 
no auto-summary
bestPracticeDescription: The Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) Support for Time to Live (TTL) Security Check feature introduces a lightweight security mechanism to protect external Border Gateway Protocol (eBGP) peering sessions from CPU utilization-based attacks using forged IP packets. The BGP Support for TTL Security Check feature provides an effective and easy-to-deploy solution to protect eBGP peering sessions from CPU utilization-based attacks. When this feature is enabled, a host cannot attack a BGP session if the host is not a member of the local or remote BGP network or if the host is not directly connected to a network segment between the local and remote BGP networks. This solution greatly reduces the effectiveness of DoS attacks against a BGP autonomous system.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Enabling this feature prevents attempts to hijack the eBGP peering session by a host on a network segment that is not part of either BGP network or by a host on a network segment that is not between the eBGP peers.
You can enable this feature by configuring a minimum TTL value for incoming IP packets received from a specific eBGP peer. When this feature is enabled, BGP will establish and maintain the session only if the TTL value in the IP packet header is equal to or greater than the TTL value configured for the peering session. If the value is less than the configured value, the packet is silently discarded, and no Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) message is generated. This feature is both effective and easy to deploy.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP TTL Security disabled

In the new IOS you can remove the vlan1, which is called as Vlan minimization and the control traffic will be sent over the management vlan when the vlan 1 is removed from the trunk.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
snmp-server engineid local
bestPracticeDescription: Within an administrative domain, an snmpEngineID is the unique identifier of an SNMP engine. Since there is a one-to-one association between SNMP engines and SNMP entities, it uniquely and unambiguously identifies the SNMP entity within the administrative domain. Note that it is possible for SNMP entities in different administrative domains to have the same value for snmpEngineID. Federation of administrative domains may necessitate assignment of new values. The combination of a contextEngineID and a contextName uniquely identifies a context within an administrative domain. Note that there may be multiple unique combinations of contextEngineID and contextName that unambiguously identify the same context. Source: RFC 2571 - An Architecture for Describing SNMP Management Frameworks IOS XR Defaults generate automatically an SNMP engine ID.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Assign the administrative domain the Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) engine ID to the
local device.
bestPracticeTitle: snmp-server engineid not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers with global networks will use a single time zone for network devices, for example Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), and may not configure a time zone system.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure using below command example:
clock timezone CET 1
bestPracticeDescription: The clock timezone command only displays the time zone when setting the time manually. The system keeps time default internally in UTC. This 'clock timezone' is required in IOS XR Global and Admin Configuration, this is done in order to get timestamped between the SDR and admin-plane. If a Kernel or coredump is taken from a process running in the admin plane, the timestamp of the associated files may be different from that of a process running in the SDR plane. The reason appears to be that the timezone configuration is plane-specific. Therefore, the SDR and Admin plane should have the same 'clock' configuration.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is a good practice to set a time zone for devices in the same region to easily correlate network events to the local time of day. This could include 'clock summertime' too.
bestPracticeTitle: Clock enabled without timezone

1. Install interfaces or remove interface under IS-IS
2. Assign interface an IPv6 address
bestPracticeDescription: IOS XR is configured feature centric, therefore the IPv6 interface addressing is 'dis-entangled' from the IGP. The IS-IS and IPv6 interface addressing configuration is checked: 1. IS-IS has interface assigned, but interface is not present in the configuration 2. IS-IS has interface assigned, but interface is present but has no IPv6 address assigned.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Review of the network design and correct IS-IS address-family IPv6 interface and interfaces IPv6 addressing is applied.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS interfaces not present OR no IPV6 address assigned

bestPracticeCaveat: Different time zones have different summer-times, and some do not implement the scheme at all.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example:
clock summer-time CET recurring last sunday march 01:00 last sunday october 02:00
bestPracticeDescription: Use the clock summer-time command if you want the system to switch automatically to summer time for display only. This command is required in IOS XR Global and Admin Configuration, in order to get timestamped between the SDR and admin-plane. If a Kernel or coredump is taken from a process running in the admin plane, the timestamp of the associated files may be different from that of a process running in the SDR plane. The reason appears to be that the timezone configuration is plane-specific. Therefore, the SDR and Admin plane should have the same 'clock' configuration.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is a good practice to set "clock summer-time" so clocks automatically change forward and backward with local time, within the Global and Admin plane.
bestPracticeTitle: Clock summer-time recurring is not enabled

OSPFv3 has an interface assigned and present ,but has no IPv6 address assigned.

bestPracticeCaveat: If the SNMP server is implemented redundantly with sufficient high-availability using 1 IP address, the exception can be ignored.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure more then one snmp-server host:
snmp-server host address [traps] [version {1 | 2c | 3 {auth | noauth | priv}}] community-string [udp-port port] [notification-type]
bestPracticeDescription: Sending SNMP traps to an external trap receiver is critical for effectively managing a network. In order to provide redundancy in NMS systems, traps should be sent to at least two different IPv4 hosts. To enable the sending of RFC 1157 Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications, use the 'snmp-server traps snmp' command in global configuration mode. Defaults: This command is disabled by default. No notifications are sent. The default UDP port is 161. When the command is entered without keywords, the default is to send all trap types to the host. If no version keyword is entered, the default is version 1. If version 3 is specified, but the security level is not specified, the default security level is noauth. SNMP notifications can be sent as traps. Traps are unreliable because the receiver does not send acknowledgments when it receives traps. The sender cannot determine if the traps were received. Traps are discarded as soon as they are sen and are sent only once. When the 'snmp-server host' command is not entered, no notifications are sent. To configure the device to send SNMP notifications, configure at least one 'snmp-server host' command. When the command is entered without keywords, all trap types are enabled for the host. To enable multiple hosts, issue a separate 'snmp-server host' command for each host. You can specify multiple notification types in the command for each host. When multiple snmp-server host commands are given for the same host and kind of notification (trap), each succeeding command overwrites the previous command. Only the last 'snmp-server host' command is in effect. For example, if an 'snmp-server host' command with the traps keyword is entered for a host and then another 'snmp-server host' command with the traps keyword is entered for the same host, the second command replaces the first. The 'snmp-server host' command is used with the 'snmp-server engineid' command. Use the 'snmp-server traps' command to specify which SNMP notifications are sent globally. For a host to receive most notifications, at least one 'snmp-server traps' command and the 'snmp-server host' command for that host must be enabled. However, some notification types cannot be controlled with this command. For example, some notification types are always enabled. Other notification types are enabled by a different command. The availability of a notification-type depends on the device type and Cisco software features supported on the device. To display which notification types are available on the system, use the question mark (?) online help function at the end of the snmp-server host command. The 'no snmp-server host' command used with 'no keywords' disables traps.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure a second SNMP trap receiver for redundancy.
bestPracticeTitle: No redundant SNMP trap receiver

bestPracticeCaveat:
If HSRP is configured on the interface and using the "standby redirects" command, then ICMP redirects may be allowed or required. As a norm, ICMP redirects should be disabled.

ICMP redirects can also be filtered with an access-list. For example:
access-list 101 deny icmp any any redirect
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Disable 'ip redirects' by configuring the following under the interface:

Switch(config)#interface Ethernet1/3
Switch(config-if)#no ip redirects
bestPracticeDescription: A Cisco router by default will send Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) redirect messages if the Cisco IOS software is forced to resend a packet through the same interface on which it was received. There may be legitimate reasons to perform this action, such as HSRP, however; ICMP redirects are used in various attacks to attempt to modify network routing or launch a denial-of-service. As described in RFC 792, in a properly used situation, the redirect message advises a host to send its traffic for a particular network directly to another gateway, as this is a shorter path to the destination. This behaviour allows the sender to bypass the device and forward future packets directly to the destination (or to a router closer to the destination). A malicious user can exploit the ability of the device to send ICMP redirects by continually sending packets to the device, which forces the device to respond with ICMP redirect messages, and results in an adverse impact on the CPU and performance of the device. In order to prevent the device from sending ICMP redirects, disable the ICMP redirects using `no ip redirects? interface configuration command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to disable ICMP redirects on each IP interface and enable only if specifically required. Loopback and mgmt0 interfaces are skipped for this check since redirects do not apply there.
bestPracticeTitle: ICMP redirects not disabled on an Interface

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no snmp-server host
bestPracticeDescription: Sending SNMP traps to an external trap receiver is critical for effectively managing a network. However, too many SNMP trap destinations can put extra load on the device and use up bandwidth unnecessarily. There should not be a need to have more than four SNMP trap receivers on any given device.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Do not configure more than four SNMP trap receivers.  Unneeded receivers should be removed from the
configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: Too many SNMP trap receivers

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:

snmp-server ifmib ifalias long
bestPracticeDescription: Use the 'snmp-server ifmib ifalias long' command to enable the IF-MIB object ifAlias to accept an interface alias name greater than 64 bytes in length. The default length for the alias name is 64 bytes. Defaults: Global interface persistence is disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable the ifAlias IF-MIB object to accept an interface alias name that exceeds the 64 byte default.

By default, this feature allows a router to bring down a peer when the number of received prefixes from that peer exceeds the configured Maximum-Prefix limit. It is recommended to configure a maximum-prefix accepted threshold for external BGP peers.

Under the cable, bundle and WAN interfaces, the 'hold-queue in' should be set to '1024'

The hold-queue is used for queuing packets on a particular interface. Cisco recommends to set 1024 for the 'in' queue on the bundle, WAN and cable interfaces. Failure to comply with the rule can lead to excessive packet drops or congestion.

The hold-queue is used for queuing packets on a particular interface. Cisco recommends to set 1024 for the 'in' queue on the bundle, WAN and cable interfaces. Failure to comply with the rule can lead to excessive packet drops or congestion.
bestPracticeTitle: Hold Queue size incorrect

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: ntp source Loopback0
bestPracticeDescription: This command sets the default IP source address for the NTP packets. Hard coding the NTP source address will make it easier to use Access-Control Lists (ACLs) to manage NTP security.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the NTP source address to the loopback interface,if applicable.

bestPracticeCaveat: Specific interfaces may be individually configured with BPDU Guard although we would recommend using the global default command 'spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default' to enable BPDU Guard on all portfast interfaces.  If you have deployed a per-port implementation of the feature and the rule is still generating an exception check for a complete deployment of the per-port solution. All that is needed to generate an exception is a single access switchport with portfast configured without the associated bpduguard in place.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for BPDU Guard(Global configuration)

spanning-tree portfast
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default

Configure as below for BPDU Guard(Interface level)

interface fastethernet1/1
spanning-tree portfast 
spanning-tree bpduguard enable
bestPracticeDescription: Spanning tree protocol(STP) STP is a network protocol designed to prevent layer 2 loops. It is standardized as 802.D protocol. STP blocks some ports on switches with redundant links to prevent broadcast storms and ensure loop-free topology. With STP in place, you can have redundant links between switches in order to provide redundancy. PortFast Bridge Protocol Data unit(BPDU)guard The BPDU guard feature can be globally enabled on the switch or can be enabled per port, but the feature operates with some differences.Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU) guard is one of the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) enhancements that Cisco created. This feature enhances switch network reliability, manageability and security.At the global level, you enable BPDU guard on Port Fast-enabled ports by using the spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default global configuration command.Spanning tree shuts down ports that are in a Port Fast-operational state if any BPDU is received on them. In a valid configuration, Port Fast-enabled ports do not receive BPDUs. Receiving a BPDU on a Port Fast-enabled port means an invalid configuration, such as the connection of an unauthorized device, and the BPDU guard feature puts the port in the error-disabled state. When this happens, the switch shuts down the entire port on which the violation occurred.To prevent the port from shutting down, you can use the errdisable detect cause bpduguard shutdown vlan global configuration command to shut down just the offending VLAN on the port where the violation occurred.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Globally enable BPDU Guard on switches with portfast enabled ports.PortFast BPDU guard prevents loops by moving a non trunking port into an err-disable state when a BPDU is received on that port.
bestPracticeTitle: BPDU Guard Not Enabled on switches

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: snmp-server traps pim interface-state-change
bestPracticeDescription: Enable Protocol Independent Multicast (PIM) interface status SNMP trap notification. The 'snmp-server traps pim interface-state-change' command to send notifications when a PIM interface changes status from up to down. When the status is up, the notification signifies the restoration of a PIM interface. When the status is down, the notification signifies the loss of a PIM interface. PIM notifications are defined in the CISCO-PIM-MIB.my and PIM-MIB.my files.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable Protocol Independent Multicast (PIM) interface status notification.
bestPracticeTitle: PIM interface-state-change SNMP Traps Not Enabled
