ntp
update-calendar
bestPracticeDescription: Some platforms have a battery-powered hardware clock, referred to in the command-line interface (CLI) as the "calendar," in addition to the software based system clock. The hardware clock runs continuously, even if the router is powered off or rebooted. If the software clock is synchronized to an outside time source via NTP, it is a good practice to periodically update the hardware clock with the time learned from NTP. Otherwise, the hardware clock will tend to gradually lose or gain time (drift), and the software clock and hardware clock may become out of synchronization with each other. The ntp update-calendar command will enable the hardware clock to be periodically updated with the time specified by the NTP source. The hardware clock will be updated only if NTP has synchronized to an authoritative time server. The hardware clocks (system calendars) is used by IOS XR for IOS XR software installation and used for timestamping rollback features.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable ntp update-calendar, where supported.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP Update Calendar Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
snmp-server location
bestPracticeDescription: The SNMP system location is the SNMP MIB variable "sysLocation". This can be queried remotely to determine the location of the device.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Configure the SNMP system location to be the actual location for the device or a reference string to
information in a location database.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP location not defined

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
snmp-server traps bgp
bestPracticeDescription: Enable Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) trap notifications, use the 'snmp-server traps' [notification type] command in global configuration mode. The notification type can be one or more of the following keywords: - bgp Enables BGP4-MIB and CISCO-BGP4-MIB traps. Note: The 'snmp-server traps' command is used in combination with with the 'snmp-server host' command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) trap notifications for BGP.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If Wake-on-LAN (WOL) is being used in the network, the "ip directed-broadcast" must be enabled on the end-user LAN interface. In these cases, the "directed-broadcast" command should be used in conjunction with an extended ACL that permits only the UDP port number (typically the discard port) that is being used by WOL. Although this still leaves you exposed to attacks based on directed broadcast, the risk is much lower due to the extended ACL.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Below configuration for "no ip directed-broadcast" on all interfaces .

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip address 172.29.214.242 255.255.255.252
 no  ip directed-broadcast
bestPracticeDescription: Router interfaces that allow directed broadcasts can be used as amplifiers in smurf Denial of Service attacks. In IOS releases prior to 12.1, the default behavior was to permit directed broadcasts. -An IP directed broadcast is an IP packet whose destination address is a valid broadcast address for some IP subnet but which originates from a node that is not itself part of that destination subnet. -If directed broadcast is enabled for an interface, incoming IP packets whose addresses identify them as directed broadcasts intended for the subnet to which that interface is attached are broadcast on that subnet.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommended to disable  "no ip directed-broadcast"  on all interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: IP directed broadcast enabled on an interface (where default is enabled)

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface 
description
bestPracticeDescription:
Cisco IOS XR provides a means to attach text descriptors to individual
interfaces. These descriptors are helpful in troubleshooting and can be
used to identify a service carrier, circuit numbers, or other information
used by network operations personnel for problem diagnosis.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Meaningful interface descriptions should be configured on all interfaces.
In the description, include a simple descriptive string describing the
interface or subnet and include the subnet address and mask.
For WAN, include Telco service numbers, neighbor devices, etc.
bestPracticeTitle: No interface description

bestPracticeCaveat: MPP has no SYSLOG capability to report IPv6 host that are denied by MPP.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the peer address (IPv4 address and/or IPv6 address) in which management traffic is allowed, as in the following configuration example:

!
control-plane
 management-plane
  inband
   interface all
    allow telnet peer 
     address ipv4 10.1.0.0/16
     address ipv6 2001:6969::/64
!
bestPracticeDescription: The Management Plane Protection (MPP) feature in Cisco IOS XR software provides the capability to restrict the interfaces on which network management packets are allowed to enter a device. The MPP feature allows a network operator to designate one or more router interfaces as management interfaces. Device management traffic may enter a device only through these management interfaces. After MPP is enabled, no interfaces except designated management interfaces accept network management traffic destined to the device. Restricting management packets to designated interfaces provides greater control over management of a device, providing more security for that device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to remove for all Network Management Applications (HTTP, SNMP, SSH, TFTP, and Telnet) the ACL and translate these ACLs to MPP.
bestPracticeTitle: No MPP peer address enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: snmp-server traps pim neighbor-change
bestPracticeDescription: The command "snmp-server traps pim neighbor-change" enables notification for PIM protocol neighbor changes. This notification results from the following conditions: 1 When a router's PIM interface is disabled or enabled (using the 'ip pim' command in interface configuration mode). 2 When a router's PIM neighbor adjacency expires or is established (defined in RFC 2934).
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Enable on a global configuration mode "snmp-server traps pim neighbor-change" benefits include:
* Allows users to identify changes in the multicast topology of their network by detecting changes in the RP mapping.
* Provides traps to monitor the PIM protocol on PIM-enabled interfaces.
* Helps users identify routing issues when multicast neighbor adjacencies expire or are established on a multicast interface.
* Enables users to monitor RP configuration errors (for example, errors due to flapping in dynamic RP allocation protocols like Auto-RP).
bestPracticeTitle: PIM neighbor-change SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
snmp-server traps entity
bestPracticeDescription: Enable Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) trap notifications, Type of notification (trap) to enable key-word: entity Controls Entity MIB modification notifications. This notification type is defined in the ENTITY-MIB (enterprise 1.3.6.1.2.1.47.2) as: (1) entConfigChange. Defaults: SNMP notifications are disabled by default.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) trap notifications for entity.
bestPracticeTitle: Entity SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Under 'multicast-routing' configure 'nsf'.
bestPracticeDescription: To turn on nonstop forwarding (NSF) capability for the multicast routing system, use the nsf command in multicast routing configuration mode.This command does not enable or disable the multicast routing system, but just the NSF capability for all the relevant components. When the no form of this command is used, the NSF configuration is returned to its default disabled state. Enable multicast NSF when you require enhanced availability of multicast forwarding. When enabled, failures of the control-plane multicast routing components Multicast Routing Information Base (MRIB) or Protocol Independent Multicast (PIM) will not cause multicast forwarding to stop. When these components fail or communication with the control plane is otherwise disrupted, existing Multicast Forwarding Information Base (MFIB) entries continue to forward packets until either the control plane recovers or the MFIB NSF timeout expires. Enable multicast NSF when you upgrade control-plane Cisco IOS XR software packages so that the live upgrade process does not interrupt forwarding. When the MFIB partner processes enter NSF mode, forwarding on stale MFIB entries continues as the control-plane components attempt to recover gracefully. Successful NSF recovery is signaled to the Multicast Forwarding Engine (MFWD) partner processes by MRIB. MRIB remains in NSF mode until Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP) has recovered state from the network and host stack and until PIM has recovered state from the network and IGMP. When both PIM and IGMP have recovered and fully updated the MRIB, MRIB signals the MFIBs that NSF is ending, and begins updating the stale MFIB entries. When all updates have been sent, the MFWD partner processes delete all remaining stale (nonupdated) MFIB entries and return to normal operation, ending the NSF mode. MFIB NSF timeout prior to the signal from MRIB may cause NSF to end, and thus forwarding to stop. When forwarding is in NSF mode, multicast flows may continue longer than necessary when network conditions change due to multicast routing protocols, unicast routing protocol reachability information, or local sender and receiver changes. The MFWD partner processes halt forwarding on stale MFIB entries when the potential for a multicast loop is detected by receipt of incoming data on a forwarding interface for the matching MFIB entry. Note: For NSF to operate successfully in your multicast network, you must also enable NSF for the unicast protocols (such as Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System [IS-IS], Open Shortest Path First [OSPF] and Border Gateway Protocol [BGP]) that PIM relies on for Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) information. See the appropriate configuration modules to learn how to configure NSF for unicast protocols. Default: nsf is disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Non Stop Forwarding within high availability networks is recommended to be implemented. Multicast-routing NSF is local significant on the network element and advise is to turn on multicast nsf.
bestPracticeTitle: multicast-routing nsf is disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
enable subslot: no hw-module shutdown location $$node-id 
remove subslot: obsolete configuration: no hw-module shutdown location $$node-id.
bestPracticeDescription: To administratively shut down a specific node, use the 'hw-module shutdown' command in administration configuration mode. Nodes that are shut down still have power but cannot load or operate Cisco IOS XR software. The shutdown should be a temporary configuration change, which is expected to be within the configuration only a limited amount of time. This rule checks the presence of nodes in shutdown.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Review the hw-module shutdowns within the configuration and determine if these CLI commands may have 
become obsolete.
bestPracticeTitle: hw-module shutdown configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: queue-limit $$value ms queue-limit $$value us
bestPracticeDescription: Queue-limit units are not in msec or usec. Queuing on networks is translated into delay/jitter of user-traffic. Building blocks can then be reused independent of the type of interface, speeds, or bandwidth allocation; the system will translate the time into bytes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Implement queue-limit units based upon milliseconds or microseconds.
bestPracticeTitle: queue-limit units not in ms or us

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: global configure: tcp path-mtu-discovery
bestPracticeDescription: To allow TCP to automatically detect the highest common maximum transfer unit (MTU) for a connection, use the TCP path-mtu-discovery in global configuration mode. Use the TCP path-mtu-discovery command to allow TCP to automatically detect the highest common MTU for a connection, so that when a packet traverses between the originating host and the destination host the packet is not fragmented and then reassembled. Using the TCP path-mtu-discovery (especially for BGP sessions), makes the data transfer rate greater than leaving them on the default path-mtu. The TCP path-mtu-discovery enablement is advised for fast convergence applications for BGP.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable TCP path-mtu-discovery for faster TCP/IP application convergence.
bestPracticeTitle: tcp path-mtu-discovery not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router bgp  
 neighbor  remote-as 
   description
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco IOS XR provides a way to include a unique description associated with each BGP neighbor.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Meaningful descriptions should be configured for all neighbors, neighbor groups, VRF neighbors, etc.  BGP neighbor descriptions help ease management and troubleshooting.  In the description, include a simple text string to uniquely identify the BGP neighbor.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Neighbor Description Missing

bestPracticeCaveat: This check should be done also against LDP Session Protection if configured and there is no alternate route to the neighbour LDP (unique MPLS interface). If Session Protection is enalbled and the topology is the one described above than this kind of warning isn't applicable since in this case the LDP session protection mechanism id more effective.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-

!
router isis 1
 address-family ipv4 unicast
 !
 interface Bundle-Ether100.1500
  address-family ipv4 unicast
   mpls ldp sync
 !
 interface Bundle-Ether100.1501
  address-family ipv4 unicast
   mpls ldp sync
!
mpls ldp
 interface Bundle-Ether100.1500
 interface Bundle-Ether100.1501
!
bestPracticeDescription: To configure Label Distribution Protocol (LDP) IS-IS synchronization, use the mpls ldp sync command in interface address family configuration mode. MPLS VPN traffic forwarded using LDP labels may be dropped in the following instances: - A new link is introduced in the network and IS-IS has converged before LDP establishes labels. - An existing LDP session goes down while IS-IS adjacency is intact over the link. In both instances, outbound LDP labels are not available for forwarding MPLS traffic. LDP IS-IS synchronization addresses the traffic drop. When the mpls ldp sync command is configured, IS-IS advertises the maximum possible link metric until LDP has converged over the link. The link is less preferred and least used in forwarding MPLS traffic. When LDP establishes the session and exchanges labels, IS-IS advertises the regular metric over the link. Note: IS-IS advertises the maximum metric -1 (16777214) if wide metrics are configured since the maximum wide metric is specifically used for link exclusion from the shortest path first algorithm (SPF) (RFC 3784). However, the maximum narrow metric is unaffected by this definition.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to implement for each mpls ldp interface, which is also configured under 'router is-is', 'interface any R/S/I/P', 'address-family ipv4 unic-cast': 'mpls ldp sync', in order to increase overall network services availability for MPLS LSP switched traffic.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS MPLS LDP Synchronization is not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: The network administrator may want to permit ICMP unreachable messages in certain environments. For example, ICMP type 3, code 1; code 3; and code 4 are required for the SMTP e-mail gateway.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface interface_name#
snip
ipv6 unreachables disable
bestPracticeDescription: If the Cisco IOS XR software receives a nonbroadcast packet destined for itself that uses a protocol it does not recognize, it sends an ICMP unreachable message to the source. Also, if the software receives a datagram that it cannot deliver to its ultimate destination because it knows of no route to the destination address, it replies to the originator of that datagram with an ICMP host unreachable message. The ipv6 unreachables command affects all types of ICMP unreachable messages and is enabled by default in support of RFC 792. However, ICMP unreachables can potentially be used to map a network by correlating which addresses generate ICMP unreachables and which do not, for networks, hosts, protocols, and ports.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Control ICMP unreachable responses in certain environments, especially along untrusted edges between administrative domains with the interface command ipv6 unreachables disable on affected interfaces. ICMP unreachables covers network, host, protocol, and port unreachable messages.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv6 ICMP Unreachables Should Be Disabled On At Least Some Interfaces

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure authentication on each HSRP groups in the interface configuration mode.
bestPracticeDescription: HSRP can be configured to authenticate the HSRP protocol messages by using clear text or MD5 Authentication. As a best practice recommendation, HSRP authentication can be configured between HSRP peers. HSRP Authentication can be configured, either by clear text authentication or MD5 Authentication.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco Advance Services recommends configuring HSRP authentication to enforce better security on the LAN network in terms of first hop device selected by the hosts and Servers on LAN network.
bestPracticeTitle: HSRP Authentication not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers desire the behavior of receiving messages via both mechanisms, and their network management systems are designed to handle this information through syslog parsing and SNMP trap filtering preferences. In those cases, it is okay to leave this trap enabled.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no snmp-server traps syslog
bestPracticeDescription: The device has been configured to send system log messages to a syslog server and SNMP trap receiver.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The syslog trap sends all syslog messages generated by the device as a SNMP trap.  If syslog analysis is already being performed by an analyzer, such as CiscoWorks Resource Manager Essentials, then it may not be useful to receive the same message as a trap as well.  The syslog trap should be turned off, as it is duplicate data.
bestPracticeTitle: Unnecessary Syslog SNMP trap configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: service timestamps log datetime localtime msec.
bestPracticeDescription: Logging messages do not contain timestamp information by default. Being able to see the time a log message happened and at what time events happened relative to the router and to each other assists problem resolution.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure timestamps for logging messages.
bestPracticeTitle: Timestamping for logging not set for datetime

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
1. Installing physical interfaces or implementing the sub-interface.
2. Removing the (sub-)interface under 'ntp'.
3. Assigning the (sub-)interface an IPv4 or IPv6 address.
bestPracticeDescription: IOS XR is configured to be feature-centric; therefore, the IPv4 or IPv6 interface addressing is 'disentangled' from the NTP. The NTP and IPv4/IPv6 interface addressing configuration is checked: 1. NTP has an interface assigned, but the interface is not present in the configuration or physical. 2. NTP has an interface assigned and present, but has no IPv4 or IPv6 address assigned.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Review the network design and apply the correct NTP interface and/or IPv4/IPv6 addressing.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP Interfaces Not Present OR No IPv4 or IPv6 Address Assigned

bestPracticeCaveat: When using Path MTU Discovery, please make sure the IOS software has a fix for this security vulnerability: CSCef60659.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure PMTU Discovery on all MP-BGP speakers.  
The BGP TCP session needs to be cleared in order for the change to take effect:

PE1(config)#ip tcp path-mtu-discovery

rr1(config)#ip tcp path-mtu-discovery 
					
rr1(config)# rr1(config)#^Z 
					
rr1#clear ip bgp 172.17.17.1 
					
rr1# *Mar 31 13:15:59.807: %BGP-5-ADJCHANGE: neighbor 172.17.17.1 Down

rr1#show ip bgp vpnv4 * neighbors 172.17.17.1
- output deleted for brevity -
SRTT: 659 ms, RTTO: 4406 ms, RTV: 1544 ms, KRTT: 0 ms 
minRTT: 28 ms, maxRTT: 300 ms, ACK hold: 200 ms 
Flags: higher precedence, nagle, path mtu capable

Datagrams (max data segment is 1436 bytes): 
Rcvd: 7 (out of order: 0), with data: 4, total data bytes: 382 
Sent: 10 (retransmit: 0, fastretransmit: 0), with data: 6, total data bytes: 996
bestPracticeDescription: Path MTU Discovery is a method for maximizing the use of available bandwidth in the network between the endpoints of a TCP connection. It is described in RFC 1191. Existing connections are not affected when this feature is turned on or off. Customers using TCP connections to move bulk data between systems on distinct subnets would benefit most by enabling this feature. The age timer is a time interval for how often TCP reestimates the path MTU with a larger MSS. When the age timer is used, TCP path MTU becomes a dynamic process. If the MSS used for the connection is smaller than what the peer connection can handle, a larger MSS is tried every time the age timer expires. The discovery process is stopped when either the send MSS is as large as the peer negotiated, or the user has disabled the timer on the router. You can turn off the age timer by setting it to infinite.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to speed up MP-BGP convergence, the TCP MSS should be increased by turning on PMTU on all MP-BGP speakers.
bestPracticeTitle: Path MTU Discovery not enabled on MP-BGP speaker

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
logging events link-status
interface $$type $$instance
No logging events link-status
bestPracticeDescription:
In IOS XR release 3.3.0 and later, the logging events link-status (interface) has been removed.
The workaround is to configure logging events link-status (Global).
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure logging events link-status in global configuration mode,to prepare for release 3.4.0 or later.
bestPracticeTitle: (interface) logging events link-status is enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
snmp-server contact Dial System Operator at beeper # 27345    
!
bestPracticeDescription: The SNMP contact is the SNMP MIB Variable "sysContact". This can be queried remotely to determine the contact for this device.snmp-server contact command to set the system contact string.This command creates an SNMP context. By default, all the SNMP MIB instances are in a default context. Create an SNMP context and map it to a particular feature to enable similar instances of the same object to co-exist in different SNMP context.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to Configure the SNMP contact to be the person or group responsible for this device.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP contact not defined

bestPracticeCaveat: If the entire OSPF domain is a completely trusted network, authentication may not be absolutely necessary. However, it is still recommended.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure MD5 authentication for all interfaces participating in OSPF using the 'authentication message-digest' OSPF configuration.
bestPracticeDescription: MD5 authentication for OSPF is recommended as a best practice for various security reasons. Other authentication methods (including not using authentication) expose an OSPF domain to compromise, DoS, spoofing, traffic hijacking, and other vulnerabilities. This rule checks for any interfaces participating in OSPF that are not configured for MD5 authentication.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure MD5 authentication for all interfaces participating in OSPF to avoid security vulnerabilities.
bestPracticeTitle: MD5 Authentication Missing from One or More OSPF Interfaces

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: 'no ip source-route'
bestPracticeDescription: Source routing is a feature of IP whereby individual packets can specify the routes they take. This feature is used in several kinds of attacks. Cisco routers normally accept and process source routes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Unless a network depends on source routing,it should be disabled.
bestPracticeTitle: IP Source Routing enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for AAA Accounting EXEC

aaa new-model

aaa accounting exec default start-stop group radius
aaa accounting exec default start-stop group tacacs+
bestPracticeDescription: Authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA, pronounced ?triple A?). AAA is a a set of services for controlling access to computer resources, enforcing policies, assessing usage, and providing the information necessary to bill for services. These processes are considered important for effective network management and security. Authentication provides a way to identify a user, typically by having the user enter a valid username and valid password before access is granted. The AAA server compares a user's authentication credentials with other user credentials stored in a database. If the credentials match, the user is permitted access to the network. If the credentials do not match, authentication fails and network access is denied. Authorization is the process of enforcing policies: determining what types of activities, resources, or services a user is permitted to access. After a user is authenticated, that user may be authorized for different types of access or activity. Accounting measures the resources a user consumes during access, which may include the amount of system time or the amount of data that a user has sent or received during a session. Accounting is carried out through the logging of session statistics and usage information, which is used for authorization control, billing, trend analysis, resource utilization, and capacity planning activities. EXEC runs accounting for the EXEC shell session. This keyword might return user profile information such as what is generated by the auto command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The customer should enable not only authentication and authorization, but also accounting for EXEC mode. The minimum method is stop-only, but the preferred option is start-stop.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA Accounting EXEC not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Some devices may not fully support SSH for a variety of reasons; however, the customer should work with their Cisco support team to resolve those exceptions where possible. This may involved upgrades to hardware or upgrades to more advanced feature sets.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the DNS domain:
Router(config)#ip domain-name example.com

Generate the SSH key to be used:
Router(config)#crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048

Enable SSH transport support for the virtual type terminal (vtys):
Router(config)#line vty 0 4
Router(config-line)#transport input ssh

Router(config)#line vty 5 15
Router(config-line)#transport input ssh
bestPracticeDescription: The traffic through an interactive management session must be encrypted so that a malicious user cannot gain access to the data that is transmitted. Traffic encryption allows a secure remote access connection to the device. If the traffic for a management session is sent over the network in cleartext, an attacker can obtain sensitive information about the device and the network. It is possible to establish an encrypted and secure remote access management connection to a device with the SSH or HTTPS (Secure Hypertext Transfer Protocol) features. SSH runs on top of a reliable transport layer and provides strong authentication and encryption capabilities. SSH provides a means to securely access and securely execute commands on another computer or device over a network. It is highly recommended to enable SSH v2 by using the command `ip ssh version 2?. The following steps can be followed for enabling SSH v2 configuration on a Cisco IOS device. Step 1: Configure the hostname if you have not previously done so. Router(config)#hostname Step 2: Configure the DNS domain of the router. Router(config)#ip domain-name Step 3: Generate an SSH key to be used with SSH. Router(config)#crypto key generate rsa Router(config)#ip ssh time-out 60 Router(config)#ip ssh authentication-retries 2 Step 4: By default the vtys' transport is Telnet. Change it to SSH. (In the following case, telnet will be disabled and only SSH will be supported.) Router(config)#line vty 0 4 Router(config-line)#transport input ssh
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco highly recommends using SSHv2 exclusively for secure remote access.
bestPracticeTitle: SSH Not Used Exclusively for Remote Access

bestPracticeCaveat: This command need not be applied if there is no need for a specific device to become VPC primary. Then it can be left on the devices to elect the primary vpc peer on their own.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Issue the following command in the vpc domain configuration mode, as described in the following example:- N5k-1(config-vpc-domain)# role priority 1000
bestPracticeDescription: Role priority determines the role of the peer as primary/secondary. It is recommended to configure this value manually to control the role election. It will optimize the topology. For example, STP root should become the VPC primary so that traffic may not take any sub optimal paths. Also, HSRP active should become the VPC primary to avoid any sub optimal routing. The configuration is done by issuing the following command:- N5k-1(config-vpc-domain)# role priority 1000 For configuration, enter the role priority that you want for the vPC switch. The range of values is 1 to 65636, and the default value is 32667.The switch with lower priority will be elected as the vPC primary switch. If the peer link fails, vPC peer will detect whether the peer switch is alive through the vPC peer keep alive link. If the vPC primary switch is alive, the vPC secondary switch will suspend its vPC member ports to prevent potential looping while the vPC primary switch keeps all its vPC member ports active. If this configuration is not done, then traffic may take the sub optimal paths.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Role priority determines the role of the peer as primary/secondary.It is recommended to configure this value manually to control the role election in order to optimize the topology. The configuration is done by issuing the following command:- N5k-1(config-vpc-domain)# role priority 1000 The role priority may be any value between 1 to 65536. By default, it is 32667. The switch with lower priority value becomes the primary.
bestPracticeTitle: vPC role priority not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: If the goal is to build a static Multicast tree (usually when testing or troubleshooting), the command igmp static-group should be used. The router does not become a part of the group, but instead acts as if a receiver exists for that group on the specified interface. The join-group command is also often used in lab enviroments, for testing a multicast tree, as it will enable the router to respond to pings for the specified Multicast group.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router igmp
 interface
  no join-group
bestPracticeDescription: The join-group under the router igmp interface command causes the router to join the specified multicast group, and all packets destined for that group will be sent to the CPU for processing. This command is useful in labs where you want the router to fully emulate an end-host(receiver that responds to IGMP queries), or need the router to respond to pings for that multicast group. The risk is that any data streams destined for that group would strain the CPU on the router.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Unconfigure join-groups on the router. Joining a multicast group results in a performance impact because all subscribed multicast packets are punted to the route processor.
bestPracticeTitle: IP IGMP Join-Group Applied To Interface

bestPracticeCaveat: TCP keepalives are not necessary if SSH is used as the exclusive remote connection protocol for interactive VTY access because SSH clients can be configured to use their own keepalives.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: service tcp-keepalives-in service tcp-keepalives-out
bestPracticeDescription: TCP keepalives enable the system to identify and terminate a "dead" TCP connection, where the other end no longer responds. Without keepalives, a system won't respond as quickly to orphaned connections due to a remote system crash. This should be used in conjunction with "exec-timeout" on the interactive VTY lines to enhance the availability of those lines. By default, "service tcp-keepalives-out" and "service tcp-keepalives-in" are not enabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure both "service tcp-keepalives-in" and "service tcp-keepalives-out" in global configuration mode.
bestPracticeTitle: TCP keepalives not enabled in both directions

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
To encrypt all passwords in configuration file, configure the following:

Router(config)# service password-encryption
bestPracticeDescription: The `service password-encryption? global configuration command directs the Cisco IOS software to encrypt the passwords, Challenge Handshake Authentication Protocol (CHAP) secrets, and similar data that are saved in its configuration file. Such encryption is useful in order to prevent casual observers from reading passwords, such as when they look at the screen over the muster of an administrator. To automatically convert unencrypted passwords to encrypted passwords, it is recommended to use the service password-encryption command in global configuration mode.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Service password encryption should be used to stop casual access to what are otherwise clear text passwords.
bestPracticeTitle: Password Encryption Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If an IOS switch has all critical interfaces on it, then you would not want to disable any interface traps.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface 
 no snmp trap link-status
bestPracticeDescription: Enable or disable SNMP trap notifications on a per-interface basis.
bestPracticeRecommendation: IOS switches should not be sending interface traps for end-user ports.
bestPracticeTitle: Interface traps not disabled on at least one interface

bestPracticeCaveat: HTTP redirection is needed when the environment  uses Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE) and the NAC Appliance (and potentially other agents as well), or when ISE Central Web Authentication is desired on wired ports.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no ip http server
bestPracticeDescription: Any network device that is running an HTTP server should be protected by a firewall, an access list, strong authentication, or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized usage. This feature is disabled by default on all Cisco devices except for low-end routers (e.g., 1003, 1004, etc).
bestPracticeRecommendation: All unnecessary services should be disabled on a device if you do not have a need for them in your network.  If web management is required, it should be done with HTTPS along and protected by an access list.
bestPracticeTitle: HTTP server enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: CDP has legitimate use for troubleshooting network issues; however, on directly connected interfaces to network edges or devices not in the same administrative domain, CDP should be disabled to prevent data mining.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-

no cdp run (global) or no cdp enable (interface)
bestPracticeDescription: CDP is media- and protocol-independent and runs on all Cisco-manufactured equipment including routers, bridges, access servers, and switches. CDP is useful for troubleshooting as well as building topology information by NMS platforms. Due to the information obtainable through CDP, care should be exercised in deploying this protocol, especially at the network edge. In earlier versions of IOS, there was a vulnerability with CDP that could lead to a Denial of Service attack. CDP is enabled globally by default and doesn't appear in a "show run".
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to disable CDP on a per-interface (no cdp enable) If CDP is required globally on a device .CDP should be disabled on all interfaces facing a different administrative domain. Your configuration can also carry an explicit "no cdp run" if CDP is not required or permitted on the device globally.
bestPracticeTitle: CDP is enabled globally and active on all interfaces.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: logging source-interface Loopback0
bestPracticeDescription: This command sets the default IP source address for the syslog messages.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the logging source address to the a specific interface,preferably a loopback interface.  Hard coding the logging source address will make it easier to correlate all syslog messages from the same device and simplifies ACLs and Firewall rules,where applicable.
bestPracticeTitle: Syslog source interface not defined

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
aaa new-model
aaa authentication login console local
aaa authentication login console group tacacs+ line
aaa new-model aaa authentication login default group radius line
bestPracticeDescription: Access to the console should be protected with strong authentication. This requires configuration of the console to use AAA. Information About AAA Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA, pronounced ?triple A?).AAA is a a set of services for controlling access to computer resources, enforcing policies, assessing usage, and providing the information necessary to bill for services.These processes are considered important for effective network management and security. Authentication: Authentication provides a way to identify a user, typically by having the user enter a valid username and valid password before access is granted. Authorization: Authorization is the process of enforcing policies: determining what types of activities, resources, or services a user is permitted to access. After a user is authenticated, that user may be authorized for different types of access or activity. Accounting: Accounting measures the resources a user consumes during access, which may include the amount of system time or the amount of data that a user has sent or received during a session. Accounting is carried out through the logging of session statistics and usage information, which is used for authorization control, billing, trend analysis, resource utilization, and capacity planning activities.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommended  to configure RADIUS or TACACS+ authentication for use by the console. You may also configure a password for the line, though this should be the fallback method.
bestPracticeTitle: Console not protected by AAA

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
no ip domain-lookup (prior to 12.2)
no ip domain lookup (12.2 and later)

or

line con 0 !or aux or vty lines
 transport preferred none
bestPracticeDescription:
IP domain lookup allows DNS-based host name-to-address translation, permitting the use of a hostname instead of an IP address for router commands like telnet, ping, and traceroute. Domain lookup is enabled by default.

A by-product of this command is, when an unrecognized command in user or enable mode is entered into a router, the router believes that this is the host name of a device that the user is attempting to reach. Therefore, the router tries to resolve the unrecognized command into an IP address by doing an IP domain lookup. If no specific domain server has been configured on the router, the router will issue a broadcast for the command to be translated into an IP address. It can take several seconds for the router prompt to return while the router waits for a response to its Domain Name System (DNS) broadcast.

The syntax of the command changed from ip domain-lookup to ip domain lookup in 12.2.

Another way to avoid this behaviour without disabling dns lookup is to configure "transport preferred none" under one of the lines (con, aux, or vty).
bestPracticeRecommendation: Disable DNS-based host name-to-address translation by entering the "no ip domain-lookup" or "no ip domain lookup" command in global configuration mode.  Alternatively,configure "transport preferred none" under at least one of the lines.
bestPracticeTitle: Incorrectly Entered Commands Generate DNS Lookup

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: logging
bestPracticeDescription: Logging syslog to an external syslog server is critical for effectively managing a network. To support redundancy, there should be at least two syslog servers in the network.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Set up a backup syslog server,preferably at a different location from the primary syslog server. Add this backup syslog server as a destination to the device.
bestPracticeTitle: No redundant syslog server

bestPracticeCaveat: This rule will give a false result on 12.2SRE and later SR code, since the default has changed to classless enabled but not displaying it in the configuration. Those devices can be ignored for this rule.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: ip classless
bestPracticeDescription: At times the router might receive packets destined for a subnet of a network that has no network default route. To have the Cisco IOS software forward such packets to the best supernet route possible, use the "ip classless" global configuration command. This is critical in networks relying on 0.0.0.0 routing and without full specific routes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable classless ip routing.
bestPracticeTitle: IP Classless disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface 
description
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco IOS provides a means to affix text descriptors to individual interfaces. These descriptors are helpful in troubleshooting and can be used to identify a service carrier, circuit numbers, or other information used by network operations personnel for problem diagnosis.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Meaningful interface descriptions should be configured on all interfaces. In the description, include a simple descriptive string describing the interface or subnet, and include the subnet address and mask. For WAN, include Telco service numbers, neighbor devices, etc.
bestPracticeTitle: No interface description

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below 

logging console informational
logging source-interface Loopback0
logging 172.29.220.31
logging host 192.168.202.169
logging trap 5
!
bestPracticeDescription: Logging system messages to a syslog server is an effective form of fault management. Reviewing the logs and checking for critical messages are important aspects for troubleshooting and incident investigation.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to set up a syslog server and configure devices to send messages to the syslog server.
bestPracticeTitle: Logging to a syslog server not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: There may be a need to use a router as a TFTP server.  It can be protected by an ACL and once not needed, it should be disabled. This rule will ignore routers configured for telephony-service and will ignore the TFTP server line added into the config by the SM-X-1T3/E3 module.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no tftp-server
bestPracticeDescription: To configure a router or a Flash memory device on the router as a TFTP server, use one of the following tftp-server global configuration commands. This command replaces the tftp-server system command. To remove a previously defined filename, use the no tftp-server command with the appropriate filename. By default, this command is disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: TFTP has no authentication mechanism beyond knowing the filename. It shouldn't be left running if not needed or in use.  Also, if you need to run a TFTP server on the router, consider protecting it with an access list.
bestPracticeTitle: Enabling TFTP server is not recommended

bestPracticeCaveat:
PAD may be used in some x25 networks.

Please Note below :
- 16.x Release - Service pad is enabled by default. If we configure, ?no service-pad? then it is available in the show run output
- 17.x Release - Service pad is disabled by default. If we configure, ?service-pad? then it is available in the show run output
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
To disable all packet assembler/disassembler (PAD) commands and connections between PAD devices and access servers, use the ?no service pad? command in global configuration mode

Router(config)# no service pad
bestPracticeDescription: Any network device that is running packet assembler/disassembler (PAD) services should be protected by a firewall or have the services disabled to protect against Denial of Service attacks or attempts at unauthorized usage. By default, all PAD commands and associated connections are enabled. (PAD services over XOT or CMNS are not enabled by default.) PADs are usually configured to enable X.25 connections between network devices.
bestPracticeRecommendation: All minor services should be disabled in the system if they are not needed in the network. In case of ?service PAD?, the configuration must carry an explicit "no service pad" unless expressly needed.
bestPracticeTitle: Disable PAD service

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: snmp-server location
bestPracticeDescription: The SNMP location is the SNMP MIB variable "sysLocation". This can be queried remotely to determine the location of this device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the SNMP location to be the actual location for the device or a reference string to information in a location database.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP location not defined

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below :

spanning-tree mode pvst
spanning-tree vlan 1
bestPracticeDescription: Spanning Tree Protocol for one or more VLANs has been disabled, opening up the risk of a layer-2 loops impacting all hosts on the VLAN.
bestPracticeRecommendation: All Catalyst switches have STP enabled by default; this is recommended even if a design is chosen that does not include active Layer 2 loops, ie. there are no STP blocked ports. It can protect against cabling errors and unintended bridged connections, eg. mis-configured wireless bridges, dual NIC hosts, or user installed switches. Spanning tree BPDU guard is also recommended to error disable edge ports (STP port fast enabled) that receive a BPDU, where none is expected.
bestPracticeTitle: Spanning Tree Disabled For Specific VLAN

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
ip route [10.10.10.10] [255.255.255.255] Null0
!
interface Null0
no ip unreachables
bestPracticeDescription: If there are static routes configured to point at dev null as the next hop, it is recommended to have a configuration that disables the generation of ICMP unreachable packets for the traffic hitting that static route.  The purpose of this is to decrease the impact on the CPU when using routes to dev null for silent dropping.  In most of the cases when sending traffic to dev null you also do not want the source of the dropped traffic to get a notification.
bestPracticeRecommendation: If there is a static route pointing toward dev null, configure the null interface and apply the command: "no ip unreachable".
bestPracticeTitle: Unreachables not disabled on Null0 interface

bestPracticeCaveat: If an IOS switch has all critical interfaces on it, then you would not want to disable any interface syslog.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below
!
logging source-interface Vlan15
logging 192.168.50.1
logging 192.168.50.2
!
interface FastEthernet0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 15
 no logging event link-status
 spanning-tree portfast
!
bestPracticeDescription: IOS Switches have syslog notifications enabled by default on all interfaces. But for many switches used to connect end-user ports, these notifications are unnecessarily "noisy" to Network Management Systems when users shut down their computers each day.If you enter both the logging event link-status default and the no logging event link-status boot commands, the interface state-change events are logged after all modules in the Cisco series router come online after system initialization. Syslog is a method to collect messages from devices to a server running a syslog daemon. A syslog service simply accepts messages, and stores them in files or prints them according to a simple configuration file. This form of logging is the best available for Cisco devices because it can provide protected long-term storage for logs. This is useful both in routine troubleshooting and in incident handling.
bestPracticeRecommendation: IOS switches should not be sending interface syslog for up/down events for end-user ports.  While the rule cannot tell which ports are end-user ports, it is recommended that at least one active (non-shutdown) interface on a switch should have the interface syslogs disabled.
bestPracticeTitle: Interface level syslog events not disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below:
!
 ip igmp snooping
!
bestPracticeDescription: IGMP snooping is disabled by default on the Catalyst switches running IOS (Catalyst 6500, 4000/4500, 3750, 3550, etc.) and it is recommended to use it, unless it is disabled by customer to workaround bug or as the workaround for specific host/application behavior. Disabling IGMP snooping will cause flooding of IP multicast on the L2 on all interfaces or on specific interface depending if IGMP snooping is disabled globally or on the specific interface.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Re-enable IGMP snooping by configuring "ip igmp snooping" globally (or on the specific interface where it was disabled). Find alternative workaround, if disabling IGMP snooping was used as the workaround.
bestPracticeTitle: IGMP Snooping disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: snmp-server trap-source
bestPracticeDescription: SNMP will choose an address from the interface IP address closest to the trap destination. Configuring the SNMP traps to come from a known and consistent address will assist with correlation and analysis when the events are received by the trap receiver.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the SNMP source address to the loopback interface.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP trap source not defined

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below 
!
ip receive access-list 110
!
bestPracticeDescription: The receive access-list feature is a very powerful feature, that can if not deployed correctly isolate the router completely. Please work together with your Cisco NCE before deploying the feature. When deployed, the receive acl feature enhances the protection from direct attacks onthe router significantly.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The receive acl feature enhances the protection from direct attacks on the router significantly. It is a recommended approach to start off with the following configuration: access-list 101 permit ip any any log ip receive access-list 101 This should be done to be able to analyze what traffic is  currently being sent to the router. Keep in mind that on the Cisco12000 platform, this rule will also filter traffic on the designated management port (Ethernet 0).
bestPracticeTitle: Receive acl not configured on 7500

bestPracticeCaveat: Redistribution route-maps may be used to set the metric also. This rule does not check for this case.If route-maps are correctly setting the metric for redistribution into OSPF, then this is not an issue.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Define the metric that should be used when redistributing into OSPF with the metric or default-metric keywords.

Below Configuration
 
!
router ospf 1 
 network 131.108.0.0 0.0.255.255 area 0 
 redistribute rip  metric 1 subnets 
 redistribute eigrp 1 metric 1 subnets
!
bestPracticeDescription: Redistribute one protocol into another, remember that the metrics of each protocol play an important role in redistribution. Each protocol uses different metrics.The OSPF metric is a cost value based on 108/ bandwidth of the link in bits/sec.If a metric is not specified, OSPF puts a default value of 20 when redistributing routes from all protocols except Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) routes, which get a metric of 1. The OSPF metric for a route is the sum of the interface costs for all outgoing interfaces in the route. By default, a router's OSPF interface cost is actually derived from the interface bandwidth. The faster the bandwidth, the lower the cost. Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) is a routing protocol for Internet Protocol (IP) networks. It uses a link state routing (LSR) algorithm and falls into the group of interior gateway protocols (IGPs), operating within a single autonomous system (AS). OSPF is a widely used IGP in large enterprise networks.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to define metric while redistributing protocols under OSPF.
bestPracticeTitle: No Redistribution Metrics Defined for OSPF

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: router-id A.B.C.D
bestPracticeDescription: Router-id config is prone to various config errors and adds instability when interface state/address is changed. Following the work done by other protocols (such as BGP, EIGRP, OSPF), router-id interface option be removed from LDP config.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Apply router-id in IPv4 format which is referencing a stable specified loopback interface and propagated through a Instance Routing Protocol.
bestPracticeTitle: router-id not in IPv4-format

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no snmp-server host
bestPracticeDescription: Sending SNMP traps to an external trap receiver is critical for effectively managing a network. However, too many SNMP trap destinations can put extra load on the device and use up bandwidth unnecessarily. There should not be a need to have more than four SNMP trap receivers on any given device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Do not configure more than four SNMP trap receivers. Unneeded receivers should be removed from the configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: Too many SNMP trap receivers

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below :
!
aaa new-model
aaa authentication login default local group tacacs+
aaa authentication login default group  tacacs+ local
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+
aaa authentication login CONSOLE line
aaa authentication login CONSOLE none 
!

OR
!
aaa new-model
aaa authentication login default group radius local
aaa authentication login default group radius 
aaa authentication login CONSOLE line
aaa authentication login CONSOLE none 
!
bestPracticeDescription: The VTY lines are the Virtual Terminal lines of the router, used solely to control inbound connections. They are virtual, in the sense that they are a function of software - there is no hardware associated with them. Authentication: Authentication provides a way to identify a user, typically by having the user enter a valid username and valid password before access is granted. Authorization: Authorization is the process of enforcing policies: determining what types of activities, resources, or services a user is permitted to access. Accounting: Accounting measures the resources a user consumes during access, which may include the amount of system time or the amount of data that a user has sent or received during a session. Access to the VTY lines should be protected with strong authentication. This requires configuration of the lines to use AAA.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure AAA for use by the VTY lines.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY line access not protected by AAA

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: service single-slot-reload-enable
bestPracticeDescription: The "service single-slot-reload-enable" command allows a failed line card on a Cisco 7500 series router to reload instead of reloading the entire device. During the single line card reload process, all physical lines and routing protocols on the other line cards of the network backplane remain active. A single line card reload is also substantially faster than the Cbus Complex process. Therefore, enabling single slot reload improves the network availability of a Cisco 7500 router. Due to bug CSCdu03912, this configuration option may appear in non-7500 devices. By default, this service is not enabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable single line card reloading for all line cards in a Cisco 7500 series router using the "service single-slot-reload-enable" global configuration command, if supported by the software. If the software doesn't support SSR, Cisco recommends taking necessary steps to upgrade to appropriate software that incorporates this feature.
bestPracticeTitle: Single line card reloading feature for the Cisco 7500 not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below

!
line vty 0 4
 exec-timeout 5 0
 login
 transport input telnet
line vty 5 10
 exec-timeout 10 0
 login
 transport input telnet
line vty 10 15
 exec-timeout 0 0
 login
 transport input none
!
bestPracticeDescription: Idle sessions consume lines and could prevent legitimate users from accessing the device. If "exec-timeout" is set to zero minutes and zero seconds, this means that timeout has been disabled. The default timeout is 10 minutes.In order to set the interval that the EXEC command interpreter waits for user input before it terminates a session, issue the exec-timeout line configuration command. VTY is a virtual port and used to get Telnet or SSH access to the device. VTY is solely used for inbound connections to the device. These connections are all virtual with no hardware associated with them.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure VTY lines to time out after an appropriate interval of inactivity.  It is recommended to time out after 30 minutes or less.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY exec timeout disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for aaa accounting tacacs and radius 

aaa new-model
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group tacacs+
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group RADIUS-SERVERS

For more recent IOS versions, the running configuration will output as below:

aaa new-model
aaa accounting commands 15 default
 action-type start-stop
 group tacacs+

aaa accounting commands 15 default
 action-type start-stop
 group RADIUS-SERVERS
bestPracticeDescription: As described in RFC 2865 for RADIUS and RFC 1492 for TACACS, these protocols provide strong authentication to protect access to network devices. AAA Accounting for each level of commands ensures that there is accountability for use of privileged commands on the router. Privilege levels are from 1 to 15, with 15 being the highest level. The record keeping can be set to stop-start or stop-only, though command accounting generates Stop records. Cisco supports command accounting with TACACS+. In later versions of IOS, the running configuration format for this command changed and this rule was updated to support those changes. Example: aaa accounting commands 15 default action-type start-stop group tacacs+ AAA (Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting) AAA is a a set of services for controlling access to computer resources, enforcing policies, assessing usage, and providing the information necessary to bill for services. These processes are considered important for effective network management and security.Authentication provides a way to identify a user, typically by having the user enter a valid username and valid password before access is granted.Authorization is the process of enforcing policies: determining what types of activities, resources, or services a user is permitted to access.Accounting measures the resources a user consumes during access, which may include the amount of system time or the amount of data that a user has sent or received during a session. Accounting is carried out through the logging of session statistics and usage information, which is used for authorization control, billing, trend analysis, resource utilization, and capacity planning activities.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Accounting should be configured using the default or a named method, for each level of user access to network devices. There should be at least a level 15 configured.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA Accounting for command level 15 not configured properly

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: snmp-server contact
bestPracticeDescription: The SNMP contact is the SNMP MIB Variable "sysContact". This can be queried remotely to determine the contact for this device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the SNMP contact to be the person or group responsible for this device.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP contact not defined

bestPracticeCaveat:
Summertime is not necessary in a couple of situations.
1)  Customer has standardized on Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) for their devices to allow for consistent time on all devices.
2)  Some countries/timezones do not follow any Daylight Savings Time schedule.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
clock summer-time  recurring
OR
clock summer-time  date
bestPracticeDescription: Network Time Protocol (NTP) is configured but without summertime change dates being configured.  Summertime allows for the automatic adjustment of the router's clock to account for Daylight Savings Time.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable summertime with either the recurring keyword,which uses the Cisco operating system default change dates and times based on U.S. Standards,or configure a specific start and end date and time.  Use of summertime allows for easier correlation of syslog and console messages for Network Management and troubleshooting.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP Summertime Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Remove the unused IS-IS process via the "no router isis" configuration command.
bestPracticeDescription: This rules detects if an unused IS-IS routing process is in a device and should be removed. It checks if there is an IS-IS process without a NET statement or IS-IS interfaces defined.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Remove any unused routing processes.
bestPracticeTitle: Unused IS-IS process

bestPracticeCaveat:
If NetBIOS UDP broadcasts are specifically needed to be forwarded by the router to the helper destination,then it should not be disabled.
These commands are not available on the Catalyst 2960 Series switches,so those exceptions can be ignored.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
no ip forward-protocol udp netbios-ns
no ip forward-protocol udp netbios-dgm
bestPracticeDescription: If a helper address is specified and UDP forwarding is enabled, broadcast packets destined to the following port numbers are forwarded by default: Trivial File Transfer Protocol (TFTP) (port 69); Domain Naming System (port 53); Time service (port 37); NetBIOS Name Server (port 137); NetBIOS Datagram Server (port 138); Boot Protocol (BOOTP) client and server datagrams (ports 67 and 68); TACACS service (port 49); and IEN-116 Name Service (port 42). Helper addresses are most commonly configured for DHCP support (BOOTP ports 67 and 68). So, the forwarding of the other port broadcasts is usually undesirable.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended that all unwanted UDP broadcast ports should be turned off. Because the NetBIOS ones are the most commonly used,these should be disabled at a minimum,unless they are specifically being used in the network. Normally,forwarding NetBIOS UDP broadcasts is not necessary.
bestPracticeTitle: NetBIOS UDP Broadcast Forwarding Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: dot11 arp-cache [optional]
bestPracticeDescription: Client ARP caching on the access point reduces the traffic on your wireless LAN and increases client battery life by stopping ARP requests for client devices at the access point.  Instead of forwarding ARP requests to client devices, the access point responds to requests on behalf of associated client devices and drops ARP requests that are not directed to clients associated to the access point.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable arp caching globally. This command is highly recommended for access points supporting voice over wireless LAN deployments.
bestPracticeTitle: ARP Caching is Disabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If wireless IP phones are not in use on this access point, this rule may be ignored.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: dot11 phone
bestPracticeDescription:
If wireless IP phones are in use on this access point, enable QBSS to help the 802.11
phones make intelligent choices about the access point to which they should associate.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable QBSS globally.
bestPracticeTitle: QBSS not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers with global networks will use a single time zone for network devices, for example Coordinated Universal Time (GMT), and may not configure a time zone system.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: clock timezone
bestPracticeDescription: Network Time Protocol (NTP) is configured but without a time zone.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is common for customers to set a time zone for devices in the same region to easily correlate network events to the local time of day. This could include summer time too.
bestPracticeTitle: NTP enabled without time zone

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
 no snmp-server community public RO 
 no snmp-server community private RW 
!
bestPracticeDescription: The SNMP community string is used in SNMP to determine authority level and to determine what access level is allowed. The default value is often "private" for the read-write community string OR "public" for the read-only community, so using this string results in weak security.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Do not use the well-known community string "private" or "public".  These community strings, as with all passwords, should be carefully chosen to ensure these are not trivial. Community strings should be changed at regular intervals and in accordance with network security policies.
bestPracticeTitle: Using SNMP well-known {public | private} community string

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers desire the behavior of receiving messages via both mechanisms,and their network management systems are designed to handle this information through syslog parsing and SNMP trap filtering preferences. In those cases,it is okay to leave this trap enabled.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no snmp-server enable traps syslog
bestPracticeDescription: The device has been configured to send system log messages to a syslog server and SNMP trap receiver.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The syslog trap sends all syslog messages generated by the device as a SNMP trap. If syslog analysis is already being performed by an analyzer,such as CiscoWorks Resource Manager Essentials,then it may not be useful to receive the same message as a trap as well. The syslog trap should be turned off,as it is duplicate data.
bestPracticeTitle: Unnecessary Syslog SNMP trap configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no logging
bestPracticeDescription: Logging syslog to an external syslog server is critical for effectively managing a network. However, too many syslog destinations can put extra load on the device and use up bandwidth unnecessarily.  During a minor or major outage, a syslog storm could have a multiplying effect on traffic loads on lower speed links and lead to dropped messages.  In addition, it puts an administrative strain on an organization to manage syslogs sent to numerous servers.   In most networks, a primary, backup, and disaster recovery destination will be all that is needed.  Another consideration is the management of the configuration on each device when a new syslog destination needs to be turned up.  Using a UDP traffic multiplexer tool (such as syslog-ng, Samplicator, etc.) allows for you to replicate a single flow to many receivers.  Turning up a new receiver only requires a change in the one tool versus changing potentially hundreds or thousands of devices.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Do not configure more than four syslog destinations. Unneeded destinations should be removed from the configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: Too many syslog servers

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Define an access list and bind it to the VTY lines. 
Example: 
access-list 10 permit 1.1.14.192 
access-list 10 permit 1.1.0.0 0.0.255.255 
access-list 10 permit 1.2.0.0 0.0.255.255 
access-list 10 permit 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255 
access-list 12 permit 172.19.5.0 0.0.0.255

line vty 0 4 
 access-class 10 in
 access-class 12 out
bestPracticeDescription: A VTY line is used for all remote network connections supported by the device, regardless of protocol (SSH, SCP, or Telnet are examples). In order to ensure that a device can be accessed via a remote management session, proper controls must be enforced on VTY lines. Cisco IOS devices have a limited number of vty lines. When all vty lines are in use, new management sessions cannot be established, which creates a DoS condition for access to the device. It is critical to implement access control for VTY lines because they are accessible via the network. One way to enforce access control on VTY lines is with the access-class configuration commands, wherein only authorized hosts will be able to attempt access to network devices. By applying an access list to an inbound VTY, the external access to the lines of the router can be controlled and by applying an access list to an outbound VTY, the destinations that the lines from a router can reach can be controlled. Also when `no exec? is configured on the vty lines, external access to the device is completely denied.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Define an access list of networks and/or devices that are authorized to connect to infrastructure devices.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY lines not protected with an access list

bestPracticeDescription: Information About AAA Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA) feature allows you to verify the identity of, grant access to, and track the actions of users managing the Cisco router. The Cisco router supports Remote Access Dial-In User Service (RADIUS) or Terminal Access Controller Access Control device Plus (TACACS+) protocols.Based on the user ID and password combination that you provide, the Cisco router performs local authentication or authorization using the local database or remote authentication or authorization using one or more AAA servers. A pre-shared secret key provides security for communication between the Cisco router and AAA servers. You can configure a common secret key for all AAA servers or for only a specific AAA server. Authentication: Authentication provides a way to identify a user, typically by having the user enter a valid username and valid password before access is granted. Authorization: Authorization is the process of enforcing policies: determining what types of activities, resources, or services a user is permitted to access. After a user is authenticated, that user may be authorized for different types of access or activity. Accounting: Accounting measures the resources a user consumes during access, which may include the amount of system time or the amount of data that a user has sent or received during a session. Accounting is carried out through the logging of session statistics and usage information, which is used for authorization control, billing, trend analysis, resource utilization, and capacity planning activities.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommended to Configure Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA).

bestPracticeDescription: The loopback interface can be considered stable because once you enable it, it will remain up until you shut it down. This makes loopback interfaces ideal for assigning Layer 3 addresses such as IP addresses when you want a single address as a reference that is independent of the status of any physical interfaces in the networking device.You can use the loopback interface as the termination address for OSPF or BGP sessions. A loopback interface can also be used to establish a Telnet session from the console port of the device to its auxiliary port when all other interfaces are down. In applications where other routers or access servers attempt to reach this loopback interface, you should configure a routing protocol to distribute the subnet assigned to the loopback address.Loopback interfaces provide a stable source interface to ensure that the IP address assigned to the interface is always reachable as long as the IP routing protocols continue to advertise the subnet assigned to the loopback interface.

bestPracticeCaveat: Some devices may not support etherchannel negotiation (XL series switches, Local Directors, device running older software, etc.). In these cases it may be necessary to configure the channel for mode "on" 6800ia NIF ports only support ?mode on? and will not work with LACP or PAgP.In VSS scenario,it is actually recommended to disable negotiation.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configiration as below  

PAgP channel mode desirable:  

interface range fastethernet 1/1 -4
 channel-group 1 mode desirable
 
LACP channel mode active

interface range fastethernet 1/1 -4
 channel-group 1 mode active
bestPracticeDescription: EtherChannel in one of these modes: Port Aggregation Protocol (PAgP), Link Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP), or On. Configure both ends of the EtherChannel in the same mode.When you configure one end of an EtherChannel in either PAgP or LACP mode, the system negotiates with the other end of the channel to determine which ports should become active. If the remote port cannot negotiate an EtherChannel, the local port is put into an independent state and continues to carry data traffic as would any other single link. The port configuration does not change, but the port does not participate in the EtherChannel.When you configure an EtherChannel in the on mode, no negotiations take place. The switch forces all compatible ports to become active in the EtherChannel. The other end of the channel (on the other switch) must also be configured in the on mode; otherwise, packet loss can occur.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable protocol negotiation on all switch-to-switch PAgP/LACP channels. The preferred method is to set desirable mode at both ends of a link for PAgP channels, or set active mode at both ends of a link for LACP channels.  PAgP is a Cisco proprietary technology that has been widely deployed in many networks. LACP is an IEEE standard (802.1ad) and is rapidly gaining acceptance.
bestPracticeTitle: Channel Protocol Negotiation Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable flood-reduction using the following CLI ! flood-reduction ! under the routing process / area / interface configuration modes
bestPracticeDescription: To suppress the unnecessary flooding of link-state advertisements (LSAs) in stable topologies The explosive growth of the Internet has placed focus on the scalability of Interior Gateway Protocols (IGPs) such as Open Shortest Path First (OSPF). The networks using OSPF are becoming larger every day and will continue to expand to accommodate the demand to connect to the Internet. Internet Service Providers and customers with large networks have regularly complained that OSPF has traffic overhead, even when the network topology is stable. By design, OSPF requires link-state advertisements (LSAs) to be refreshed as they expire after 3600 seconds. Some implementations have tried to improve the flooding by reducing the frequency to refresh from 30 minutes to around 50 minutes or so. This solution reduces the amount of refresh traffic but requires at least one refresh before the LSA expires. The OSPF Flooding Reduction feature works by reducing unnecessary refreshing and flooding of already known and unchanged information. To achieve this reduction, the LSAs are now flooded with the higher bit set, thus making them DoNotAge (DNA) LSAs. Benefits: All OSPF customers with generally stable networks benefit from the reduced traffic overhead provided by the OSPF Flooding Reduction feature. Restrictions: All routers and access servers in the area must be running Cisco IOS Release 11.2 or higher, or the feature will not be active.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Internet Service Providers and customers with large networks have regularly complained that OSPF has traffic overhead, even when the network topology is stable. Use the OSPF Flooding Reduction feature that works by reducing unnecessary refreshing and flooding of already known and unchanged information.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPFv3 Flood Reduction is not Enabled

Internet Service Providers and customers with large networks have regularly complained that OSPF has traffic overhead, even when the network topology is stable.
