bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below,
!
router ospf 100
 max-metric router-lsa on-startup 100
 network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
 network 1.1.1.1 0.0.0.0 area 0
!
OR
!
router ospf 100
 max-metric router-lsa on-startup wait-for-bgp
 network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
 network 1.1.1.1 0.0.0.0 area 0
!
bestPracticeDescription: OSPF maximum metric feature allows you to bring a new router into a network without immediately routing traffic through the new router. One can use the max-metric router-lsa command in the following scenarios: Reloading a router. After a router is reloaded, Interior Gateway Protocols (IGPs) converge very quickly, and other routers may try to forward traffic through the newly reloaded router. If the router is still building BGP routing tables, the packets that are destined for other networks that the router has not learned through BGP may be ropped. Introducing a router into a network without routing traffic through it. You might want to connect a router to an OSPF network but not want real traffic to flow through the router if there are better alternate paths. If no alternate paths exist, then this router would still accept transit traffic. The following two configuration options enable a router to advertise a maximum metric at startup: 1.You can configure a timer to advertise a maximum metric when the router is started or reloaded. When this option is configured, the router will advertise a maximum metric, which forces neighbor routers to select alternate paths until the timer expires. When the timer expires, the router will advertise accurate (normal) metrics, and other routers will send traffic to this router depending on the cost. The configurable range of the timer is from 5 to 86,400 seconds. 2.You can configure a router to advertise a maximum metric at startup until BGP routing tables converge or until the default timer expires (600 seconds). Once BGP routing tables converge or the default timer expires, the router will advertise accurate (normal) metrics and other routers will send traffic to this router, depending on the cost.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Use the max-metric router-lsa command to originate LSAs with a maximum metric (LSInfinity: 0xFFFF) through all nonstub links. This command allows Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) routing tables to converge without attracting transit traffic (if there are not alternate lower cost paths to the router). The router advertises accurate (normal) metrics after the configured or default timers expire or after BGP sends a notification that routing tables have converged.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF Max Metric on startup not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable cost fallback using the following routing process interface command. cost-fallback cost threshold bandwidth. Note : The cost defined in the command should be a higher cost than the normal interface cost.
bestPracticeDescription: To apply higher cost than the normal interface cost when the cumulative bandwidth of a bundle interface goes below the threshold specified and to revert to the original cost if the cumulative bandwidth goes above the configured threshold The motivation of setting the fallback cost is to cost out an interface or disfavor an interface without shutting it down when its cumulative bandwidth goes below the user specified threshold, so that the traffic can take an alternative path. The normal interface cost will take over when the cumulative bandwidth reaches or exceeds user-specified threshold. This is applicable for bundled interfaces where there chances of link failure resulting in the cumulative bandwidth to be lower than the maximum available.
bestPracticeRecommendation: To apply higher cost than the normal interface cost when the cumulative bandwidth of a bundle interface goes below the threshold specified and to revert to the original cost if the cumulative bandwidth goes above the configured threshold, use the cost-fallback command.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF Cost Fallback not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration is below :
!
ipv4 access-list ABC
 10 deny ipv4 any 225.0.0.0 0.255.255.255
 20 permit ipv4 any any
!
router pim
 address-family ipv4
  accept-register ABC
 !
bestPracticeDescription: The accept-register command prevents unauthorized sources from registering with the rendezvous point. If an unauthorized source sends a register message to the rendezvous point, then the rendezvous point immediately sends back a register-stop message. The auto-rp candidate-rp command is used by the rendezvous point for a multicast group range. The router sends an Auto-RP announcement message to the well-known group CISCO-RP-ANNOUNCE (224.0.1.39). This message announces the router as a candidate rendezvous point for the groups in the range described by the access list. When the interval keyword is specified, the interval between Auto-RP announcements is set to number of seconds with the total hold time of the announcements automatically set to three times the interval time. The recommended interval time range is from 1 to 180 seconds. The hold time of the Auto-RP announcement is the time for which the announcement is valid. After the designated hold time, the announcement expires and the entry is purged from the mapping cache until there is another announcement.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommend to configure "accept-register" on PIM RP in order to control valid registrations and to minimize the DOS or DDOS attacks for IPv4-multicast network services.
bestPracticeTitle: PIM RP Registrations not Controlled

Configure the maximum-prefix limiting for EIGRP redistribution with the below commands:

router eigrp 200
 vrf ABC 
  address-family ipv4
   redistribute maximum-prefix 5000 95 warning-only
   redistribute ospf 200
!
bestPracticeDescription: To limit the number of prefixes redistributed into an Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP)process, use the redistribute maximum-prefix command in IPv4 VRF address family configuration mode. then the maximum-prefix limit is exceeded, sessions with remote peers are torn down. All routes learnt from remote peers and through redistribution are removed from the topology and routing tables. And redistribution and peering is suspended for the default or user-defined time period.This way there is a control at the point of redistribution and will not jeopardize the network due to misconfigured neighboring autonomous systems.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommend to enable redistribute maximum-prefix to control the number of redistributed prefixes and this can help in preventing a network meltdown due to a misconfigured neighboring AS.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enabled IP Event dampening with the following command under the interface configuration:
dampening [half-life-period reuse-threshold] [suppress-threshold max-suppress [restart-penalty]]
bestPracticeDescription: Interface state changes occur when interfaces are administratively brought up or down or if an interface changes the state. When an interface changes its state or flaps, routing protocols are notified of the status of the routes that are affected by the change in the state. Every interface state change requires all affected devices in the network to recalculate best paths, install or remove routes from the routing tables, and then advertise valid routes to peer routers. An unstable interface that flaps excessively can cause other devices in the network to consume substantial amounts of system processing resources and cause routing protocols to lose synchronization with the state of the flapping interface.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
The IP Event Dampening feature introduces a configurable exponential decay mechanism to suppress the effects of excessive interface flapping events on routing protocols and routing tables in the network. This feature allows the network operator to configure a router to automatically identify and selectively dampen a local interface that is flapping. Dampening an interface removes the interface from the network until the interface stops flapping and becomes stable. Configuring the IP Event Dampening feature improves convergence times and stability throughout the network by isolating failures so that disturbances are not propagated. This in turn,reduces the utilization of system processing resources by other devices in the network and improves overall network stability. The following are the benefits:
- Reduced Processing Load
- Faster Convergence
- Improved Network Stability
bestPracticeTitle: IP event dampening not enabled for EIGRP

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
interface ethernet 4/2
 switchport mode trunk 
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 200
!
bestPracticeDescription: It is a best practice to carry only necessary vlans on a trunk port and manually prune the unnecessary ones.Pruning VLANs from Trunks helps prevent STP being propogated to switches that do not need it for that VLAN. It also provides an added security.A trunk port can carry traffic in one or more VLANs on the same physical link (VLANs are based on the trunk-allowed VLANs list). By default, a trunk interface can carry traffic for all VLANs. To specify that only certain VLANs are allowed on the specified trunk.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends pruning VLANs from trunks where they are not needed to maximize STP stability and to improve convergence for bundle interface up scenario.
bestPracticeTitle: VLAN trunk allowed list not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable the maximum prefix limit within the address-family with the following commands:
vrf [name]
 address-family [protocol] [family]
  maximum prefix [maximum] [mid-threshold]
 !
!
bestPracticeDescription: Each address-family within each VRF should have a maximum prefix limit configured. The maximum prefix feature is designed to protect the router from local resource exhaustion that can occur as a result of receiving too many prefixes from routing peers. Excessive prefix generation by peers could result onto misconfiguration (such as, accidentally redistributing global BGP table into an IGP) or malicious attacks. When the maximum prefix limit is exceeded within an Address-Family (AF) the AF's RIB will not accept any additional prefixes until the prefix count drops below the threshold. If the optional 'mid-threshold' parameter is specified in the command, a warning log message and/or SNMP trap will be generated once the prefix count exceeds the specified percentage of the configured limit. This limit applies to all dynamic, static, and connected routes within the AF.
bestPracticeRecommendation: A maximum prefix limit should be configured within each address-family in each VRF. This protects the router from local resource exhaustion that can result from misconfiguration on a peer router or malicious attacks. The value of the prefix limit should be based on network requirements and platform resource capacity. The optional 'mid-threshold' value should be configured to ensure warning logs are created at the desired threshold.
bestPracticeTitle: Maximum Prefix Limit Not Configured Within VRF Address-Family

bestPracticeCaveat: Athough the logging ACL feature is useful and rate-limits the events, it can significantly improve the volume of syslogs . Alternative is checking the local ACL counters on the ACE to check for matches, but this provided less information.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure access-control list violation log feature to log the packets that violate the access-control policies.
bestPracticeDescription: When using access lists to filter traffic, administrators are advised to log some packets that violate the filtering criteria to find out what type of traffic is being sent to the router. Because access list log messages are rate-limited, the performance impact on Cisco IOS XR devices is minimal.
bestPracticeRecommendation: While using access-lists to filter traffic, it is advised to log some packets that violate the filter criteria. This is to find out the type of traffic being sent to the router.  Since the access-list log messages are rate-limited, the performance impact on the Cisco IOS XR devices is minimal.
bestPracticeTitle: Access Control List violation log feature is not enabled.

bestPracticeCaveat: In flat topologies, proxy-arp may be required.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: interface interface type no proxy-arp !
bestPracticeDescription: Need an IOS XR rule to mimic the IOS rule that exists today for Proxy ARP is enabled. The Cisco IOS XR software uses proxy ARP (as defined in RFC 1027) to help hosts with no knowledge of routing determine the media addresses of hosts on other networks or subnets. Normally, ARP transactions are confined to a particular LAN segment. A Cisco router can act as an intermediary for ARP responding to ARP queries on selected interfaces and thus enabling transparent access between multiple LAN segments. Proxy ARP effectively extends a LAN at layer 2 across multiple segments.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Proxy ARP should be used only between LAN segments at the same trust level and only when needed to support flat network architectures. Disable proxy ARP where it is not needed on IP configured interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: Proxy ARP is enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration is below :-

!
mpls ldp 
 address-family ipv4
  label local advertise
   disable
   for pfx_acl_1 to peer_acl_1
   for pfx_acl_2 to peer_acl_2
   for pfx_acl_3
   interface POS 0/1/0/0
   interface POS 0/2/0/0
!
!
ipv4 access-list pfx_acl_1
    10 permit ipv4 host 10.0.0.0 any
!
ipv4 access-list pfx_acl_2
    10 permit ipv4 host 20.0.0.0 any
!
ipv4 access-list peer_acl_1
    10 permit ipv4 host 10.1.1.1 any
    20 permit ipv4 host 10.1.1.2 any
!
ipv4 access-list peer_acl_2
    10 permit ipv4 host 20.2.2.2 any
!
bestPracticeDescription: To control the advertisement of local labels, use the label advertise command in MPLS LDP configuration mode. The disable key-word disables label advertisement to all peers for all prefixes. This will enforce to apply 'prefix-acl' and/or 'peer-acl' to be configured to control label advertisement. By default, a label switched router (LSR) advertises all incoming label prefixes to each neighboring router. we can control the exchange of label binding information using the mpls ldp label advertise command. Using the optional keywords, we can advertise selective prefixes to all neighbors, advertise selective prefixes to defined neighbors, or disable label advertisement to all peers for all prefixes. Best Practice is to control label advertisements based upon prefix-acl and/or peer-acl.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to control label advertisements based upon prefix-acl and/or peer-acl and disable the "mpls label advertise disable" to disable the label advertisement to all peers for all prefixes to control label advertisements.
bestPracticeTitle: LDP label advertise disable is not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: The incremental SPF feature is no longer supported after version 16.5.1
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable ISPF with the following routing process 
!
router ospf
 ispf
!
bestPracticeDescription: OSPF uses Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to compute the shortest path tree (SPT). During the computation of the SPT, the shortest path to each node is discovered. The topology tree is used to populate the routing table with routes to IP networks. When changes to a Type-1 or Type-2 link-state advertisement (LSA) occur in an area, the entire SPT is recomputed. In many cases, the entire SPT need not be recomputed because most of the tree remains unchanged. Incremental SPF allows the system to recompute only the affected part of the tree. Recomputing only a portion of the tree rather than the entire tree results in faster OSPF convergence and saves CPU resources. Note that if the change to a Type-1 or Type-2 LSA occurs in the calculating router itself, then the full SPT is performed. Incremental SPF is scheduled in the same way as the full SPF. Routers enabled with incremental SPF and routers not enabled with incremental SPF can function in the same internetwork.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable incremental spf for faster and efficient path calculation in OSPF networks. This is not enabled by default.
bestPracticeTitle: Incremental SPF not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: Certain customers prefer loopback address to be source of all UDP packet source. But this would require per protocol definition of the source of the packet.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the use of virtual management ip address for system logging using the 'ipv4 virtual address use-as-src-addr' global command
bestPracticeDescription: To ensure that UDP traffic flow, such as syslog and SNMP traps is not interrupted during the failover administrators are advised to use virtual addresses to source all management traffic.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to enable use of a virtual ip address for management traffic based on UDP (SNMP and SYSLOG) to have continuous delivery of logging in times of a failover.
bestPracticeTitle: Virtual Management IP Address not utilized for system logging

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-
!
no service unsupported-transceiver
interface 
  no transceiver permit pid all
!
bestPracticeDescription: Configuring the command service unsupported-transceiver in conjunction with transceiver permit pid all allows 3rd party transceivers to be installed in the chassis. Unsupported transceivers hinders troubleshooting by Cisco engineers in a network down situation.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to replace any unsupported transceivers and then configure no service unsupported-transceiver.
bestPracticeTitle: Unsupported transceiver should be replaced

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable maximum-prefix under the router bgp process per neighbor / address-family.
Note: Ensure to analyze the network and create a baseline number of routes to configure the allowed number.
bestPracticeDescription: BGP prefixes are stored by a router in memory. The more prefixes a router holds, the greater is the memory consumed by BGP. Both malicious and inadvertent (misconfiguration) processes can cause excessive prefixes to be seen by a BGP peer. To prevent memory exhaustion, it is important to configure the maximum number of prefixes accepted on a per-peer basis. Administrators are advised to configure limits for each BGP peer. The BGP maximum-prefix feature allows users to control the number of prefixes that can be installed from a neighbor. When configured, this feature will bring down a neighbor relationship when the number of received prefixes from that peer exceeds the configured maximum-prefix limit. The maximum-prefix threshold would be set with some margin of error over a target value to accommodate some degree of unexpected changes in topology.
bestPracticeRecommendation: When configured, this feature will bring down a neighbor relationship when the number of received prefixes from that peer exceeds the configured maximum-prefix limit.  If configured with warning-only, a log message will be generated and the peer will not be terminated.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP maximum-prefix protection not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Revert the internal-distance and local-distance modification in bgp and revisit the design to avoid the same.
	!
	distance bgp external-distance internal-distance local-distance
	!

	Command Mode 
	IPv4 address family configuration
	IPv6 address family configuration
	VRF IPv4 address family configuration
	VRF IPv6 address family configuration

	Defaults

	external-distance : 20
	internal-distance : 200
	local-distance : 200
bestPracticeDescription:
Check for the command "distance bgp external-distance internal-distance local-distance"
	external-distance could be 20 / 200

	Internal-distance and local-distance should not be anything other than 200. If found, then flag it.

	Eg 
	distance bgp 20 20 200 --> Should flag an exception as internal-distance has been changed to 20
	distance bgp 200 200 200 --> should be a pass
	distance bgp 20 200 200 -->
bestPracticeRecommendation: Changing the administrative distance of BGP internal routes is considered risky and is not recommended. This could create routing table inconsistencies, which can interfere with routing and could result in loops.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Internal administrative distance modified

bestPracticeCaveat:
In special cases you may want to implement completely separate OSPFv3 domains on the same router.  
Sometimes you may desire to use a process for the default VRF and another one for other VRFs.
Just keep in mind that in absence of a compelling reason, it is recommended to use the minimum possible number of OSPFv3 processes, usually one.
In any case no more than 4 OSPFv3 processes must be used even if there is a configuration limit of 10.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
To correct the configuration you must put all the VRFs under the same process.
Example:
!
router ospfv3 1
 area 0
  interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
!
router ospfv3 vpn
 vrf vpn
  redistribute bgp 65000
  area 0
   interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/2.45
!
can become:
!
router ospfv3 1
 area 0
  interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
 vrf vpn
  redistribute bgp 65000
  area 0
   interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/2.45
!
bestPracticeDescription: When OSPFv3 is enabled on various VRFs, it is best practice to configure multiple VRFs (possibly including the default one) under the same OSPFv3 process, to avoid unnecessary resource usage. Multiple OSPFv3 processes should usually be used only to implement multiple OSPFv3 domains on the same router. Note that although 10 processes can be configured as of IOX 5.1.x, it is recommended not to exceed 4.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Verify whether you really need multiple OSPFv3 processes or you can configure all the VRFs under the same OSPFv3 process.
When there is no specific reason to use different processes, it is recommended to configure all the VRFs under the same process, to avoid unnecessary overhead and resource usage.
bestPracticeTitle: More than one OSPFv3 process

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable maximum prefix-limit with the following CLI in the router configuration mode:redistribute maximum-prefix max [ threshold ] [ warning-only | withdraw [ num-retries timeout ]]
bestPracticeDescription: To limit the aggregate number of routes that may be redistributed into an OSPFv3 process, administrators are advised to use the maximum redistributed-prefix command in the appropriate mode. This would prevent accidental redistribution errors and prevent a network meltdown.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
RIB entries require memory to be allocated, uncontrolled redistribution may result in memory depletion. It is recommended to configure maximum-prefix limit to redistribution as this would protect the network, if the number of redistributed routes reaches the
 maximum value configured, no more routes will be redistributed (unless the warning-only keyword was configured).
bestPracticeTitle: Redistribution seen without prefix-limit protection

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable Core Isolation detection by configuring the backbone interface command in redundancy iccp group configuration mode. Ensure to list all the interfaces that can be used to forward traffic from the DHD, that is all the uplink interfaces.

Example: 
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router# configure
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router#(config) redundancy iccp group 2
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router#(config-redundancy-iccp-group) # backbone interface GigabitEthernet0/1/0/2
bestPracticeDescription: When configuring MCLAG, it is recommended that the backbone interface command is used in the redundancy group ICCP configuration mode. All uplink interfaces must be configured in the backbone section so that the POA realizes when it becomes isolated from the backbone. When all listed interfaces are down, this POA declares a Core Isolation event and sends a dying gasp LACP packet to the DHD. If this feature is not configured, this POA will remain or become active after isolation and blackholing traffic.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
In the following example, all uplink interfaces must be listed in the backbone section.
When all the listed interfaces are down, this POA is not able to forward traffic from the DHD, so it ensures it is not LACP active by sending a Dying Gasp and stopping LACP negotiations.
redundancy.

  iccp 
    group 2
      mlacp node 1  
      mlacp system mac 0000.deaf.0300 
      mlacp system priority 1  
      member 
       neighbor 10.0.0.133
      !
      backbone
        interface TenGigE0/0/0/0
        interface TenGigE0/0/0/1
      !  
      isolation recovery-delay 100
    !
  !
 !
bestPracticeTitle: Backbone Interface not Configured in MCLAG

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Remove a Key if it configured without encryption method. eg "no tacacs-server key " and configure a key by using "tacacs-server key 7 " as a global config command.
Alternatively, for each tacacs-server host block, set the key with encryption using:
tacacs-server host a.b.c.d
  key 7 xxxxxxxxxx
bestPracticeDescription: To set the authentication encryption key used for all TACACS communications between the router and the TACACS server, use the tacacs-server key command in global configuration mode. Using an integer value 7 with the key specifies an encrypted shared key. Alternatively, you can set the key per host.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Ensure your keys are encrypted to secure the communication between the router and the TACACS server.
bestPracticeTitle: TACACS server key not encrypted

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Generate DSA key pairs: crypto key generate dsa (exec mode)
Configure SSHv2: ssh server v2 (config mode)
bestPracticeDescription: SSHv2 features both security and feature improvements over SSHv1. Better security, for example, comes through Diffie-Hellman key exchange and strong integrity checking via message authentication codes. New features of SSHv2 include the ability to run any number of shell sessions over a single SSH connection. Version 2: - Strong cryptographic integrity check - Supports password changing - Any number of session channels per connection (including none) - Full negotiation of modular cryptographic and compression algorithms,including bulk encryption,MAC,and public-key Version 1: - Weak CRC-32 integrity check; admits an insertion attack in conjunction with some bulk ciphers. - Exactly one session channel per connection (requires issuing a remote command even when you don't want one) - Negotiates only the bulk cipher; all others are fixed.
bestPracticeRecommendation: By default both SSHv1 and SSHv2 are supported.  Recommendation is to enable SSHv2 only.
bestPracticeTitle: SSHv1 enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable Loop Free Alternate (FRR) for the IS-IS by the following command fast-reroute per-prefix
bestPracticeDescription: LFA is a node other than the primary neighbor. Traffic is redirected to LFA after a network failure. LFA makes the forwarding decision without any knowledge of the failure. It must neither use a failed element nor use a protecting node to forward traffic. LFA must not cause loops. By default, it is enabled on all supported interfaces as long as the interface can be used as a primary path. Advantages of using per-prefix LFAs are as follows: The repair path forwards traffic during transition when the primary path link is down. All destinations having a per-prefix LFA are protected. This leaves only a subset (a node at the far side of the failure) unprotected.
bestPracticeRecommendation: LFA has quickly become the FRR technology of choice because of its 50ms or better convergence time and easy of deployment.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS LFA not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: LDP supports IPv4 standard access lists only.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable inbound label filtering on the note mpls ldp! label accept for prefix-acl from ip-address!
!
mpls ldp
 label accept for pfx_acl_2 from 192.168.1.1
!
bestPracticeDescription: To control the receipt of labels (remote bindings) for a set of prefixes from a peer, use the label accept command in MPLS LDP configuration mode. By default, LDP accepts labels (as remote bindings) for all prefix from all its peers. To save resources (such as memory) configure the access list to specify label and binding acceptance for a set of prefixes from a peer. If the inbound label filtering policy changes such that it allows previously-denied prefix from a peer, then reset the LDP session with the peer using the clear mpls ldp neighbor command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Best Practice is to control the acceptance of labels from ldp peers based upon prefix-acl and/or peer-neighbor.
bestPracticeTitle: MPLS label accept for prefix-acl is not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Control label allocation using 'label allocate for prefix-acl' in the mpls ldp configuration mode.
bestPracticeDescription: To control allocation of local label only for a set of prefixes, use the label allocate command in MPLS LDP configuration mode. Local label allocation control lets you override the default label allocation policy and provides many benefits,including reduced memory usage and fewer forwarding and network updates. By default, LDP allocates local labels for all learned routes. There are times when you may want to limit label allocation for a given set of prefixes; for example, when using LDP in the core network to provide MPLS transport from one edge to another edge. In such cases, it is necessary only to set up LSPs for Loopback /32 addresses for PE routers (rendering it unnecessary to allocate and advertise local labels for other IGP prefixes).
bestPracticeRecommendation: In order to conserve memory, reduce forwarding tables, network updates it is recommended to control label allocation with filters.
bestPracticeTitle: Label allocation not filtered

bestPracticeCaveat: This BP can be ignored if heavy traffic conditions are not expected on this router.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the maximum number of external SA entries that can be learned by the router or a msdp peer using one of the below syntaxes:

MSDP configuration mode:
!
router msdp
 maximum peer-external-sa 20000
!
bestPracticeDescription: The maximum peer-external-sa and maximum external-sa commands configure the maximum number of external SA entries that can be learned for each configured MSDP peer or the entire router. This command is used to control router resource utilization under heavy traffic conditions. When configured from MSDP configuration mode, the maximum external-sa command configures the total number of external SA entries (that is, the total cumulative SA state for all peers) that can be learned by the router. Note: The configuration fails if we configure the maximum number of external SA entries to be lower than the current accumulated SA state. When configured from MSDP peer configuration mode, the maximum external-sa command configures the total number of external SA entries that can be learned by a specific MSDP peer. This command can also be used to configure a specific MSDP peer to override the maximum external SA entry value configured with the maximum peer-external-sa command. Note: The configuration fails if we configure the maximum number of external SA entries for a specific MSDP peer to be higher than the maximum number of external SA entries that can be learned by the router.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SA-Limit for maximum number of external SA entries that can be learned by the router or a msdp peer should be configured and set to its default value (20000).
bestPracticeTitle: Maximum Peer-External-SA is not Default

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Use the below command: aaa authorization commands {default | list-name} {none | local | group {tacacs+ | radius | group-name}}
bestPracticeDescription: AAA authorization for all EXEC shell commands is not configured on the device. Authorization commands are used to verify that an authenticated user (or principal) is granted permission to perform a specific task.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure AAA Authorization on the device. Cisco IOS XR software uses the first method listed to authorize users for specific network services. If that method fails to respond, Cisco IOS XR software selects the next method listed in the method list. The commands keyword configures authorization for all EXEC shell commands. Command authorization applies to the EXEC mode commands issued by a user. It attempts authorization for all EXEC mode commands.
bestPracticeTitle: Use AAA Authorization for Commands

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable unknown unicast, broadcast and multicast rate-limiting by the following command under bridge-domain configuration. The rate-limit value to be derived by trending the network traffic: storm-control unknown-unicast pps {TBD} >>> TBD based on statistics storm-control multicast pps {TBD} storm-control broadcast pps {TBD}
bestPracticeDescription: In VPLS solution provided by SP, broadcast storms were caused due to unknown unicast. Broadcast and multicast packets can result in severe performance degradation and network instability.
bestPracticeRecommendation: This can be avoided by best practice of rate limiting such packets per VPLS instance (i.e. per bridge domain).
bestPracticeTitle: Configuring Storm Control under VPLS Bridge

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: To specify the recipient of a Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notification operation,use the 'snmp-server host' command.
bestPracticeDescription: SNMP traps are enabled but if the recipient host is not configured, then the traps enabled without specifying a host could be a misconfiguration. It is recommended that a SNMP recipient host be configured so that the traps enabled do not lead to any misconfiguration.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure a recipient of the SNMP traps generated on the device. It is recommended that a SNMP recipient host be configured so that the traps enabled do not lead to any misconfiguration.
bestPracticeTitle: SNMP traps enabled without snmp-server host

bestPracticeCaveat: This may not apply when all the devices in the network are Cisco switches that are running ISL. ISL does not support native VLAN.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable the following command in the global configuration mode:-
!
vlan dot1q tag native
!
bestPracticeDescription: Whenever the traffic is being sent on the trunk link, frames get a VLAN tag for the VLAN the traffic is being sent to. When the switch encounters a tagged frame, it looks at the VLAN ID to know out of which port the traffic has to be forwarded, strips it off and sends data frame out appropriate port. In case of a native VLAN, no tag is present in the frame. So when such a frame comes to the switches ingress port, switch notices the absence of VLAN ID and assumes that it is to be sent to the native VLAN, and forwards it out the appropriate port. This is a security vulnerability as any intruder can tamper with such a frame and add own VLAN id to it, causing traffic to be sent to a different VLAN. This can lead to intruder getting access to resources of that VLAN and he can cause a lot of damage. So, it is recommended to tag the native VLAN frames as well, to prevent any intrusion. Use the following command to tag the native VLAN: vlan dot1Q tag native
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to tag the native VLAN frames and drop untagged frames, to prevent any intrusion.
Use the following command to tag the native VLAN:
vlan dot1q tag native
In case of a native VLAN, no tag is present in the frame. So when such a frame comes to the switch's ingress port, switch notices the absence of VLAN ID and assumes that it is to be sent to the native VLAN, and forwards it out the appropriate port. This is a security vulnerability as any intruder can tamper with such a frame and add own VLAN id to it, causing traffic to be sent to a different VLAN.  If not adhered, some malicious intruder might gain access into the organization's network by tampering with the frames and can lead to significant loss of information.
bestPracticeTitle: Native vlan not getting tagged posing a security threat

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Use the 'session-limit ' command as per the below example. 
n7000(config)# line vty 
n7000(config-line)# session-limit 5
bestPracticeDescription: The VTY session limit determines the number of SSHv2 sessions, telnet sessions, or both that can be active simultaneously. The session-limit defaults to 32 active sessions.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure a practical session limit such as 5 or 10 sessions to improve security.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY session limit between 5 and 10 not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
line vty 
  ip access-class  in
bestPracticeDescription:
An access class should be applied to the VTY port to increase security by restricting SSH and 
    Telnet access to specific source and destination IP addresses. An access class configured on the VTY port is applicable when using an in-band or out-of-band management strategy. 
    An access-class is configured per traffic direction, "in" applies to inbound sessions and "out" applies to outbound sessions.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure ACL for vty line to be secured.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY lines not protected with an access list

bestPracticeCaveat: This is a static configuration to be done on the redistribution points and care has to be taken when doing routing changes increasing prefixes.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable limiting prefixes redistributed prefixes by the below command under the routing process:
  redistribute maximum-prefix maximum [seconds] [warning-only | withdraw]
bestPracticeDescription:
Enabling redistribution without proper control
						would cause network meltdown. If someone
						mistakenly injects a large number of IP routes
						into IS-IS, perhaps by redistributing Border
						Gateway Protocol (BGP) into IS-IS, the network
						can be severely flooded. Limiting the number of
						redistributed routes prevents this potential
						problem.
bestPracticeRecommendation: When the redistributed maximum-prefix command is configured, if the number of redistributed routes reaches the maximum value configured, no more routes will be redistributed (unless the warning-only keyword was configured).
bestPracticeTitle: Prefix limit not enabled for redistribution

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure: rcc ipv4 unicast
bestPracticeDescription: The Route Consistency Checker is a command-line tools that can be used to verify consistency between control plane and data plane route and label programming in IOS XR software. Routers in production networks may end up in a state where the forwarding information does not match the control plane information. Possible causes of this include fabric or transport failures between the Route Processor (RP) and the line cards (LCs), or issues with the Forwarding Information Base (FIB). RCC/LCC can be used to identify and provide detailed information about resultant inconsistencies between the control plane and data plane. This information can be used to further investigate and diagnose the cause of forwarding problems and traffic loss.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The Best Practice recommendation is to enable Route Consistency Checker (RCC) for each Internet Protocol Version [4, 6] [unicast, multicast] enabled on the network element.
bestPracticeTitle: RCC IPv4 Unicast not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure: rcc ipv6 unicast
bestPracticeDescription: The Route Consistency Checker is a command-line tool that can be used to verify consistency between control plane and data plane route and label programming in IOS XR software. Routers in production networks may end up in a state where the forwarding information does not match the control plane information. Possible causes of this include fabric or transport failures between the Route Processor (RP) and the line cards (LCs), or issues with the Forwarding Information Base (FIB). RCC/LCC can be used to identify and provide detailed information about resultant inconsistencies between the control plane and data plane. This information can be used to further investigate and diagnose the cause of forwarding problems and traffic loss.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The Best Practice recommends to enable Route Consistency Checker (RCC) for each Internet Protocol Version [4, 6] [unicast, multicast] enabled on the network element.
bestPracticeTitle: RCC IPv6 Unicast Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Review the network design to ensure the labeled and non-labeled unicast does not fall on the same peering. If cannot be avoided, create two BGP sessions and enable the address-families respectively.
bestPracticeDescription: An address family must be explicitly configured in the router configuration mode for the address family to be active in BGP. Similarly, an address family must be configured under the neighbor for the BGP session to be established for that address family. An address family must be configured in router configuration mode before it can be configured under a neighbor.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use either labelled address-family or unlabelled address-family configuration for a given bgp peer, not both. With both address-family config, user may run into issue where prefixes may not get advertised due to race condition situation in code If it is necessary to run two address-family then create two different BGP sessions for each
bestPracticeTitle: BGP address-family enabled for labelled and unlabeled unicast for the same peer

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: configure "service divert-rate-limit ip {divert-code} rate {value} limit {value} "
bestPracticeDescription:
The PXF DRL Enhancement feature prevents congestion of the FP-to-RP interface by identifying and rate-limiting entities that would otherwise cause congestion. 
						Diverted packets are sent from the forwarding processor to the Route Processor through the FP-to-RP interface. 
						This interface gets congested when packets (that require diversion) arrive at the FP at a faster rate than they can be transmitted to the RP. 
						When the interface gets congested, valid packets in the FP-to-RP queues are tail-dropped. 
						This situation can be caused deliberately by DoS attacks directed at the CMTS, or by faulty external hardware. 
						The PXF DRL Enhancement feature identifies packet streams that cause congestion on the FP-to-RP interface. 
						Packets in the stream are then dropped according to the configured rate-limiting parameters. 
						Rate-limiting occurs before the packets are placed in the FP-to-RP queues, thereby allowing other valid packets to reach the RP.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to have "service divert-rate-limit ip.*" command configured under globale configuration mode to prevent congestion of the FP-to-RP interface by identifying and rate-limiting entities that would otherwise cause congestion.
bestPracticeTitle: CMTS Divert Rate Limit Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure as below for switchport block unicast


interface FastEthernet 0/1
switchport mode access
switchport access vlan 2
switchport block unicast
bestPracticeDescription: By default, a switch floods packets with unknown destination MAC addresses to all ports. If unknown unicast traffic is forwarded to a switch port, there might be security issues. To prevent forwarding such traffic, you can configure a port to block unknown unicast packets. Blocking of unicast traffic is not automatically enabled on a switch port; you must explicitly configure it.The interface can be a physical interface (for example, GigabitEthernet 1/1) or an EtherChannel group (such as port-channel 5). When you block unicast traffic for a port channel, it is blocked on all ports in the port channel group.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enabling unknown unicast / multicast traffic blocking would help protect the control plane from denial-of-service attacks. It is recommended to enable unknown unicast/multicast protection for the control plane.
bestPracticeTitle: switchport block unicast not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable HSRP authentication with the following hsrp interface configuration command:
hsrp [group-number] authentication string
bestPracticeDescription: The Hot Standby Router Protocol (HSRP) processes running on a network can be protected from attack using authentication. An authentication string for the Hot Standby Router Protocol (HSRP) may be set using the command standby [group-number] authentication text string . If the authentication string is not set, the default string is 'cisco'. Having the default authentication string could result in compromising the security of the device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to enable authentication for HSRP.  Enable a strong password for the authentication string and ensure to configure the same on all participating routers in HSRP group.
bestPracticeTitle: HSRP Authentication not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: This rule is not mandatory. It is a requirement. If the customer does not want to enable MPLS TE between two different areas on their network, then this rule does not apply. In addition, if customer does not have different area, then also this rule does not apply.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable 'mpls traffic-eng' globally, then apply mpls traffic-eng requirement parameter on router ospf / router isis, enable rsvp & mpls te on all interfaces from head end to tail end. Configure explicit-path globally and define loose next-hop address, apply this explicit-path under tunnel interface.
bestPracticeDescription: Inter-Area MPLS TE is required for the customer, if they want to enable MPLS TE between two different areas. For example: there is Area 1 and Area 2 and both are connected to Area 0. In addition, if the customer wants to enable MPLS TE between Area 1 and Area 2, this rule also needs to be followed.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to follow this rule if customer needs to enable MPLS TE between two different areas. This rule should strictly be followed only for Inter-Area MPLS and loose command required only between ABR (border router between two Area).
bestPracticeTitle: MPLS TE Not Enabled Between Two Different Areas

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
mpls ldp
  log
bestPracticeDescription:
To enable logging of notices, describing session
						changes for mpls neighbor, graceful-restart and
						session-protection.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enabling logging under mpls ldp will help the user to troubleshoot mpls neighbor,GR or session-protection by receiving syslog or console message.
bestPracticeTitle: MPLS LDP Logging is not enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
- Rule can be ignored for L2 Only devices.
- Support starts with:
  - Nexus 7000 version 4.2(8) and 5.1(1) 
  - Nexus 5000/6000 version 6.0(2)N1(1)
  - Nexus 3000 version 5.0(3)U2(1)
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: hardware ip glean throttle
bestPracticeDescription: When forwarding an incoming IP packet in a line card, if the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) request for the next hop is not resolved, the line card forwards the packets to the supervisor in order to generate an ARP request. Once the ARP request responds to the supervisor, it resolves the MAC address for the next hop and programs the hardware. If the supervisor cannot resolve the ARP entry, then the linecard sends all packets destined to that address to the supervisor. The supervisor generates ARP requests indefinitely until the ARP entry is resolved. There is a hardware rate limiter called glean placed in order to protect the supervisor's processor (CPU) from excessive traffic. An issue that can arise is a single destination IP drops off the network due to maintenance or a hardware problem, and suddenly all traffic destined to it is being sent to the CPU. Since the rate limiter is in place, the CPU does not go high but this single destination IP can consume the entire rate limiter and not give other legitimate IP's access to the CPU. It is for this scenario that 'hardware ip glean throttle' was created. With the 'hardware ip glean throttle' configuration, a single packet is sent to the CPU for each destination IP in order to generate the ARP request. Then the software adds a /32 drop adjacency in the hardware in order to prevent additional packets to the same next-hop IP address to be forwarded to the supervisor. While this drop adjacency is added, subsequent packets are dropped yet the supervisor continues to generate ARP requests until the next-hop is resolved. The drop adjacency is installed for a short period of time, which is configurable. Once the timer expires, one packet is again sent to the CPU and the process repeats. The number of entries that is installed in this fashion is limited to 1000 by default, but is configurable to a larger number of desired. This is to limit the impact on the Routing Information Base (RIB) table size.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable glean throttling on all devices with L3 interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: IP Glean Throttling Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the config traps with the 'snmp-server enable traps config' command.
bestPracticeDescription: This command controls Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications for configuration change events, as defined in the CISCO-CONFIG-MAN-MIB.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the config traps to ensure that notifications for configuration change events are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: Configuration Management SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable "security ttl hops" under the ospf routing process. Ensure to calculate the hops according to the topology and configure accordingly.
bestPracticeDescription: The Generalized TTL Security Mechanism (GTSM) (RFC 3682) is designed to protect the control plane of a router from CPU utilization-based attacks. GTSM recognizes that the vast majority of protocol peerings are established between routers that are adjacent to or, in a worst case scenario, between loopbacks on adjacent routers. Because TTL spoofing is considered nearly impossible, a mechanism that is based on an expected TTL value can provide a simple and reasonably robust defense from infrastructure attacks that are based on forged protocol packets. GTSM is equally applicable to both TTL (IPv4) and Hop Limit (IPv6). OSPF is a link state protocol that requires networking devices to detect topological changes in the network; flood Link State Advertisement (LSA) updates to neighbors; and quickly converge on a new view of the topology. However, while receiving LSAs from neighbors, network attacks can occur, because no checks occur to distinguish whether unicast or multicast packets are originating from a neighbor that is one hop away or multiple hops away over virtual links. For virtual links, OSPF packets travel multiple hops across the network; therefore, the TTL value can be decremented several times. For virtual links, a minimum TTL value must be allowed and accepted for multiple hop packets.
bestPracticeRecommendation: This feature protects the control plane from CPU utilization based attacks. Hence it is a recommended practice to enable TTL Security for OSPF.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF TTL security not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Add a specific next-hop to the static route.

Examples:

   10.0.3.0/24 GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
becomes
   10.0.3.0/24 GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0 192.168.1.3 

  2003::100/120 GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
becomes
  2003::100/120 GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0 2002::2
bestPracticeDescription: Static routes pointing to broadcast interfaces should always reference a specific next hop to avoid excessive use of ARP/NS for each destination address.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
It is recommended that static routes pointing to broadcast interfaces always specify the next hop IP address.
Failing to adhere to this best practice will cause unnecessary traffic and CPU load to discover the L2 address of a suitable next-hop for each destination address.
bestPracticeTitle: Static Routes Configured Without Nexthop

bestPracticeCaveat: It is recommended that when AAA server is unreachable, local usernames should be used for authentication. Therefore use in the 'aaa authentication'-command local key word should be specified in the method-list.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable line console authentication using the following CLI: ! line console 0 login authentication { list-name } !
bestPracticeDescription: Access to the console should be protected with strong authentication. This requires configuration of the console to use AAA.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure RADIUS or TACACS+ authentication for use by the console. You may also configure a password for the line, though this should be the fallback method.
bestPracticeTitle: Console not protected by AAA

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure redundant AAA servers. A sample configuration would look like the following: 
aaa authentication login console group radius
radius-server host 10.10.1.1 key 0 PlIjUhYg
radius-server host 101.10.1.1 key 0 PlIjUhY23
bestPracticeDescription: The authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) servers that are leveraged in an environment should be redundant and deployed in a fault-tolerant manner. This ensures that interactive management access, such as Secure Shell Protocol (SSH) is possible if an AAA server is unavailable.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
When you design or implement a redundant AAA server solution,keep following considerations in mind: 
  1. Availability of AAA servers during potential network failures. 
  2. Geographically dispersed placement of AAA servers. 
  3. Load on individual AAA servers during steady-state and failure conditions. 
  4. Network latency between Network Access Servers and AAA servers. 
  5. AAA server database synchronization.
bestPracticeTitle: Redundant AAA server unavailable

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
A sample configuration would look like:
						tacacs-server host 1.1.1.1 port 1 key abc
						tacacs-server host 2.2.2.2 port 2 key def
						tacacs-server host 3.3.3.3 port 3 key ghi
bestPracticeDescription: The authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) servers that are leveraged in an environment should be redundant and deployed in a fault-tolerant manner. This helps ensure that interactive management access, such as Secure Shell Protocol (SSH) is possible if an AAA server is unavailable.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
When you design or implement a redundant AAA server solution, keep these considerations in mind: 
					1. Availability of AAA servers during potential network failures 
					2. Geographically dispersed placement of AAA servers. 
					3. Load on individual AAA servers during steady-state and failure conditions 
					4. Network latency between Network Access Servers and AAA servers 
					5. AAA server database synchronization.
bestPracticeTitle: Redundant AAA server unavailable

bestPracticeCaveat: If this design has been thoroughly planned for the consequences of multiple OSPF processes, then it could be a legitimate configuration.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Redesign the network to remove the multiple OSPF processes.
bestPracticeDescription: It is possible to run multiple OSPF processes on the same router, but it is not recommended since it creates multiple database instances that add extra overhead to the router.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Redesign the routing within the network to use only one OSPF process ID. For L3VPN run PE-CE and use vrf (OSPF) as an alternative under one single OSPF process-name.
bestPracticeTitle: More than one OSPF process

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable redistribution prefix limit with the following command under the routing process
router isis 
  maximum-redistributed-prefixes maximum [ level { 1 | 2 } ]
bestPracticeDescription: To limit the aggregate number of routes that may be redistributed into an IS-IS process, administrators are advised to use the maximum redistributed prefix command in the appropriate mode. This would prevent accidental redistribution errors and prevent a network meltdown.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure a maximum prefix limit for the redistributed routes when redistribution is enabled. This should be done after trending the number of prefixes which are to be received during normal working of the network.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS Redistribution limit not applied

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable ntp access control via the global command

access-group{peer | query-only | serve | serve-only} access-list-name
bestPracticeDescription: The access list-based restriction scheme allows you to grant or deny certain access privileges to an entire network, a subnet within a network, or a host within a subnet. NTP communication consists of time requests and control queries. A time request is a request for time synchronization from an NTP server. A control query is a request for configuration information from an NTP server. The access group options are scanned in the following order, from least restrictive to most restrictive: peerAllows time requests and NTP control queries and allows the system to synchronize itself to a system whose address passes the access list criteria. serveAllows time requests and NTP control queries, but does not allow the system to synchronize itself to a system whose address passes the access list criteria. serve-onlyAllows only time requests from a system whose address passes the access list criteria. query-onlyAllows only NTP control queries from a system whose address passes the access list criteria. If the source IP address matches the access lists for more than one access type, the first type is granted. If no access groups are specified, all access types are granted to all systems. If any access groups are specified, only the specified access types are granted.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enabling NTP without necessary access controls can result in denial of service attacks and enables a hacker to exploit various vulnerabilities
bestPracticeTitle: NTP configured without access restrictions

bestPracticeCaveat:
When MPP is configured, SNMP should be allowed from a specific interfaces and IPs via MPP configuration.
If you allow everything from MPP (or do not configure MPP) and then use the ACLs, you are using a feature implemented in software at application level, which may have an impact, especially in case of attempted DOS.
If you configure MPP and then still configure ACLs for the same IPs, you are configuring an unnecessarily redundant check.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure MPP to allow SNMP from specified interfaces and possibly IPs:

control-plane
 management-plane
  inband | out-of-band
   interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0 | all
    allow SNMP peer
     address ipv4 10.11.12.13
     address ipv4 10.11.13.0/24
    !

Please note that MgmtEth interfaces are dedicated out-of-band and always allowed.

Once you have configured MPP, traffic from other interfaces/IPs is not allowed by the LC NP (LPTS service) and you can and should remove redundant ACLs at the end of the SNMP commands:

snmp-server user    [ auth  encrypted  [ priv { 3des | aes  | des56 }  encrypted  ] ] [ SDROwner | SystemOwner ]   [] 
snmp-server group    [ read  ]  [ write  ]  [ notify  ]  [ context  ]   [] 
snmp-server community  [ encrypted ]   [ view  ]  [ RO | RW ]  [ SDROwner | SystemOwner ]   []
bestPracticeDescription:
When MPP is configured, SNMP access should be limited via MPP, not via ACLs, which become redundant.
MPP is implemented in hardware on the LC NP, while ACLs are processed in software at application level.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Allow SNMP from selected interfaces and possibly IPs via MPP configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: MPP configured but ACLs still used to limit SNMP access

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable BFD and use for faster convergence. 

!
bfd
!
bfd minimum-interval <>
bestPracticeDescription: A shorter hello interval gives quicker convergence, but increases bandwidth and CPU usage. It might also add to instability in the network. BFD is supported in hardware and has limited detrimental affects to processing.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enabling BFD instead of tweaking the hello interval is recommended as Bi-Directional Forwarding Detection is a lightweight re-usable protocol, which can be used by multiple processes in the router for convergence.
bestPracticeTitle: BFD not used in place of IS-IS Hello timers

bestPracticeCaveat: In certain customer network designs, syslog may be specifically limited to fewer messages. In this case, if the standard is a lower level ensure that no higher level messages of interest would be missed. Debugging level may be used periodically for troubleshooting, but is usually not left at that level all the time.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: logging trap informational
bestPracticeDescription: The 'logging trap' command controls the level of syslog messages sent to a syslog server. The default syslog messages at level 0 to level 6 are generated, but will be sent only to a remote host if the logging host command is configured. Do not use the word "trap" in the command with SNMP traps. This command strictly controls level of logging of syslog (UDP port 514) messages to a syslog server.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Use the default setting of level 6 (Informational). Settings less than 6 will result in key syslog messages not being sent to the syslog server. Logging at level 7 (Debugging) on an ongoing basis generates too many syslog.
bestPracticeTitle: Syslog level not set to informational

bestPracticeCaveat:
If HSRP is configured on the interface and use the "standby redirects" command, then ICMP redirects may be allowed or required. As a norm, ICMP redirects should be disabled.

ICMP redirects can filtered with an access-list. For example:
access-list 101 deny icmp any any redirect
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface x/x
 no ip redirects
bestPracticeDescription: By default, NX-OS devices send Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) redirect messages if the Cisco NX-OS software is forced to resend a packet through the same interface on which it was received. There are legitimate reasons to perform this action, such as HSRP, however ICMP redirects are also used in various attacks to attempt to modify network routing or launch a denial-of-service. As described in RFC 792, the redirect message advises a host to send its traffic for a particular network directly to another gateway, as this is a shorter path to the destination.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to disable ICMP redirects on each IP interface and enable only if specifically required.  Additionally, before using BFD echo mode, ICMP redirect messages should be disabled to avoid high CPU utilization. Loopback and mgmt0 interfaces are skipped for this check since redirects do not apply there.
bestPracticeTitle: ICMP redirects not disabled on an Interface

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
For Netflow BGP Data export function to operate accurately, enable the attribute download for BGP with the following command in the address-family configuration


!
bgp attribute-download
!
bestPracticeDescription: In order to get the BGP information like src AS or peer AS into the Netflow records, it must be configured unlike in classical IOS under router BGP AS. The 'bgp attribute-download' command must be configured under address-family ipv4 unicast. When BGP attribute download is enabled using the 'bgp attribute-download' command, BGP reinstalls all routes whose attributes are not currently in the RIB. Likewise, if the user disables BGP attribute download using the no form of the command, BGP reinstalls previously installed routes with a null key, and removes the attributes from the RIB. Use the 'bgp attribute-download' command to enable the Netflow BGP data export function.
bestPracticeRecommendation: When BGP attribute download is enabled, BGP downloads the attribute information for prefixes (community, extended community, and as-path) to the Routing Information Base (RIB) and Forwarding Information Base (FIB). This enables FIB to associate the prefixes with attributes and send the NetFlow statistics along with the associated attributes. Therefore, to collect the BGP Attribute information for analysis, it is mandatory to have the attribute-download turned on in BGP.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Netflow Data Export: Attribute download missing in BGP

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no ip domain-lookup
bestPracticeDescription: IP domain lookup allows DNS-based host name-to-address translation, permitting the use of a hostname instead of an IP address for router commands like telnet, ping, and traceroute. Domain lookup is enabled by default. A by-product of this command is when an unrecognized command in use or enable mode is entered into a router, the router believes that this is the host name of a device that the user is attempting to reach. Therefore, the router tries to resolve the unrecognized command into an IP address by doing an IP domain lookup. If no specific domain server has been configured on the router, the router will issue a broadcast for the command to be translated into an IP address. It can take several seconds for the router prompt to return while the router waits for a response to its Domain Name System (DNS) broadcast.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Disable DNS-based host name-to-address translation by entering the "no ip domain-lookup".
bestPracticeTitle: Incorrectly Entered Commands Generate DNS Lookup

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
mpls ldp
session protection
bestPracticeDescription: MPLS LDP Session Protection. This feature provides faster label distribution protocol convergence when a link recovers following an outage. It protects a label distribution protocol (LDP) session between directly connected neighbors or a LDP session established for a traffic engineering (TE) tunnel.
bestPracticeRecommendation: When a link comes up, IP converges earlier and much faster than MPLS LDP and may result in MPLS traffic loss until MPLS convergence. If a link flaps, the LDP session will also flap due to loss of link discovery. LDP session protection minimizes traffic loss, provides faster convergence, and protects existing LDP (link) sessions.
bestPracticeTitle: MPLS LDP Session Protection Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure PPPoE session throttle in the pppoe bba-group block using the following command:

conf t
pppoe bba-group bba1
 sessions circuit-id throttle 1000 50 25
 sessions mac-iwf access-interface throttle 5000 100 50
bestPracticeDescription: The PPPoE Session Throttle support on BNG limits the number of PPPoE session requests coming to BNG within a specified period of time. This, in turn, ensures that the session establishment of other client requests coming to the BNG server is not impacted. This offers configuration flexibility in the BNG router by throttling the number of session requests based on circuit-id, mac-address, remote-id, combination of circuit and remote-id, Vlan tags and so on.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends configuring the PPPoE Throttle as it limits the number of PPPoE session requests coming to BNG within a specified period of time. This, in turn ensures that the session establishment of other client requests are not impacted and also prevents DOS attacks. The session limit can be enforced based on linecard, interface, peer mac, circuit-id, remote-id, vlan tags and so on.
bestPracticeTitle: PPPoE Session throttle not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure BFD under VRRP control block.

configure 
  router vrrp 
   interface type interface-path-id 
     address-family {ipv4 | ipv6}
       vrrp vrid version { 2 | 3 }bfd fast-detect peer {ipv4 | ipv6} address
bestPracticeDescription: BFD provides failure detection in less than one second. Enabling BFD for VRRP between routers detect link failure and facilitate fast failover times without excessive control packet overhead.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use BFD in VRRP for failure detection, as it detects failure much faster and also facilitate fast failover times without excessive control packet overhead.
bestPracticeTitle: BFD not Used by VRRP for Failure Detection

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the following:
interface Bundle-ether 
   bundle minimum-active bandwidth 

A sample configuration is given below:
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:Router# config
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:Router(config)# interface Bundle-Ether 3
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:Router(config-if)# ipv4 address 1.2.3.4/24
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:Router(config-if)# bundle minimum-active bandwidth 620000
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:Router(config-if)# bundle minimum-active links 1
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:Router(config-if)# exit
bestPracticeDescription: Bundled interfaces increase bandwidth availability, because traffic is forwarded over all available members of the bundle. Therefore, traffic can flow on the available links if one of the links within a bundle fails. Minimum active bandwidth configuration would avoid a potential congested link situation due to multiple failures in a bundle.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
It is recommended to define the minimum amount of bandwidth required in kbps, using the command ?bundle minimum-active bandwidth? under bundle interface configuration.
The default setting is 1 and ranges to a number that is equal to the combined bandwidths of 8 TenGigabitEthernet interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: Minimum-active bandwidth required in a bundle

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: ip domain-name
bestPracticeDescription: The 'ip domain-name' command is used to define a default domain name that the Cisco NX-OS software uses to complete unqualified hostnames (names without a dotted-decimal domain name). This domain name is also appended to the hostname to make up the sysname returned in Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) and is part of the neighbor device ID in Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) neighbor output.
bestPracticeRecommendation: A domain name should be configured that is relevant to the network environment.
bestPracticeTitle: Domain Name Not Configured

bestPracticeCaveat:
1. Keychain to be defined for the feature to work.
2. Ensure all devices in the RIP topology are using the same authentication mode and passwords.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Below example shows how to apply an authentication keychain on a RIP non-default interface:
!
router rip
 vrf rip_keychain_vrf
  interface POS0/6/0/0
   authentication keychain key1 mode md5
  !
 !
!
bestPracticeDescription: Authentication using Keychain in Cisco IOS XR Routing Information Protocol (RIP) provides mechanism to authenticate all RIP protocol traffic on RIP interface, based Keychain authentication. This mechanism uses the Cisco IOS XR security Keychain infrastructure to store and retrieve secret keys and use it to authenticate in-bound and out-going traffic on per-interface basis. Keychain management is a common method of authentication to configure shared secrets on all entities that exchange secrets such as keys, before establishing trust with each other. Routing protocols and network management applications on Cisco IOS XR software often use authentication to enhance security while communicating with peers.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends RIP authentication for all incoming and outgoing packets as part of security recommendation of the feature.
bestPracticeTitle: RIP Authentication Keychain Not Configured

bestPracticeCaveat: If logging output is required for troubleshooting purposes,you should enable it only temporarily,to monitor for vty sessions,and avoid using it on the console. Be sure to disable logging to monitor sessions after troubleshooting is completed.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Remove the 'logging monitoring' command using 'no logging monitor' from the global configuration mode
bestPracticeDescription: Logging Monitor must be disabled.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
With Cisco NX-OS you can send log messages to monitor sessions. However,doing so can elevate the CPU load of a Cisco NX-OS device and therefore it is not recommended. Furthermore,you are advised to send logging information to the local log buffer or the local log file,which can be viewed using the 'show logging' command.
If logging output is required for troubleshooting purposes you must enable it only temporarily to monitor for vty sessions and avoid using it on the console. Ensure to disable logging to monitor sessions after troubleshooting is completed.
bestPracticeTitle: Logging Monitor Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure BFD under HSRP control block.

configure 
  router hsrp 
     interface type interface-path-id 
        address-family ipv4 
           hsrp [group number] version version-no bfd fast-detect [peer ipv4 ipv4-address interface-type interface-path-id]
bestPracticeDescription: BFD provides failure detection in less than one second. Enabling BFD for HSRP between routers detects link failure and facilitates fast failover times without excessive control packet overhead.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use BFD in HSRP for failure detection as it  detects failure much faster and also facilitates fast failover times without excessive control packet overhead.
bestPracticeTitle: BFD Not Used for HSRP Failure Detection

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure PPPoE session limit in the pppoe bba-group block.
bestPracticeDescription: The PPPoE Session Limit support limits the number of PPPoE sessions that can be created on a BNG router. As a result, it reduces excessive memory usage by the BNG router for virtual access.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends configuring session limit, as it limits the number of PPPoE sessions that can be created, which in turn prevents wastage of memory on the BNG. The session limit can be enforced based on line card, interface, peer mac, circuit-id, remote-id, vlan tags and so on.
bestPracticeTitle: PPPoE Session Limit Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
line console
  stopbits 1
bestPracticeDescription: Communication protocols provided by devices such as terminals and modems often require a specific stop-bit setting. IOS XR line console's default configuration for stopbits is 2 which is different from other Cisco devices.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Since users users usually configure terminal applications with stopbits 1 it may cause them to receive unexpected response for any character input from line console access.  It is recommend that the stopbits should be changed to 1 to make it easier for the users to manage the device.
bestPracticeTitle: Line Console with Default Stopbit

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Dc1-N7K1# config t
Enter configuration commands,one per line.  End with CNTL/Z.
Dc1-N7K1(config)# feature netflow
Dc1-N7K1(config)# flow timeout active 60
Dc1-N7K1(config)#
bestPracticeDescription: It is good to have an optimal netflow active aging timeout value configured. Active aging specifies the period of time in which a flow should be removed from the software flow cache after the flow is created. Generally, this parameter is used to periodically notify external collection devices about active flows. The default value is 1800sec(30min) which can be reduced to 60 sec(1min). This will allow for more accurate statistics while low impact on CPU. It is recommended to monitor the CPU and Netflow creation failures to detect any issues.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to have optimal netflow active aging time out configured. The default value used is 1800 sec. This can be optimized to 60 seconds with low impact to CPU. This provides more accurate netflow statistics.
bestPracticeTitle: NetFlow Active Aging Timeout Not configured Optimally

bestPracticeCaveat: If you configure NTP in a VRF, ensure that the NTP server and peers can reach each other through the configured VRFs.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Dc1-N7K1#config t
Dc1-N7K1(config)#feature ntp
Dc1-N7K1(config)#ntp server 1.1.1.1 use-vrf management
Dc1-N7K1(config)#ntp server 2.2.2.2 use-vrf management
bestPracticeDescription: Multiple NTP servers should be configured for redundancy.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to have a redundant NTP server configured. VRF instance must be configured to use the management VRF instance for out-of-band connectivity.
bestPracticeTitle: No redundant NTP server

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Use SSH for management purposes.
bestPracticeDescription: Telnet sends the password in clear text format. Additionally, an unencrypted session is subject to session hijacking.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use Secure Shell Protocol (SSH) or IP Security (IPSec) in order to secure the administrative sessions.
bestPracticeTitle: Telnet server is enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Use max-lsa command under ospf- configuration to specify the number of LSAs.
max-lsa X Y warning-only
X Maximum number of non self-generated LSAs this process can receive<1-4294967294>
Y Threshold value (%) at which to generate a warning msg <0-100>

Eg:
!
router ospf 100
 router-id 209.165.201.0
 network non-broadcast
 max-lsa 12000 75 warning-only
 area 0
 !
!
bestPracticeDescription: To limit the number of nonself-generated link-state advertisements (LSAs) that an Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) routing process can keep in the OSPF link-state database (LSDB), use the max-lsa command in router configuration mode. To remove the limit of non self-generated LSAs that an OSPF routing process can keep in the OSPF LSDB, use the no form of this command. The percentage of the maximum LSA number, as specified by the maximum-number argument, at which a warning message is logged. The default is 75 percent. With the notification, we can immediately find the cause of the increasing OSPF LSA, preserving RP CPU and memory that might be consumed by the huge amount of LSA.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use the 'max-lsa' command in router configuration mode to limit the number of non-self-generated link-state advertisements (LSAs) that an Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) routing process can keep in the OSPF link-state database (LSDB).
bestPracticeTitle: Limit the OSPF LSA Generation

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers with global networks will use a single timezone for network devices (for example, Coordinated Universal Time [GMT]) and may not configure a timezone system.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: clock timezone EST -5 0
bestPracticeDescription: Nexus switches, by default, utilize Universal Coordinated Time (UTC), which is the same as Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). This can be configured to a local timezone utilizing the "clock timezone" command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is common for customers to set a timezone for devices in the same region to easily correlate network events to the local time of day.
bestPracticeTitle: Clock enabled without timezone

bestPracticeCaveat:
Summertime is not necessary in a couple of situations.
1)  Customer has standardized on Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) for their devices to allow for consistent time on all devices.
2)  Some countries/timezones do not follow any Daylight Savings Time schedule.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
clock summer-time          

Current US standard:
