bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
This example shows how to enable an error-disabled recovery from storm control error disabled state:
! 
errdisable recovery cause storm-control
!

This example shows how to enable error disable recovery time interval to 100 seconds:
!
errdisable recovery interval 100
!
bestPracticeDescription: When ingress traffic exceeds the traffic storm control level that is configured on a port, the traffic storm control puts the port into the error-disabled state. To re-enable this port, you can use either the 'shutdown' and 'no shutdown' options on the configured interface, or the error-disable detection and recovery feature. You are recommended to use the 'errdisable recovery cause storm-control' command for error-disable detection and recovery along with the 'errdisable recovery interval' command for defining the recovery interval. To configure the recovery time interval to bring the interface out of the error-disabled (err-disabled) state, use the 'errdisable recovery interval' command. To revert to the defaults, use the 'no' form of this command. Error disable recovery time interval range is from 30 to 65535 seconds. We can configure traffic storm control to perform the following optional corrective actions when traffic exceeds the configured level:1) Shut down . 2) Trap.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use the errdisable recovery cause storm-control command for error-disable detection and recovery along with the errdisable recovery interval command for defining the recovery interval. The interval can range between 30 and 65535 seconds.
bestPracticeTitle: Storm Control Error Disabled Recovery Not Enabled

bestPracticeDescription:
Best Practice procedure for defining static routes on Nexus devices has to be changed to include Next-Hop and Interface as parameters as in the example below:
 
ip route 10.1.0.0 255.255.255.0 Serial 3/3 192.168.20.1
 
There are scenarios in which, when specifying only Next-Hop as parameter for a static route with the next-hop interface down, then the router will do recursive look for the next-hop not for the subnet.
It will use a more specific route or it will use a default route, if present, which could lead to traffic being blackholed.
 
Specifying a numerical nexthop on a directly connected interface prevents the router from performing ARP on each destination address.
 
However, if the interface with the nexthop goes down and the numerical nexthop is reachable through a recursive route, you should specify both the nexthop IP address and the interface through which the nexthop should be found.
 
Below are the examples of reconfiguring a static route for N7k:
 
vrf context XYZ
ip route 83.147.190.84/32 Ethernet1/28.922 172.17.124.89
ip route 83.147.190.86/32 Ethernet1/28.922 172.17.124.89
ip route 204.151.96.149/32 Ethernet1/28.922 172.17.124.89
no ip route 83.147.190.84/32 172.17.124.89
no ip route 83.147.190.86/32 172.17.124.89
no ip route 204.151.96.149/32 172.17.124.89
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to change the best Practice procedure for defining static routes on Nexus devices to include Next-Hop and Interface as parameters.
bestPracticeTitle: Static Routes Configured Without Nexthop

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration is given below :
!
feature vpc
vpc domain 100
delay restore <1-3600 sec>
!

As a general recommendation, configure the delay restore value to 150 seconds for Layer 3 devices and 90 seconds for devices with 16 or more FEX modules.
bestPracticeDescription: VPC Delay Restore feature delays VPCs leg bringup on the recovering VPC peer device. VPC Delay Restore allows for Layer 3 routing protocols to converge and all FEX to come online before allowing any traffic on VPC leg. The result is a more graceful restoration and zero packet loss during the recovery phase (traffic still get diverted on the alive VPC peer device). This feature is enabled by default with a VPC restoration default timer of 30 seconds. The timer should be tuned according to a specific Layer 3 convergence baseline and time for FEX to come online.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to Configure a 'delay restore' value underneath the VPC domain which is higher than the convergence time of the routing protocol.
bestPracticeTitle: VPC Delay Restore Not Configured

bestPracticeCaveat:
Temporary access via Telnet might be permissible on production systems which are migrating to SSH.
Also, it might just be acceptable, depending on security policy, to excuse Telnet access when outband management is completely secured from any malicious user.
Finally, lab systems which are accessible only to specific personnel might be excusable for Telnet access.
In all cases, SSHv2 should be the preferred longer term access solution.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Remove telnet from the MPP configuration and delete any telnet server configuration, after first configuring and testing SSHv2. Make sure that SSHv2 is configured and tested fully first, before disallowing telnet from the MPP configuration and then disabling the telnet server configuration; this is to prevent the risk of being locked out from the device in a production network. In case of complete inband lock out and without any recourse to outband methods, console access would be the only alternative to access the network element. Use commit confirmed in case of any doubt.
bestPracticeDescription: Telnet is not secure because it sends everything in clear text and can be used by a malicious user to gain unauthorised access. Telnet should not be allowed in Management Plane Protection (MPP) configuration. MPP filters management plane traffic in hardware (LPTS), so MPP offers strong protection in preventing high volume attacks against those protocols which are not allowed, as well as rate-limiting those management plane protocols which are allowed. Telnet should be replaced by SSH. Secure Shell (SSH) is a network protocol that supports encrypted data exchange between two devices. SSH supports authentication with public-key cryptography. SSHv2 is recommended to be implemented. Before SSHv2 can first be used on a IOS-XR node, an RSA keypair needs to be created using the EXEC command ?crypto key generate rsa?. After executing this command, the system prompts for the size of the modulus, the recommended minimum modulus size is 1024 bits (default).
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to Use SSHv2 to encrypt management session traffic, which prevents access of the data being transmitted by a malicious user.
bestPracticeTitle: Telnet Allowed By Management Plane Protection

bestPracticeCaveat: This command does not apply to directly connected routes. Use a route map to set the default metric for directly connected routes.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Define the metric that should be used when redistributing into OSPF with the default-metric keyword.
bestPracticeDescription: When configuring redistribution into OSPF, always define the metric that OSPF should use with the default-metric keywords. Each protocol uses different metrics so it is important to define the cost parameter that OSPF should use.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
When configuring redistribution into OSPF,always define the metric that OSPF should use with the default-metric keywords. 
router ospf 201
!
redistribute bgp route-map FilterExternalBGP
!
default-metric 25
bestPracticeTitle: No Redistribution Metrics Defined for OSPF

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the description using the below commands:

router(config)# nv satellite 100
router(config-nV)# type asr9000v
router(config-satellite)# description Mathilda Sunnyvale
bestPracticeDescription: Configuring descriptions for satellite cards on ASR9k is recommended, especially when installing multiple satellite cards per box. It is easier to understand which satellite is being worked upon based on description over just the satellite number.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure satellite description.
bestPracticeTitle: nV Satellite Description Not Configured

bestPracticeCaveat: This rule is only for subscriber deployments.  Check for "dynamic-template" or "subscriber" in global mode.   If one of them is configured, then it is subscriber deployment.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface Bundle-Ether100.10  (Interface can be a bundle or normal ethernet interface)
 arp learning disable
!
bestPracticeDescription: In an IPoE subscriber session, subscribers run IPv4 or IPv6 on the CPE device and connect to the BNG through a Layer-2 aggregation or Layer-3 routed network. IP subscriber sessions that connect through a Layer-2 aggregation network are called L2-connected and sessions that connect through routed access network are called L3-connected or routed subscriber sessions. IPoE subscriber sessions are always terminated on BNG and then routed into the service provider network. IPoE relies on DHCP to assign IP address. The process of provisioning an IPoE session involves: Enabling the processing of IPv4 or IPv6 protocol on an access interface. Creating dynamic template that contains the settings for the IPoE sessions. Creating policy-map to activate dynamic template. Enabling IPoE subscriber creation on access interface by activating service-policy. The service-policy will apply the policy-map on the access interface.
bestPracticeRecommendation: For subscriber deployments, while enabling the processing of IPv4 or IPv6 protocol on an access interface, it is recommended that Dynamic ARP learning be disabled in the access-interface using the arp learning disable command in the access-interface configuration mode.
bestPracticeTitle: Dynamic ARP Learning enabled in Subscriber Deployment

bestPracticeCaveat:
If the 'tunnel protection' keyword is included in the tunnel interface configuration, then keepalives should not be used.

GRE tunnel keepalive is not supported with tunnel key. If the configuration for the GRE keepalives and tunnel key are present at the same time, the tunnel is brought down and a warning message indicating that tunnel keepalive is not supported when tunnel key is configured is displayed.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: For a GRE tunnel, Under the interface tunnel-ip X where X is the tunnel interface number, enter the command: 'keepalive Y Z' where Y is the keepalive period, and Z is the number of keepalive retries.
bestPracticeDescription: Normally, a generic routing encapsulation (GRE) tunnel interface comes up as soon as it is configured, and it stays up as long as there is a valid tunnel source address or interface that is up. The tunnel destination IP address must also be routable. This is true even if the other side of the tunnel has not been configured. This means that a static route or policy-based routing (PBR) forwarding of packets via the GRE tunnel interface remains in effect even though the GRE tunnel packets do not reach the other end of the tunnel. Before GRE keepalives were implemented, there were only three reasons for a GRE tunnel to shut down: 1. There is no route to the tunnel destination address. 2. The interface that anchors the tunnel source is down. 3. The route to the tunnel destination address is through the tunnel itself. These three rules (missing route, interface down, and misrouted tunnel destination) are problems local to the router at the tunnel endpoints and do not cover problems in the intervening network. For example, these rules do not cover the case in which the GRE tunneled packets are successfully forwarded but are lost before they reach the other end of the tunnel. This causes data packets that go through the GRE tunnel to be "black holed", even though an alternate route that uses PBR or a floating static route via another interface is potentially available. Keepalives on the GRE tunnel interface are used to solve this issue in the same way as keepalives are used on physical interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Keepalives on the GRE tunnel interface are used to solve this issue in the same way as keepalives are used on physical interfaces. If not, GRE can result in an up on one side even though down on the other. To avoid this situation, it is recommended to always configure a keepalive under the tunnel.
bestPracticeTitle: Keepalive not set on GRE Tunnel

bestPracticeCaveat: Syslog is used for operational management of these issues,instead of SNMP traps.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the coldStart traps with the  'snmp-server enable traps snmp coldstart' command  in global configuration mode.
bestPracticeDescription: To enable the RFC 1157 Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications, use the 'snmp-server enable traps snmp' command in global configuration mode. SNMP notifications can be sent as traps or inform requests. This command enables both traps and inform requests for the specified notification types. A coldStart trap signifies that the SNMP entity, supporting a notification originator application, is reinitializing itself and that its configuration may have been altered.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the coldstart SNMP traps to ensure that notifications for coldstart events are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: ColdStart SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
as-path-set 
no ios-regex \S+
bestPracticeDescription: An AS path set comprises operations for matching an AS path attribute. The matching operations are: dfa-regex: - DFA style regular expression - ios-regex: Traditional IOS style regular expression - length: Length of BGP AS-path - neighbor-is: BGP AS-path neighbor is - originates-from: BGP AS-path originates-from - passes-through: BGP AS-path passes-through - unique-length: Length of BGP AS-path ignoring duplicates Regular expression matching is CPU intensive. The policy performance can be substantially improved by either collapsing the regular expression patterns together to reduce the total number of regular expression invocations or by using equivalent native as-path match operations such as ?as-path neighbor-is?, ?as-path originates-from? or ?as-path passes-through?.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to reduce the total number of regular expression invocations by using equivalent native as-path match operations such as ?as-path neighbor-is?, ?as-path originates-from? or ?as-path passes-through?.
bestPracticeTitle: Regular Expression used under AS Path Set

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
ipv6 access-list myIPv6acl
  10 permit ipv6 2001:0DB8:0:4::/64 any

line vty
  ipv6 access-class myIPv6acl in
bestPracticeDescription:
When IPv6 protocol is enabled on a device, it is necessary to protect access to the VTY ports. An IPv6 access-class should be applied to the VTY port to increase security by restricting SSH and Telnet access to specific source and destination IPv6 addresses.


An IPv6 access-class configured on the VTY port is applicable when using an in-band or out-of-band management strategy. An IPv6 access-class is configured per traffic direction, "in" applies to inbound sessions and "out" applies to outbound.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Create and enable 'ipv6 access-class in' to protect access to VTY lines.
bestPracticeTitle: VTY lines not protected with an access list when IPv6 enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Do not configure traffic storm control on interfaces that are members of a port-channel interface. Configuring traffic storm control on interfaces that are configured as members of a port channel puts the ports into a suspended state.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-
!
interface Ethernet1/1
 storm-control broadcast level 40
!
bestPracticeDescription: A traffic storm occurs when packets flood the LAN, creating excessive traffic and degrading network performance. You can use the traffic storm control feature to prevent disruptions on Layer 2 ports by a broadcast traffic storm on physical interfaces. Traffic storm control (also called traffic suppression) allows you to monitor the levels of the incoming broadcast, multicast, and unicast traffic over a 10-millisecond interval. During this interval, the traffic level, which is a percentage of the total available bandwidth of the port, is compared with the traffic storm control level that you configured. When the ingress traffic reaches the traffic storm control level that is configured on the port, traffic storm control drops the traffic until the interval ends. Note: Specify the level as a percentage of the total interface bandwidth: The level can be from 0 to 100. The optional fraction of a level can be from 0 to 99. 100 percent means no traffic storm control. 0 percent suppresses all traffic.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure storm-control broadcast at interface level.
bestPracticeTitle: Storm Control Broadcast Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: In the global configuration mode,enter 'snmp-server enable traps aaa' command to enable AAA traps.
bestPracticeDescription:
If NX-OS device has AAA configured and running and SNMP Traps enabled then flag an exception if globally you don't find:  

snmp-server enable traps aaa
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are helpful to log the event related to AAA process and can be used for troubleshooting when required.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: LACP does not enforce same interval values on both ends.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
interface gigabitethernet 0/3/0/1
    no bundle id 2 mode active
    no lacp period short 
    commit
!
bestPracticeDescription: To enable a short period time interval for the transmission and reception of Link Aggregation Control Protocol (LACP) packets, use the lacp period short command in interface configuration mode. To return to the default short period, use the no form of this command. Always configure the transmit interval at both ends of the connection before you configure the receive interval at either end of the connection. Failure to configure the transmit interval at both ends first results in route flapping (a route going up and down continuously). When you remove a custom LACP short period, you must do it in reverse order. You must remove the receive intervals first and then the transmit intervals. Traditional LACP heartbeat intervals: Long (default): 30 sec (90 sec failure detection) Short (optional): 1 sec (3 sec failure detection) Heartbeats are sent from the supervisor, so there is no SSO/ISSU guarantee with aggressive timers.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use normal LACP period interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: Normal LACP Interval Not Used

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable 'ip dhcp relay information option'.

If DHCP snooping is in use,enable 'ip dhcp snooping information option'.
bestPracticeDescription:
The relay information option commands add a special option called 'Option
 82', or the relay information option, to the relayed Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) packet when 
the CMTS relays DHCP packets. 'Option 82' is populated with a sub-option, 
the Agent Circuit ID, which references the physical interface on the 
CMTS where the DHCP request was heard. In addition to this, another 
sub-option, the Agent Remote ID, is populated with the 6-byte MAC address
 of the cable modem from which the DHCP request was received or through 
which the DHCP request was passed. So, for example, if a PC with MAC 
address 99:88:77:66:55:44 that is behind cable modem aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff 
sends a DHCP request, the CMTS will forward the DHCP request setting the
 Agent Remote ID sub-option of 'Option 82' to the MAC address of the Cable 
Modem, aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff. By having the Relay Information Option 
included within the DHCP request from a customer premises equipment 
(CPE) device, the DHCP server is able to store information about which 
CPE belongs behind which cable modems. This becomes especially useful 
when 'cable source-verify dhcp' is configured on the CMTS, as the DHCP 
server is able to reliably inform the CMTS about not only what MAC 
address a particular client should have, but also which cable modem to 
which the particular client is meant to be connected.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable 'ip dhcp relay information option' command. If DHCP snooping is in use,Cisco recommends to enable 'ip dhcp snooping information option' command so that it acts as a firewall between untrusted hosts and trusted DHCP servers.
bestPracticeTitle: IP DHCP Relay Information Option Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
VTP worked only in transparent mode in the Cisco Nexus 9000 Series devices, allowing you to extend a VTP domain across the device.

Switches operating in VTP transparent mode are safe from propagated accidental VLAN database changes, but all VLAN configuration must be done individually on each switch.

Some customers prefer the ease of management of VTP client/server mode, despite some considerations that the best practice notes. The recommendation is to have two server mode switches in each domain for redundancy, typically the two distribution-layer switches. Set the rest of the switches in the domain to client mode.

When you implement client/server mode with the use of VTPv2, remember that a higher revision number is always accepted in the same VTP domain. If a switch that is configured in either VTP client or server mode is introduced into the VTP domain and has a higher revision number than the VTP servers that exists, this overwrites the VLAN database within the VTP domain. 

If the configuration change is unintentional and VLANs are deleted, this overwrite can cause a major outage in the network. In order to ensure that client or server switches always have a configuration revision number that is lower than that of the server, change the client VTP domain name to something other than the standard name, and then revert back to the standard. This action sets the configuration revision on the client to 0.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
If VTP is not required, disable the feature using 'no feature vtp' rather than using 'vtp mode off'

If VTP is required, configure VTP into transparent mode by entering 'vtp mode transparent'
bestPracticeDescription: This rule checks if VTP is enabled on the switch and recommends disabling it, if it is not required. The rule checks if using the recommended transparent mode is required.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable VTP, as the VLANs can be pruned from trunks down to switches that do not have ports in the VLAN. This results in frame flooding to be more bandwidth-efficient.
bestPracticeTitle: VTP Not Configured In Transparent Mode

bestPracticeCaveat: This feature is not available for DS Lite.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Use command 'refresh-direction Outbound' in the CGN configuration for NAT44:
service cgn 
 service-type nat44 
  refresh-direction Outbound
Use command 'refresh-direction Outbound' in the CGN configuration for NAT64:
service cgn 
 service-type nat64 stateful 
  refresh-direction Outbound
 !
bestPracticeDescription: Timers for the connection are refreshed when packets are translated in the Inside-to-Outside (i2o) or the Outside-to-Inside (o2i) direction. This represents potential for an external attacker to send regularly one packet for every DB entry and eventually create a resource depletion from the o2i direction. To change this default behaviour and protect against this happening, the timer refresh is recommended to only take into consideration i2o packets. This feature is not available for DS Lite.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure timer refresh to only take into consideration Inside-to-Outside (i2o) packets.
bestPracticeTitle: Refresh Direction Outbound Not Enabled For NAT

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: configure "feature password encryption aes"
bestPracticeDescription: Enable strong, reversible 128-bit Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) password encryption, also known as type-6 encryption. To start using type-6 encryption, you must enable the AES password encryption feature and configure a master encryption key, which is used to encrypt and decrypt passwords. After you enable AES password encryption and configure a master key, all existing and newly created clear-text passwords for supported applications (currently RADIUS and TACACS+) are stored in type-6 encrypted format, unless you disable type-6 password encryption. Configure Cisco NX-OS to convert all existing weakly encrypted passwords to type-6 encrypted passwords.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure password encryption.
bestPracticeTitle: Password Encryption Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure the following: diagnostic eem action conservative
bestPracticeDescription: Beginning in 6.2(8), a port failing loopback diagnostic testing can be put into an error-disabled state. It is the best practice to disable these ports until the underlying issue causing the diagnostic failure is addressed.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable the conservative diagnostic action, to detect the failures on the loopback tests.
bestPracticeTitle: Conservative Action With Failed Loopback Diagnostic Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: The upstream DHCP server or DHCP relay interface must be configured to accept this type of packet using the relay information option allow-untrusted configuration. This configuration prevents the server or relay from dropping the DHCP message.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
dhcp ipv4 
 profile client relay 
  relay information option
bestPracticeDescription: The relay information option commands add a special option called Option 82, or the relay information option, to the relayed DHCP packet when the router relays DHCP packets. Option 82 is populated with a suboption. The relay information option command automatically adds the circuit identifier suboption and the remote ID sub-option to the DHCP relay agent information option.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) IPv4 relay or DHCP snooping Relay to insert relay agent information option in forwarded BOOTREQUEST messages to a DHCP server, use the relay information option command in DHCP IPv4 relay profile relay configuration or DHCP IPv4 profile snoop submode.
bestPracticeTitle: IP DHCP Relay Information Option Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router ospf 
    passive enable
    area 0
        interface TenGigE
            passive disable
bestPracticeDescription: Passive interfaces advertise the connected prefixes but do not form adjacencies. It is desirable to only disable passive on interfaces where an OSPF adjacency needs to form.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to globally configure passive under the OSPF process and then disable it only on interfaces where OSPF adjacencies need to form.
bestPracticeTitle: Passive Enable Not Configured Under The OSPF Process

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Router(config)#{ ipv4 | ipv6 } access-list log-update threshold 300
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco IOS XR software can provide logging messages about packets permitted or denied by a standard IP access list. That is, any packet that matches the access list causes an informational logging message about the packet to be sent to the console. The level of messages logged to the console is controlled by the 'logging console' command in global configuration mode. The first packet that triggers the access list causes an immediate logging message, and subsequent packets are collected over 5-minute intervals before they are displayed or logged. The logging message includes the access list number, whether the packet was permitted or denied, the source IP address of the packet, and the number of packets from that source permitted or denied in the prior 5-minute interval. However, you can use the '{ ipv4 | ipv6 } access-list log-update threshold' command to set the number of packets that, when they match an access list (and are permitted or denied), cause the system to generate a log message. You might do this to receive log messages more frequently than at 5-minute intervals. If you set the update-number argument to 1, a log message is sent right away, rather than caching it; every packet that matches an access list causes a log message.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends not to set the update number argument to 1 as the volume of the log messages can overwhelm the system.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv4 Access List Log Update Threshold Set To 1

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: In the global configuration mode,enter 'snmp-server enable traps ospf' command to enable OSPF traps.
bestPracticeDescription:
SNMP traps for the OSPF enables an agent to notify the management station of significant events related to OSPF by way of an unsolicited SNMP message.


Flag an exception, if:
1. NX-OS device has feature 'OSPF' enabled and running, and
2. SNMP Traps are enabled, and
3. 'snmp-server enable traps ospf' is not configured globally.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable OSPF SNMP Traps as OSPF traps are helpful to log the event related to OSPF process and can be used for troubleshooting when required.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Conditions:
If "router ospf " and "mpls ldp" (in global config mode) present, then:

Action:
Configure the following lines:
router ospf 
    mpls ldp auto-config
commit
bestPracticeDescription: By default, "MPLS LDP auto-config" command in the router OSPF configuration mode is disabled. When a network operator provisions a new interface with OSPF enabled, the operator should manually enable MPLS LDP on the interface to allow labelled forwarding on the interface, assuming LDP is the preferred protocol to distribute MPLS label. If LDP is not enabled on the interface, MPLS VPN traffic going through the interface will be dropped, which creates a service outage.
bestPracticeRecommendation: If OSPF is the IGP, and MPLS LDP (instead of RSVP-TE, or segment routing) is the label distribution protocol for a MPLS network, configure "MPLS LDP auto-config" in the router OSPF configuration mode. The "MPLS LDP auto-config" command automatically enables MPLS LDP on all interfaces with OSPF enabled, and distributes MPLS labels with the router's OSPF neighbors. It is one of the MPLS measures to prevent MPLS traffic outage. The command can be configured in OSPF router configuration mode, area configuration mode, or interface configuration mode. It is recommended to configure in the router configuration mode to protect all the OSPF areas and interfaces. Choose the appropriate configuration modes to suit your network environment.
bestPracticeTitle: LDP auto-config In OSPFv2 Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: In the global configuration mode,enter 'snmp-server enable traps vtp' command to enable system traps.
bestPracticeDescription:
SNMP traps for VTP enables an agent to notify the management station of significant events related to VTP by way of an unsolicited SNMP message.


Flag an exception if:
- The NX-OS device has VTP configured and running, and
- SNMP Traps are enabled, and
- 'snmp-server enable traps vtp' command is not configured globally.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable SNMP traps as these are helpful to log the event related to VTP process and can be used for troubleshooting when required.
bestPracticeTitle: VTP SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
As a best practice, configure "set-overload-bit on-startup " under "router isis" in the router config mode
conf t
router isis
set-overload-bit on-startup
bestPracticeDescription: Setting the overload bit provides the router adequate time to build its BGP and CEF tables prior to the router being used as a transit node.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to set the overload bit on-startup to avoid any potential routing loop issues.
bestPracticeTitle: Overload bit on startup Not set under IS-IS

bestPracticeCaveat: CDP has legitimate use for troubleshooting network issues; however, on interfaces directly connected to network edges or devices not in the same administrative domain, CDP should be disabled to prevent data mining.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
'no cdp' (global)
or 
'no cdp' (interface)
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) is media- and protocol-independent and runs on all Cisco-manufactured equipments including routers, bridges, access servers, and switches. CDP is useful for troubleshooting and building topology information by network management system (NMS) platforms. Due to the importance of the information obtained through CDP, care should be exercised in deploying this protocol, especially at the network edge. In IOS-XR, CDP is not enabled globally by default and will appear in a 'show run'. If CDP is required globally on a device, it can be disabled on a per-interface basis using the 'no cdp' command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: CDP should be disabled on all interfaces facing a different administrative domain. Your configuration can also carry an explicit 'no cdp' if CDP is not globally required or permitted on the device.
bestPracticeTitle: CDP is enabled globally and active on all interfaces.

bestPracticeCaveat: No known caveat.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Condition: If "router ospf " exists, search for the "timers throttle spf" command under the router ospf config section. If the "timers throttle spf" command exists, check the "timers throttle spf spf-start spf-hold spf-max-wait" command timer values. 

If: 
spf-start >50ms, or if: spf-hold <100ms, or spf-hold  >200ms, of if: spf-max-wait<1000ms, or spf-max-wait >5000ms, Corrective action: remove the "timers throttle spf" command completely, and return to the default timer values. or if spf-start < 50m, change the config to:

timers throttle spf  200 5000
bestPracticeDescription: Modern networks have much less unstable links and nodes than the networks, 10 - 20 years ago. The IOS XR default OSPF 'timers throttle spf' values are considered well-tuned and suitable for most of OSPF networks from network convergence, stability and scalability perspective. Unless a network has very specific conditions, or requirement, it is recommended not to change the OSPF 'timers throttle spf' configuration. In case a network operator would like to tune the IOS XR OSPF 'timers throttle spf' timers due to specific network conditions or requirement, please use the following guideline: timers throttle spf spf-start spf-hold spf-max-wait The spf-start default value is 50ms. After receiving the first LSA, OSPF will run the SPF after this timer expires. If a network is usually very stable, to optimize for the most likely events (isolated link failure), 0ms is an acceptable value. Network operator can reduce it to 0 to reduce the convergence time, but at the expense of some potential drawbacks: Upon node (router) or SRLG (shared risk link group) failure, multiple LSAs are changed. The SPF triggered by the receipt of the first changed LSA may miss part of the new topological information and hence may result in partially incorrect routes. There are ways to mitigate the drawbacks: First, confirm that isolated link failures represent the vast majority of events on the network. Second, leverage the fact that most likely the first received LSA holds enough information to generate correct routes. Third, if a node or SRLG failure occurs and the first LSA leads to incorrect routes, then anyway, the second SPF is triggered 200 msec later and will correct these issues. The spf-hold default value is 200ms. It is one of the exponential back off-timers. There is little or no benefit to tune this timer. If the value is too low (less than 100ms), a rapidly flapping interface could cause unnecessary SPF runs in all routers in the same area. The spf-max-wait default value is 5000ms. There is little or no benefit to tune this timer. If the value is too low (less than 1000ms), some major network event could cause many SPF runs in the network. It forces OSPF process constantly re-calculates, it could cause potentially high CPU utilization, and destabilizes the network.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to keep the 'timers throttle spf spf-start' value within 0-50ms range. Set it to '0' for the fastest network convergence when the first event happens. Keep the default 50ms if super fast convergence is not required. 
Cisco recommends to keep the "'imers throttle spf spf-hold' value as default 200ms. Do not set the value to be lower than 100ms, keep it in the 100-200ms range if you want to change it for whatever reason. This is to ensure the network stability. 
Cisco recommends to keep the 'timers throttle spf spf-max-wait' value as default 5000ms. Do not set the value to be lower than 1000ms, keep it in the 1000-5000ms range if you want to change it. This is to ensure the network stability.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF Throttle SPF Timers Improperly Tuned

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
It is recommended to specify the key in encrypted form using password option under configuration  block of "key chain"
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router:# configure
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)# key chain isis-keys
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-isis-keys)# key 8
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-isis-keys-0x8)# key-string password 850aefd
bestPracticeDescription: For Keychain Management, key-string should be encrypted.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure the key-string in encrypted form under the key chain configuration block.
bestPracticeTitle: Keychain Management Key-string Is Not Encrypted

bestPracticeCaveat: None
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Option 1: Reset the timer value to the default value of 300 seconds through the command 'no fast-reroute timers promotion'
Option 2: Explicitly configure a timer value higher than 300 seconds through the command 'fast-reroute timers promotion '
bestPracticeDescription: Fast reroute promotion timer is configured to a value less than the default value of 300 seconds. Setting the interval to a low value puts more load on the CPU because it has to scan all protected LSPs more frequently. It is not recommended that the timer be configured below the default value of 300 seconds. Pacing mechanisms have been implemented to distribute the load on the CPU when backup promotion is active. Because of this,when a large number of protected LSPs are promoted,some delay is noticeable in backup promotion. If the promotion timer is configured to a very low value (depending on the number of protected LSPs) some protected LSPs may never get promoted.
bestPracticeRecommendation: To configure how often the router considers switching a protected MPLS-TE tunnel to a new backup tunnel in case additional backup-bandwidth or a better backup tunnel becomes available, Cisco recommends to use the 'fast-reroute timers promotion' command in MPLS-TE configuration mode.
bestPracticeTitle: Fast Reroute Promotion Timer Less Than Default Value

bestPracticeCaveat: No known caveat.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Condition:
If "router ospf " exists, search for the "timers throttle lsa all" command under the router ospf config section. If the "timers throttle lsa all" command exists, check the "timers throttle lsa all start-interval hold-interval max-interval" command timer values. 

If:
start-interval >50m, or if:
hold-interval <100ms, or   hold-interval >200ms, of if:
max-interval <1000ms, or   max-interval >5000ms, Corrective action:
remove the  "timers throttle lsa all" command completely, and return to the default timer values.
bestPracticeDescription: Modern networks have much less unstable links and nodes than the networks 10-20 years ago. The IOS XR default OSPF 'timers throttle lsa all' values are considered well-tuned and suitable for most of OSPF networks from network convergence, stability and scalability perspective. Unless a network has very specific conditions, or requirement, it is recommended not to change the OSPF 'timers throttle lsa all' configuration. In case a network operator would like to tune the IOS XR OSPF 'timers throttle lsa all timers' due to specific network conditions or requirement, please use the following guideline: timers throttle lsa all start-interval hold-interval max-interval The start-interval default value is 50ms. After receiving the first local change trigger, OSPF will generate a Link-state Advertisements (LSA) after this timer expires. Network operator can reduce it to 0 to reduce the convergence time, but at the expense of two potential drawbacks. First, transient interface status changes are no longer filtered out. Second, quasi-simultaneous interface failures are no longer groomed together. If a network is usually very stable, 0ms is an acceptable value. But changing the value to larger than 50ms will delay network convergence. hold-interval default value is 200ms. It's one of the exponential back off-timers. There is little or no benefit to tune this timer. If the value is too low (less than 100ms), a rapidly flapping interface could cause unnecessary LSAs in the network. The max-interval default value is 5000ms. There is little or no benefit to tune this timer. If the value is too low (less than 1000ms), a constantly flapping interface could cause many unnecessary LSAs in the network. It forces OSPF process to constantly re-calculate, and potentially causes high CPU utilization.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to keep the timers throttle lsa all start-interval value within 0-50ms range. Set it to '0' for the fastest network convergence when the first event happens. Keep the default 50ms if super fast convergence is not required.
Cisco recommends to keep the timers throttle lsa all hold-interval value as default 200ms. Do not set the value to be lower than 100ms, keep it in the 100-200ms range if you want to change it for whatever reason. This is to ensure the network stability.
Cisco recommends to keep the timers throttle lsa all max-interval value as default 5000ms. Do not set the value to be lower than 1000ms, keep it in the 1000-5000ms range if you want to change it for whatever reason. this is to ensure the network stability.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF Throttle LSA Timers Improperly Tuned

bestPracticeCaveat: Single RP device.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router ospfv3 
 nsr
bestPracticeDescription: The Non-Stop Routing (NSR) feature should be enabled for OSPFv3 (Open Shortest Path First version 3) on the network element. The NSR feature allows an OSPFv3 process on the active RP to synchronize all necessary data and states with the OSPFv3 process on the standby RP. When an RP switchover happens, the OSPFv3 process on the newly active RP has all the necessary data and states to continue forwarding and operating, without requiring any help from its neighbors, for example, those which do not support OSPFv3 Graceful Restart (GR).
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco Recommends to review the network environment and consider enabling NSR in combination with Graceful Restart (GR) for all devices with OSPFv3 routing protocol processes.
bestPracticeTitle: NSR (OSPFv3) Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the router id with the 'router-id' command.
router ospfv3 
router-id
bestPracticeDescription:
If the router-id is not explicitly set, IPv6 OSPFv3 automatically selects an IPv4 address to use as the router ID when the process is started. The highest local IPv4 address is selected and loopback interfaces are preferred. If there isnt any IPv4 address configured in the box, process will not start since an OSPF for IPv6 protocol instance requires a router ID before it can start running.


 Because loopback interfaces are not affected by circuit failure, greater stability in the routing table is achieved.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure ospfv3 router id  explicitly with the 'router-id' command.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPFv3 Router-Id Not Explicitly Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'tcp synwait-time 10' globally.
bestPracticeDescription: 'TCP Syn wait time' is the time software waits while attempting to establish a TCP connection before it times out. A higher value puts the device at risk for DDoS attacks.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Set the Syn wait time to 10 seconds (default is 30 seconds).
bestPracticeTitle: TCP Syn Wait Time Above 15 Seconds

bestPracticeCaveat: This feature is currently only supported on the Nexus 5000 and 6000.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure pre-provisioning for all FEX and GEM modules:

slot   (found in 'show inventory')
  provision model
bestPracticeDescription:
During a disruptive upgrade, FEX and GEM configuration may be lost due to the configuration being applied before the FEX comes online (CSCul22703).

FEX and GEM pre-provisioning was introduced to allow for the FEX to be configured before it comes online. The pre-provisioning feature allows you to pre-configure 
interfaces before inserting or attaching a module. If a module goes 
offline, you can also use pre-provisioning to make changes to the 
interface configurations for the offline module. When a pre-provisioned 
module comes online, the pre-provisioning configurations are applied. If
 any configurations were not applied, a Syslog is generated. The Syslog 
lists the configurations that were not accepted.
In some virtual Port Channel (vPC) topologies, pre-provisioning is 
required for the configuration synchronization feature. Pre-provisioning
 allows you to synchronize the configuration for an interface that is 
online with one peer but offline with another peer.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure FEX pre-provisioning,to prevent any loss in the configuration due to disruptive upgrade.
bestPracticeTitle: FEX And GEM Pre-Provisioning Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
RP/0/0/CPU0:router# configure
RP/0/0/CPU0:router(config)# bfd multipath include location 0/5/CPU0
bestPracticeDescription: IOS XR release 4.3.0 has introduced full support for IPv4 and IPv6 BFD sessions over bundle interfaces and sub-interfaces. For disambiguation from the BoB feature, this implementation is called BLB, and sessions are often referred to as native BFD sessions over bundle interfaces and/or sub-interfaces. BFD multipath must be enabled for BLB BFD flavour to work. All other configuration aspects are identical to ?BFD over VLAN over bundle? configuration. The 'multipath' command includes specific linecards to host BFD multiple path sessions.
bestPracticeRecommendation: BFD multipath should be configured using command 'bfd multipath include location '.
bestPracticeTitle: BFD Multipath Not Enabled For BLB

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Use 'ip pim register-rate-limit' on devices requiring additional protection.
bestPracticeDescription:
PIM register messages are unicast to the RP by designated routers 
(DRs) that are directly connected to multicast sources. The PIM register
 message has the following functions:
To notify the RP that a source is actively sending to a multicast group.

To deliver multicast packets sent by the source to the RP for delivery down the shared tree.


The
 DR continues to send PIM register messages to the RP until it receives a
 Register-Stop message from the RP. The RP sends a Register-Stop message
 in either of the following cases:

The RP has no receivers for the multicast group being transmitted.

The RP has joined the SPT to the source but has not started receiving traffic from the source.



This process can be taxing on the CPU of the Designated Router (DR) and the RP if the source is running at a high data rate and/or there are many new sources starting at the same time. This scenario can potentially occur immediately after a network failover.

In Cisco NX-OS, PIM register messages are rate limited via CoPP to avoid overwhelming the RP.  However, it may be necessary to further rate limit the register packets.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Make sure the latest CoPP is applied to the device.  If additional protection is needed,enable 'ip pim register-rate-limit' on the device.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv4 PIM Register Rate Limits Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: This IC will just handle the situation where "group" configuration is presented, but there is no "apply-group" anywhere in the configuration.  It does not differentiate whether particular "group" is applied if there are more "group" configuration than "apply-group" commands.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Use "apply-group" to apply the defined "group" configuration under interface or routing process.
bestPracticeDescription: 'Flexible Command Line Interface Configuration Group' feature was introduced from IOS-XR version 4.3.1. It provides the ability to minimize repetitive configurations by defining a series of configuration statements in a configuration group, and then applying this group to multiple hierarchical levels in the router configuration tree. Flexible CLI configuration groups utilize regular expressions that are checked for a match at multiple sub-modes of the configuration tree based on where the group is applied within the hierarchy. Under IOS-XR, when using the FlexCLI 'group' feature, make sure to apply using 'apply-group' command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use 'apply-group' command whenever you use 'group' feature in FlexCLI under IOS-XR.
bestPracticeTitle: Group Feature In FlexCLI Not Applied

bestPracticeCaveat:
Admin VDCs are supported on Supervisor 1 and Supervisor 2/2e modules. When an admin VDC is enabled,only the mgmt0 port is allocated to the admin VDC.

Sup 1 support starting in 6.2(2)
Sup2/2e support starting in 6.1

If the default VDC has Fabric Extenders that are enabled and configured,the migration of the default VDC configuration can take several minutes.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable the system admin VDC utilizing one of the following methods:

-  Enter the system admin-vdc command after bootup. The default VDC becomes the admin VDC. All the nonglobal configuration in the default VDC is lost after you enter this command. This option is recommended for existing deployments where the default VDC is used only for administration and does not pass any traffic.

-  You can change the default VDC to the admin VDC with the system admin-vdc migrate new vdc name command. After entering this command,the nonglobal configuration on a default VDC is migrated to the new migrated VDC. This option is recommended for existing deployments where the default VDC is used for production traffic whose downtime must be minimized.
bestPracticeDescription: With Admin VDC, the VDC that was originally reserved can be used for other purposes. While using Admin VDC, you will see a change to the way Cisco describes the total number of VDC. Traditionally with Supervisor 1 modules, the maximum number of VDCs was 4. If a customer reserved the default VDC for administrative purposes, they had 3 VDCs left to use. With Admin VDC on Supervisor 2, this is illustrated as 4+1 and on Supervisor 2E as 8+1 with the '+1' being the Admin VDC.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure an admin VDC on a Nexus 7000 for more security.
bestPracticeTitle: System Admin VDC Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: No known caveat.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the following:

performance-mgmt thresholds node cpu template 
 AverageCpuUsed gt 
 sample-interval 
!
performance-mgmt apply thresholds node cpu location all
bestPracticeDescription: IOS XR performance management, node CPU threshold monitoring is an efficient way to monitor the health of a router. Unlike SNMP polling, a router does not need to respond to periodic polling, which consumes considerable amount of router resources. Instead, the router monitors the node CPU threshold, and generates Syslog messages when the threshold is crossed, which could be an indication of potential issue. Performance management node CPU threshold monitoring consumes less router resource than responding to periodic SNMP polling, and provides timely Syslog messages when the router CPU utilization crosses a set threshold. It is very easy to configure and consume. A network operator can choose suitable thresholds and sample intervals for their network environment, the network operator can also choose to monitor the route process CPU, or any specific line card CPU. But it is the simplest way to just monitor all RP and line cards.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the node CPU threshold monitoring to efficiently monitor the health of a router.
bestPracticeTitle: Node CPU Threshold Monitoring Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Option 1: Reset to default

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router# configure
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)# flow monitor-map map1
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-fmm)# no cache timeout active      >>>> default value is 1800 seconds
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-fmm)# no cache timeout inactive   >>>> default value is 15 seconds

Option 2: Explicitly configure values

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router# configure
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)# flow monitor-map map1
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-fmm)# cache timeout active 5000
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-fmm)# cache timeout inactive 100
bestPracticeDescription:
Netflow inactive timeout value is not smaller than the active timeout value.


To configure the active, inactive, and update flow cache timeout, enter the 'cache timeout' command in 'flow monitor map configuration' mode. To remove the configured timeout value and return the cache to its default timeout value, use the 'no' form of this command.
Command Default
For active timeout, the default value is 1800 seconds.
For inactive timeout, the default value is 15 seconds.
For update timeout, the default value is 1800 seconds.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to restore the Netflow inactive and active timeout values to the defaults or manually configure them so that the inactive timeout value is smaller than the active timeout value.
bestPracticeTitle: Netflow Inactive Timeout Value Not Less Than Active Timeout Value

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable the BFD traps with the 'snmp-server traps bfd' command.
bestPracticeDescription: This command controls (enables or disables) Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications for Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD). BFD objects are defined in CISCO-IETF-BFD-MIB. If the BFD session is up/down between the router and it's BFD peer, using 'snmp-server traps bfd' command will generate a message.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the BFD traps to ensure that BFD notifications are sent to Network Management Systems.
bestPracticeTitle: BFD SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Disable loopguard globally using 'no spanning-tree loopguard default'.

Disable it on vPC interfaces using 'no spanning-tree guard loop'
bestPracticeDescription: Loopguard puts STP blocked ports into a loop inconsistent state when BPDUs are no longer received.  On a vPC port, this is not necessary since all ports are non-blocking.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends not to enable STP loopguard globally or on vPC interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: Loopguard Enabled Globally with vPC and on vPC Ports

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable MPLS LDP OSPF Synchronization by configuring OSPF process

Example:

!
router ospf 2
 mpls ldp sync
!
bestPracticeDescription: MPLS LDP OSPF Synchronization synchronizes LDP and OSPF so that OSPF advertises link with regular metrics only when MPLS LDP is converged on that link. LDP considers a link converged when at least one LDP session is up and running on the link for which LDP has sent its applicable label bindings and received at lease one label binding from the peer. LDP communicates this information to OSPF upon link up or session down events and OSPF acts accordingly. If OSPF MPLS Label Distribution Protocol (LDP) sync feature is not enabled, MPLS VPN traffic forwarded using LDP labels may be dropped in the following instances: 1. A new link is introduced in the network and OSPF has converged before LDP establishes labels. 2. An existing LDP session goes down while OSPF adjacency is intact.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable MPLS LDP OSPF Synchronization to prevent MPLS LDP labeled traffic forwarding black hole.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF MPLS LDP Synchronization Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: (config)#no feature vtp
bestPracticeDescription: VTP feature should not be enabled if it is not in use. Accidental VTP configuration can cause massive issues on a production network. VLAN database can become corrupted and overwritten. It is recommended to disable VTP if it is not planned to be used.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Disable VTP feature when it is not in use to avoid making the database corrupted and overwritten.
bestPracticeTitle: VTP Enabled But Not in Use

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configures the rate limit in packets per second based on the design of the multicast network. The range is from 1 to 65,535
bestPracticeDescription:
When a new source starts transmitting in a IPv6 PIM Sparse Mode network, the packets will be encapsulated and sent using unicast to the Rendezvous Point (RP). This process can be taxing on the CPU of the Designated Router (DR) and the RP if the source is running at a high data rate and/or there are many new sources starting at the same time. This scenario can potentially occur
immediately after a network failover.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to rate limit the traffic. Use the command on the Designated Router. On a designated router,rate limit IPv6 pim register messages.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv6 PIM Register Rate Limits Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configured 'set-overload-bit on-startup wait-for-bgp' under the IS-IS process.
bestPracticeDescription: After a reload, traffic can be lost while the BGP process is converging if IS-IS converges first. Setting the overload bit until BGP has initialized, prevents traffic from being sent to the device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: When both BGP and IS-IS are configured, set the IS-IS overload bit until BGP can converge.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS Overload Bit Not Set With BGP

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)# router hsrp
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-hsrp)# interface TenGigE  0/2/0/1
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-hsrp-if)# no mac-address 4000.1000.1060
bestPracticeDescription: HSRP is used to help end stations locate the first-hop gateway for IP routing. The end stations are configured with a default gateway. However, HSRP can provide first-hop redundancy for other protocols. Some protocols, such as Advanced Peer-to-Peer Networking (APPN), use the MAC address to identify the first-hop for routing purposes. In this case, it is often necessary to specify the virtual MAC address; the virtual IP address is unimportant for these protocols. Use the 'mac-address' command to specify the virtual MAC address. We do not recommend this command, except for IBM networking environments in which first-hop redundancy is based on being able to use a virtual MAC address, and in which you cannot change the first-hop addresses in the PCs that are connected to an Ethernet switch. Sample configuration: RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)# router hsrp RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-hsrp)# interface TenGigE 0/2/0/1 RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-hsrp-if)# mac-address 4000.1000.1060
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends not to use virtual MAC command, except for certain Advanced Peer-to-Peer Networking (APPN) network.
bestPracticeTitle: Virtual HSRP MAC Address Configured

bestPracticeCaveat: RFD implementation is beneficial in an unstable environment provided the implementation characteristics are consistent across and within the devices.  If there is a specific need for BGP Route Flap Dampening,Route dampening parameters can either be applied to all prefixes with equal penalties or to longer prefixes with higher penalty than the short ones. The later is preferred since it is flexible and gives more granular control.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Disable route-flap dampening in an Internet environment;" no bgp dampening " in the BGP router block.
bestPracticeDescription:
Route Flap Dampening is a mechanism for BGP speaking routers. It is intended to improve the overall stability of the Internet Routing Table and reduce the load on the CPUs of the core routers. However, recent studies suggest that BGP dampening no longer has any positive effects on performance. For example:
1. Today's routers' performance can easily cope with route flaps.
2. RFD implementations may differ in RFD parameter values. This inconsistency may result in inconsistent path selection.
3. Modality of route advertisement may differ. Some implementations pass on the update without waiting at all, others wait for 30 seconds, etc.
This will likely result in a different best-path offering to neighbors as message updates arrive.
The main drawback of RFD is well explained in the following study:
?Route-flap Dampening Exacerbates Internet Routing Congerence Sigcomm 2002
http://www.eecs.umich.edu/~zmao/Papers/sig02.pdf ?
Basically, it shows that a single route flap (a withdrawn followed by an announcement) will make the route to be dampened further in the backbone because several updates path (an attribute change is considered as a flap) will be generated due to the next best path selection in the previous hops. Also the fact is that each AS may use a different MRAI (Min Route Adv Interval) reinforces, and this behavior is called withdrawal triggered suppression.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to disable route-flap dampening in an Internet environment,as this no longer has any positive effects on performance.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Route Flap Dampening Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: This IC only checks whether globally defined "ethernet oam profile" has been applied under any live interface.   It does not check whether the global profile has been applied with overwriting.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Remove unused "ethernet oam profile".
bestPracticeDescription: IOS-XR supports link OAM Configuration with "Ethernet OAM profile" to simply duplicate the configuration under each individual interface. Make sure that the defined ethernet OAM profile is applied under interface.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use "Ethernet OAM profile" to simplify the configuration, if there are multiple interfaces with the same OAM configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: Unused Ethernet OAM Profile

bestPracticeCaveat:
Note that the OSPFv3 configuration supports only SHA-1 for authentication. IPSec is supported only for Open Shortest Path First version 3 (OSPFv3).
Also prerequisite for this feature is to install and activate the hfr-k9sec-px.pie file.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
crypto fips-mode
!
router ospfv3 test
 area 1
  authentication ipsec spi 256 sha1 password pa1    >>>> required
 !
 encryption ipsec spi 256 esp 3des password pwd    >>>> required
 max-metric router-lsa
 max-metric router-lsa on-startup wait-for-bgp
!
bestPracticeDescription: The Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 140-2 is the U.S. and Canadian government certification standard that defines requirements that the cryptographic modules must follow. The FIPS specifies best practices for implementing cryptographic algorithms, handling key material and data buffers, and working with the operating system. When the FIPs mode is on, its best practice to Encrypts and authenticates the OSPFv3 packets. Ensure that the OSPFv3 configuration uses the following for encryption in the configuration: - 3DES: Specifies the triple DES algorithm. - AES: Specifies the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) algorithm. Ensure that SHA1 is chosen if the authentication option is specified.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
When the FIPs mode is on, its best practice to Encrypts and authenticates the OSPFv3 packets. Ensure that the OSPFv3 configuration uses the following for encryption in the configuration.
? 3DES: Specifies the triple DES algorithm.
? AES: Specifies the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) algorithm.
Ensure that SHA1 is chosen if the authentication option is specified.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPFv3 Configuration Is Not FIPS-Compliant

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Define local user account using the command:
'username  

{password | secret} '
bestPracticeDescription: Local login should be set as a contingency in case the authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) server cannot be accessed. In such cases, local username and password need to be configured to provide local authentication.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to ensure that the local database is not empty while using local database as backup.
bestPracticeTitle: Local username not defined when local auth used as backup AAA

bestPracticeCaveat: This feature is introduced in IOS-XR 4.2.1
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
This example shows how to configure SSH as the transport mechanism and to use port 1 for SSH communication:
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router#configure
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)#router bgp 100
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-bgp)#rpki server 172.168.35.40 
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-bgp-rpki-cache)# transport ssh port 1
bestPracticeDescription: To choose a transport mechanism for the RPKI cache-server configuration, establish and manage transport connections and send or receive byte streams from the network, use the transport command in rpki-server configuration mode. The transport can be set to either TCP or SSH. An SSH transport session is the recommended transport between router and RPKI cache for security reasons.
bestPracticeRecommendation: An SSH transport session is the recommended transport between router and RPKI cache for security reasons.
bestPracticeTitle: Transport Mechanism For The RPKI Cache-server Is Not Secure

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
You can specify to use the loopback interface with the update-source option for the iBGP peer. For example, the BGP command below configures a router to use its loopback 10 interface to peer with 10.10.10.1. 
neighbor 10.10.10.1 update-source loopback10. (Example)
bestPracticeDescription: For iBGP sessions, it is recommended to use a logical interface, such as a loopback as the source of the session, instead of a physical interface. This is because, unlike physical interfaces, logical interfaces are not prone to state changes, which can result in the tearing down of BGP sessions. Further to this, using logical interfaces allows routers running BGP with multiple links between them to load balance over the available paths.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Peer between internal BGP peers using loopback addresses instead of physical addresses.
bestPracticeTitle: IBGP Peers Not Peering Via Loopback

bestPracticeCaveat: There is at least one exception, but it only happens to a small number of companies: an ISP or large enterprise has networks using multiple public AS numbers due to companies merger etc. In this case, when a router is doing eBGP peering with a router in another public AS number in the same company, remove-private-AS command may not necessarily be required. Because the company may have private-AS connecting to multiple public-AS belonging to the company, and the private AS number is needed to prevent BGP routing loop.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
bgp 
   neighbor 
       address-family ipv4 unicas
            remove-private-AS
bestPracticeDescription: If an IOS XR router using public AS (autonomous system) number has eBGP peering with other companies which are also using public AS number, the router should remove all private-AS numbers (64512 to 65535) from the AS-path before advertising BGP routes to the other companies. Private autonomous system numbers (ASNs) are used by ISPs and customer networks to conserve globally unique AS numbers. Private AS numbers cannot be used to access the global Internet because they are not unique. AS numbers appear in eBGP AS paths in routing updates. Removing private ASNs from the AS path is necessary if you have been using private ASNs and you want to access the global Internet.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the 'remove-private-AS' command for the eBGP internet peering to remove private AS numbers from AS paths when generating updates to external neighbors.
bestPracticeTitle: Private AS Configuration For Internet Peering is not recommended.

bestPracticeCaveat: Applicable 4.2.x release onwards
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
dhcp ipv6
 interface 
  destination
