Following is the configuration for the same: RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router# configure RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config) # dhcp ipv6 RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-dhcpv6)# RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-dhcpv6) # interface tenGigE 0/5/0/0 relay RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-dhcpv6-if) # destination 10:10::10
bestPracticeRecommendation: If customer is using DHCP Relay services for IPv6, it is recommended to configure an interface, and the corresponding IPv6 destination address.
bestPracticeTitle: DHCP Relay IPv6 Configured Without Interface or Destination

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration is given Below 
!
router bgp 3
 bgp router-id 20.20.20.20
!  
router ospf 1
  router-id 20.20.20.20
!
bestPracticeDescription: If BGP and OSPF are configured, check if the router-ids are configured. The router-ids for both protocols must be same. If the BGP/OSPF router IDs are different and synchronization is also enabled, then a BGP prefix is not installed when a matching path is coming from an OSPF neighbor, where OSPF router ID and iBGP router IDs are different.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the BGP and OSPF router-ids to be the same.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP and OSPF Router IDs are different

bestPracticeCaveat:
All keychains need to be configured in Cisco IOS XR keychain database using configuration commands described in Implementing Keychain Management module of Cisco ASR 9000 Series Aggregation Services Router System Security Configuration Guide before they can be applied to a RIP interface/VRF.

The authentication keychain keychain-name and mode md5 configurations will accept the name of a keychain that has not been configured yet in the IOS XR keychain database or a keychain that has been configured in IOS XR keychain database without MD5 cryptographic algorithm. However, in both these cases, all incoming packets on the interface will be dropped and outgoing packets will be sent without authentication data.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuring RIP Authentication Keychain: Example

This example shows how to apply an authentication keychain on a RIP default VRF interface:

router rip
 interface POS0/6/0/0
  authentication keychain key1 mode md5
 !
!
end


This example shows how to apply an authentication keychain on a RIP non-default interface:

router rip
 vrf TEST
  interface POS0/6/0/0
   authentication keychain key1 mode md5
  !
 !
!
end
bestPracticeDescription: It is advisable to enable authentication between routing peers. This will prevent an attacker from injecting invalid or fraudulent route entries. In addition invalid route entries may result from poor configuration or a malfunctioning router. RIPv2 supports two modes of authentication: Plain Text Message Digest 5 (MD5). Plain Text authentication mode sends the unencrypted plain text password in every RIPv2 packet. Therefore, MD5 authentication should be used, as it is more secure.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable message digest 5 authentication between RIPv2 routing peers.
bestPracticeTitle: MD5 Authentication Not Enabled For RIP

bestPracticeCaveat: Logging can be enabled for troubleshooting purposes.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
mpls ldp
 mldp
  no logging notifications
  no logging internal

Or
mpls ldp
 no mldp logging notifications 
 no mldp logging internal
bestPracticeDescription:
Multicast Label Distribution Protocol (MLDP) provides extensions to the Label Distribution Protocol (LDP) for the setup of point-to-multipoint (P2MP) and multipoint-to-multipoint (MP2MP) Label Switched Paths (LSPs) in Multiprotocol Label Switching (MPLS} networks.



MLDP Logging will produce a log message for every FEC entry created on the MLDP database. This causes a big amount of logs to be generated which can overrun other relevant logs on the local log buffer.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to disable MLDP logging to avoid the big amount of logs generated by every FEC entry.
bestPracticeTitle: MLDP Logging Notifications Not Disabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
The default hello-interval depends on the network type:

For low-speed,nonbroadcast multiaccess (NBMA) networks: 60 seconds
For all other networks: 5 seconds
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Reset the hello-interval and hold-time to the defaults through the following:

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config)# router eigrp 1
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-eigrp)# address-family ipv4
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-eigrp-af)# interface GigabitEthernet 0/1/0/0
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-eigrp-af-if)# no hello-interval
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-eigrp-af-if)# no hold-time

Else,configure the specific values under the interface as shown in the following example:

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-eigrp-af-if)# hello-interval 20
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:router(config-eigrp-af-if)# hold-time 60
bestPracticeDescription: It is recommended that the hold time be at least three times the hello interval. To ensure nonstop forwarding during RP failovers, it is recommended that the hold time be increased to 30 seconds.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
On very congested and large networks,the default hold time might not be sufficient time for all routers to receive hello packets from their neighbors. It is recommended that the hold time be at least three times the hello interval. If a router does not receive a hello packet within the specified hold time,routes through this router are considered unavailable.

Increasing the hold time delays the route convergence across the network. To ensure nonstop forwarding during RP failovers,we recommend that the hold time be increased to 30 seconds.
bestPracticeTitle: EIGRP Hold Time Not Optimally Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
IOS-XR(config)#router hsrp 
IOS-XR(config-hsrp)#interface 
IOS-XR(config-hsrp-if)#hsrp delay minimum 30 reload 60
bestPracticeDescription: When configuring Hot Standby Router Protocol (HSRP), Cisco recommends using the 'standby preempt delay minimum|reload' command to prevent blackholing of traffic. This allows time for all ports to come up after a reload or module reset. In a subsecond timer scenario, hellos could be lost with CPU busy after reload. If the active router fails or is removed from the network, the standby router will automatically become the new active router. If the former active router comes back online, you can control whether it takes over as the active router by using the standby preempt command. However, in some cases, even if the standby preempt command is not configured, the former active router will resume the active role after it reloads and comes back online. Use the standby delay minimum reload command to set a delay period for HSRP group initialization. This command allows time for the packets to get through before the router resumes the active role. We recommend that all HSRP routers have the standby delay minimum reload configured with a minimum delay time of 30 seconds and a minimum reload time of 60 seconds. The delay will be cancelled if an HSRP packet is received on an interface. The standby delay minimum reload interface configuration command delays HSRP groups from initializing for the specified time after the interface comes up.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the 'standby preempt delay minimum|reload' command with a minimum delay time of 30 seconds and a minimum reload time of 60 seconds, to prevent blackholing of traffic.
bestPracticeTitle: Standby Delay Value Not Configured

bestPracticeCaveat:
This also depends on the other end of the PW and in the legacy implementations we did not have "control word". Hence this might not be a perfect case always. Please go through below abstract.

As per RFC4448 - https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc4448.txt

   In all cases, the egress PE MUST be aware of whether the ingress PE
   will send a control word over a specific PW.  This may be achieved by
   configuration of the PEs, or by signaling, as defined in [PWE3-CTRL].
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: If the control word does'nt exist then enable it, then it should be enabled
bestPracticeDescription: Whenever supported, 'control word' is also recommended for pseudowires(PW), as it enables proper load balancing without packet de-sequencing. This is independent of L2VPN packet content. Without 'control word', the heuristics used to perform load balancing cannot achieve optimal results in all cases.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable control word under encapsulation of the L2VPN
bestPracticeTitle: L2VPN PW Control Word Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Use a private vrf instead of the global routing table to configure the interfaces used for satellite management traffic.

R1(config)# vrf NV_MGMT_VRF
R1(config)# address ipv4 unicast

R1(config)# interface Loopback 1000
R1(config-if)# vrf NV_MGMT_VRF
R1(config-if)# ipv4 address 10.0.0.1 / 24

R1(config)# interface TenGige 0/1/0/3 
R1(config-if)# vrf NV_MGMT_VRF
R1(config-if)# ipv4 point-to-point 
R1(config-if)# ipv4 unnumbered Loopback 1000
R1(config-if)# nv 
R1(config-int-nv)# satellite-fabric-link satellite 500
R1(config-int-nv)# remote-ports GigabitEthernet 0/0/28-39
R1(config)# nv satellite 500 
R1(config-nv)# ipv4 address 10.0.0.2 / 24
bestPracticeDescription: The Interface Control Plane Extender (ICPE) infrastructure has a mechanism to provide the Control Plane of an interface physically located on the Satellite device in the local Cisco IOS XR software. After this infrastructure is established, the interfaces behave like other physical ethernet interfaces on the router. Inter-Chassis Links (ICLs) need to be explicitly configured, in order to indicate which satellite is expected to be connected to the router. The recommended option is to use a private VRF for NV IP addresses. A special private VRF can be used instead of the global default routing table, to configure the loopback interface and ICLs used for satellite management traffic. IP addresses in this VRF will not conflict with any other addresses used on the router. Consider the following example. NV_MGMT_VRF is the vrf created for configuring the satellite interfaces. R1(config)# vrf NV_MGMT_VRF R1(config)# address ipv4 unicast R1(config)# interface Loopback 1000 R1(config)# vrf NV_MGMT_VRF R1(config)# ipv4 address 10.0.0.1 / 24 R1(config)# interface TenGige 0/1/0/3 R1(config)# vrf NV_MGMT_VRF R1(config)# ipv4 point-to-point R1(config)# ipv4 unnumbered Loopback 1000 R1(config)# nv R1(config-nv)# satellite-fabric-link satellite 500 R1(config-nv)# remote-ports GigabitEthernet 0/0/28-39 R1(config)# nv satellite 500 R1(config)# ipv4 address 10.0.0.2 / 24
bestPracticeRecommendation:
While configuring an Inter-chassis link between the router and the satellite, it is recommended to use a private VRF instead of the global routing table to configure the interfaces used for satellite management traffic.

Customer can use a special private VRF instead of the global default routing table, to configure the loopback interface and ICLs used for satellite management traffic. IP addresses in this VRF will not conflict with any other addresses used on the router.
bestPracticeTitle: Satellite Management Not Using Private VRF

bestPracticeCaveat: FEX fabric ports do not support LACP.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure channel-group mode active/passive under the interface.
Enable LACP using 'feature lacp' and configure physical interfaces in a port-channel bundler with 'channel-group  mode active'.
For connections that you want the remote side to initiate LACP communication use 'channel-group  mode passive' on the physical interface.
bestPracticeDescription:
LACP should be used for the following reasons:
- Configuration consistency
- Wiring consistency
- Bundle member keep-alives (LACP BPDUs)
- Detection of unidirectional links
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use LACP on all port-channels unless the remote side does not support it,to detect unidirectional links,and maintain configuration and wiring consistency.
bestPracticeTitle: LACP Not Enabled On All Port-Channels

bestPracticeCaveat: If a customer doesn't use unique v4/v6 names for the prefix lists,could have false positives.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Check the 'prefix-list ' statement to see if it is missing or matches the prefix-list name in the 'ip prefix-list' or 'ipv6 prefix-list'
statement to the  where this prefix-list is applied.
bestPracticeDescription:
The 'ip prefix-list' or 'ipv6 prefix-list' commands are used to configure prefix filtering. Prefix-lists are configured with permit or deny keywords to either permit or deny the prefix based on the matching condition. A prefix-list consists of an address and a bit mask. The address can be a classful network, a subnet, or a single-host route.


The purpose of this rule is to identify if there are any prefix-lists referred in a configuration but not defined. Prefix-list could be referred at various instances such as with neighbor statement, distribute-list, route-maps, etc.


To create an entry in a prefix list, use the 'ip prefix-list' or 'ipv6 prefix-list' global configuration command.


For example: 


ip prefix-list CISCO seq 10 permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 19
ipv6 prefix-list CISCO6 seq 10 permit 0::0


It also could be referenced in the neighbor statement or distribute-list or route-map, etc. using the following command: 'neighbor {ip-address | peer-group-name} prefix-list prefixlistname {in | out}'


For example: 
!
neighbor 172.16.1.2 remote-as 65000
  address-family ipv4 unicast
  prefix-list CISCO in
!
neighbor 20:1 remote-as 65000
  address-family ipv6 unicast
  prefix-list CISCO6 in
!
For the prefix-list to work correctly, it is important to make sure that the prefix-list name in the prefix-list statement matches the prefix-list name in the configuration where it is referenced.


An empty prefix list permits all routes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: When the device configuration refer a prefix list,ensure the prefix list is defined on the device.
bestPracticeTitle: Neighbor prefix-list Statement Not Defined

bestPracticeCaveat:
The ttl-security command should not be configured for a peer that is already configured with the neighbor ebgp-multihop command. The simultaneous configuration of these commands is permitted; however, the ttl-security command overrides the ebgp-multihop command.

This command is not supported for internal BGP (iBGP) peers.

This command is not effective against attacks from a directly adjacent peer that has been compromised.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Under the 'neighbor' configuration in the BGP routing process enable:
 - ttl-security for directly connected neighbors
 - ebgp-multihop [ttl-value] for eBGP multi-hop neighbors

Ensure to calculate the hops according to the topology and configure accordingly.
bestPracticeDescription: The BGP time-to-live (TTL)-based security check is designed to protect BGP processes from CPU utilization-based attacks. The BGP protocol defines two types of sessions: "internal" BGP sessions (iBGP) that are established between peers within the same Autonomous System (AS), and external BGP (eBGP) sessions that are established between peers in two different ASs. Of particular interest are the eBGP sessions, which are the type of BGP sessions that would be established between an Enterprise and its upstream service provider. By default and per RFC 3682, when eBGP is configured, the IP header TTL for all neighbor session packets is set to "1". This configuration was originally assumed to be useful because it prevented the establishment of an eBGP session beyond a single hop; however, the configuration does not take into account an attacker who could be located up to 255 hops away and could have the ability to send spoofed packets to BGP-speaking routers. For example, an attacker could send large amounts of TCP SYN packets at the BGP peer to overwhelm the BGP process. The BGP TTL security check leverages ISP eBGP peering sessions between routers that are adjacent to each other (either between directly connected interfaces or possibly between loopbacks). Because TTL spoofing is considered nearly impossible, a mechanism that is based on an expected TTL value was developed to provide a simple yet robust defense from infrastructure attacks that are based on forged BGP packets. When the BGP TTL security check is enabled, the initial value for eBGP is set to "255" (instead of "1"), and a minimum TTL value is enforced on all BGP packets that are associated with that eBGP session. Because the IP header TTL value is decremented by each hop (router interface) along its path to its final destination, using BTSH restricts possible attack origins to the directly connected routers.
bestPracticeRecommendation: This feature protects the control plane from CPU utilization based attacks. Hence it is a recommended practice to enable TTL Security for BGP.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP TTL Security disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Check if the route policy is configured to filter out any default or aggregate route under router bgp and the corresponding address family as shown below. If is not already present, configure it in the below format for the required AFI.
bestPracticeDescription: In router bgp, under the corresponding afi, make sure that a route-policy is used to filter out any default or aggregate route. Without this, if the nexthop address disapears from the rib (nexthop router goes down for example), the local router could falsely think that it is still reachable via a default route or an aggregate route, and would not remove the prefix from its bgp routing table and would consequently keep the network from reconverging.
bestPracticeRecommendation: In router bgp, under the corresponding afi, make sure that a route-policy is used to filter out any default or aggregate route.
bestPracticeTitle: Aggregate Routes Used To Track BGP Nexthop

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: authenticate md5 .*
bestPracticeDescription: MD5 authentication protects the HSRP processes running on a network.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure MD5 authentication for HSRP Group to protect from attacks.
bestPracticeTitle: HSRP MD5 Authentication Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: logging source-interface loopback-interface
bestPracticeDescription: A physical interface being down does not mean the router is out of commission. A router's ethernet port can go down, while the other physical interfaces on that router are still operational. Since a loopback interface is logical, nothing can physically go wrong with it.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends you to specify loopback interface as logging source. In case of pure layer 2 devices, VLAN interface must be used as source interface.
bestPracticeTitle: Logging Source Is Not Set To Loopback

bestPracticeCaveat:
There is at least one exception,but it only happens to a small number of companies:
An ISP or large enterprise has networks using multiple public AS numbers due to companies merger and so on. In this case,when a router is doing eBGP peering with a router in another public AS number in the same company,remove-private-AS command may not necessarily be required. Because the company may have private-AS connecting to multiple public-AS belonging to the company,and the private AS number is needed to prevent BGP routing loop.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure:
bgp 
   neighbor 
       address-family ipv6 unicast
            remove-private-AS
bestPracticeDescription:
If an IOS XR router using public-AS (autonomous system) number has IPv6 eBGP peering with other companies which are also using public AS number, the router should remove all private-AS numbers (64512 to 65535) from the AS-path before advertising BGP routes to the other companies.

AS-path is used as a loop prevention mechanism in BGP. Private-AS number is intended for internal use only. It should not be sent to other companies when doing internet peering.

It is very common that ISPs (Internet service provider) assign private-AS number to their customers if the customers do not connect to other ISPs. Hence when the ISP advertises its customers' routes to Internet (transit provider, and other ISP peers etc.), it is best practice to remove the private-AS number from the AS-path of the customer's routes. Large enterprises may also own public AS number and use private-AS numbers as well. So they should also use the same practice.


This particular best practice rule is applicable to internet peering only. It is not applicable to MPLS VPN peering.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure 'remove-private-AS' command for IPv6 Internet Peering,if missing.
bestPracticeTitle: Remove Private AS Not Configured For IPv6 Internet Peering

bestPracticeCaveat: Ethernet links with a switch between and where multiple OSPF neighbors are present on the broadcast media. The recommendation is to configure Ethernet and Bundle interfaces as OSPF network type point-to-point.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router ospf <>
 network point-to-point
!
or:
router ospf <>
 area <>
  network point-to-point
!
or:
router ospf <>
 area <>
  interface <>
   network point-to-point
!
bestPracticeDescription: A layer 3 Ethernet interface (including 10Mbps, 100Mbps, 1Gbps, 10Gbps, 100Gbps Ethernet, Link Bundle or Link Aggregation) is typically configured as a point-to-point connection to another router. It is unusual to have a layer 2 switch between two routers connected through Ethernet links. OSPF classifies different media into the following types of networks: NBMA networks Point-to-point networks (POS) Broadcast networks (Ethernet and Bundle) Point-to-multipoint Use of the point-to-point network type does not require DR/BDR election, and uses fewer OSPF LSAs. Network convergence is faster and less resources are consumed when the media is configured as an OSPF point-to-point network type instead of the broadcast network type, which is default for Ethernet and Bundle interfaces. The recommendation is to configure Ethernet and Bundle interfaces as OSPF network type point-to-point. This should be configured at the appropriate level under OSPF at process, area, or interface mode: if this command is not specified in interface configuration mode, then the interface adopts the network parameter specified by the area; if this command is not specified in area configuration mode, then the interface adopts the network parameter specified for the process; if this command is not specified at any level, then the OSPF network type is the default of the given medium for which, Ethernet and Bundle interfaces is broadcast.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the ?network point-to-point? command at the appropriate level under OSPF.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF Network Type Not Point-to-Point

bestPracticeCaveat: Ethernet links with a switch between and where multiple OSPFv3 neighbors are present on the broadcast media.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router ospfv3 <>
 network point-to-point
!
or:
router ospfv3 <>
 area <>
  network point-to-point
!
or:
router ospfv3 <>
 area <>
  interface <>
   network point-to-point
!
bestPracticeDescription:
A layer 3 Ethernet interface (including 10Mbps, 100Mbps, 1Gbps, 10Gbps, 100Gbps Ethernet, Link Bundle or Link Aggregation) is typically configured as a point-to-point connection to another router; it's unusual to have a layer 2 switch in between two routers connected via Ethernet links. 

OSPFv3 classifies different media into the following types of networks:

 NBMA networks

 Point-to-point networks (POS)

 Broadcast networks (Ethernet and Bundle)

 Point-to-multipoint


Use of the point-to-point network type does not require DR/BDR election and uses fewer LSAs. Network convergence is faster and less resources are consumed when the media is configured as a point-to-point network type instead of the broadcast network type, which is the default for Ethernet and Bundle interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to configure Ethernet and Bundle interfaces as OSPFv3 media type ?network point-to-point?. 
This should be configured at the appropriate level under OSPFv3 at process,area or interface mode:
If this command is not specified in interface configuration mode,then the interface adopts the network parameter specified by the area.
If this command is not specified in area configuration mode,then the interface adopts the network parameter specified for the process.
If this command is not specified at any level,then the network type is the default of the given medium,which for Ethernet and Bundle interfaces is broadcast.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPFv3 Network Type Not Point-to-Point

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: no cdp
bestPracticeDescription: By default, CDP is disabled globally. CDP should not be enabled if not used for any critical functions. CDP should not be enabled on Internet facing interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to disable CDP on Internet facing interfaces.
bestPracticeTitle: CDP Enabled On Interface

bestPracticeCaveat: The ARP and MAC timers should match across all platforms in the same Layer 2 domain.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: mac-address-table aging-time 1800
bestPracticeDescription:
The default MAC aging timer value for a Nexus 5000/5500 is 300 seconds.  This is below the default ARP timer across other NX-OS products (1500 seconds).

Having a MAC timer below the ARP timer can cause unidirectional traffic to flood.  Additionally, in a FabricPath/vPC+ environment traffic destined to a FEX will blackhole if there is no MAC entry due to a hardware limitation (CSCud25862).
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Configure the MAC aging interval to be equal to or more than the ARP aging timer (default 1500 seconds on other NX-OS platforms).

Recommended MAC aging timer value is 1800 seconds (as in Nexus 7000 and 6000 platforms) in an NX-OS environment.

If a catalyst switch is being used as an L3 gateway,its default ARP timer is 14400 seconds.  The recommendation in this case is to decrease the ARP timer to 1500 seconds and use the 1800 second MAC timer.
bestPracticeTitle: Default Mac Aging Time Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure tracking under VRRP configuration

!
interface TenGigE0/0/0/0
 no shut
!
interface TenGigE0/0/1/0.100
 ipv4 address 10.10.10.10 255.255.255.0
 encapsulation dot1q 100
!
router vrrp
 interface TenGigE0/0/2/1.10
  address-family ipv4
   vrrp 2 version 3
   priority 120
   track interface  TenGigE0/0/1/0.100
   address 10.10.10.1
!
bestPracticeDescription: To configure the Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) to track an interface, use the vrrp track interface command in VRRP interface configuration mode. In case of subinterfaces configured for one physical interface, tracking command should include subinterface itself but not physical. If physical interface will be used in this situation, feature will not work and interface will be indicated as down from start in show vrrp output.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure tracking under VRRP configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: VRRP Interface Tracking Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: configure "snmp-server enable traps bridge newroot"
bestPracticeDescription: The newRoot trap indicates that the sending agent has become the new root of the Spanning Tree. A bridge sends a trap soon after its election as the new root. For example, upon expiration of the Topology Change Timer, immediately subsequent to its election.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable STP new root bridge notifications.
bestPracticeTitle: New Root SNMP Trap Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
Changes to the Chassis Discovery Policy is disruptive and should be done only in a maintenance window or outside production hours.

Only the UCS 62xx and 63xx Fabric interconnect and the 22xx and 23xx IOM are capable of forming fabric port channels. The platform should be limited to only the above models... aka "UCS on 6200" or "UCS on 6300"
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Move the link aggregation preference for the chassis discovery policy from none to port-channel outside business hours or in a maintenance window.  Equipment tab, select Equipment and then choose Policies > Global Policies and under Chassis/FEX discovery policy, set Link grouping preference to Port-Channel.
bestPracticeDescription: On UCS 6200 with 2200 IO modules, UCS can form fabric port channels for all links between the IOM and FI server ports. Configure the "Link Grouping Preference" option in Global Policy to "Port Channel". The Link Grouping Preference determines whether all of the links from the IOM to the fabric interconnect are grouped into a fabric port channel during chassis discovery. If the link grouping preference is set to port channel, all of the links from the IOM to the fabric interconnect are grouped in a fabric port channel.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The best practice is to set Link Grouping Preference to "Port Channel", which provides greater availability in the event of link failure.
bestPracticeTitle: Link Grouping Preference Set To None

bestPracticeCaveat: In certain customer network designs, syslog may be specifically limited to fewer messages. In this case, if the standard is a lower level, ensure that no higher level messages of interest would be missed.  Debugging level may be used periodically for troubleshooting, but is usually not left at that level all the time.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Set the level under each Syslog remote destination to "Information".
bestPracticeDescription: When configuring a syslog remote destination, the export alarm level is set to "Critical" by default. If we keep the alarm level as "Critical", it is possible that lower level alarms warning an administrator that there is an issue with the system may be missed. It is recommended that, syslog level to be set to "Information".
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Set the Syslog remote destination level to "Information". 
Settings level lower than "Information" will result in key syslog messages not being sent to the syslog server and logging at level "Debugging" on an ongoing basis generates too many syslogs.
bestPracticeTitle: Syslog level not set to informational

bestPracticeCaveat: This feature requires connectivity from the device to Cisco.com. If direct connectivity is not available, a Transport Gateway may be needed.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Refer to the sample below:

scope monitoring
  scope callhome
      enable
     	set contact "Steve Jones"
	set email admin@MyCompany.com
	set phone-contact +1-001-408-555-1234
	set street-address "123 N. Main Street, Anytown, CA, 99885"
	set customer-id 1234567
	set contract-id 99887766
	set site-id 5432112
	set from-email person@MyCompany.com
	set reply-to-email person@MyCompany.com
	set hostname 192.168.100.12
	set port 25
	set throttling on
	set urgency information
	commit-buffer
bestPracticeDescription: This proactive support capability is provided at no additional cost when you have an active SMARTnet Service, SP Base, Unified Computing Support Service, or Mission Critical Support Service contract for the designated products. Problems can be identified before they affect your business. Spend less time troubleshooting, experience a speedier resolution to network issues, and gain higher network availability.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the Call Home feature to receive near real-time notifications of device faults and to help reduce Mean Time to Repair.
bestPracticeTitle: Call Home is not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: Disable UDLD on ports where this functionality is not required or not supported.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Globally enable UDLD with:
Switch(config)#feature udld
bestPracticeDescription: For maximum protection against symptoms resulting from unidirectional links, Cisco recommends enabling UDLD on point-to-point Ethernet links between Cisco switches, as there is no other method to validate bi-directional connectivity.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends disabling UDLD, if the following is configured:

- Physical uni-directional failures are communicated by Layer 1 mechanisms (i.e., 10GE).
- STP Bridge Assurance is configured on the link.
- LACP is used on the bundled ports.
- Layer 3 / FabricPath p2p link with bidirectional protocol already running.

If you require, then the recommendation would be to keep the default timers.
bestPracticeTitle: UDLD Globally Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
From the UCSM user interface, perform the following:

1) On the Navigation pane, click the Equipment tab. 
2) On the Equipment tab, click the Equipment node. 
3) On the Work pane, click the Policies tab.
4) Click the Global Policies sub-tab.
5) In the Power Policy area, click one of the following radio buttons in the Redundancy field:

? Non Redundant
All installed power supplies are turned on and the load is evenly balanced. Only smaller configurations (requiring less than 2500W) can be powered by a single power supply.

? N+1
The total number of power supplies to satisfy non-redundancy, plus one additional power supply for redundancy, are turned on and equally share the power load for the chassis. If any additional power supplies are installed, Cisco UCS Manager sets them to a "turned-off" state.

? Grid
Two power sources are turned on, or the chassis requires greater than N+1 redundancy. If one source fails (which causes a loss of power to one or two power supplies), the surviving power supplies on the other power circuit continue to provide power to the chassis.

6) Click Save Changes to save the changes.

From UCSM CLI:
UCS-A# scope org /
UCS-A /org # scope psu-policy
UCS-A /org/psu-policy # set redundancy 
UCS-A /org/psu-policy # commit-buffer
bestPracticeDescription: The power policy is a global policy that specifies the redundancy for power supplies in all chassis in the Cisco UCS domain. This policy is also known as the PSU policy. Setting the redundancy mode can be done through UCSM CLI or GUI. In non-redundant mode, the system may go down with the loss of any supply or power grid associated with any particular chassis. It is not recommended to run a production system in non-redundant mode. To operate in non-redundant mode, each chassis should have at least two power supplies installed. Supplies that are not used by the system are placed into standby. The N+1 redundancy configuration implies that the chassis contains a total number of power supplies to satisfy non-redundancy, plus one additional power supply for redundancy. All the power supplies that are participating in N+1 redundancy are turned on and equally share the power load for the chassis. If any additional power supplies are installed, Cisco UCS Manager recognizes these ?unnecessary? power supplies and places them on standby. If a power supply fail, the surviving supplies can provide power to the chassis. In addition, UCS Manager turns on any "turned-off" power supplies to bring the system back to N+1 status. The grid redundant configuration is sometimes used when you have two power sources to power a chassis or you require greater than N+1 redundancy. If one source fails (which causes a loss of power to one or two power supplies), the surviving power supplies on the other power circuit continue to provide power to the chassis. A common reason for using grid redundancy is if the rack power distribution is such that power is provided by two PDUs and you want the grid redundancy protection in the case of a PDU failure.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends that the power policy must be set to N+1 or Grid Redundancy based on the power facility available to the racks. This must be done to ensure high availability of power supply to the servers.
bestPracticeTitle: UCS Power Policy Set To Non-redundant

bestPracticeCaveat: It is not recommended to disable CoPP. Tune the default CoPP, as needed if required.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Do not change strict unless for other customer specific scenario
bestPracticeDescription: After a packet is classified, the packet can also be marked and used to assign different priorities based on the type of packets. Conform, exceed, and violate actions (transmit, drop, mark-down) can be set. If no policer is attached to a class, then a default policer is added whose conform action is drop. Glean packets are policed with default-class. One rate, two color, and two rate, three color policing are supported. Only the traffic sent through the Inband interface is subject to CoPP, because this is the only traffic that reaches the Supervisor module through the forwarding engines (FEs) on the line cards. The Nexus 7000 Series Switch implementation of CoPP is hardware-based only, which means that CoPP is not performed in software by the Supervisor module. By default, CoPP strict profile is applied.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Retain default setting to strict
bestPracticeTitle: CoPP Not Set to Strict For Nexus 7k

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure at least 1 syslog server on UCSM. Syslog configuration can be done via the Java client by navigating to Admin > All > Faults, Events and Audit Log > Syslog.
bestPracticeDescription: Logging system messages to a syslog server is an effective form of fault management. Reviewing the logs and checking for critical messages are important aspects of troubleshooting and incident investigation.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Set up a syslog server and configure devices to send messages to the syslog server.
bestPracticeTitle: Logging to a syslog server not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the following in the SEL Policy:

1. Remote path.
2. Action with "Log Full".
3. "Clear On Backup" to be set to "yes".
bestPracticeDescription: The system event log (SEL) resides on the CIMC in NVRAM. It records most server-related events, such as over and under voltage, temperature events, fan events, and events from BIOS. The SEL is mainly used for troubleshooting purposes. The SEL file is approximately 40KB in size, and no further events can be recorded when it is full. It must be cleared before additional events can be recorded. You can use the SEL policy to backup the SEL to a remote server, and optionally clear the SEL after a backup operation occurs. Backup operations can be triggered based on specific actions, or they can occur at regular intervals. You can also manually backup or clear the SEL.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SEL policy should contain a remote destination with the "Log Full" and "Clear On Backup" action set.
bestPracticeTitle: UCS - System Event Log Policy not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-

!
feature vpc
!
interface port-channel1
  switchport
  switchport mode trunk
  switchport trunk allowed vlan 10-20
  spanning-tree port type network
  vpc peer-link
!
bestPracticeDescription: A virtual port channel (vPC) allows links that are physically connected to two different Cisco Nexus Series devices to appear as a single port channel by a third device (see figure). The third device can be a switch, server, or any other networking device that supports port channels. A vPC can provide Layer 2 multipathing, which allows you to create redundancy and increase the bisectional bandwidth by enabling multiple parallel paths between nodes and allowing load balancing traffic.One important aspect related to vPC peer-link is VLAN pruning. For a vPC VLAN to become operational, it must be defined on vPC peer-link.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to perform VLAN pruning on vPC peer-link with allowed list of vPC VLAN. vPC VLAN must have been pruned on vPC member port previously.
bestPracticeTitle: VLAN Pruning Not Enabled On A vPC Peer Link

bestPracticeCaveat: You must have admin or aaa privileges to enable the password strength check.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: "set enforce-strong-password yes " should be enabled.
bestPracticeDescription: Each locally authenticated user account requires a password. A user with admin or aaa privileges can configure Cisco UCS Manager to perform a password strength check on user passwords. Cisco recommends using a strong password; otherwise, the password strength check for locally authenticated users, Cisco UCS Manager rejects any password that does not meet the requirement. Refer link to know the password requirements: https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/unified_computing/ucs/ucs-manager/GUI-User-Guides/Admin-Management/3-1/b_Cisco_UCS_Admin_Mgmt_Guide_3_1/b_Cisco_UCS_Admin_Mgmt_Guide_3_1_chapter_011.html#concept_3F744DE7A5E54EF693EAFE2F108B674D A locally authenticated user account is authenticated directly through the fabric interconnect and can be enabled or disabled by anyone with admin or aaa privileges. After a local user account is disabled, the user cannot log in. The database does not delete the configuration details for disabled local user accounts. If you re-enable a disabled local user account, the account becomes active with the existing configuration, including the username and password.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Strong-password check should be enabled.
bestPracticeTitle: Password Strength Check Not Enabled For Locally Authenticated Users

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: config core file exporter
bestPracticeDescription: Critical failures in the Cisco UCS components, such as a fabric interconnect or an I/O module, can cause the system to create a coredump file. Cisco UCS Manager uses the Core File Exporter to immediately export the coredump files to a specified location on the network through TFTP. This functionality allows you to export the tar file with the contents of the coredump file. The CoreFile Exporter provides system monitoring and automatic export of coredump files that need to be included in TAC cases.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
BP Recommendation : 
Trivial File Transfer Protocol (TFTP) is the recommended method to configure a core dump. 

In order to use TFTP to configure CoreFile Exporter on the UCS for a core dump, use these commands:

Using CLI:
scope monitoring
	scope sysdebug
	enable
	core-export-target
	set core-export-target path /root/CoreFiles/core
	set core-export-target port 45000
	set core-export-target server-description CoreFile192.168.10.10
	set core-export-target server-name 192.168.10.10
	commit-buffer

Using GUI:
Step 1 In the Navigation pane, click Admin.
Step 2 Expand All > Faults, Events, and Audit Log.
Step 3 Click Settings.
Step 4 In the Work pane, click the TFTP Core Exporter tab.
Step 5 On the TFTP Core Exporter tab, complete the following fields:
   Admin State field
   Description field
   Port field
   Hostname field
   Path field
Step 6 Click Save Changes.
bestPracticeTitle: TFTP Core Exporter not enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Specify at least one AAA provider and create an appropriate Authentication domain.
bestPracticeDescription: RBAC - Overview: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is a method of restricting or authorizing system access for users based on user roles and locales. A role defines the privileges of a user in the system and the locale defines the organizations (domains) that a user is allowed access. Because users are not directly assigned privileges, management of individual user privileges is a matter of assigning the appropriate roles and locales. A user is granted write access to desired system resources only if the assigned role grants the access privileges and the assigned locale allows access. For example, a user with the Server Administrator role in the Engineering organization could update server configurations in the Engineering organization but could not update server configurations in the Finance organization unless the locales assigned to the user include the Finance organization. UCS - Overview: UCS supports TACACS+/Radius/LDAP providers in addition to local user authentication; multiple providers can be specified within a provider group for redundancy.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure a TACACS+/Radius or LDAP Provider which should be specified within an Authentication domain to utilise AAA services.
bestPracticeTitle: AAA not configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
CLI Steps
scope org

     set auto-sync user-acknowledge

GUI Steps
Step 1	
In the Navigation pane, click Equipment.

Step 2	
Click the Equipment node.

Step 3	
In the Work pane, click the Policies tab.

Step 4	
Click the Global Policies subtab.

Step 5	
In the Firmware Auto Sync Server Policy area, select one of the following values as the Sync State:

No Action?No firmware upgrade is initiated on the server.

User Acknowledge?Firmware on the server is not synchronized until the administrator acknowledges the upgrade in the Pending Activities dialog box.
This option is selected by default.

bestPracticeDescription: Firmware auto-sync feature does the following: Every time you install a new server in an existing UCS environment, the server will most probably be delivered with a different firmware version than the one that is already running on the rest of the servers. The firmware auto-sync option will initiate an automatic firmware upgrade to the default version in the system. It is recommended to enable this feature with the option "user-ack". In this way the system will not upgrade the server automatically but will ask for approval first.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Enable the firmware-auto-sync feature with user-ack option.
bestPracticeTitle: Firmware Auto-Sync User-Ack Is Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers may require larger session limit values due to different management implementations and use cases; in which case the session limit should be configured to the lowest level at which normal operational functions are achievable.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Reduce the number of web sessions as follows:

User Session Limit:  2
Total Session Limit: 5
bestPracticeDescription: Shell session limits are configurable for the entire system and per user. The default for the system is 32 and 32 for each user. It is suggested to have a limit on the number of user sessions and a limit for the maximum amount of connections for the total system based on how many users there are. You do not want one user to be able to take complete control of access to the system.
bestPracticeRecommendation: User/Total Shell Session limit should be reduced from the default (32/32).

bestPracticeCaveat: Some customers may require larger session limit values due to different management implementations and use cases; in which case the session limit should be configured to the lowest level at which normal operational functions are achievable.

You do not want one user to be able to take complete control of access to the system.

bestPracticeDescription: The log file exporter allows you to export the log files to a remote server before they are deleted. If you do not enable log exporting, the oldest log files are deleted whenever the maximum backup file limit (5) is reached. Guidelines and limitations: On a HA setup, the log files from each side are exported separately. If one side fails to export logs, the other side does not compensate.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends that log file exporter is configured where possible to assist in troubleshooting and improve monitoring capabilities.
bestPracticeTitle: UCS Log File Exporter Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as given below :
!
feature bgp
 router bgp 100
!
bestPracticeDescription: Enabling BGP will allocate internal resources to the process. It is recommended not to enable the BGP feature unless you want to use it. BGP uses TCP port 179 to create a TCP session with a peer. When a TCP connection is established between peers, each BGP peer initially exchanges all of its routes?the complete BGP routing table?with the other peer. After this initial exchange, the BGP peers send only incremental updates when a topology change occurs in the network or when a routing policy change occurs. In the periods of inactivity between these updates, peers exchange special messages called keepalives .
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to enable the bgp feature only when the feature needs to be used.
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Enabled But Not In Use

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
scope org  
   enter lacppolicy default
         set suspend-individual true
bestPracticeDescription: By default, UCS will put physical uplink ports in a port-channel in an individual state when bundling fails. This can cause undesired behavior like Layer 2 loops on network-to-network links. Therefore, it is recommended to apply LACP policy with 'lacp suspend-individual' enabled for all LAN port-channel uplinks. When you set suspend-individual on a port-channel with lacp, if a port that is part of the port-channel does not receive PDUs from the peer port, it will go into suspended state.
bestPracticeRecommendation: For all LAN port-channel uplinks, LACP policy should be applied with 'lacp suspend-individual' enabled.
bestPracticeTitle: LACP Suspend Individual Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
UCS Manager GUI: 
Step 1  	In the Navigation pane, click Admin.

bestPracticeDescription: By default, if user roles are not configured in Cisco UCS Manager read-only access is granted to all users logging in to Cisco UCS Manager from a remote server using the LDAP, RADIUS, or TACACS protocols. For security reasons, it might be desirable to restrict access to those users matching an established user role in Cisco UCS Manager.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'create pre-login-banner' in scope security/banner.
bestPracticeDescription: Usage of system banners provides acceptable use parameters and can have legal basis that depend on local jurisdiction. The 'create pre-login-banner' command is used to define and enable message-of-the-day (MOTD) banner. Banners are not enabled by default. This banner shows after a user has logged into the device with valid credentials, this message should typically be a maintenance message telling authorized users any planned works or reminding them this is for official work only.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Use the 'create pre-login-banner' to configure appropriate notices to state specifically what constitutes authorized use and the actions taken for unauthorized access. Do not advertise site name or network information that may provide information to unauthorized users.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure a full state, all config or both export policies:

     scope backup-policy default
         set adminstate enable
         set descr "Database Backup Policy"
         set hostname 1.1.1.1
         set protocol ftp
 !       set password
         set remote-file backup.bak
         set schedule daily
         set user test
     exit

     scope cfg-export-policy default
         set adminstate enable
         set descr "Configuration Export Policy"
         set hostname 1.1.1.1
         set protocol ftp
 !       set password
         set remote-file backup.bak
         set schedule daily
         set user test
bestPracticeDescription: Scheduled Backup You can create a backup operation in advance and leave the admin state disabled until you are ready to run the backup. Cisco UCS Manager does not run the backup operation, save, or export the configuration file until you set the admin state of the backup operation to enable. You can configure policies in Cisco UCS to schedule the following types of backups: Full state All configuration Full State Backup Policy The full state backup policy allows you to schedule regular full state backups of a snapshot of the entire system. You can choose whether to configure the full state backup to occur on a daily, weekly, or biweekly basis. Cisco UCS Manager maintains a maximum number of backup files on the remote server. The maxfiles parameter is used when Cisco UCS Manager is registered with Cisco UCS Central. The maxfiles parameter is user configurable on Cisco UCS Central and controls the number of backup files stored on Cisco UCS Central. If Cisco UCS Manager is not registered with Cisco UCS Central, and the user is storing backup files on a remote backup server, the backup files are not managed by Cisco UCS Manager. The remote machine server administrator must monitor the disk usage and rotate the backup files to create space for new backup files. Note: You can only use a full state backup file to restore a system that is running the same version as the system from which the backup file was exported. All Configuration Export Policy The all configuration backup policy allows you to schedule a regular backup and export of all system and logical configuration settings. This backup does not include passwords for locally authenticated users. You can choose whether to configure the all configuration backup to occur on a daily, weekly, or bi-weekly basis. Cisco UCS maintains a maximum number of backup files on the remote server. When that number is exceeded, Cisco UCS overwrites the oldest backup file.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure a recurring backup job, to periodically save the config to an external location.
bestPracticeTitle: No Scheduled Backup Jobs Configured

bestPracticeCaveat: Check the latest version available at the time of IC reporting
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Step 1  	In the Navigation pane, click the Servers tab.
Step 2  	On the Servers tab, expand Servers > Service Profile Templates.
Step 3  	Expand the node for the organization where you want to create the service profile template.
If the system does not include multi-tenancy, expand the root node.

Step 4  	Right-click the organization and select Create Service Profile Template.
bestPracticeDescription: With a service profile template, you can quickly create several service profiles with the same basic parameters, such as the number of vNICs and vHBAs, and with identity information drawn from the same pools. For example, if you need several service profiles with similar values to configure servers to host database software, you can create a service profile template, either manually or from an existing service profile. You then use the template to create the service profiles. Cisco UCS supports the following types of service profile templates: Initial template Service profiles created from an initial template inherit all the properties of the template. However, after you create the profile, it is no longer connected to the template. If you need to make changes to one or more profiles created from this template, you must change each profile individually. Updating template Service profiles created from an updating template inherit all the properties of the template and remain connected to the template. Any changes to the template automatically update the service profiles created from the template.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends to create all the service profiles using Service profile templates. 

Reason: For swift deployment of service profiles and any changes to service profiles can be done via updating service profile templates.
bestPracticeTitle: Service Profiles Not Created Using Service Profile Templates

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Creating sub-organizations under root or under sub-organization to achieve a logical isolation between organizations without providing a dedicated physical infrastructure for each organization.

Creating an Organization under the Root Organization 

Procedure:

Step 1: On the toolbar, choose New > Create Organization. 
Step 2: In the Name field of the Create Organization dialog box, enter a unique name for the organization. 
This name can be between 1 and 16 alphanumeric characters. You cannot use spaces or any special characters other than - (hyphen), _ (underscore), : (colon), and . (period), and you cannot change this name after the object is saved. 
Step 3: In the Description field, enter a description for the organization. 
Step 4: Click OK. 


Creating an Organization under a Sub-Organization

Procedure:

Step 1: In the Navigation pane, click Servers. 
Step 2: Expand Service Profiles > root. 
You can also access the Sub-Organizations node under the Policies or Pools nodes. 
Step 3: Expand the Sub-Organizations node and do one of the following: 
? To create an organization directly under root, right-click Sub-Organizations and choose Create Organization. 
? To create an organization under a lower-level sub-organization, expand the sub-organization nodes in the hierarchy and then right-click the sub-organization under which you want to create the new organization and choose Create Organization. 
Step 4: In the Name field of the Create Organization dialog box, enter a unique name for the organization. 
This name can be between 1 and 16 alphanumeric characters. You cannot use spaces or any special characters other than - (hyphen), _ (underscore), : (colon), and . (period), and you cannot change this name after the object is saved. 
Step 5: In the Description field, enter a description for the organization. 
Step 6: Click OK.
bestPracticeDescription: Multi-tenancy allows you to divide the large physical infrastructure of an Cisco UCS domain into logical entities known as organizations. You can assign unique resources to each tenant through the related organization in the multi-tenant environment. These resources can include different policies, pools, and quality of service definitions. You can also implement locales to assign or restrict user privileges and roles by organization, if you do not want all users to have access to all organizations. If you set up a multi-tenant environment, all organizations are hierarchical. The top-level organization is always root and we can create sub-org under that. However, any policies and pools created in other organizations are only available to organizations that are above it in the same hierarchy.
bestPracticeRecommendation: create a sub-organization under ?root? directory
bestPracticeTitle: Sub Organization Not Created Under Root

https://www.cisco.com/en/US/docs/unified_computing/ucs/sw/gui/config/guide/141/UCSM_GUI_Configuration_Guide_141_chapter26.html
bestPracticeDescription: Use of default policy as a name does not provide quick description to what is the function of the policy. From boot policy, maintenance policy to all the other policies exist in UCS they mostly have something in common "default policy". the policy name cannot be changed. In order to find out what are the properties of the policy, you have to click on the policy and navigate and find out what is the purpose and function of the policy before you can apply it to service profile template.

bestPracticeCaveat:
In the event of a management interface failure on a fabric interconnect, the managing instance may not change if one of the following occurs:
A path to the end-point through the subordinate fabric interconnect does not exist.
The management interface for the subordinate fabric interconnect has failed.
The path to the end-point through the subordinate fabric interconnect has failed.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
scope monitoring
   set mgmt-if-mon-policy admin-state enabled
bestPracticeDescription: This policy defines how the mgmt0 Ethernet interface on the fabric interconnect should be monitored. If Cisco UCS detects a management interface failure, a failure report is generated. If the configured number of failure reports is reached, the system assumes that the management interface is unavailable and generates a fault. By default, the management interfaces monitoring policy is disabled. In the event of a management interface failure on a fabric interconnect, the managing instance may not change if one of the following occurs: A path to the end-point through the subordinate fabric interconnect does not exist. The management interface for the subordinate fabric interconnect has failed. The path to the end-point through the subordinate fabric interconnect has failed.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
Cisco recommends enabling the feature.

Configuring the Management Interfaces Monitoring Policy

Procedure

Step 1   	Enter monitoring mode.

UCS-A# scope monitoring

Step 2   	Enable or disable the management interfaces monitoring policy.

UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy admin-state {enabled | disabled}

Step 3   	Specify the number of seconds that the system should wait between data recordings.

UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy poll-interval

Enter an integer between 90 and 300.

Step 4   	Specify the maximum number of monitoring attempts that can fail before the system assumes that the management interface is unavailable and generates a fault message.

UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy max-fail-reports num-mon-attempts

Enter an integer between 2 and 5.
Step 5   	Specify the monitoring mechanism that you want the system to use. UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy monitor-mechanism {mii-status | ping-arp-targets | ping-gateway

    mii-status ?The system monitors the availability of the Media Independent Interface (MII).

    ping-arp-targets ?The system pings designated targets using the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP).

    ping-gateway ?The system pings the default gateway address specified for this Cisco UCS instance in the management interface.

Step 6   	If you selected mii-status as your monitoring mechanism, configure the following properties:

    Specify the number of seconds that the system should wait before requesting another response from the MII if a previous attempt fails.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy mii-retry-interval num-seconds

    Enter an integer between 3 and 10.
    Specify the number of times that the system polls the MII until the system assumes that the interface is unavailable.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy mii-retry-count num-retries

    Enter an integer between 1 and 3.

Step 7   	If you selected ping-arp-targets as your monitoring mechanism, configure the following properties:

    Specify the first IP address the system pings.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy arp-target1 ip-addr

    Type 0.0.0.0 to remove the ARP target.
    Specify the second IP address the system pings.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy arp-target2 ip-addr

    Type 0.0.0.0 to remove the ARP target.
    Specify the third IP address the system pings.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy arp-target3 ip-addr

    Type 0.0.0.0 to remove the ARP target.
    Specify the number of ARP requests to send to the target IP addresses.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy arp-requests num-requests

    Enter an integer between 1 and 5.
    Specify the number of seconds to wait for responses from the ARP targets before the system assumes that they are unavailable.

 UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy arp-deadline num-seconds

    Enter a number between 5 and 15.

Step 8   	If you selected ping-gateway as your monitoring mechanism, configure the following properties:

    Specify the number of times the system should ping the gateway.

  UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy ping-requests

    Enter an integer between 1 and 5.
    Specify the number of seconds to wait for a response from the gateway until the system assumes that the address is unavailable.

  UCS-A /monitoring # set mgmt-if-mon-policy ping-deadline

    Enter an integer between 5 and 15.

Step 9   	Commit the transaction to the system configuration.

  UCS-A /monitoring # commit-buffer
bestPracticeTitle: Management Interface Monitoring Policy Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
set the maintenance policy to user-ack

Also,  verify that the policy used is applied to their service profiles or not.
bestPracticeDescription: The maintenance policy specifies how Cisco UCS Manager deploys the service profile changes. The deployment can occur in one of the following ways: Immediately When acknowledged by a user with administrator privileges Automatically at the time specified in a schedule When the option(user-ack) is selected, disruptive changes are staged to each affected service profile, but the profile is not immediately rebooted. Instead, each profile will show the pending changes in its status field, and will wait for the administrator to manually acknowledge the changes when it is acceptable to reboot the node. This will bypass any critical incident from happening because with immediate option, the server reboots as soon as the change is made to the service profile.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure user-ack option for Maintenance policies applied to service profiles.
bestPracticeTitle: Maintenance Policy Not Set To user-ack

bestPracticeCaveat: Ethernet links with a switch between and where multiple OSPFv3 neighbors are present on the broadcast media would be left as network type broadcast.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface x
 ospfv3 network point-to-point
OR 
 medium p2p
bestPracticeDescription:
A Layer 3 interface (including 10Mbps, 100Mbps, 1Gbps, 10Gbps, 100Gbps Ethernet, Link Bundle or Link Aggregation) is typically configured as a point-to-point connection to another router. It is unusual to have a layer 2 switch in between two routers connected via Ethernet links. 

OSPFv3 classifies different media into the following types of networks:
 NBMA networks
 Point-to-point networks (POS)
 Broadcast networks (Ethernet, Port-channel and VLAN)
 Point-to-multipoint

Use of the point-to-point network type does not require DR/BDR election and uses fewer LSAs. Network convergence is faster and less resources are consumed when the media is configured as an OSPFv3 point-to-point network type instead of the broadcast network type, which is the default for Ethernet, Port-channel and VLAN interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure Layer 3 interfaces as medium p2p whenever possible.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPFv3 Network Type Not Point-to-Point

bestPracticeCaveat: Ethernet links with a switch between and where multiple OSPF neighbors are present on the broadcast media would be left as network type broadcast.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
interface x
 ip ospf network point-to-point
OR 
 medium p2p
bestPracticeDescription:
A Layer 3 interface (including 10Mbps, 100Mbps, 1Gbps, 10Gbps, 100Gbps Ethernet, Link Bundle or Link Aggregation) is typically configured as a P2P connection to another router. It's unusual to have a Layer 2 switch in between two routers connected via Ethernet links. 

OSPF classifies different media into the following types of networks:
 NBMA networks
 P2P networks (POS)
 Broadcast networks (Ethernet, Port-channel and VLAN)
 Point-to-multipoint

Use of the P2P network type does not require DR/BDR election and uses fewer OSPF LSAs. Network convergence is faster and less resources are consumed when the media is configured as an OSPF P2P network type instead of the broadcast network type, which is the default for Ethernet, Port-channel and VLAN interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Configure Layer 3 Interfaces as P2P whenever possible.
bestPracticeTitle: OSPF Network Type Not Point-to-Point

bestPracticeCaveat: To avoid service disruptions, you should not disable the grace period before you install a permanent license.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'no license grace-period'
bestPracticeDescription: The Cisco NX-OS software gives you a 120-day grace period. This grace period starts or continues when you are evaluating a feature for which you have not installed a license. The grace period stops if you disable a feature that you are evaluating, but if you enable that feature again without a valid license, the grace period countdown continues where it left off. The grace period operates across all features in a license package. License packages can contain several features. If you disable a feature during the grace period and there are other features in that license package that are still enabled, the countdown does not stop for that license package. To suspend the grace period countdown for a license package, you must disable every feature in that license package. The Cisco NX-OS license counter keeps track of all licenses on a device. If you are evaluating a feature and the grace period has started, you will receive console messages, SNMP traps, system messages, and Call Home messages on a daily basis. The frequency of these messages become hourly during the last seven days of the grace period.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Disable license grace period to prevent use of a feature that does not have a permanent license.
bestPracticeTitle: License Grace Period not disabled (N5K and N6K)

bestPracticeCaveat: Not necessary if HTTP is disabled. In this case UCSM will only be managed via the secure HTTPs.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Do not change the default behaviour. Make sure HTTP to HTTPs redirection is left enabled.

Procedure:
Step 1	
In the Navigation pane, click the Admin menu.

Step 2	
In the Admin menu, click Communication Services.

Step 3	
In the HTTP Properties area, update the following properties:

Name	                                                     Description

HTTPS Enabled check box

                                                                    Warning 	
                                                                    Disabling this option terminates the exiting Cisco IMC Web GUI session. Disabling this option, disables both 
                                                                    HTTP and HTTPS services to access Cisco IMC.

                                                                    This option enables only HTTPS services to access Cisco IMC.

HTTP Enabled check box

                                                                    Warning 	
                                                                    To successfully save any changes for this option, Cisco IMC Web GUI is restarted automatically. Communication   
                                                                    with the management controller is lost momentarily and you must log in again after the restart.

                                                                    This option enables only HTTP services to access Cisco IMC.

                                                                     Note 	
                                                                     If HTTPS is disabled, HTTP services to access Cisco IMC are also disabled.

Redirect HTTP to HTTPS Enabled check box

                                                                     Note 	
                                                                     This option is applicable only when HTTP Enabled is checked.

                                                                    If enabled, all attempts to communicate via HTTP are redirected to the equivalent HTTPS address.

                                                                    We strongly recommend that you enable this option if you enable HTTP.



Step 4	
Click Save Changes.
bestPracticeDescription: UCSM Communication Services - HTTP This service is enabled on port 80 by default. You must enable either HTTP or HTTPS to run Cisco UCS Manager GUI. If you select HTTP, all data is exchanged in clear text mode.
bestPracticeRecommendation: By default, Cisco UCS redirects any attempt to communicate via HTTP to the HTTPS equivalent. We recommend that you do not change this behavior.
bestPracticeTitle: HTTP To HTTPS Redirection Disabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Remove vrf globally if not in use:
no vrf context
bestPracticeDescription: A VRF is used as a container to create/separate virtual routing tables and communication channels for each user allowing for overlapping IP address and secured communication. VRF's can be associated with Routing Protocols, Interfaces, Clock Source, SNMP, ACLs etc. VRFs utilize the system resources and hence they should only be enabled, if required.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Creating VRFs will utilize the system resources and hence they should only be enabled, if required.
bestPracticeTitle: VRF Configured Globally But Not In Use

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
scope system    
     set description UCS
     set owner Cisco
     set site Cisco
bestPracticeDescription: In an environment where multiple domains are in use system description, site, and owner should be used.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommended to configure a system owner/site/description.
bestPracticeTitle: UCS System Details Should Be Set

bestPracticeCaveat: Default route from upstream device can not be validated in current tool.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration is below :-
!
feature eigrp
!
router eigrp TEST
  address-family ipv4 unicast
    stub direct
!
bestPracticeDescription: A Nexus 5500 with an L3 GEM is designed to be used as an L3 access device. The amount of adjacencies it supports is limited by the FIB table of the given GEM module. For EIGRP the recommendation is to send a default route from the upstream devices and configure the L3 access N5500 as a stub.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure stub direct underneath the EIGRP process.
bestPracticeTitle: EIGRP Direct Stub Not Configured

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example configuration as below :-
!
policy-map Cisco
!
interface Ethernet1/1
 service-policy type qos input Cisco
!
bestPracticeDescription: Policy-map?Defines a policy map that represents a set of policies to be applied on a class-by-class basis to class maps. The policy map defines a set of actions to take on the associated traffic class, such as limiting the bandwidth or dropping packets. Service policies are configured at the interface level, the interface-level policy always takes precedence over the system class configuration or defaults.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco Recommends to attach the policy map to the configuration using service policy
bestPracticeTitle: Policy Map Not Used

bestPracticeCaveat: Certain designs will dictate many routing processes. If this has been clearly thought through for the design, then it may be legitimate.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: It is recommended to evaluate the need for the protocols and optimize the network if possible to reduce potential for routing loops.
bestPracticeDescription: Although it is technically feasible to configure numerous routing processes on any given router, having too many routing processes/protocols unnecessarily consumes router memory and creates risks with route distribution.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to review the design to simplify the routing design wherever possible.
bestPracticeTitle: Too many routing processes

bestPracticeCaveat: Switchports will revert to shutdown state and will need to be configured with 'no shutdown'.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'system default switchport shutdown'
bestPracticeDescription: In NX-OS, you can specify whether or not an interface is administratively shutdown by default. It is recommended to keep interfaces shut down by default. To change the default interface mode for the system from Layer 3 routing to Layer 2 switching, use the 'system default switchport' command. To return the system to Layer 3 routing default interface mode, use the 'no' form of 'system default switchport' command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to keep interfaces shut down by default.
bestPracticeTitle: Ports Not Shutdown By Default

bestPracticeCaveat: Multicast is not utilized within the customers environment.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure Multicast Optimize to the relevant QoS class.
bestPracticeDescription: Multicast Optimization is an option to be configured on a per QoS basis for a class of traffic which contains multicast; the multicast optimization option allows for improved utilisation of traffic queues for multicast traffic. Optimized multicasting allows use of the unused multicast queues to achieve better throughput for multicast frames. If optimized multicast is enabled for the default drop system class, the system will use all 128 queues to service the multicast traffic. When optimized multicast is enabled for the default drop system class, all 128 queues are given equal priority. If you define a new system class, a dedicated multicast queue is assigned to that class. This queue is removed from the set of queues available for the optimized multicast class.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Multicast Optimization should be enabled  for a class of traffic where relevant to assign multicast fan-outs in that class to any unused CoS queues on a round robin basis.
bestPracticeTitle: Multicast Optimization Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: If the vHBA is reconfigured when you bind it to a template, Cisco UCS Manager reboots the server associated with the service profile.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Binding a vHBA to a vHBA Template
1) In the Navigation pane, click the Servers tab.
2) On the Servers tab, expand Servers > Service Profiles.
3) Expand the node for the organization that includes the service profile with the vHBA you want to bind. If the system does not include multi-tenancy, expand the root node.
4) Expand Service_Profile_Name > vHBAs. 
5) Click the vHBA you want to bind to a template.
6) In the Work pane, click the General tab.
7) In the Actions area, click Bind to a Template.
8) In the Bind to a vHBA Template dialog box, do the following:
     a) From the vHBA Template drop-down list, choose the template to which you want to bind the vHBA. 
     b) Click OK.
9) In the warning dialog box, click Yes to acknowledge that Cisco UCS Manager may need to reboot the server if the binding causes the vHBA to be reconfigured.
bestPracticeDescription: You can bind a vHBA associated with a service profile to a vHBA template. When you bind the vHBA to a vHBA template, Cisco UCS Manager configures the vHBA with the values defined in the vHBA template. If the existing vHBA configuration does not match the vHBA template, Cisco UCS Manager reconfigures the vHBA. You can only change the configuration of a bound vHBA through the associated vHBA template. You cannot bind a vHBA to a vHBA template if the service profile that includes the vHBA is already bound to a service profile template. Cisco UCS supports the following types of vHBA templates: Initial Template?vHBAs created from this template are not updated if the template changes. Updating Template?vHBAs created from this template are updated if the template changes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Create all the vHBA's using the vHBA templates for swift deployment of vHBA's. Any changes to the vHBA's can be done via updating vHBA templates.
bestPracticeTitle: vHBA Not Created Using vHBA Templates

bestPracticeCaveat: If the vNIC is reconfigured when you bind it to a template, Cisco UCS Manager reboots the server associated with the service profile.
