9) In the warning dialog box, click Yes to acknowledge that Cisco UCS Manager may need to reboot the server if the binding causes the vNIC to be reconfigured.
bestPracticeDescription: You can bind a vNIC associated with a service profile to a vNIC template. When you bind the vNIC to a vNIC template, Cisco UCS Manager configures the vNIC with the values defined in the vNIC template. If the existing vNIC configuration does not match the vNIC template, Cisco UCS Manager reconfigures the vNIC. You can only change the configuration of a bound vNIC through the associated vNIC template. You cannot bind a vNIC to a vNIC template if the service profile that includes the vNIC is already bound to a service profile template. Cisco UCS supports the following types of vNIC templates: Initial Template?vNICs created from this template are not updated if the template changes. Updating Template?vNICs created from this template are updated if the template changes.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to create all the vNIC's using the vNIC templates for swift deployment of vNIC's. Any changes to the vNIC's can be done via updating vNIC templates.

bestPracticeCaveat: Syslog is used for operational management of these issues, instead of SNMP traps.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
In the global config mode:
snmp-server enable traps link
bestPracticeDescription: To enable the RFC 1157 SNMP notifications, use the 'snmp-server enable traps snmp' command in global configuration mode. When you use the optional 'Linkup' keyword, the Linkup trap signifies that the sending device recognizes one of the communication links represented in the agent's configuration coming up. When you use the optional 'Linkdown' keyword, the Linkdown trap signifies that the sending device recognizes a failure in one of the communication links represented in the agent's configuration.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. Enable the Linkup and Linkdown traps to ensure that notifications for linkup and linkdown events are sent to Network Management systems.
bestPracticeTitle: Linkup and Linkdown SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
1. For FNF records, export the interface table for FNF. The option interface-table command enables the
periodic sending of an options table. This provides interface names through the NetFlow export.
So, we can periodically (typically every 10 minutes) send this ?lookup table? of IfIndex numbers to names by attaching the 'interface-table' to the flow exporter configuration.

flow exporter [exporter name]
 option interface-table
 template data timeout 600

2. If you are using an NBAR flow record, export the NBAR application table. The option application-table
command enables the periodic sending of an options table that allows the collector to map the NBAR application
IDs provided in the flow records to application names.

flow exporter [exporter name]
 option application-table 

3. If you are using an NBAR flow record, export the NBAR application attributes. The option application attributes
command causes the periodic sending of NBAR application attributes to the collector.

flow exporter [exporter name]
 option application-attributes

Example : 
!
flow exporter Test
 template data timeout 
 option interface-table
 option application-table
 option application-attributes
!
bestPracticeDescription: The NetFlow data that is stored in the cache of the network device can be more effectively analyzed when exported to an external collector. Creating a flow exporter with options is only required when exporting data to an external collector.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to create Flow exporter with options when exporting data to external netflow exporter.
bestPracticeTitle: Recommended FNF ( Flexible Netflow) exporter with options

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration as below 

buffers huge size 64000
bestPracticeDescription: With large number of group members (1000+) and large policy statements, the COOP and rekey message sizes can become quite large. Small buffers prevent messages from being transmitted efficiently and increase the potential for failed transmission of announcement messages and rekey messages.The COOP Announcement packets carry the GM database information, and thus can grow big in a large GETVPN deployment. From past experience, a GETVPN network that consists of 1500+ GMs will produce Announcement packets larger than 18024 bytes, which is the Cisco IOS default Huge buffer size. Cisco Group Encrypted Transport VPN (GET VPN) is a set of features that are necessary to secure IP multicast group traffic or unicast traffic over a private WAN that originates on or flows through a Cisco IOS device. GET VPN combines the keying protocol Group Domain of Interpretation (GDOI) with IP security (IPsec) encryption to provide users with an efficient method to secure IP multicast traffic or unicast traffic. GET VPN enables the router to apply encryption to nontunneled (that is, ?native?) IP multicast and unicast packets and eliminates the requirement to configure tunnels to protect multicast and unicast traffic.
bestPracticePrimaryTechnology: *Security and VPN^IPSec Architecture and Implementation^Group Encrypted Transport VPN (GETVPN)
bestPracticeRecommendation: The HUGE buffer sizes should be set to the maximum values of 64000.
bestPracticeTitle: GETVPN buffer huge size for COOP

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Use the 'snmp-server group   v3' command to enable SNMPv3
bestPracticeDescription: SNMP Version 3 is not enabled on the device.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended that SNMPv3 be enabled. SNMPv3 is more secure as it has features such as Authentication, Privacy (Encryption), Authorization and Access Controls (Views).

The timeout value is specified in minutes and seconds. You will set the timeout value to 15 minutes and 0 seconds on vty lines 0 through 4 using the exec-timeout command.

The grace period stops if you disable a feature that you are evaluating, but if you enable that feature again without a valid license, the grace period countdown continues where it left off. If you disable a feature during the grace period and there are other features in that license package that are still enabled, the countdown does not stop for that license package. To suspend the grace period countdown for a license package, you must disable every feature in that license package. The Cisco NX-OS license counter keeps track of all licenses on a device. If you are evaluating a feature and the grace period has started, you will receive console messages, SNMP traps, system messages, and Call Home messages on a daily basis. The frequency of these messages become hourly during the last seven days of the grace period.

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
conf t
snmp-server mibs cbqosmib persist
bestPracticeDescription: To enable persistent storage of the CISCO-CLASS-BASED-QOS-MIB data across process restarts, switchovers, and device reloads, use the 'snmp-server mibs cbqosmib persist' command in global configuration mode. Without 'mibs persist' for the sbqosmib, monitoring platforms may not report the MIB values correctly if an upgrade, switchover, SMU, process restart etc. occurs. This is similar the the 'ifindex persist' command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to use 'snmp-server mibs cbqosmib persist' command in global configuration mode if SNMP is being used in the configuration.
bestPracticeTitle: snmp-server mibs cbqosmib persist Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat:
Enter the following commands in global configuration mode before configuring TiLFA or Microloop Avoidance:

Router(config)# ipv4 unnumbered mpls traffic-eng Loopback0
Router(config)# mpls traffic-eng
Router(config-mpls-te)# exit
Router(config)#
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable 'microloop avoidance segment-routing' under router IS-IS where Ti-LFA has been enabled. Below is how the configuration snippet will look:

router isis cisco
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  microloop avoidance segment-routing
  segment-routing mpls
 !
 interface GigabitEthernet 0/0/0/0
  address-family ipv4 unicast
   fast-reroute per-prefix
   fast-reroute per-prefix ti-lfa
bestPracticeDescription: Topology-Independent Loop-Free Alternate (TI-LFA) uses segment routing to provide link, node, and Shared Risk Link Groups (SRLG) protection in topologies where other fast reroute techniques cannot provide protection. Microloops are brief packet loops that occur in the network following a topology change (link down, link up, or metric change events). Microloops are caused by the non-simultaneous convergence of different nodes in the network. If nodes converge and send traffic to a neighbor node that has not converged yet, traffic may be looped between these two nodes, resulting in packet loss, jitter, and out-of-order packets. The Segment Routing Microloop Avoidance feature detects if microloops are possible following a topology change. If a node computes that a microloop could occur on the new topology, the node creates a loop-free SR-TE policy path to the destination using a list of segments. After the RIB update delay timer expires, the SR-TE policy is replaced with regular forwarding paths.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The main goal to implement TI-LFA on the network is to provide rapid restoration services. TI-LFA covers well scenarios with link failures. Yet, it is possible for microloops (uloops) to occur during network events (new links come up, etc). It is recommended to enable both TI-LFA and uloop avoidance. With uloop avoidance enabled, even after IGP converges over the new network topology, RIB/FIB holds the TI-LFA provided backup path and delays an update allowing other network nodes to converge.
bestPracticeTitle: Microloop Avoidance Not Configured with TiLFA for IS-IS

bestPracticeCaveat: The SRGB upper bound cannot exceed the platform's capability.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
The SRGB can be configured as follows:

Enter the lowest value that you want the SRGB range to include as the starting value. Enter the highest value that you want the SRGB range to include as the ending value.


For Example:
Device(config)# router isis 1
Device(config-isis)#segment-routing global-block 45000 55000

No form of this command:
Device(config)# router isis 1
Device(config-isis)#no segment-routing global-block 45000 55000

SRGB can also be configured globally on the SR enabled router.
bestPracticeDescription: The most generic and high-level guiding principle for Segment Routing Global Block space allocation is to keep it consistent for the whole network. Regardless of how SIDs are assigned in the network, either per domain or continuous for the entire network, the recommended way is to keep SRGB the same on all routers. Default SRGB range is 16000 through 23999. When Segment Routing is enabled on a router, this SRGB default range is used.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure SRGB explicitly even if the default SRGB range is used. To ensure control plane correct operation sufficient MPLS label space should remain available for other protocols, such as BGP VPNv4, BGP-LU, LDP, RSVP, if they are configured on this router.
bestPracticeTitle: Segment Routing Global Block Range is Not Defined for IS-IS

bestPracticeCaveat: The SRGB upper bound cannot exceed the platform's capability.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'segment-routing global-block <> <>' under router ospf to enable SRGB. Also can be enabled globally under segment-routing configuration mode.
bestPracticeDescription: The most generic and high-level guiding principle for Segment Routing Global Block (SRGB) space allocation is to keep it consistent for the whole network. Regardless of how SIDs are assigned in the network, either per domain or continuous for the entire network, the recommended way is to keep SRGB the same on all routers. Default SRGB range is 16000 through 23999. When Segment Routing is enabled on a router, this SRGB default range is used. However, It is recommended to configure SRGB explicitly even if the default SRGB range is used. To ensure control plane correct operation sufficient MPLS label space should remain available for other protocols, such as BGP VPNv4, BGP-LU, LDP, RSVP, if they are configured on this router.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure SRGB explicitly even if the default SRGB range is used. To ensure control plane correct operation sufficient MPLS label space should remain available for other protocols, such as BGP VPNv4, BGP-LU, LDP, RSVP, if they are configured on this router.
bestPracticeTitle: Segment Routing Global Block Range is Not Defined for OSPF

bestPracticeCaveat: Neither Novell NetWare nor AppleTalk is appropriately robust in response to packet loss, therefore you should not use RED for them.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the RED to avoid congestion.
Example:
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router# configure
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config)# policy-map policy1
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap)# class class1
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# random-detect default
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# bandwidth percent 30
or
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# bandwidth remaining percent 20
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# shape average percent 50
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# exit
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap)# exit
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config)# interface TenGigE 0/2/0/0
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-if)# service-policy output policy1
Use the commit or end command.
bestPracticeDescription: Underlying the RED mechanism is the premise that most traffic runs on data transport implementations that are sensitive to loss and will temporarily slow down when some of their traffic is dropped. TCP, which responds appropriately?even robustly?to traffic drop by slowing down its traffic transmission, effectively allows the traffic-drop behavior of RED to work as a congestion-avoidance signalling mechanism. The Random Early Detection (RED) congestion avoidance technique takes advantage of the congestion control mechanism of TCP. By randomly dropping packets prior to periods of high congestion, RED tells the packet source to decrease its transmission rate. Assuming the packet source is using TCP, it decreases its transmission rate until all packets reach their destination, indicating that the congestion is cleared. You can use RED as a way to cause TCP to slow transmission of packets. TCP not only pauses, but it also restarts quickly and adapts its transmission rate to the rate that the network can support. RED distributes losses in time and maintains normally low queue depth while absorbing traffic bursts. When enabled on an interface, RED begins dropping packets when congestion occurs at a rate you select during configuration.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use RED to monitor traffic flow in an effort to anticipate and avoid congestion at common network bottlenecks.
bestPracticeTitle: Random Early Detection (RED) not configured

bestPracticeCaveat:
WRED is supported only in network port queues and is not supported on internal CPU queues and stack
queues.
Each WRED physical queue can support three subqueues, with unique WRED threshold pair configuration.
WRED for priority traffic is not supported.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the WRED to avoid congestion.
Example:
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router# configure
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config)# policy-map policy1
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap)# class class1
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# random-detect dscp af11 1000000 bytes 2000000 bytes
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# bandwidth percent 30
or
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# bandwidth remaining percent 20
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# bandwidth percent 30
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# bandwidth remaining percent 20
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# shape average percent 50
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# queue-limit 50 ms
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap-c)# exit
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-pmap)# exit
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config)# interface TenGigE 0/2/0/0
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-if)# service-policy output policy1
Use the commit or end command.
bestPracticeDescription: Queue-limit is used to fine-tune the number of buffers available for each queue. It can only be used on a queuing class. Default queue limit is 100 ms of the service rate for the given queue. The service rate is the sum of minimum guaranteed bandwidth and bandwidth remaining assigned to a given class either implicitly or explicitly.The queue-limit is rounded up to the nearest power of 2, and depending on the line cards on your system, the queue-limit values vary. To check the current queue-limit for class-default, use the show qos interface command. Because WRED needs a queue to operate on, the class that WRED is applied on must have either a bandwidth statement or a parent policy with a shaper if WRED is applied only on a class default queue.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use WRED to monitor traffic flow in an effort to anticipate and avoid congestion at common network bottlenecks.
bestPracticeTitle: Weighted Random Early Detection (WRED) not configured

bestPracticeCaveat: When configuring the queue-limit command in a class, you must configure one of the following commands: priority , shape average , bandwidth , or bandwidth remaining , except for the default class.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configuration is given below :
!
policy-map policy ABC 
 class classX
  queue-limit 1000000 bytes 
!
bestPracticeDescription: Tail drop is a congestion avoidance technique that drops packets when an output queue is full until congestion is eliminated. Tail drop treats all traffic flow equally and does not differentiate between classes of service. It manages the packets that are unclassified, placed into a first-in, first-out (FIFO) queue, and forwarded at a rate determined by the available underlying link bandwidth.The default queue-limit is set to bytes of 100 ms of queue bandwidth. The following formula is used to calculate the default queue limit (in bytes):??bytes = (100 ms / 1000 ms) * queue_bandwidth kbps)) / 8
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use tail drop to monitor traffic flow in an effort to anticipate and avoid congestion at common network bottlenecks.
bestPracticeTitle: ASR9K Tail Drop is not enable

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure authentication IPsec CLI under Router configuration mode for OSPFv3 using the following:
authentication ipsec spi [spi] [auth] [ 0 | 3 | 7 ] [key]

Configure authentication IPsec CLI under Interface configuration mode for OSPFv3 using the following:
authentication ipsec spi [spi] [auth] [ 0 | 3 | 7 ] [key]

Configure authentication IPsec CLI under Area configuration mode for OSPFv3 using the following:
area [area-num] authentication ipsec spi [spi] [auth] [ 0 | 3 | 7 ] [key]

You can specify the security policy index through spi and define the authentication algorithm through auth which can be md5 or sha1. Numbers 0, 3 and 7 specify the format of key.

Sample Config:

switch(config)# interface ethernet 2/1
switch(config-if)# no switchport
switch(config-if)# ipv6 router ospfv3 1 area 0
switch(config-if)# ospfv3 authentication ipsec spi 256 md5 0 11111111111111111111111111111111
bestPracticeDescription: When Open Shortest Path First version 3 (OSPFv3) runs on IPv6, OSPFv3 requires the IPv6 encapsulating security payload (ESP) header or IPv6 authentication header to ensure integrity, authentication, and confidentiality of routing exchanges. In order to ensure that OSPFv3 packets are not altered and resent to the device, causing the device to behave in a way not desired by its system administrators, OSPFv3 packets must be authenticated. OSPFv3 uses the IPsec secure socket to add authentication to OSPFv3 packets. OSPFv3 authentication can be configured at the following levels: 1. Router 2. Area 3. Interface
bestPracticeRecommendation: In order to ensure that OSPFv3 packets are not altered and resent to the device, causing the device to behave in a way not desired by its system administrators, Cisco recommends that OSPFv3 packets must be authenticated. In addition to that, IPsec is a framework of open standards for ensuring secure private communications over the Internet. Based on standards developed by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), IPsec ensures confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data communications across a public network. IPsec provides a necessary component of a standards-based, flexible solution for deploying a network-wide security policy.
bestPracticeTitle: IPv6 OSPFv3 Authentication not enabled using IPSEC

bestPracticeCaveat:
Enter the following commands in global configuration mode before configuring TiLFA or Microloop Avoidance:

Router(config)# ipv4 unnumbered mpls traffic-eng Loopback0
Router(config)# mpls traffic-eng
Router(config-mpls-te)# exit
Router(config)#
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Enable 'microloop avoidance segment-routing' under router ospf where Ti-LFA has been enabled. Below is how the configuration snippet will look:

!
router ospf cisco
 segment-routing mpls
 fast-reroute per-prefix
 fast-reroute per-prefix ti-lfa
 microloop avoidance segment-routing
 area 0
  mpls traffic-eng
  mpls traffic-eng router-id Loopback0
bestPracticeDescription: Topology-Independent Loop-Free Alternate (TI-LFA) uses segment routing to provide link, node, and Shared Risk Link Groups (SRLG) protection in topologies where other fast reroute techniques cannot provide protection. Microloops are brief packet loops that occur in the network following a topology change (link down, link up, or metric change events). Microloops are caused by the non-simultaneous convergence of different nodes in the network. If nodes converge and send traffic to a neighbor node that has not converged yet, traffic may be looped between these two nodes, resulting in packet loss, jitter, and out-of-order packets. The Segment Routing Microloop Avoidance feature detects if microloops are possible following a topology change. If a node computes that a microloop could occur on the new topology, the node creates a loop-free SR-TE policy path to the destination using a list of segments. After the RIB update delay timer expires, the SR-TE policy is replaced with regular forwarding paths.
bestPracticeRecommendation: The main goal to implement TI-LFA on the network is to provide rapid restoration services. TI-LFA covers well scenarios with link failures. Yet, it is possible for microloops (uloops) to occur during network events (new links come up, etc). It is recommended to enable both TI-LFA and uloop avoidance. With uloop avoidance enabled, even after IGP converges over the new network topology, RIB/FIB holds the TI-LFA provided backup path and delays an update allowing other network nodes to converge.
bestPracticeTitle: Microloop Avoidance Not Configured with TiLFA for OSPF

bestPracticeCaveat: Another effective management method is already in place and not based on SNMP Traps, such as accurate syslog event tracking and log correlation.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Enable IS-IS traps with the 'snmp-server enable traps isis' command with its options for restricting to specific types of events.
bestPracticeDescription: To enable Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) notifications for Integrated Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS), use the 'snmp-server enable traps isis' command with options in global configuration mode. IS-IS is an event driven routing protocol, where an event can be a change in an IS-IS interface's link-level status or the expiration of an IS-IS timer. Many of the actions that IS-IS takes as a result of these events will result in a change of the routing topology. As routing topologies become large and complex it is often difficult to locate the source of a topology change or unpredicted routing path by polling a large number or routers or relying on syslog messages alone. Another approach is to notify a network manager of potentially critical ISIS events with SNMP traps.
bestPracticeRecommendation: SNMP traps are vital for effective network management. When IS-IS is in use it is recommended to enable IS-IS SNMP traps to ensure that notifications for the appropriate events are sent to Network Management systems.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS SNMP Traps Not Enabled

bestPracticeCaveat: The lower the circuit speed, the higher the percentage of padding overhead.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Global Config
hello-padding {disable | sometimes} [level {1 | 2}]
no hello-padding [disable | sometimes] [level {1 | 2}

Interface Config
RP/0/0/CPU0:router(config)# router isis XXX
RP/0/0/CPU0:router(config-isis)# interface Bundle-Ether X
RP/0/0/CPU0:router(config-isis-if)# hello-padding disable
bestPracticeDescription: A user would disable hello padding in order avoid wasting network bandwidth in case the MTU of both interfaces are the same or, in case of translational bridging. While hello padding is disabled, Cisco routers still send the first five IS-IS hellos padded to the full MTU size. This is to maintain the benefits of discovering MTU mismatches. Consecutive hellos are no longer padded. To configure padding on Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS) hello protocol data units (IIH PDUs) for all IS-IS interfaces on the router, use the hello-padding command in interface configuration mode. To suppress padding, use the no form of this command. hello-padding {disable | sometimes} [level {1 | 2}] no hello-padding [disable | sometimes] [level {1 | 2}
bestPracticeRecommendation: Hello padding is enabled by default, it is recommended to disable hello padding. It is recommended to suppress hello padding to conserve network resources.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS Hello-padding is not Disabled

bestPracticeCaveat: MACsec needs to be running on the device
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Configure the cipher suite to be used for MACsec encryption.
Example:
Enter the global configuration mode, and enter a name (mac_policy) for the MACsec policy.
Example:
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router# configure 
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config)# macsec-policy mac_policy

Configure the cipher suite to be used for MACsec encryption.
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-mac_policy)# cipher-suite GCM-AES-XPN-256
RP/0/RSP0/CPU0:router(config-mac_policy)#GCM-AES-128 
GCM-AES-256 
GCM-AES-XPN-128 
GCM-AES-XPN-256
bestPracticeDescription: Security breaches can occur at any layer of the OSI model. At Layer 2, some of the common breaches at Layer 2 are MAC address spoofing, ARP spoofing, Denial of Service (DoS) attacks against a DHCP server, and VLAN hopping. MACsec secures data on physical media, making it impossible for data to be compromised at higher layers. As a result, MACsec encryption takes priority over any other encryption method such as IPsec and SSL, at higher layers. MACsec is configured on Customer Edge (CE) router interfaces that connect to Provider Edge (PE) routers and on all the provider router interfaces.
bestPracticeRecommendation: A 256-bit encryption algorithm uses a larger key that requires more rounds of hacking to be cracked. 256-bit algorithms provide better security against large mass security attacks, and include the security provided by 128-bit algorithms. Extended Packet Numbering (XPN) is used to reduce the number of key rollovers while data is sent over high speed links. It is therefore highly recommended to use GCM-AES-XPN-256 encryption algorithm for higher data ports.
bestPracticeTitle: 256 bit not used for cipher-suite for MACsec policy

bestPracticeDescription: Prevent flooding of unknown unicast traffic from the access interface to the EVPN core. MAC learning is the method of learning the MAC addresses of all devices available in a VLAN. The MAC addresses learned on one device needs to be learned or distributed on the other devices in a VLAN. EVPN Native with software MAC Learning feature enables the distribution of the MAC addresses learned on one device to the other devices connected to a network. The MAC addresses are learnt from the remote devices using BGP.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use unknown-unicast-suppress.
bestPracticeTitle: Unknown-Unicast-Suppress is Not Configured for EVPN

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
If the router is configured as a route-reflector then configure cluster-id as shown below:

conf t
router bgp xxx
bgp cluster-id yyyy
bestPracticeDescription: A route reflector and its clients form a cluster. A cluster of clients usually has a single route reflector. In such instances, the cluster is identified by the software as the router ID of the route reflector. To increase redundancy and avoid a single point of failure in the network, a cluster might have more than one route reflector. If it does, all route reflectors in the cluster must be configured with the same 4-byte cluster ID so that a route reflector can recognize updates from route reflectors in the same cluster. A single route reflector can also support multiple clusters. Each cluster is identified by a unique cluster-id. The cluster-id configured by the bgp cluster-id command is taken as the default. If bgp cluster-id is not configured, the router ID for the default VRF identifies the default cluster. A neighbor can be associated with one cluster only, and the corresponding cluster-id is configured in neighbor configuration mode. If the cluster-id is not configured for a neighbor and the neighbor is a route reflector client, then the neighbor is assigned to the default cluster. Configure cluster-id proactively to be able to define a cluster strategy for upcoming route-reflectors.
bestPracticeRecommendation:
If the router is configured as a route-reflector then configure cluster-id as shown below:

conf t
router bgp xxx
bgp cluster-id yyyy
bestPracticeTitle: BGP Cluster-id not configured on a Route reflector

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
The BFD  commands have to be explicitly mentioned in the global configuration mode and removing the hello and dead interval at the interface and ospf level
router ospf 1
 bfd minimum-interval 150
 bfd multiplier 3
 no dead-interval 20
 no hello-interval 5
 area 0.0.0.0
  !
  interface TenGigE0/0/0/0
   bfd minimum-interval 150
   bfd fast-detect
   bfd multiplier 3
   no dead-interval 20
   no hello-interval 5
  !
  interface TenGigE0/0/0/1
   bfd minimum-interval 150
   bfd fast-detect
   bfd multiplier 3
   no dead-interval 20
   no hello-interval 5
bestPracticeDescription: BFD (bidirectional failure detection) has been widely deployed as the preferred failure detection mechanism. It's a best practice to use BFD, instead of non-default OSPF hello-interval and dead-interval timers, to minimize failure detection time, and improve the overall network convergence.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Instead of non-default OSPF hello-interval and dead-interval timers, to minimize failure detection time BFD should be used with OSPF
bestPracticeTitle: Non-default Hello and Dead Timers used instead of BFD in OSPF

bestPracticeCaveat: Networks may be designed to run both rapid-pvst or mst during transition stages, but a stable network should be designed around one paradigm only.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure all switches in the domain to run either "spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst" or "spanning-tree mode mst" depending on the design requirements.
bestPracticeDescription: The legacy Spanning Tree Protocol definition of the original 802.1D standard should no longer be used in modern networks because they cause networks to converge much more slowly than with the changes introduced by the 802.1w RSTP amendment. Not only that, but the enhancements of UplinkFast and BackboneFast that are included in 802.1w and subsequently 802.1s (MST) make the new modes much more robust and help with ensuring loop-free topologies while aiding in fast convergence.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends that spanning Tree Protocol networks should be running the spanning-tree mode that provides the latest loop-avoidance enhancements and rapid timers for faster convergence. These are found either in the rapid-pvst or the mst modes of operation.
bestPracticeTitle: Legacy 802.1D STP instead of RSTP

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Example for configuring routing level while enabling IS-IS

router(config)# router isis isp
router(config-isis)# net 47.0004.004d.0001.0001.0c11.1110.00
router(config-isis)# is-type level-2 only

Please use level-1 or level-1-2 or level-2 only as required for the network.
bestPracticeDescription: The following are the steps to enable IS-IS and configure the routing level for an area: 1. router isis instance-id 2. net network-entity-title 3. is-type { level-1 | level-1-2 | level-2 only } Configuring the routing level in Step 3 is optional, but is highly recommended to establish the proper level of adjacency.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to configure the routing level for IS-IS, to establish the proper level of adjacency.
bestPracticeTitle: Routing Level Not Configured for IS-IS

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Using "show logging" instead of "terminal monitor" Exec command
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco IOS XR Software allows administrators to send log messages to monitor sessions. Monitor sessions are interactive management sessions in which the EXEC command, terminal monitor, is issued to the console. The use of the terminal monitor command is not recommended because it can increase the CPU load of a Cisco IOS XR device. Instead, administrators are advised to send logging information to the local log buffer that can be viewed by issuing the show logging command.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Administrators are advised to send logging information to the local log buffer that can be viewed by issuing the show logging command.
bestPracticeTitle: Terminal monitor should not be configured

bestPracticeCaveat: A PM statistics collection first gathers statistics from all the attributes associated with all the instances of an entity in the PM system. It then exports the statistical data in the binary file format to a TFTP server.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
router# configure
router(config)# performance-mgmt statistics xx default
bestPracticeDescription: Use the performance-mgmt statistics command to create a PM statistics collection template. You can define multiple templates for any given entity; however, only one PM statistics collection template for a given entity can be enabled at a time. Specifying a small sample interval increases CPU utilization, where as specifying a large sample size increases memory utilization. The sample size and sample interval, therefore, may need to be adjusted to prevent system overload. PM statistics collection template guidelines: When configuring a template, you must name the template. You can designate the template for the entity as the default template using the default keyword or name the template with the template keyword and template-name argument. The default template contains the following default settings: ? A sample interval of 10 minutes. ? A sample size of five sampling operations The sample interval sets the frequency of the sampling operations performed during the sampling cycle. You can configure the sample interval with the sample-interval keyword and minutes argument. The range is from 1 to 60 minutes. The default is 10 minutes. Syntax Description entity Specify an entity for which you want to create a statistics template: bgp ?Creates a statistics collection template for Border Gateway Protocol (BGP). interface basic-counters ?Creates a statistics collection template for basic counters. interface data-rates ?Creates a statistics collection template for data rates. interface generic-counters ?Creates a statistics collection template for generic counters. mpls ldp ?Applies a template for monitoring an MPLS Label Distribution Protocol (LDP) neighbor. node cpu ?Creates a statistics collection template for the central processing unit (CPU). node memory ?Creates a statistics collection template for memory utilization. node process ?Creates a statistics collection template for processes. ospf v2protocol ?Creates a statistics template for Open Shortest Path First v2 (OSPFv2) protocol instances. ospf v3protocol ?Creates a statistics template for OSPFv3 protocol instances. template Specifies that a template will be used for collection. template-name A template name can be any combination of alphanumeric characters, and may include the underscore character (_). Use the show running performance-mgmt to display information about templates, and to display the templates that are being used. default Applies the settings of the default template. The default template contains the following statistics and values. Values are in minutes. Each entity has a default template. In each default template, the sample interval is 10 minutes, and the default sample count is 5. sample-size size (Optional) Sets the number of samples to be taken. sample-interval minutes (Optional) Sets the frequency of each sample, in minutes. history-persistent (Optional) Maintains the history of statistics collections persistently. regular-expressionregular-expression-group-name (Optional) Sets instance filtering by regular expression.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use appropriate Sample-interval to prevent system overload.
bestPracticeTitle: Performance-Mgmt Sample Interval is Not Optimal

bestPracticeCaveat:
This is actions is for Segment routing traffic engineering where PCE's are used. Below are commands to verify:

segment-routing
 traffic-eng
  pcc
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
By default PCE-PCC communication assumes pce-centric mode, to change that the following config snippet is required:

PCC:
segment-routing
 traffic-eng
  pcc
   redundancy pcc-centric
bestPracticeDescription: Recommended redundancy mode for SR-PCE is pcc-centric. Let?s assume a PCC is configured to have PCEP sessions with two SR-PCEs. SR-PCE with higher configured preference becomes the primary, and the other one becomes a backup SR-PCE. Whenever a PCC needs to request a path computation from SR-PCE it sends such a request only to the primary SR-PCE. Strictly speaking, a PCC delegates a path computation to primary SR-PCE. It is primary SR-PCE, which does the actual path computation and responds back to PCC with resulting SID-list. Next, the PCC reports the instantiated SR-TE policy and actual path it is using for this policy to both, primary and backup SR-PCE. This mechanism synchronizes SR-TE policies databases on primary and backup SR-PCEs. No additional configuration is required for this synchronization to occur, which means it is not recommended to configure ?pce state-sync ipv4 ?, if the sole purpose of using this command to sync the state between SR-PCEs. The meaning of this command is to prevent split-brain scenarios for disjoint path computation. The delegation of SR-TE policy is indicated by a special bit in PCEP report message. Only report to a primary SR-PCE should have this bit set. This bit is managed by SR-PCE in the pce-centric model and is managed by PCC in the pcc-centric model. In a pce-centric model in order to re-delegate a policy to another, backup SR-PCE, the primary SR-PCE needs to release delegation and another SR-PCE to request it. Which can cause an outage depending on the type of a failure primary SR-PCE experiences. On the other hand, in pcc-centric model it is PCC who controls delegation. As soon as PCC detects that primary SR-PCE is gone, it re-sends a report to a backup SR-PCE with delegation bit set, which tells a backup SR-PCE that it is now responsible for any path updates from now on.
bestPracticeRecommendation: By default PCE-PCC communication assumes pce-centric mode, Cisco Recommends redundancy be pcc-centric
bestPracticeTitle: PCC-Centric Redundancy is Not Used for SR-PCE

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure 'isis metric' on interfaces that are part of isis network.
bestPracticeDescription: It is recommend that you configure the metrics on all IS-IS enabled interfaces. If you do not do so, all links will have the same cost and the cost to reach any node in the network will be logically equivalent to the number of hops.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Metric value should be updated on all interfaces to incorporate appropriate wights to make routing optimal for the network.
bestPracticeTitle: IS-IS Metric Not Configured on Interface

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Configure "backup device {username username } {password {0 | 7} password}" in pnp profile.
bestPracticeDescription: Cisco Open Plug-n-Play agent is a software application running on a Cisco IOS or IOS-XE device. When a device is powered on for the first time, the Open Plug-n-Play agent discovery process, which is embedded in the device, wakes up in the absence of the startup configuration file and attempts to discover the address of the Open Plug-n-Play server. The Open Plug-n-Play agent uses methods like DHCP, Domain Name System (DNS), and others to acquire the desired IP address of the Open Plug-n-Play server. The Open Plug-n-Play agent converges existing solutions into a unified agent and adds functionality to enhance the current deployment solutions. Every profile can have one primary server and a backup server configuration. The PnP agent attempts to initiate a connection with the primary server first and if it fails, it will try the backup server. If the backup server fails, the PnP agent will attempt to connect to the primary server again. This will continue until a connection is established with one of the servers.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco suggests customers to use both primary and backup server for PnP agent.
bestPracticeTitle: Backup device not configured for PNP Profile

bestPracticeCaveat: gRPC is supported in only 64-bit platforms.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: TLS is enabled by default. Non-TLS mode should only be used in secure internal network. Remove no-tls configuration under grpc.
bestPracticeDescription: gRPC is an open-source RPC framework. It is based on Protocol Buffers (Protobuf), which is an open source binary serialization protocol. gRPC provides a flexible, efficient, automated mechanism for serializing structured data, like XML, but is smaller and simpler to use. The user needs to define the structure by defining protocol buffer message types in .proto files. Each protocol buffer message is a small logical record of information, containing a series of name-value pairs. gRPC encodes requests and responses in binary. gRPC is extensible to other content types along with Protobuf. The Protobuf binary data object in gRPC is transported over HTTP/2.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to configure TLS before enabling gRPC. Enabling gRPC protocol uses the default HTTP/2 transport with no TLS enabled on TCP. gRPC mandates AAA authentication and authorization for all gRPC requests. If TLS is not configured, the authentication credentials are transferred over the network unencrypted. Non-TLS mode can only be used in secure internal network.
bestPracticeTitle: Disabling TLS for GRPC is not recommended

bestPracticeCaveat: gRPC is supported in only 64-bit platforms.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction: Remove 'protocol grpc no-tls' configuration for the Telemetry Destination-group configuration. If only the protocol grpc is configured without tls option, tls is enabled by default and tls-hostname defaults to the IP address of the destination.
bestPracticeDescription: Streaming telemetry provides a mechanism to select data of interest from IOS XR routers and to transmit it in a structured format to remote management stations for monitoring. This mechanism enables automatic tuning of the network based on real-time data, which is crucial for its seamless operation. The finer granularity and higher frequency of data available through telemetry enables better performance monitoring and therefore, better troubleshooting. It helps a more service-efficient bandwidth utilization, link utilization, risk assessment and control, remote monitoring and scalability. Streaming telemetry, thus, converts the monitoring process into a Big Data proposition that enables the rapid extraction and analysis of massive data sets to improve decision-making.  In a dial-out mode, the router initiates a session to the destinations based on the subscription. All 64-bit IOS XR platforms (except for NCS 6000 series routers) support gRPC and TCP protocols. gRPC protocol supports TLS and model-driven telemetry uses TLS to dial-out by default. The certificate must be copied to /misc/config/grpc/dialout/. To by-pass the TLS option, use protocol grpc no-tls.
bestPracticeRecommendation: It is recommended to use TLS for Telemetry. If TLS is disabled,the authentication credentials are transferred over the network unencrypted. Non-TLS mode can only be used in secure internal network.
bestPracticeTitle: No-TLS is not recommended for Telemetry Destination-Group

bestPracticeCaveat: This applicable to only VPDN scenarios.
bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
Sets the MSS value of TCP packets going through a Cisco ASR 9000 Series Router for a LAC subscriber. The TCP MSS Adjust maximum segment size ranges from 1280 to 1460 (in bytes).
router(config)# configure
router(config)# vpdn
router(config-vpdn)# l2tp tcp-mss-adjust 1420
bestPracticeDescription: The TCP MSS Adjustment feature allows the configuration of the maximum segment size (MSS) on transient packets that traverse a Cisco ASR 9000 Series Router. When dealing with PPPoE or L2TP cases, an additional header that the client initiating a TCP session may not be aware of is added to the packet. This can result in lost packets, broken transmissions, or fragmentation when packet sizes exceed the maximum transmission units (MTUs) due to the added headers. When the HTTP server picks up a large file, it segments it into 1460 byte chunks (assuming that there are no http headers for now). When the HTTP server sends the packet, the first Cisco ASR 9000 Series Router (on the right) detects that the MTU is 576 downstream to the client and requires a 1300 byte packet to be fragmented. If the server sets the DF ("don't fragment") bit, then the packet is dropped. And, if the packet does not have the DF bit set, then it gets fragmented, requiring the client to reassemble the packets. In digital subscriber line (DSL) or fibre-to-the-home (FTTH) like access, a CPE may block incoming fragments as a security mechanism, causing this transmission to be lost. In a typical scenario, having packets that are dropped causes partial downloads, an obstruction, or a delay in displaying images in web pages. MSS adjust overcomes this scenario by intercepting the TCP SYN packet, reading the MSS option, and adjusting the value so that the server does not send packets larger than the configured size (plus headers). In the case of L2TP, an extra 40 bytes is added to the packet. The recommended MSS adjust values are 1420 for L2TP scenarios, assuming a minimum MTU of 1500 end-to-end.
bestPracticeRecommendation: Cisco recommends to set the recommended MSS adjust values as 1420 for L2TP scenarios, assuming a minimum MTU of 1500 end-to-end.
bestPracticeTitle: Recommended MSS Adjust Values Not Set For L2TP

bestPracticeCorrectiveAction:
The auto-IP feature assigns an IP address in the format 10.x.y.1 automatically, where:

? y is the bottom 8 bits (the rest) of the satellite ID
? show run nv satellite XXXX
? nv

  type asr9000v
!

transceiver permit pid all
nv
  satellite-fabric-link satellite XXXX
   remote-ports GigabitEthernet X/X/X-X
 !
!
!
show run nv satellite XXX
nv

type ncs5002
!
Interface HundredGigE0/1/0/0
nv
